We jointly evaluate models by their ability to play the games, match human learning behavior, and predict brain activity during the same task, comparing a suite of frontier Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) against model-free and model-based deep reinforcement learning agents and a Bayesian theory-based agent. We find that frontier LRMs most closely match human behavioral patterns during game discovery and predict brain activity an order of magnitude better than both reinforcement learning alternatives across cortical and subcortical regions, with effects robust to permutation controls. Through targeted manipulations, we further show that brain alignment reflects the model's in-context representation of the game state rather than its downstream planning or reasoning. Our results establish LRMs as compelling computational accounts of human learning and decision making in complex, naturalistic environments. Project page with interactive replays: https://botcs.github.io/reason-to-play/

</details>


### [79] [MPD$^2$-Router: Mask-aware Multi-expert Prior-regularized Dual-head Deferral Router in Glaucoma Screening and Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08024)
*Wenxin Zhan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MPD²-Router is a mask-aware multi-expert deferral framework for glaucoma screening that intelligently routes cases to either AI or available human experts based on case difficulty, uncertainty, and expert availability to minimize clinical costs while maintaining diagnostic accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard learning-to-defer formulations for glaucoma screening overlook critical real-world constraints: expert availability, heterogeneous reader behavior, workload imbalance, asymmetric diagnostic harm, case difficulty from morphology, and deployment shifts between training and real-world settings.

Method: The framework uses a dual-head deferral/allocation policy with mask-aware Gumbel-sigmoid gating that strictly enforces per-sample expert availability. It fuses uncertainty, morphology, image-quality, and out-of-distribution signals. Training employs an asymmetric cost-sensitive objective with augmented-Lagrangian deferral budget, group-specific distribution prior, and rank-majorization JS regularizer to prevent expert collapse without forcing uniform allocation.

Result: Across three cross-national glaucoma cohorts (REFUGE, CHAKSU, ORIGA) with a frozen backbone, MPD²-Router substantially lowers clinical cost and improves Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) over AI-only at moderate deferral rates. It achieves Pareto-optimal trade-offs in F1-MCC-cost, shows robustness under cross-domain shift, and yields balanced expert utilization.

Conclusion: MPD²-Router effectively addresses key limitations of standard L2D frameworks by modeling real-world constraints in ophthalmology triage, providing a practical solution for safe and cost-effective glaucoma screening through intelligent human-AI routing that accounts for expert availability and case characteristics.

Abstract: Learning-to-defer (L2D) can make glaucoma screening safer by routing difficult/uncertain cases to humans, yet standard formulations overlook expert availability, heterogeneous readers behavior, workload imbalance, asymmetric diagnostic harm, case difficulty from morphology and deployment shift. We introduce MPD$^2$-Router, a mask-aware multi-expert deferral framework that recasts ophthalmic triage as constrained human--AI routing: whether to defer and to which available expert. It couples a dual-head deferral/allocation policy with mask-aware Gumbel--sigmoid gating that strictly enforces per-sample availability, and fuses uncertainty, morphology, image-quality, and OOD signals. Training uses an asymmetric cost-sensitive objective with an augmented-Lagrangian deferral budget, a group-specific distribution prior, and a rank-majorization JS regularizer that jointly prevent expert collapse without forcing uniform allocation. Across three cross-national glaucoma cohorts (REFUGE, CHAKSU, ORIGA) with a frozen REFUGE-trained backbone, MPD$^2$-Router substantially lowers clinical cost and improves MCC over AI-only at a moderate deferral rate. It is Pareto-optimal in F1--MCC--cost, robust under cross-domain shift, and yields balanced expert utilization.

</details>


### [80] [Rubric-Grounded RL: Structured Judge Rewards for Generalizable Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08061)
*Manish Bhattarai,Ismael Boureima,Nishath Rajiv Ranasinghe,Scott Pakin,Dan O'Malley*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces rubric-grounded reinforcement learning, where an LLM is trained using multi-criteria rewards based on scientific document rubrics, showing improved performance on both rubric-specific tasks and transfer to other reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional reinforcement learning often uses binary or single holistic scores, which lack granular feedback. The paper argues for decomposing rewards into weighted, verifiable criteria to provide richer optimization signals and improve training efficiency.

Method: Proposed rubric-grounded RL framework: 1) Decompose reward into weighted, verifiable criteria, 2) Use frozen LLM judge to score responses along multiple task-specific criteria, 3) Derive rubrics from OSTI corpus of ~100k scientific documents, 4) Train Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).

Result: GRPO-tuned model achieves 71.7% normalized reward on held-out rubric evaluation. The policy also improves over base model on four reasoning benchmarks: GSM8K, MATH, GPQA Main, and GPQA Diamond, showing transferable reasoning skills.

Conclusion: Structured, document-grounded rewards can improve performance on held-out rubric tasks and induce transferable reasoning behaviors beyond the training corpus, validating the effectiveness of rubric-grounded RL for language model optimization.

Abstract: We argue that decomposing reward into weighted, verifiable criteria and using an LLM judge to score them provides a partial-credit optimization signal: instead of a binary outcome or a single holistic score, each response is graded along multiple task-specific criteria. We formalize \emph{rubric-grounded reinforcement learning (RL)}: a framework in which the policy is optimized against a structured, multi-criterion reward produced by a frozen LLM judge that conditions on auxiliary grounding the policy never sees. We instantiate the framework by deriving rubrics from an Office of Scientific and Technical Information (OSTI)-derived corpus of roughly 100,000 scientific and technical documents and training Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). With GRPO-based training, the model achieves $71.7\%$ normalized reward on held-out rubric evaluation. The GRPO-tuned policy also improves over the base model on four reasoning benchmarks not derived from the training corpus -- GSM8K, MATH, GPQA Main, and GPQA Diamond. These results provide evidence that structured, document-grounded rewards can improve held-out rubric performance and induce transferable reasoning behaviors beyond the corpus used to construct the training environment.

</details>


### [81] [VecCISC: Improving Confidence-Informed Self-Consistency with Reasoning Trace Clustering and Candidate Answer Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08070)
*James Petullo,Sonny George,Dylan Cashman,Nianwen Xue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VecCISC is a lightweight framework that reduces the computational cost of weighted majority voting in LLM self-consistency by filtering semantically similar reasoning traces before confidence scoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Weighted majority voting (like CISC) improves accuracy over standard self-consistency but requires expensive critic LLM calls on each candidate answer to produce confidence scores, greatly increasing overhead and cost.

Method: VecCISC uses semantic similarity measures to filter reasoning traces that are semantically equivalent, degenerate, or hallucinated, reducing the number of candidate answers that must be evaluated by the critic LLM.

Result: Evaluation on five challenging datasets spanning mathematics, chemistry, biology, commonsense reasoning, and humanities shows VecCISC reduces total token usage by 47% while maintaining or exceeding CISC's accuracy.

Conclusion: VecCISC provides an effective way to reduce the computational expense of weighted majority voting techniques while preserving their performance benefits, making them more practical for real-world deployment.

Abstract: A standard technique for scaling inference-time reasoning is Self-Consistency, whereby multiple candidate answers are sampled from an LLM and the most common answer is selected. More recently, it has been shown that weighted majority voting (e.g. Confidence-Informed Self Consistency (CISC)), which assigns a confidence value to each candidate answer and chooses the answer with the largest accumulated score, tends to be more accurate on a wide range of popular benchmarks. In practice, weighted majority voting necessitates calling a critic LLM on each candidate's reasoning trace to produce the answer's confidence score. This secondary series of LLM calls greatly increases the overhead and cost of weighted majority voting, despite its potential performance benefits. To reduce this expense, we propose VecCISC, a lightweight, adaptive framework that uses a measure of semantic similarity to filter reasoning traces that are semantically equivalent to others, degenerate, or hallucinated, thus decreasing the number of candidate answers that must be evaluated by the critic. To ensure adequate experimental thoroughness, we evaluate VecCISC on five challenging, widely-adopted datasets spanning the domains of mathematics, chemistry, biology, commonsense reasoning, and the humanities. Our results demonstrate that VecCISC reduces the total token usage by 47%, while maintaining or exceeding the accuracy of CISC.

</details>


### [82] [XDecomposer: Learning Prior-Free Set Decomposition for Multiphase X-ray Diffraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05866)
*Hanyu Gao,Bin Cao,Yunyue Su,Tong-Yi Zhang,Qiang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: XDecomposer is a prior-free framework that jointly decomposes and identifies multiphase XRD patterns without requiring candidate phase lists or prior knowledge of phase numbers, using a set prediction approach to infer phase-resolved components, mixture proportions, and structural representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multiphase powder X-ray diffraction analysis is a fundamental bottleneck in structure identification because real-world synthesis produces complex mixtures whose constituent phases cannot be reliably disentangled. Existing approaches assume single-phase inputs and break down in multiphase settings.

Method: The authors formulate multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem where the model infers an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their mixture proportions, and corresponding structural representations. They use a phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism with diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction to enable accurate source separation while preserving crystallographic fidelity.

Result: Extensive experiments on both simulated and experimental datasets show that XDecomposer substantially improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems while maintaining strong generalization to unseen mixtures.

Conclusion: XDecomposer provides a practical route toward data-driven, source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis and reduces long-standing dependence on prior-guided iterative phase matching, with open-source code available.

Abstract: Multiphase powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis remains a fundamental bottleneck in structure identification, as real-world synthesis often produces complex mixtures whose constituent phases (components) cannot be reliably disentangled. While recent advances in representation-based crystal retrieval and generation suggest the possibility of inferring structures directly from PXRD, existing approaches largely assume single-phase inputs and break down in multiphase settings. Here, we present XDecomposer, a prior-free framework for joint decomposition and identification of multiphase XRD patterns without requiring candidate phase lists, structural templates, or prior knowledge of phase number. We formulate multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem, where the model infers an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their mixture proportions, and corresponding structural representations within a unified architecture. A phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism, together with diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction, enables accurate source separation while preserving crystallographic fidelity. Extensive experiments on both simulated and experimental datasets show that XDecomposer substantially improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems, while maintaining strong generalization to unseen mixtures. These results provide a practical route toward data-driven, source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis and reduce long-standing dependence on prior-guided iteratively phase matching. The code is openly available at https://github.com/Licht0812/XDecomposer

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [83] [RateQuant: Optimal Mixed-Precision KV Cache Quantization via Rate-Distortion Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06675)
*Fei Zuo,Zikang Zhou,Hao Cong,Xiaoyan Xi,Ho Fai Leung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RateQuant addresses KV cache quantization inefficiencies by modeling per-quantizer distortion rates and solving bit allocation via reverse waterfilling, achieving significant perplexity improvements without inference overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current KV cache quantization methods waste memory by uniformly allocating bits across all attention heads despite varying importance. Mixed-precision allocation fails due to distortion model mismatch between different quantizers.

Method: RateQuant calibrates per-quantizer distortion models from small datasets, then uses reverse waterfilling from rate-distortion theory to optimally allocate bits based on head importance.

Result: On Qwen3-8B at 2.5 average bits, RateQuant reduces KIVI's perplexity from 49.3 to 14.9 (70% reduction) and improves QuaRot by 6.6 PPL with 1.6s calibration time and zero inference overhead.

Conclusion: Distortion model mismatch explains mixed-precision quantization failures. RateQuant's calibration-based approach enables optimal bit allocation, significantly improving performance while maintaining efficiency.

Abstract: Large language models cache all previously computed key-value (KV) pairs during generation, and this KV cache grows linearly with sequence length, making it a primary memory bottleneck for serving. Quantizing the KV cache to fewer bits reduces this cost, yet all current quantizers assign the same bit-width to every attention head, ignoring the large variation in head importance. A natural idea is to allocate more bits to important heads and fewer to the rest. We show, however, that such mixed-precision allocation has a hidden pitfall: each quantizer follows a different distortion curve D(b)=alpha*beta^{-b}, and the decay rate beta varies from 3.6 to 5.3 across quantizer designs. Applying one quantizer's distortion model to another inverts the allocation order and makes performance worse than uniform quantization. We call this failure mode distortion model mismatch and propose RateQuant to resolve it. RateQuant fits a per-quantizer distortion model from a small calibration set, then solves the resulting bit-allocation problem in closed form via reverse waterfilling from rate-distortion theory. On Qwen3-8B at 2.5 average bits, calibrated RateQuant reduces KIVI's perplexity from 49.3 to 14.9 (70% reduction) and improves QuaRot by 6.6 PPL. The entire calibration takes 1.6 s on a single GPU and adds zero overhead at inference time.

</details>


### [84] [LKV: End-to-End Learning of Head-wise Budgets and Token Selection for LLM KV Cache Eviction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06676)
*Enshuai Zhou,Yifan Hao,Chao Wang,Rui Zhang,Di Huang,Jiaming Guo,Xing Hu,Zidong Du,Qi Guo,Yunji Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LKV introduces learned KV eviction, an end-to-end differentiable optimization method for KV cache compression that outperforms heuristic approaches, achieving near-lossless performance with only 15% KV cache retention on LongBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context inference in LLMs is bottlenecked by the linear growth of KV cache memory, and existing KV cache compression paradigms are fundamentally limited by heuristics, causing resource misallocation and relying on static biases rather than task objectives.

Method: LKV integrates two components: LKV-H to learn task-optimized global budgets, and LKV-T to derive intrinsic KV importance without materializing attention matrices, formulating KV compression as an end-to-end differentiable optimization problem.

Result: Extensive evaluations on LongBench and RULER benchmarks show LKV achieves state-of-the-art performance at high compression rates, achieving near-lossless performance on LongBench with only 15% KV cache retention, with learned budgeting identified as the dominant driver of fidelity.

Conclusion: LKV demonstrates that learned, task-optimized KV cache compression is superior to heuristic approaches, with learned budgeting being the key factor for achieving near-lossless performance at high compression rates.

Abstract: Long-context inference in Large Language Models (LLMs) is bottlenecked by the linear growth of Key-Value (KV) cache memory. Existing KV cache compression paradigms are fundamentally limited by heuristics: heuristic budgeting relies on statistical priors rather than task objectives, causing resource misallocation, while heuristic selection relies on coupled query-key interactions or static inductive biases (e.g., attention sinks). To address this limitation, we introduce LKV (Learned KV Eviction), which formulates KV compression as an end-to-end differentiable optimization problem. LKV integrates LKV-H to learn task-optimized global budgets, and LKV-T to derive intrinsic KV importance without materializing attention matrices. This design bypasses heuristic proxies, strictly aligning compression with task objectives. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that LKV achieves state-of-the-art performance on both LongBench and RULER benchmarks at high compression rates. In particular, on LongBench, LKV achieves near-lossless performance with only 15\% KV cache retention. Crucially, our analysis identifies learned budgeting as the dominant driver of fidelity, demonstrating that data-driven allocation is essential to overcome the limitations of hand-crafted heuristics.

</details>


### [85] [A Wasserstein GAN-based climate scenario generator for risk management and insurance: the case of soil subsidence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06678)
*Antoine Heranval,Olivier Lopez,Didier Ngatcha,Daniel Nkameni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AI framework (SwiGAN) uses Conditional GANs to simulate future drought patterns (Soil Wetness Index) up to 2050, helping insurers adapt to climate change risks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increasing economic costs of natural disasters, particularly droughts which account for ~30% of indemnities in France's natural catastrophe insurance scheme, necessitate long-term adaptive strategies beyond the one-year horizon of current regulations like Solvency II. There is a need for tools to simulate future climate-related risks to inform insurance and risk management.

Method: The paper introduces SwiGAN, a Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (Conditional GAN) framework designed to generate future spatio-temporal trajectories of the Soil Wetness Index (SWI), a key drought indicator in France. The model simulates plausible drought propagation patterns up to 2050 for a region highly exposed to drought.

Result: SwiGAN successfully generates realistic sequences of SWI maps, providing insights into drought dynamics under climate change scenarios. This supports the design of adaptive risk management and insurance strategies. The methodology is also generalizable to other climate perils and actuarial applications like economic scenario generation.

Conclusion: SwiGAN offers a novel AI-driven approach for simulating future drought patterns, aiding insurers in developing adaptive strategies and long-term risk management plans under climate change.

Abstract: According to the United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (2025), the average annual cost of natural catastrophes increased from 70--80 billion USD between 1970 and 2000 to 180--200 billion USD between 2001 and 2020. Reports from organizations such as the IFOA and the WWF highlight the need for the insurance sector to adapt to this rapidly evolving context by developing medium- to long-term strategies that go beyond the one-year horizon of prudential regulations such as Solvency II. This paper introduces an artificial intelligence framework based on Conditional Generative Adversarial Networks (Conditional GANs) to generate future spatio-temporal trajectories of climatic indices. The approach focuses on the Soil Wetness Index (SWI), a key indicator used in France to assess drought severity. Drought accounts for approximately 30% of the indemnities paid under the French natural catastrophe insurance scheme. The proposed model, SwiGAN, simulates plausible drought propagation patterns up to 2050 for a region of France particularly exposed to this hazard. By generating realistic sequences of SWI maps, SwiGAN provides insights into drought dynamics under climate change scenarios and supports the design of adaptive risk management and insurance strategies. The methodology is also generalizable to other climate-related perils and actuarial applications such as economic scenario generation.

</details>


### [86] [Breaking the Illusion: When Positive Meets Negative in Multimodal Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06679)
*Yubo Jiang,Yitong An,Xin Yang,Abudukelimu Wuerkaixi,Xuxin Cheng,Fengying Xie,Zhiguo Jiang,Cao Liu,Ke Zeng,Haopeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A training-free inference framework called Positive-and-Negative Decoding (PND) that reduces object hallucination in Vision-Language Models by amplifying visual features and penalizing linguistic priors during decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-Language Models often generate content contradicting visual reality (object hallucination) due to over-reliance on linguistic priors and attention imbalance where visual features are under-weighted.

Method: PND introduces a dual-path contrast during decoding: a positive path amplifies visual evidence while a negative path constructs counterfactuals to penalize prior-dominant generation. The outputs from both paths are contrasted to steer generation toward visually grounded results.

Result: Experiments on POPE, MME, and CHAIR benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance without requiring retraining, showing improved visual fidelity.

Conclusion: PND provides an effective training-free approach to mitigate object hallucination in VLMs by addressing attention imbalance and enforcing visual fidelity through contrastive decoding.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are frequently undermined by object hallucination, generating content that contradicts visual reality, due to an over-reliance on linguistic priors. We introduce Positive-and-Negative Decoding (PND), a training-free inference framework that intervenes directly in the decoding process to enforce visual fidelity. PND is motivated by our finding of an attention imbalance in VLMs, where visual features are under-weighted. Our framework introduces a dual-path contrast: a positive path that amplifies visual evidence and a negative path that constructs counterfactuals to penalize prior-dominant generation. By contrasting outputs from both paths during decoding, PND steers generation toward visually grounded results. Experiments on POPE, MME, and CHAIR demonstrate state-of-the-art performance without retraining.

</details>


### [87] [On the Role of Strain and Vorticity in Numerical Integration Error for Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06680)
*Chenxi Tao,Seung-Kyum Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes integration error in flow matching models, showing that strain rate causes exponential error amplification while vorticity only causes linear error, leading to Jacobian regularization methods that reduce error 2.7x at 5 integration steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flow matching models require numerical integration of learned velocity fields, where the number of integration steps (NFE) directly impacts inference cost. However, understanding what properties of the velocity field affect integration error is crucial for improving efficiency without sacrificing quality.

Method: The authors decompose the velocity Jacobian into symmetric strain rate (S) and antisymmetric vorticity (Omega) components. They prove strain controls exponential error amplification via logarithmic norm, while vorticity contributes only linearly to local truncation error. They then propose weighted Jacobian regularization with separate weights alpha for strain and beta for vorticity.

Result: Experiments on 2D synthetic data show up to 2.7x lower integration error at NFE=5 compared to baseline. Preliminary CIFAR-10 experiments show consistent trends, with lightweight fine-tuning improving FID by 14% at NFE=10 while preserving high-NFE quality. Theoretical analysis shows optimal transport velocity fields are irrotational with zero material derivative.

Conclusion: Different components of the velocity Jacobian play distinct roles in integration error: strain causes exponential error growth while vorticity causes only linear error. Weighted Jacobian regularization based on this understanding can significantly reduce integration error and improve inference efficiency in flow matching models.

Abstract: Flow matching generates data by integrating a learned velocity field, where the number of integration steps (NFE) directly determines inference cost. We analyze which properties of the velocity field govern integration error by decomposing the velocity Jacobian into its symmetric part S (strain rate) and antisymmetric part Omega (vorticity). We prove that strain and vorticity play different roles: strain controls exponential error amplification through the logarithmic norm, while vorticity contributes only linearly to the local truncation error. We further show that the optimal transport velocity field is irrotational and has zero material derivative, implying second-order Euler accuracy; for exact displacement interpolation, the associated Lagrangian particle dynamics are integrated exactly by Euler. Motivated by this analysis, we study weighted Jacobian regularization with strain weight alpha and vorticity weight beta. Experiments on 2D synthetic data confirm the main theoretical predictions, showing up to 2.7x lower integration error at NFE=5. Preliminary CIFAR-10 experiments show consistent trends, with a lightweight fine-tuning procedure improving FID by 14 percent at NFE=10 while preserving high-NFE quality.

</details>


### [88] [A Hierarchical Ensemble Pipeline for Anomaly Detection in ESA Satellite Telemetry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06681)
*Lorenzo Riccardo Allegrini,Geremia Pompei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hierarchical ensemble pipeline for anomaly detection in ESA satellite telemetry data that combines shapelet and statistical features with multi-level modeling and validation strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need to detect subtle anomalies in multivariate telemetry data from European Space Agency satellites, requiring robust methods that can handle complex time-series patterns and prevent information leakage during validation.

Method: Hierarchical ensemble pipeline integrating shapelet-based and statistical feature extraction, per-channel modeling, intra-channel stacking, and final cross-channel aggregation. Uses time-series cross-validation with two-level masking strategies to prevent information leakage.

Result: Demonstrates strong generalization performance on the European Space Agency Anomaly Detection Benchmark (ESA-ADB) challenge, showing effectiveness in detecting subtle anomalies in realistic satellite telemetry.

Conclusion: Hierarchical modeling approaches are effective for anomaly detection in multivariate satellite telemetry data, with the proposed ensemble pipeline showing promising generalization capabilities for practical space applications.

Abstract: A hierarchical ensemble pipeline is introduced to address anomaly detection in multivariate telemetry data provided by European Space Agency (ESA). The method integrates shapelet-based and statistical feature extraction, per-channel modeling, intra-channel stacking, and a final cross-channel aggregation. The pipeline is trained and validated using time-series cross-validation and two-level masking strategies to prevent information leakage. Results on the European Space Agency Anomaly Detection Benchmark (ESA-ADB) challenge demonstrate strong generalization, highlighting the effectiveness of hierarchical modeling in detecting subtle anomalies in realistic satellite telemetry.

</details>


### [89] [Toeplitz MLP Mixers are Low Complexity, Information-Rich Sequence Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06683)
*Benjamin L. Badger,Ethan Roland*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Toeplitz MLP Mixer (TMM) is a transformer-like architecture that replaces attention with triangular-masked Toeplitz matrix multiplication, achieving better computational efficiency (O(dn log n) time during training) while maintaining strong performance on information retention and in-context learning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformer-based LLMs are limited by quadratic time and space complexity of attention operations, which restricts their scalability and efficiency. There's a need for architectures that maintain performance while reducing computational overhead.

Method: Proposed Toeplitz MLP Mixer (TMM) replaces attention mechanism with triangular-masked Toeplitz matrix multiplication over sequence dimension. This yields O(dn log n) time and O(dn) space complexity during training, and O(dn) time/space at inference prefill. The architecture lacks sophisticated input modulation or state maintenance present in other sub-quadratic architectures.

Result: TMMs achieve greater training efficiency (loss per compute and memory), better copying ability, improved information retention, superior information retrieval and in-context learning benchmark accuracy compared to comparable architectures. Surprisingly, trained Toeplitz layers in causal non-invertible models are more likely to be invertible or nearly invertible than models that are actually invertible over inputs.

Conclusion: TMM architecture demonstrates that simpler, computationally efficient designs without attention can achieve competitive or superior performance on key tasks like information retention and in-context learning, challenging assumptions about necessary architectural complexity in transformer-like models.

Abstract: Transformer-based large language models are in some respects limited by the quadratic time and space computational complexity of attention. We introduce the Toeplitz MLP Mixer (TMM), a transformer-like architecture that swaps attention for triangular-masked Toeplitz matrix multiplication over the sequence dimension resulting in $\mathcal{O} (dn \log n)$ time and $\mathcal O(dn)$ space complexity during training and $\mathcal O(dn)$ time and space at inference prefill. Despite the lack of sophisticated input modulation or state maintenance present in other sub-quadratic architectures, TMMs yield greater training efficiency in terms of loss achieved per compute and device memory. We demonstrate that TMMs are capable of retaining more input information resulting in improved copying ability, which we argue results from a lack of architectural biases. Consistent with higher input information retention, TMMs exhibit superior information retrieval and in-context learning benchmark accuracy compared to comparable architectures. We conclude with an analysis from the perspective of operator index theory and show that, counterintuitively, trained Toeplitz layers of causal non-invertible models are more likely to be invertible or nearly so than models that are actually invertible over their inputs.

</details>


### [90] [From Canopy to Collision: A Hybrid Predictive Framework for Identifying Risk Factors in Tree-Involved Traffic Crashes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06684)
*Abdul Azim,Ahmed Hossain,Soumyadip Maitra,Panick Kalambay*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study develops an ML-based framework to identify risk factors for tree-involved crash severity using CRSS data, finding restraint non-use as the top predictor of severe outcomes with key interactions between factors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tree-involved crashes are particularly severe ROR collisions often resulting in fatal or incapacitating injuries, creating a need to systematically identify and quantify contributing risk factors to inform targeted safety interventions.

Method: Multi-step analytical framework using CRSS data (2020-2023): 1) CatBoost ML classification for binary injury severity (KA vs BC), 2) SHAP analysis to quantify factor importance, 3) binary logistic regression to validate SHAP results, 4) SHAP interaction plots for combined factor effects.

Result: Restraint non-use was the most influential predictor (unrestrained occupants ~3x more likely to experience severe outcomes). Vehicle age, speeding violations, and driver impairment showed substantial effects. Critical interactions identified between lighting/vehicle age, speeding/lighting, restraint use/vehicle age, and road surface/speeding.

Conclusion: Findings provide critical insights for Safe System interventions: enhanced seat belt enforcement, speed management in low visibility conditions, vehicle fleet modernization, and targeted safety measures addressing identified risk interactions to reduce tree-involved crash severity.

Abstract: Tree-involved crashes represent a critical subset of run-off-road (ROR) collisions, often resulting in fatal or severe injuries due to high-energy impacts. This study develops a comprehensive analytical framework to identify and quantify risk factors contributing to crash severity in tree-involved collisions using the Crash Report Sampling System (CRSS) database spanning 2020-2023. The modeling framework follows a multi-step process. First, a machine learning based classification model (CatBoost) identifies key factors associated with binary crash injury severity (KA: fatal or incapacitating injury versus BC: non-incapacitating or possible injury). Second, SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) tool is used to quantify and visualize the marginal effects of top influential factors on crash severity. Third, a binary logistic regression model estimates factor effects and validates SHAP-derived importance measures. Finally, SHAP interaction plots examine the combined effects of key contributing factors. Results reveal restraint non-use as the most influential predictor, with unrestrained occupants nearly three times more likely to experience severe outcomes due to ejection risk. Vehicle age, speeding violations, and driver impairment demonstrate substantial effects, reflecting reduced crashworthiness, increased impact forces, and reduced control capabilities. Critical interactions emerge between lighting conditions and vehicle age, speeding and lighting conditions, restraint use and vehicle age, and road surface and speeding, demonstrating additive risk effects with specific interactions. These findings provide critical insights for targeted safe system-based interventions, including enhanced seat belt enforcement, speed management in reduced visibility conditions, and vehicle fleet modernization.

</details>


### [91] [Conformal Agent Error Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06788)
*Naihe Feng,Yi Sui,Shiyi Hou,Ga Wu,Jesse C. Cresswell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a conformal prediction-based framework for error attribution in multi-agent systems, providing guaranteed coverage for identifying decisive errors and enabling automated rollback recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Error attribution in multi-agent systems (MAS) is challenging due to long interaction traces generated by LLM-based agents. Identifying where decisive errors occur is crucial for automated recovery, but current methods lack principled guarantees.

Method: Proposes a framework using conformal prediction (CP) with finite-sample, distribution-free coverage guarantees. Introduces new filtration-based CP algorithms for sequential data (agent trajectories) that predict contiguous sequence sets for efficient recovery.

Result: Theoretical guarantees verified on various agents and datasets. Errors can be precisely isolated, and prediction sets enable MAS rollback to correct their own errors. The approach is model-agnostic and provides principled uncertainty quantification.

Conclusion: The CP-based error attribution framework offers a principled uncertainty layer for MAS, enabling precise error isolation and automated recovery through rollback, addressing fundamental challenges in MAS reliability.

Abstract: When multi-agent systems (MAS) fail, identifying where the decisive error occurred is the first step for automated recovery to an earlier state. Error attribution remains a fundamental challenge due to the long interaction traces that large language model-based MAS generate. This paper presents a framework for error attribution based on conformal prediction (CP) which provides finite-sample, distribution-free coverage guarantees. We introduce new algorithms for filtration-based CP designed for sequential data such as agent trajectories. Unlike existing CP algorithms, our approach predicts sets that are contiguous sequences to enable efficient recovery and debugging. We verify our theoretical guarantees on a variety of agents and datasets, show that errors can be precisely isolated, then use prediction sets to rollback MAS to correct their own errors. Our overall approach is model-agnostic, and offers a principled uncertainty layer for MAS error attribution. We release code at https://github.com/layer6ai-labs/conformal-agent-error-attribution.

</details>


### [92] [Robustness of Refugee-Matching Gains to Off-Policy Evaluation Choices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06686)
*Kirk Bansak,Elisabeth Paulson,Dominik Rothenhäusler,Jeremy Ferwerda,Jens Hainmueller,Michael Hotard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates the stability of counterfactual impact evaluation results for refugee matching in the US using various off-policy evaluation methods, confirming consistency with previous findings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To validate the robustness and stability of counterfactual impact evaluation results in refugee matching contexts, building on previous research by Bansak et al. (2018) that investigated refugee matching for improving refugee outcomes.

Method: Employed multiple off-policy evaluation methods including inverse probability weighting (IPW), multiple variants of augmented inverse probability weighting (AIPW), alternative modeling architectures, and different assignment procedures to estimate counterfactual impact and test robustness.

Result: Impact estimates remained consistent in magnitude across all scenarios and statistically significant in most cases. The results aligned with those originally presented in Bansak et al. (2018), demonstrating stability of the findings.

Conclusion: The counterfactual impact evaluation results for refugee matching in the United States are robust and stable across various evaluation methods and modifications, confirming the reliability of previous findings on the potential benefits of refugee matching.

Abstract: Previous research has investigated the potential of refugee matching for boosting refugee outcomes, first considered by Bansak et al. (2018). This paper demonstrates the stability of counterfactual impact evaluation results in the context of refugee matching in the United States using a range of off-policy evaluation methods. In order to estimate counterfactual impact and test the robustness of our results, we employ several evaluation methods, including inverse probability weighting (IPW) and multiple variants of augmented inverse probability weighting (AIPW). We also consider various modifications, including alternative modeling architectures and different assignment procedures. The impact estimates remain consistent in magnitude in all scenarios as well as statistically significant in most cases. Furthermore, the estimates are also consistent with the results originally presented in Bansak et al. (2018).

</details>


### [93] [Conditional generation of antibody sequences with classifier-guided germline-absorbing discrete diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06720)
*Justin Sanders,Luca Giancardo,Lan Guo,Yue Zhao,Kemal Sonmez,Nina Cheng,Melih Yilmaz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Germline absorbing diffusion model improves antibody design by reducing germline bias and enabling flexible conditional generation for better binding and developability properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current protein language models for antibody design have two key limitations: they predominantly memorize germline sequences rather than modeling somatic variation, and offer limited support for flexible classifier-guided conditional generation.

Method: Two primary contributions: 1) discrete diffusion fine-tuning for strong language modeling on antibody sequences with flexible classifier conditioning, and 2) germline absorbing diffusion - a novel modification where germline sequence (not masked sequence) serves as absorbing state, restricting model to learn trajectory from germline to observed sequence.

Result: Germline diffusion improves non-germline residue prediction accuracy from 26% to 46%, approaching theoretical upper bound set by true biological variability. Model shows improved tradeoff between class adherence and sample quality on conditional generation tasks for improved hydrophobicity and predicted binding affinity, significantly outperforming EvoProtGrad.

Conclusion: Germline absorbing diffusion effectively mitigates germline bias in antibody design models and enables more flexible conditional generation for optimizing antibody properties, representing an advance over current protein language model approaches.

Abstract: Antibody therapeutics are among the most successful modern medicines, yet computationally designing antibodies with desirable binding and developability properties remains challenging. While protein language models (pLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for antibody sequence design, existing approaches largely suffer from two key limitations: they predominantly memorize germline sequences rather than modeling biologically meaningful somatic variation, and they offer limited support for flexible classifier-guided conditional generation. We address these challenges through two primary contributions. First, we demonstrate that discrete diffusion fine-tuning achieves strong language modeling performance on antibody sequences while allowing for generation conditioned on any off-the-shelf classifier. Second, we introduce germline absorbing diffusion, a novel modification of the discrete diffusion noise process in which the germline sequence - rather than a masked sequence - serves as the absorbing state. This biologically motivated inductive bias restricts the model to learning the trajectory from germline to observed sequence, effectively excluding genetic variation and V(D)J recombination statistics from the learned distribution and dramatically mitigating germline bias. We show that germline diffusion improves non-germline residue prediction accuracy from 26 percent to 46 percent, approaching the theoretical upper bound set by true biological variability. We then demonstrate the utility of our germline diffusion model on the conditional generation tasks of sampling antibodies with improved hydrophobicity and predicted binding affinity. On both tasks our model shows an improved tradeoff between class adherence and sample quality, significantly outperforming EvoProtGrad, a popular strategy to sample from pLMs with gradient-based discrete Markov Chain Monte Carlo.

</details>


### [94] [MAGIQ: A Post-Quantum Multi-Agentic AI Governance System with Provable Security](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06933)
*Sepideh Avizeh,Tushin Mallick,Alina Oprea,Cristina Nita-Rotaru,Reihaneh Safavi-Naini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MAGIQ is a framework that combines post-quantum cryptography with policy enforcement for multi-agent AI systems, providing quantum-resistant security with formal guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses two critical emerging trends: 1) the need for secure governance architectures in agentic AI systems to ensure policy compliance and accountability, and 2) the urgent requirement for quantum-resistant cryptography as NIST recommends deprecating current public-key algorithms by 2030-2035.

Method: The authors present MAGIQ framework that uses novel quantum-resistant cryptographic protocols to: 1) allow users to define rich communication/access-control policies for agent sessions, 2) enforce policies using post-quantum primitives, 3) support session-based policy enforcement for various agent interactions, and 4) provide agent accountability through message attribution. The framework's correctness and security are formally modeled and proven using the Universal Composability (UC) framework.

Result: MAGIQ provides a working framework for quantum-secure agentic AI systems with proven security guarantees. The paper includes evaluation of computation and communication overhead compared to the state-of-the-art SAGA framework, demonstrating MAGIQ's efficiency as the first step toward post-quantum-secure solutions for agentic AI systems.

Conclusion: MAGIQ represents a pioneering approach to integrating post-quantum cryptography with agentic AI governance, addressing both the security vulnerabilities of quantum computing and the policy enforcement needs of autonomous AI agents, with formal security guarantees through the UC framework.

Abstract: Our computing ecosystem is being transformed by two emerging paradigms: the increased deployment of agentic AI systems and advancements in quantum computing. With respect to agentic AI systems, one of the most critical problems is creating secure governing architectures that ensure agents follow their owners' communication and interaction policies and can be held accountable for the messages they exchange with other agents. With respect to quantum computing, existing systems must be retrofitted and new cryptographic mechanisms must be designed to ensure long-term security and quantum resistance. In fact, NIST recommends that standard public-key cryptographic algorithms, including RSA, Diffie-Hellman (DH), and elliptic-curve constructions (ECC), be deprecated starting in 2030 and disallowed after 2035.
  In this paper, we present MAGIQ, a framework for policy definition and enforcement in multi-agent AI systems using novel, highly efficient, quantum-resistant cryptographic protocols with proven security guarantees. MAGIQ (i) allows users to define rich communication and access-control policy budgets for agent-to-agent sessions and tasks, including global budgets for one-to-many agent sessions; (ii) enforces such policies using post-quantum cryptographic primitives; (iii) supports session-based enforcement of policies for agent-to-agent and one-to-many agent sessions; and (iv) provides accountability of agents to their users through message attribution. We formally model and prove the correctness and security of the system using the Universal Composability (UC) framework. We evaluate the computation and communication overhead of our framework and compare it with the state-of-the-art agentic AI framework SAGA. MAGIQ is a first step toward post-quantum-secure solutions for agentic AI systems.

</details>


### [95] [Enabling Unsupervised Training of Deep EEG Denoisers With Intelligent Partitioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06724)
*Qiyu Rao,Haozhe Tian,Homayoun Hamedmoghadam,Danilo Mandic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: iPSD enables self-supervised EEG denoising without clean references by partitioning noisy segments into independent realizations with the same underlying signal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wearable EEG denoising is challenging because neural signals are subtle and overlap spectrally with artifacts. Classical methods with fixed rules can't handle time-varying artifacts, while deep learning needs unobtainable artifact-free EEG for training.

Method: Intelligent Partitioning for Self-supervised Denoising (iPSD) learns to partition an input EEG segment into independent noisy realizations with the same underlying signal, enabling self-supervised training without clean references.

Result: iPSD achieves state-of-the-art performance, especially under extremely low signal-to-noise ratios (down to -10 dB) and challenging artifacts like EMG, with spectral fidelity orders of magnitude higher than baselines.

Conclusion: iPSD provides an effective self-supervised solution for wearable EEG denoising that eliminates the need for clean reference data and works even in zero-shot settings with only a single EEG segment available.

Abstract: Denoising wearable electroencephalogram (EEG) is inherently challenging since neural activity is not only subtle but also inseparable from spectrally overlapping noise artifacts. Classical signal processing methods, relying on fixed or heuristic rules, cannot handle the time-varying pervasive artifacts in wearable EEGs. Deep learning methods, on the other hand, show promise in decomposition-free EEG denoising using highly expressive neural networks, but the training requires artifact-free EEG, which is inherently unobtainable. To address this, we propose Intelligent Partitioning for Self-supervised Denoising (iPSD). Our method eliminates the need for clean references by learning to partition an input EEG segment into independent noisy realizations with the same underlying signal. This enables self-supervision of deep learning denoisers, even in zero-shot settings where only a single EEG segment to be denoised is available. We validate iPSD through extensive experiments, including validations on wearable EEG from in-ear sensors. The results show that iPSD achieves state-of-the-art performance, most notably under extremely low signal-to-noise ratios (down to -10 dB) and challenging artifacts (e.g., EMG), with spectral fidelity orders of magnitude higher than competitive baselines.

</details>


### [96] [Transformer-Based Wildlife Species Classification from Daily Movement Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06726)
*Obed Irakoze,Prasenjit Mitra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer sequence models outperform LSTM/CNN baselines for wildlife species identification from GPS trajectories, with feature augmentation improving results for underrepresented species and 1-hour resolution being optimal for cross-study consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identifying wildlife species from GPS movement data alone is challenging but important for ecological monitoring and conservation. Current methods need improvement for accurate species classification across different temporal resolutions and species representation.

Method: Train Transformer-based sequence models on large-scale GPS trajectories from Movebank (7 species). Use study/region holdout protocol for evaluation. Compare Transformers to LSTM, CNN, and Temporal Convolutional Networks. Experiment with displacement-based vs. augmented feature encoding (speed, direction, turning behavior). Test different temporal resolutions (1-hour vs. 30-minute).

Result: Transformers consistently outperform baseline models by 8-22 percentage points in balanced accuracy. For elephant binary classification (1-hour resolution): balanced accuracy 0.83, AUC 0.92. Feature augmentation provides clear gains for underrepresented species (lions, zebras). 1-hour resolution yields more consistent performance across studies than 30-minute resolution by reducing missing data.

Conclusion: Transformer sequence models with appropriate feature engineering and 1-hour temporal resolution provide effective solutions for wildlife species identification from GPS trajectories, particularly benefiting underrepresented species through feature augmentation.

Abstract: Inferring the identity of wildlife species from daily movement data alone is a challenging task. We train sequence models on large-scale, 7-species GPS trajectories from the Movebank platform. Trajectories models are evaluated using a protocol in which entire telemetry studies or regions are heldout during testing. We compare Transformer-based sequence models to LSTM, CNN, and Temporal Convolutional Networks, and find that Transformers consistently achieve higher balanced accuracy with gains of approximately 8 to 22 percentage points, depending on the species and experimental setting. In an elephant binary classification task with 1-hour resolution, the Transformer achieves a balanced accuracy of 0.83 and an AUC of 0.92, substantially outperforming all baseline models. We examine, under data-limited conditions, feature representations by analyzing the differences between a basic displacement-based encoding and an expanded range of movement descriptors that include speed, direction, and turning behavior. With feature augmentation, we see clear performance gains, especially for underrepresented and sparsely represented species, such as large carnivores, lions, and Zebras. Finally, experiments comparing 1-hour and 30-minutetemporal resolutions show that while finer sampling can capture short-term movement patterns for some species, a unified 1-hour resolution yields more promising performance across studies by reducing missing data and ensuring consistent temporal coverage.

</details>


### [97] [Learning Material-Aware Hamiltonian Risk Fields for Safe Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07038)
*Aditya Sai Ellendula,Yi Wang,Chandrajit Bajaj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A risk-aware navigation policy that selectively activates evasive maneuvers only when safer alternatives exist, using context-energy terms in a port-Hamiltonian framework with CVaR tail-risk optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current navigation policies lack selectivity - they either activate evasive maneuvers prematurely or fail to activate when needed. There's a need for policies that can intelligently decide when to use evasive degrees of freedom based on local risk assessment and available safer alternatives.

Method: Proposes adding a context-energy term to port-Hamiltonian navigation policy to create learned force channels. Uses Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR) tail-risk objective to focus gradient updates on rare but consequential risk transitions. The approach learns when to activate lateral forces toward lower-risk directions or suppress them when no safe escape exists.

Result: Achieves selective activation: In delayed-required-escape benchmark, reduces premature force activation from 0.950 to 0.180 vs DWA while raising success from 0.480 to 0.810. On RELLIS-3D off-road terrain: correct activation 0.837, false activation 0.114 vs 0.378/0.752 for scalar risk gradients. On DFC2018 semantic maps: reduces catastrophic failure from 0.60 to 0.10, oscillation by 90.7%. In highway traffic: collisions drop from 100% to 0% when escape feasible, suppresses lateral maneuver when no escape exists.

Conclusion: The proposed context-energy approach enables selective, risk-aware navigation that intelligently activates evasive maneuvers only when safer alternatives exist. The selectivity property emerges from the gradient structure rather than training-time tuning, making it robust across diverse environments from off-road terrain to highway traffic.

Abstract: Risk-aware navigation should be selective: a policy should expose evasive degrees of freedom only when the local scene admits a lower-risk feasible maneuver, and suppress them when no safer alternative exists. We show that adding one context-energy term to a port-Hamiltonian navigation policy produces a learned force channel with exactly this falsifiable signature. When the local risk field contains a feasible lower-risk direction, the induced context force activates toward it; when the apparent escape is blocked or not yet available, a route-aware gate suppresses lateral force rather than hallucinating an unsafe maneuver. A CVaR tail-risk objective focuses gradient updates on rare but consequential risk transitions. We validate the selectivity signature across four settings. In the primary delayed-required-escape benchmark, route-aware CVaR reduces premature force activation from 0.950 to 0.180 versus DWA while raising success from 0.480 to 0.810 with zero replans. On real off-road terrain (RELLIS-3D), route-aware enrichment achieves correct activation rate 0.837 and false activation rate 0.114, compared to 0.378/0.752 for scalar risk gradients. On static semantic maps (DFC2018), enrichment reduces catastrophic failure from 0.60 to 0.10 and oscillation by 90.7% while preserving path efficiency. In highway traffic, collisions drop from 100% to 0% when a lane escape is feasible; when no escape exists, the policy suppresses the lateral maneuver. The selectivity property follows from the gradient structure of the context energy rather than from training-time tuning.

</details>


### [98] [Medical Imaging Classification with Cold-Atom Reservoir Computing using Auto-Encoders and Surrogate-Driven Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06727)
*Nuno Batista,Ana Morgado,Oscar Ferraz,Sagar Silva Pratapsi,Jorge Lobo,Gabriel Falcao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hybrid quantum-classical pipeline combining guided auto-encoders with neutral-atom reservoir computing for polyp detection in medical images, featuring differentiable quantum layer emulation for end-to-end training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the high dimensionality challenge in medical image classification (specifically polyp detection) and overcome the 'gradient barrier' created by non-differentiable quantum measurements in quantum reservoir computing systems.

Method: 1) Integration of a guided auto-encoder to learn compact discriminative representations; 2) Use of a differentiable surrogate model to emulate the quantum layer for end-to-end backpropagation; 3) Joint optimization for both classification accuracy and faithful image recovery; 4) Encoding learned latent representations as pulse detuning parameters in a Rydberg Hamiltonian; 5) Obtaining quantum embeddings through expectation values; 6) Feeding embeddings to a linear classifier.

Result: The method outperforms traditional approaches using PCA or unguided autoencoders. Ablation studies demonstrate robustness and flexibility for real-world medical imaging applications in the NISQ era.

Conclusion: The proposed hybrid quantum-classical pipeline effectively addresses high-dimensional medical image classification challenges, overcomes quantum measurement gradient barriers through differentiable emulation, and shows promising performance for practical applications even with current noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices.

Abstract: We introduce a hybrid quantum-classical pipeline, based on neutral-atom reservoir computing, for medical image classification, focusing on the binary classification task of polyp detection. To deal effectively with the high dimensionality, we integrate a guided auto-encoder. This pipeline learns compact and discriminative representations of image data that are also well-suited for quantum reservoir computing. A key challenge in such systems is the non-differentiable nature of quantum measurements, which creates a 'gradient barrier' for standard training. We overcome this barrier by incorporating a differentiable surrogate model that emulates the quantum layer, enabling end-to-end backpropagation through the entire system. This guided training process is jointly optimized for classification accuracy and for faithful image recovery from the auto-encoder. The learned latent representations are encoded as pulse detuning parameters within a Rydberg Hamiltonian, and quantum embeddings are subsequently obtained through expectation values. These embeddings are then passed to a linear classifier. Our simulations show that this method outperforms some traditional approaches that use PCA or unguided autoencoders. We also conduct ablation studies to assess the impact of various quantum and training parameters, demonstrating the robustness and flexibility of our proposed pipeline for real-world medical imaging applications, even in the current NISQ era.

</details>


### [99] [The E$Δ$-MHC-Geo Transformer: Adaptive Geodesic Operations with Guaranteed Orthogonality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06729)
*Arash Shahmansoori*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel transformer architecture combining manifold-constrained hyper-connections with orthogonal residual connections using Cayley transforms and Householder reflections, achieving better stability and rotation performance than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods like DDL have limitations in maintaining orthogonality across all parameters, and JPmHC cannot handle eigenvalue -1 cases (reflections) due to its Cayley-based design. There's a need for a unified approach that can access both connected components of the orthogonal group O(n).

Method: The EΔ-MHC-Geo Transformer combines three components: 1) Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections (mHC), 2) Deep Delta Learning (DDL), and 3) Cayley transform for input-adaptive orthogonal residual connections. It introduces a hybrid architecture with a learned gate to select between Cayley rotation and Householder reflection, plus a midpoint-collapse regularizer.

Result: Achieves best long-horizon stability (1.9x over JPmHC, 3.8x over GPT), best near-π rotation loss (4.5x over JPmHC), strong norm preservation (0.001 mean deviation), and 0.96 negation cosine alignment in reflection probe, all with 33% fewer layers than baselines.

Conclusion: The hybrid approach combining Cayley rotations with Householder reflections via a learned gate enables access to both connected components of O(n), overcoming limitations of pure Cayley-based methods while maintaining orthogonality and achieving superior performance across various metrics.

Abstract: We present the E$Δ$-MHC-Geo Transformer, a novel architecture that unifies Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections (mHC), Deep Delta Learning (DDL), and the Cayley transform to obtain input-adaptive, unconditionally orthogonal residual connections. Unlike DDL, whose Householder operator is orthogonal only at $β\in \{0,2\}$, our Data-Dependent Cayley rotation $Q(x)=(I+(β/2)A(x))^{-1}(I-(β/2)A(x))$ preserves orthogonality for all $β$ and all inputs. To handle negation, an eigenvalue $-1$ case that Cayley provably excludes, we introduce the E$Δ$-MHC-Geo Hybrid, which combines Cayley rotation with Householder reflection via a learned operator-selection gate $X'=γ(X)Q(X)X+(1-γ(X))H_2(X)X$. A midpoint-collapse regularizer, $4γ(1-γ)$, encourages boundary gate decisions, where each selected component is orthogonal. In matched-parameter comparisons, with approximately 1.79M parameters per model and mean +/- standard deviation over 3 seeds, against four baselines including the concurrent JPmHC, E$Δ$-MHC-Geo achieves the best long-horizon stability, 1.9x over JPmHC and 3.8x over GPT; the best near-$π$ rotation loss, 4.5x over JPmHC on single-plane; strong norm preservation, with 0.001 mean deviation; and 0.96 negation cosine alignment in a diagnostic reflection probe, all with 33% fewer layers. While JPmHC's wider representation excels on pure rotation, its finite Cayley residual mixer excludes an exact $λ=-1$ operator and has no reflection branch, motivating our hybrid approach for accessing both connected components of $O(n)$.

</details>


### [100] [Semantic State Abstraction Interfaces for LLM-Augmented Portfolio Decisions: Multi-Axis News Decomposition and RL Diagnostics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06730)
*Likhita Yerra,Remi Uttejitha Allam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SSAI is a framework for mapping sparse text into K interpretable dimensions for decision systems, evaluated via multiple methods showing performance gains but statistical fragility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create an auditable, interpretable method for converting sparse unstructured text data into named coordinates that separates representation hypotheses from optimization variance in sequential decision systems.

Method: Introduces Semantic State Abstraction Interfaces (SSAI) template with K=4 axes (sentiment, risk, confidence, volatility forecast). Applied to US equity panel using 30 NASDAQ-100 stocks with FNSPID news (2019-2023). Evaluated through three approaches: 1) Direct factor portfolios, 2) Supervised ridge forecasters, 3) Reinforcement learning agents (DP-PPO, SAC).

Result: Four-factor portfolio showed 307.2% cumulative return (Sharpe 1.067) vs buy-and-hold 243.6%, but gains failed coverage-stratified controls, reversed at ≥0.2% costs, and were statistically fragile vs sentiment-only baseline. PC1 composite and FinBERT portfolio baselines were stronger ranking signals. Ridge and RL blocks helped diagnose representation vs optimizer effects.

Conclusion: SSAI serves as an interpretability-performance diagnostic tool and reusable protocol for sparse-text decision systems, not necessarily outperforming denser alternatives but providing a methodological template for systematic evaluation.

Abstract: We introduce Semantic State Abstraction Interfaces (SSAI): a methodological template for mapping sparse unstructured text into $K$ auditable, named coordinates with neutral defaults on no-news days, designed to separate representation hypotheses from optimisation variance in sequential decision systems. Our contribution is the framework and its evaluation protocol, not a claim that SSAI outperforms denser alternatives.
  We instantiate SSAI with $K=4$ axes (sentiment, risk, confidence, volatility forecast) on a US-equity panel (30 NASDAQ-100 names, FNSPID news, 2019--2023 test), and evaluate it across direct factor portfolios, supervised ridge forecasters, and RL agents (DP-PPO, SAC) that share the same fixed $φ$. The four-factor factor portfolio reaches 307.2% cumulative return and Sharpe 1.067, but apparent gains versus buy-and-hold (243.6%) fail coverage-stratified controls, reverse at $\geq 0.2$% costs, and are statistically fragile versus a sentiment-only baseline; a PC1 composite and a FinBERT portfolio baseline are stronger ranking signals in this setting. Ridge and RL blocks diagnose representation versus optimiser effects. We position SSAI as an interpretability-performance diagnostic and reusable protocol for sparse-text decision systems.

</details>


### [101] [On Training in Imagination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06732)
*Nadav Timor,Ravid Shwartz-Ziv,Micah Goldblum,Yann LeCun,David Harel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes how errors in learned dynamics and reward models affect model-based RL, derives optimal sample allocation ratios, and examines policy optimization tradeoffs between rollout quantity and reward noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model-based RL methods use imagined rollouts from learned dynamics and reward models without querying the true environment. The motivation is to understand how errors in these learned models impact policy performance and to optimize sample allocation between dynamics and reward learning, while also exploring tradeoffs between rollout quantity and reward noise quality.

Method: 1) Extends Asadi et al.'s (2018) analysis to MDPs with learned reward models, deriving optimal sample allocation ratio between dynamics and reward samples that minimizes return error bounds under power-law scaling assumptions. 2) Identifies lower Lipschitz constants of learned models as representation desideratum. 3) Examines how REINFORCE policy optimization tolerates noisy rewards, showing zero-mean noise leaves gradient estimator unbiased. 4) Reduces the rollout quantity vs. reward noise tradeoff to a one-dimensional optimization problem.

Result: 1) Derived optimal sample allocation ratio for dynamics vs. reward samples. 2) Established connection between lower Lipschitz constants and temporal-straightening objective of Wang et al. (2026). 3) Showed zero-mean reward noise doesn't bias REINFORCE gradient but adds variance that decreases with rollout count. 4) Characterized optimal tradeoff between buying more rollouts with cheaper/noisier rewards vs. fewer with more expensive/less noisy rewards.

Conclusion: The paper provides theoretical analysis and practical guidelines for model-based RL: it establishes optimal sample allocation strategies for dynamics and reward learning, identifies desirable model properties (low Lipschitz constants), and quantifies tradeoffs between rollout quantity and reward noise quality for policy optimization efficiency.

Abstract: State-of-the-art model-based reinforcement learning methods train policies on imagined rollouts. These rollouts are trajectories generated by a learned dynamics model and are scored by a learned reward model, but without querying the true environment during policy updates. We study this training paradigm by quantifying how errors in learned dynamics and reward models affect returns and policy optimization. First, we extend the analysis of Asadi et al. (2018) to MDPs with learned reward models, and derive the optimal sample allocation--the ratio of dynamics samples to reward samples that minimizes a bound on return error under power-law scaling assumptions. We identify lower Lipschitz constants of the learned dynamics, reward, and policy as a representation desideratum that tightens this bound, and we connect this perspective to the temporal-straightening objective of Wang et al. (2026). Second, we examine how policy optimization with REINFORCE tolerates noisy rewards, which are often cheaper to obtain. We show that zero-mean reward noise leaves the gradient estimator unbiased and adds at most a variance term that decreases with the number of rollouts. This introduces a practical tradeoff: given a fixed budget, should one buy more rollouts with cheaper but noisier rewards, or fewer rollouts with more expensive but less noisy rewards? We reduce this choice to a one-dimensional optimization problem and characterize the optimum.

</details>


### [102] [Beyond Factor Aggregation: Gauge-Aware Low-Rank Server Representations for Federated LoRA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06733)
*Jinqian Chen,Chang Liu,Jihua Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GLoRA introduces a gauge-aware representation for federated LoRA that properly aggregates client updates by addressing the gauge equivalence problem in low-rank factorization, enabling semantically meaningful aggregation across heterogeneous clients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing federated LoRA methods have a fundamental flaw: directly averaging LoRA factors is representation-dependent due to gauge equivalence. The same intrinsic update can have infinitely many factorizations, making factor-level aggregation arbitrary and semantically meaningless despite the underlying update being unchanged.

Method: GLoRA estimates a consensus update subspace from client projectors and aggregates client updates in shared reference coordinates. It represents semantic update aggregation entirely in low-rank form and includes a rank-compatible readout mechanism that supports heterogeneous client capacities by instantiating adapters of different ranks from the same server state.

Result: GLoRA consistently outperforms federated LoRA baselines on GLUE and SuperNI benchmarks under various heterogeneous conditions including heterogeneous client ranks, sparse participation, larger backbones, and unseen-task evaluation. It achieves a favorable efficiency-performance trade-off.

Conclusion: Effective federated LoRA requires not just averaging low-rank factors, but defining a semantically meaningful server-side representation for aggregation. GLoRA successfully addresses the gauge equivalence problem and enables robust federated fine-tuning across heterogeneous clients.

Abstract: Federated LoRA enables parameter-efficient adaptation of large language models under decentralized data and limited client resources.However, directly averaging LoRA factors is representation-dependent: the same intrinsic update admits infinitely many gauge-equivalent factorizations, so factor-level aggregation can change under arbitrary coordinate choices while the underlying update remains unchanged. This reveals a semantic mismatch in existing federated LoRA aggregation rules. We propose \textbf{GLoRA}, a gauge-aware server representation for federated LoRA.Instead of aggregating raw factors, GLoRA estimates a consensus update subspace from client projectors and aggregates client updates in shared reference coordinates, thereby representing semantic update aggregation entirely in low-rank form. To support heterogeneous client capacities, GLoRA further provides a rank-compatible readout that instantiates adapters of different ranks from the same server state without dense update reconstruction. Experiments on GLUE and SuperNI show that GLoRA consistently outperforms federated LoRA baselines under data, resource, and task heterogeneity, including heterogeneous client ranks, sparse participation, larger backbones, and unseen-task evaluation. GLoRA also achieves a favorable efficiency--performance trade-off, suggesting that effective federated LoRA requires not merely averaging low-rank factors, but defining a semantically meaningful server-side representation for aggregation.

</details>


### [103] [Gated QKAN-FWP: Scalable Quantum-inspired Sequence Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06734)
*Kuo-Chung Peng,Samuel Yen-Chi Chen,Jiun-Cheng Jiang,Chen-Yu Liu,En-Jui Kuo,Yun-Yuan Wang,Prayag Tiwari,Andrea Ceschini,Chi-Sheng Chen,Yu-Chao Hsu,Chun-Hua Lin,Tai-Yue Li,Antonello Rosato,Massimo Panella,Simon See,Saif Al-Kuwari,Kuan-Cheng Chen,Nan-Yow Chen,Hsi-Sheng Goan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes gated QKAN-FWP, a quantum-inspired fast-weight framework that combines Fast Weight Programmers with single-qubit quantum-inspired circuits, achieving better time-series forecasting performance than classical baselines with fewer parameters and demonstrating NISQ device compatibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Quantum Fast Weight Programmers (QFWPs) rely on multi-qubit variational quantum circuits that are difficult to scale on NISQ devices and expensive to simulate classically. There's a need for more scalable, parameter-efficient quantum-inspired approaches to sequence modeling.

Method: Integrates Fast Weight Programmers with Quantum-inspired Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (QKAN) using single-qubit data re-uploading circuits as learnable nonlinear activation (DARUAN). Introduces a scalar-gated fast-weight update rule with theoretical analysis of adaptive memory kernel, geometric boundedness, and parallelizable gradient paths.

Result: Achieves lower scaled MSE, peak amplitude error, and peak timing error than classical baselines (LSTM, WaveNet-LSTM, etc.) with up to 13x fewer parameters. Demonstrates real-world solar cycle forecasting with 528-month input window and 132-month forecast horizon. Successfully deploys on IonQ and IBM Quantum processors with forecasting accuracy within 0.1% relative MSE of noiseless simulator.

Conclusion: Gated QKAN-FWP is a scalable, parameter-efficient, and NISQ-compatible approach to quantum-inspired sequence modeling that outperforms classical recurrent baselines while being deployable on current quantum hardware.

Abstract: Fast Weight Programmers (FWPs) encode temporal dependencies through dynamically updated parameters rather than recurrent hidden states. Quantum FWPs (QFWPs) extend this idea with variational quantum circuits (VQCs), but existing implementations rely on multi-qubit architectures that are difficult to scale on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices and expensive to simulate classically. We propose gated QKAN-FWP, a fast-weight framework that integrates FWP with Quantum-inspired Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (QKAN) using single-qubit data re-uploading circuits as learnable nonlinear activation, known as DatA Re-Uploading ActivatioN (DARUAN). We further introduce a scalar-gated fast-weight update rule that stabilizes parameter evolution, supported by a theoretical analysis of its adaptive memory kernel, geometric boundedness, and parallelizable gradient paths. We evaluate the framework across time-series benchmarks, MiniGrid reinforcement learning, and highlight real-world solar cycle forecasting as our main practical result. In the long-horizon setting with 528-month input window and 132-month forecast horizon, our 12.5k-parameter model achieves lower scaled Mean Square Error (MSE), peak amplitude error, and peak timing error than a suite of classical recurrent baselines with up to 13x more parameters, including Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks (25.9k-89.1k parameters), WaveNet-LSTM (167k), Vanilla recurrent neural network (11.5k), and a Modified Echo State Network (132k). To validate NISQ compatibility, we further deploy the trained fast programmer on IonQ and IBM Quantum processors, recovering forecasting accuracy within 0.1% relative MSE of the noiseless simulator at 1024 shots. These results position gated QKAN-FWP as a scalable, parameter-efficient, and NISQ-compatible approach to quantum-inspired sequence modeling.

</details>


### [104] [STDA-Net: Spectrogram-Based Domain Adaptation for cross-dataset Sleep Stage Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06736)
*Unaza Tallal,Shruti Kshirsagar,Ankita Shukla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STDA-Net is a cross-dataset sleep staging framework using 2D spectrograms with CNN feature extraction, BiLSTM temporal modeling, and DANN domain adaptation, achieving superior performance and stability compared to 1D EEG methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
