Motivation: Cross-dataset sleep stage classification faces challenges due to variability in EEG channel montages, sampling rates, recording environments, and subject populations. While deep learning shows promise, most existing methods use 1D EEG signals, and 2D spectrogram-based approaches with unsupervised domain adaptation remain unexplored.

Method: Proposes STDA-Net (Spectrogram-based Temporal Domain Adaptation Network) combining: 1) CNN for spectrogram-based feature extraction, 2) BiLSTM module for temporal modeling of sleep dynamics, and 3) Domain-Adversarial Neural Network (DANN) for source-to-target feature alignment without requiring labeled target-domain data.

Result: Achieves average accuracy of 89.03% and average macro F1-score of 87.64% across three datasets (Sleep-EDF, SHHS-1, SHHS-2) under six cross-dataset transfer settings. Consistently outperforms existing 1D baseline methods with substantially lower variance across five independent runs.

Conclusion: 2D spectrogram-based representations combined with temporal modeling and adversarial domain adaptation provide a robust and competitive alternative to conventional 1D EEG inputs for cross-dataset sleep staging, offering improved stability and reproducibility.

Abstract: Accurate sleep stage classification across datasets remains challenging due to variability in EEG channel montages, sampling rates, recording environments, and subject populations. Although deep learning has shown considerable promise for automated sleep staging, most existing cross-dataset methods rely on one-dimensional EEG signal representations, whereas the use of two-dimensional spectrogram-based inputs within an unsupervised domain adaptation framework has remained largely unexplored. Here, we propose STDA-Net (Spectrogram-based Temporal Domain Adaptation Network), a framework that combines a convolutional neural network (CNN) for spectrogram-based feature extraction, a bidirectional long short-term memory (BiLSTM) module for temporal modeling of sleep dynamics, and a domain-adversarial neural network (DANN) for source-to-target feature alignment without requiring any labeled target-domain data during training. Experiments are conducted on three publicly available datasets Sleep-EDF, SHHS-1, and SHHS-2 under six cross-dataset transfer settings. Results show that the proposed framework achieves an average accuracy of 89.03% and an average macro F1-score of 87.64%, consistently outperforming existing 1D baseline methods in terms of balanced classification performance, with substantially lower variance across five independent runs, indicating improved stability and reproducibility. Overall, these findings demonstrate that 2D spectrogram-based representations, combined with temporal modeling and adversarial domain adaptation, provide a robust and competitive alternative to conventional 1D EEG inputs for cross-dataset sleep staging.

</details>


### [105] [Geometric Kolmogorov--Arnold Network (GeoKAN)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06740)
*Abhijit Sen,Bikram Keshari Parida,Giridas Maiti,Mahima Arya,Denys I. Bondar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GeoKAN introduces geometry-aware Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks that learn task-adapted coordinate transformations through Riemannian metrics to improve representation of non-uniform functions and physics problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current KAN models operate in fixed Euclidean coordinates, limiting their ability to handle functions with sharp variations, stiffness, and non-uniform behavior common in scientific machine learning and differential equation problems.

Method: The paper learns a diagonal Riemannian metric that warps input coordinates before basis expansion. This creates geometry-adapted coordinates through local length scaling and volume distortion. Three main variants are developed: GeoKAN-NNMetric, GeoKAN-γ, and LM-KAN (with RBF, wavelet, and Fourier basis versions).

Result: GeoKAN reallocates representational resolution by stretching regions with rapid variation and compressing smoother regions, allowing capacity to be placed where most needed. This enables better handling of sharp, stiff, localized, and non-uniform regimes.

Conclusion: GeoKAN provides a geometry-aware framework for KAN models that is particularly well-suited for scientific machine learning and differential equation problems where traditional fixed-coordinate approaches struggle with non-uniform function behavior.

Abstract: We introduce Geometric Kolmogorov--Arnold Networks (GeoKANs), a family of geometry-aware KAN-type models in which approximation is carried out in learned, geometry-adapted coordinates rather than in fixed Euclidean input coordinates. GeoKAN achieves this by learning a diagonal Riemannian metric that warps the input before basis expansion and feature mixing. The learned metric provides a geometric inductive bias through local length scaling and volume distortion, and in physics-informed settings it also affects the differential structure seen by the model. Within this framework, we develop three main variants, namely GeoKAN-NNMetric, GeoKAN-$γ$, and LM-KAN. For LM-KAN, we further consider three basis-specific versions, LM-KAN-RBF, LM-KAN-Wav, and LM-KAN-Fourier. These variants allow us to study geometry-aware KAN models both as general function approximators and as surrogates in physics-informed learning. By stretching regions with rapid variation and compressing smoother regions, GeoKAN reallocates representational resolution in a task-dependent manner, allowing the model to place capacity where it is most needed. As a result, GeoKAN is well suited to sharp, stiff, localized, and strongly non-uniform regimes arising in scientific machine learning and differential-equation problems.

</details>


### [106] [A Closed-Form Upper Bound for Admissible Learning-Rate Steps in Belief-Space Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06741)
*Zixi Li,Youzhen Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Learning-rate steps can be determined by a formula based on geometric contractivity in belief space, rather than being treated as hyperparameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current practice treats learning-rate steps as hyperparameters that need tuning, but there should be a principled mathematical approach to determine admissible step sizes.

Method: Models updates as projected forward steps on the probability simplex, analyzes admissibility through contractivity in the natural KL/Bregman geometry, and derives an upper bound formula for admissible steps.

Result: The paper establishes that admissible learning-rate steps are not arbitrary hyperparameters but can be determined by a specific formula based on geometric properties.

Conclusion: Learning-rate steps should be treated as calculable quantities derived from geometric properties of the optimization problem, not as hyperparameters requiring heuristic tuning.

Abstract: Learning-rate steps are usually treated as hyperparameters. This paper isolates a local beliefspace calculation: when an update is modeled as a projected forward step on the probability simplex, admissibility means contractivity in the natural KL/Bregman geometry. Under this model, the upper bound of an admissible step is not a tuning slogan but a formula.

</details>


### [107] [Gradient Extrapolation-Based Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06755)
*Ismam Nur Swapnil,Aranya Saha,Tanvir Ahmed Khan,Mohammad Ariful Haque,Ser-Nam Lim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GXPO improves reinforcement learning for language model reasoning by approximating multi-step lookahead with only 3 backward passes instead of many, achieving better performance than standard GRPO and other methods while maintaining computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard GRPO-style training for reasoning tasks only uses current-step updates, missing the benefits of full multi-step lookahead which is too computationally expensive due to requiring many backward passes. There's a need for efficient lookahead approximation that maintains GRPO compatibility.

Method: GXPO approximates longer local lookahead using just three backward passes: (1) takes two fast optimizer steps, (2) measures gradient changes, (3) predicts a virtual K-step lookahead point, (4) moves policy partway toward that point, (5) applies corrective update using true gradient at new position. It automatically switches back to standard GRPO when lookahead becomes unstable, reuses existing rollouts/rewards/advantages without new computations.

Result: Across Qwen2.5 and Llama math-reasoning experiments, GXPO improves average sampled pass@1 by +1.65 to +5.00 points over GRPO and +0.14 to +1.28 points over strongest SFPO setting. Achieves up to 4.00x step speedup, 2.33x wall-clock speedup, and 1.33x backward-pass speedup in reaching GRPO's peak accuracy, while keeping active-phase cost fixed at three backward passes.

Conclusion: GXPO provides an effective plug-compatible policy-update rule for GRPO-style reasoning RL that approximates beneficial multi-step lookahead at minimal computational cost, offering significant performance improvements while maintaining computational efficiency, with automatic fallback to standard GRPO when needed.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning is widely used to improve the reasoning ability of large language models, especially when answers can be automatically checked. Standard GRPO-style training updates the model using only the current step, while full multi-step lookahead can give a better update direction but is too expensive because it needs many backward passes. We propose Gradient Extrapolation-Based Policy Optimization (GXPO), a plug-compatible policy-update rule for GRPO-style reasoning RL. GXPO approximates a longer local lookahead using only three backward passes during an active phase. It reuses the same batch of rollouts, rewards, advantages, and GRPO loss, so it does not require new rollouts or reward computation at the lookahead points. GXPO takes two fast optimizer steps, measures how the gradients change, predicts a virtual K-step lookahead point, moves the policy partway toward that point, and then applies a corrective update using the true gradient at the new position. When the lookahead signal becomes unstable, GXPO automatically switches back to standard single-pass GRPO. We also give a plain-gradient-descent surrogate analysis that explains when the extrapolation is exact and where its local errors come from. Across Qwen2.5 and Llama math-reasoning experiments, GXPO improves the average sampled pass@1 by +1.65 to +5.00 points over GRPO and by +0.14 to +1.28 points over the strongest SFPO setting, while keeping the active-phase cost fixed at three backward passes. It also achieves up to 4.00x step speedup, 2.33x wall-clock speedup, and 1.33x backward-pass speedup in reaching GRPO's peak accuracy.

</details>


### [108] [Physics-based Digital Twins for Integrated Thermal Energy Systems Using Active Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06756)
*Umme Mahbuba Nabila,Paul Seurin,Linyu Lin,Majdi I. Radaideh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An active learning framework combining Modelica simulations with physics-informed and data-driven surrogate models (SINDyC, MvG-SINDyC, FNN, GRU) achieves comparable accuracy using only 20% of simulation data through model-specific query strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time supervisory control of thermal energy systems requires digital twins that are accurate, interpretable, uncertainty-aware, and computationally efficient. High-fidelity simulations are costly, while purely data-driven surrogates lack robustness, creating a need for hybrid approaches.

Method: Proposes an active learning framework coupling Modelica simulations with four surrogate models: deterministic SINDyC, probabilistic MvG-SINDyC, FNN, and GRU neural networks. Uses model-specific query strategies - Mahalanobis-distance sampling for MvG-SINDyC and error-based sampling for others - to prioritize informative trajectories.

Result: Achieves comparable predictive accuracy using only 1/5 of simulation trajectories compared to random sampling. GRU achieves highest predictive fidelity, SINDyC is most computationally efficient and interpretable, while MvG-SINDyC enables uncertainty quantification and shows largest computational gains under active learning.

Conclusion: The active learning framework successfully addresses the trade-off between accuracy and efficiency for thermal energy digital twins, with different surrogate models offering varying benefits (GRU for accuracy, SINDyC for interpretability, MvG-SINDyC for uncertainty quantification), making it suitable for real-time supervisory control applications.

Abstract: Real-time supervisory control of thermal energy distribution systems requires digital twins that are accurate, interpretable, and uncertainty-aware, yet remain data and computationally efficient. High-fidelity simulations alone are costly, while purely data-driven surrogates often lack robustness. To address these challenges, this work proposes an active learning (AL) framework that couples system-level Modelica simulations with four simpler physics-informed and data-driven surrogate modeling approaches: deterministic Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics with Control (SINDyC), its probabilistic multivariate-Gaussian extension (MvG-SINDyC), feedforward neural network (FNN), and gated recurrent unit (GRU) network. Tailored to each surrogate, model-specific AL query strategies are employed, including Mahalanobis-distance sampling in coefficient space for MvG-SINDyC and error-based sampling in prediction space for SINDyC, FNN, and GRU, allowing the learning process to prioritize dynamically informative trajectories.
  The proposed approach is demonstrated on the glycol heat exchanger (GHX) subsystem of the Thermal Energy Distribution System (TEDS) at Idaho National Laboratory. Across key GHX outputs--the bypass mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{\mathrm{GHX}}$ and heat transfer rate $Q_{\mathrm{GHX}}$-the AL framework achieves comparable predictive accuracy using as few as one-fifth of the simulation trajectories required by random sampling. Among the evaluated surrogates, the GRU achieves the highest predictive fidelity, while SINDyC remains the most computationally efficient and interpretable. The probabilistic MvG-SINDyC surrogate further enables uncertainty quantification and exhibits the largest computational gains under AL.

</details>


### [109] [Sparse Attention as a Range Searching Problem: Towards an Inference-Efficient Index for KV Cache](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06763)
*Mohsen Dehghankar,Abolfazl Asudeh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Louver is a novel KV cache index structure for sparse attention that guarantees zero false negatives while being computationally efficient and hardware-aware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing sparse attention methods degrade accuracy by omitting critical KV entries, causing error spikes in long reasoning tasks. Current approaches lack theoretical guarantees for zero false negatives across varying important tokens during decoding steps.

Method: Reformulate sparse attention as a halfspace range searching problem, then design Louver - a lightweight, hardware-aware index structure tailored for efficient KV cache retrieval with theoretical recall guarantees.

Result: Louver outperforms prior sparse attention methods in both accuracy and runtime, and is faster than optimized dense attention methods like FlashAttention, while providing zero false negative guarantees.

Conclusion: Recall guarantees are a critical overlooked dimension in sparse attention, and Louver opens a new direction for building theoretically grounded, efficient KV cache indices.

Abstract: Sparse attention improves LLM inference efficiency by selecting a subset of key-value entries, but at the cost of potential accuracy degradation. In particular, omitting critical KV entries can induce substantial errors in model outputs. Existing methods typically operate under fixed or adaptive token budgets and provide empirical robustness or partial theoretical guarantees, yet they do not ensure zero false negatives in decoding steps, particularly since the set of relevant tokens is both query- and step-dependent. Our empirical observations confirm that missing even one critical key can lead to sharp error spikes, especially in long reasoning tasks where the set of important tokens varies throughout decoding. This observation motivates the need for indexing methods that dynamically adapt to these variations across decoding steps while guaranteeing a full recall of the relevant keys above a certain threshold. We address this challenge by reformulating sparse attention as the halfspace range searching problem. However, existing range searching indices are not suitable for modern LLM inference due to their computational and implementation overheads. To overcome this, we introduce Louver, a novel index structure tailored for efficient KV cache retrieval. Louver (i) guarantees zero false negatives with respect to a specified threshold in both theory and practice, (ii) is lightweight to integrate into existing LLM pipelines, and (iii) incorporates hardware-aware optimizations for both CPU and GPU executions. Our experiments demonstrate that Louver outperforms prior sparse attention methods in both accuracy and runtime, and is faster than highly optimized dense attentions such as FlashAttention. These results highlight that recall guarantees are a critical and overlooked dimension of sparse attention, and open a new direction for building theoretically grounded, efficient KV cache indices.

</details>


### [110] [Revisiting Adam for Streaming Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06764)
*Florin Gogianu,Adrian Catalin Lutu,Razvan Pascanu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Online deep RL agents can achieve competitive performance without replay buffers by using bounded gradient updates and variance-adjusted optimization, with C51 and proposed Adaptive Q(λ) performing well in Atari games.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to revisit online deep reinforcement learning without replay buffers or parallel sampling, which has been largely abandoned due to instability issues, to develop simpler and more efficient algorithms that learn directly from streamed interactions.

Method: The authors systematically test established RL algorithms (DQN and C51) in pure online settings without replay buffers. They analyze how optimization algorithms, particularly Adam, interact with these updates, identifying two essential properties for robust performance. Based on insights, they derive a variance-adjusted algorithm called Adaptive Q(λ) using eligibility traces.

Result: C51 performs competitively with StreamQ across 55 Atari games due to its bounded gradient and variance-adjusted properties. The proposed Adaptive Q(λ) algorithm achieves nearly double the human baseline on the same subset, surpassing existing methods across all performance metrics.

Conclusion: Online deep RL can work effectively without replay buffers when using updates with bounded derivatives and variance adjustment. The success of C51 and Adaptive Q(λ) demonstrates that established algorithms, when properly analyzed for their optimization properties, can achieve robust online learning performance.

Abstract: Learning from a sequence of interactions, as soon as observations are perceived and acted upon, without explicitly storing them, holds the promise of simpler, more efficient and adaptive algorithms. For over a decade, however, deep reinforcement learning walked the contrary path, augmenting agents with replay buffers or parallel sampling routines, in an effort to tame learning instability. Recently, this topic has been revisited by Elsayed et al. (2024), focusing on update computation through eligibility traces and modifications to the optimisation routine, resulting in the StreamQ algorithm. In this work we take a step back, investigating the efficacy of established updates, such as those implemented by DQN and C51 within this online setting. Not only do we find that they perform well, but through analysing how the optimisation algorithm generally, and Adam in particular, interacts with these updates, we contend that two properties are essential for robust performance: i) the derivative of the objective is to be bounded and ii) weight updates are variance-adjusted. Rigorous and exhaustive experimentation demonstrates that C51, which exhibits both characteristics, is competitive with StreamQ across a subset of 55 Atari games. Using these insights, we derive a variance-adjusted algorithm based on eligibility traces, termed Adaptive Q$(λ)$, which approaches double the human baseline on the same subset, surpassing existing methods by all performance metrics.

</details>


### [111] [Distributional Process Reward Models: Calibrated Prediction of Future Rewards via Conditional Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06785)
*Rachel Ma,Dylan Hadfield-Menell,Kristjan Greenewald*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes using conditional optimal transport (CondOT) to calibrate Process Reward Models (PRMs), which tend to overestimate success probabilities, by learning monotonic conditional quantile functions to produce valid confidence bounds for instance-adaptive scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Process Reward Models (PRMs) used in inference-time scaling methods are often poorly calibrated and overestimate success probabilities, making their uncertainty estimates unreliable for decision-making in instance-adaptive scaling frameworks.

Method: The authors adapt conditional optimal transport (CondOT) from Bunne et al. (2022) to estimate monotonic conditional quantile functions of success probabilities given PRM hidden states. This approach provides structurally valid quantile estimates and enables efficient extraction of confidence bounds at arbitrary levels, which are integrated into the instance-adaptive scaling (IAS) framework of Park et al. (2025).

Result: The method was evaluated on mathematical reasoning benchmarks (MATH-500 for moderate difficulty and AIME for harder out-of-distribution problems). For PRMs with reliable ranking signals, the approach substantially improved calibration over both uncalibrated PRMs and quantile regression. On downstream Best-of-N IAS performance, the method generally outperformed uncalibrated PRMs.

Conclusion: Conditional optimal transport provides a principled and practical approach to PRM calibration, offering structural guarantees and flexible uncertainty estimation, establishing it as another viable method alongside existing calibration techniques.

Abstract: Inference-time scaling methods rely on Process Reward Models (PRMs), which are often poorly calibrated and overestimate success probabilities. We propose, to our knowledge, the first use of conditional optimal transport for calibrating PRMs, modifying conditional OT (CondOT) map learning \cite{bunne2022supervised} to estimate a monotonic conditional quantile function over success probabilities estimated by the PRM, conditioned on PRM hidden states. This yields structurally valid quantile estimates and enables efficient extraction of confidence bounds at arbitrary levels, which we integrate into the instance-adaptive scaling (IAS) framework of \cite{park2025know}. We evaluate on mathematical reasoning benchmarks spanning moderate-difficulty problems (MATH-500) and harder out-of-distribution problems (AIME). For PRMs with reliable ranking signals, our method substantially improves calibration over both uncalibrated PRMs and quantile regression. On downstream Best-of-N IAS performance, our method generally improves over uncalibrated PRMs. These results establish conditional optimal transport as another principled and practical approach to PRM calibration, offering structural guarantees and flexible uncertainty estimation.

</details>


### [112] [MIND: Monge Inception Distance for Generative Models Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06797)
*Quentin Berthet,Yu-Han Wu,Clement Crepy,Romuald Elie,Klaus Greff,Michael Eli Sander*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MIND is a new metric for evaluating generative models that uses sliced Wasserstein distance, offering much better sample efficiency, faster computation, and greater robustness compared to the standard FID metric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The widely used Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) has limitations including poor sample complexity, vulnerability to adversarial attacks, and dependency on estimating high-dimensional means and covariance matrices.

Method: MIND leverages the sliced Wasserstein distance, which compares distributions by averaging one-dimensional optimal transport distances that can be efficiently computed via sorting, avoiding the need for high-dimensional statistical estimates.

Result: MIND demonstrates three key advantages: (1) one order of magnitude better sample efficiency, (2) two orders of magnitude faster computation, and (3) greater robustness to adversarial attacks. MIND with 5k samples matches FID with 50k samples, and smaller sample sizes (1k-2k) remain highly informative.

Conclusion: MIND provides a superior alternative to FID for evaluating generative models, offering substantial improvements in efficiency, speed, and robustness while maintaining high correlation with the standard benchmark.

Abstract: We propose the Monge Inception Distance (MIND), a metric for evaluating generative models that addresses key limitations of the widely adopted Fréchet Inception Distance (FID). The MIND metric leverages the sliced Wasserstein distance to compare distributions by averaging one-dimensional optimal transport distances, efficiently computed via sorting. This approach circumvents the estimation of high-dimensional means and covariance matrices, which underlie FID's poor sample complexity and vulnerability to adversarial attacks. We empirically demonstrate three primary advantages: (i) it is more sample-efficient by one order of magnitude, (ii) it is faster to compute by two orders of magnitude, (iii) it is more robust to adversarial attacks such as moment-matching. We show that MIND with 5k samples can replace the evaluation performance of FID with 50k samples, providing high correlation with this standard benchmark and superior discriminative performance. We further demonstrate that even smaller sample sizes (e.g., 1k or 2k) remain highly informative for rapid model iteration.

</details>


### [113] [From Model to Data (M2D): Shifting Complexity from GNNs to Graphs for Transparent Graph Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06814)
*Debolina Halder Lina,Arlei Silva*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new Model-to-Data (M2D) distillation framework transfers GNN model complexity into data space to improve architectural transparency while preserving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current GNNs achieve high performance but are opaque to humans, making it difficult to understand and compare different architectures. Existing explainability methods only explain individual predictions but don't provide architectural transparency or explain performance gaps between simple and complex models.

Method: Introduces Model-to-Data (M2D) distillation, which transfers model complexity into the data space by distilling a teacher model into an augmented graph with enriched features and structure, enabling a simple student model to match the teacher's performance.

Result: M2D allows humans to inspect architectural advantages directly by materializing model behavior in the data. It reveals underlying mechanisms like fairness objectives and attention-based aggregation in an interpretable way while preserving performance.

Conclusion: M2D distillation successfully enhances GNN transparency by making architectural advantages inspectable in the data space, addressing the limitation of existing explainability methods and enabling better understanding of why complex models outperform simple ones.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) achieve high performance but can be opaque to humans, making it difficult to understand and compare the many proposed architectures. While existing explainability methods attribute individual predictions to nodes, edges, or features, they do not provide architectural transparency or explain the fundamental performance gap between simple and more complex models. To address this limitation, we introduce Model-to-Data (M2D) distillation, a new framework that increases transparency by transferring model complexity into the data space. M2D distills the teacher model into an augmented graph with enriched features and structure, enabling a simple student to match the teacher's performance. By materializing model behavior in the data, our approach allows humans to inspect architectural advantages directly. We show that M2D reveals underlying mechanisms such as fairness objectives and attention-based aggregation in an interpretable way, enhancing GNN transparency while preserving performance.

</details>


### [114] [A Theory of Online Learning with Autoregressive Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06819)
*Ilan Doron-Arad,Idan Mehalel,Elchanan Mossel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Online learning framework for autoregressive generation with two feedback models (End-to-End vs Chain-of-Thought), showing different mistake-bound dependencies on generation horizon M, with chain-of-thought eliminating M dependence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the online learnability of input-output maps from autoregressive generation in language models, distinguishing between two forms of feedback (End-to-End vs Chain-of-Thought) and analyzing how mistake bounds depend on the generation horizon M.

Method: Developed an online analogue of PAC framework for autoregressive learning, analyzing two feedback models: End-to-End (observes only final token) and Chain-of-Thought (observes entire M-step trajectory). Studied mistake-bound dependencies on generation horizon M.

Result: End-to-End model shows taxonomy of mistake-bound growth rates in M (any rate between constant and logarithmic, with logarithmic ceiling). Chain-of-Thought model eliminates dependence on M altogether. Also analyzed autoregressive linear threshold classes with optimal mistake bounds.

Conclusion: Online theory of autoregressive learning parallels statistical theory but with different scale dependence on M. Chain-of-Thought feedback dramatically improves learnability by eliminating dependence on generation horizon, resolving open questions from prior work.

Abstract: Autoregressive generation lies at the heart of the mechanism of large language models. It can be viewed as the repeated application of a next-token generator: starting from an input string (prompt), the generator is applied for $M$ steps, and the last generated token is taken as the final output. [Joshi et al., 2025] proposed a PAC model for studying the learnability of the input-output maps arising from this process. We develop an online analogue of this framework, focusing on the mistake bound of learning the final output induced by an unknown next-token generator.
  We distinguish between two forms of feedback. In the End-to-End model, after each round the learner observes only the final token produced after $M$ autoregressive steps. In the Chain-of-Thought model, the learner is additionally shown the entire $M$-step trajectory. Our goal is to understand how the optimal mistake bound depends on the generation horizon $M$, and to what extent observing intermediate tokens can reduce this dependence.
  Our main results show that the online theory of autoregressive learning exhibits a qualitative picture analogous to the statistical one found by [Hanneke et al., 2026], but with a different scale of dependence on the generation horizon. In the End-to-End model, we prove a taxonomy of possible mistake-bound growth rates in the generation horizon $M$: essentially any rate between constant and logarithmic can arise. We further show that this logarithmic ceiling is unavoidable. In the Chain-of-Thought model, we show that access to the full generated trajectory eliminates the dependence on $M$ altogether.
  We also analyze autoregressive linear threshold classes, and prove optimal mistake bounds, as well as a new lower bound for the statistical setting. Along the way, our results resolve several questions left open by [Joshi et al., 2025].

</details>


### [115] [A Rod Flow Model for Adam at the Edge of Stability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06821)
*Eric Regis,Sinho Chewi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper extends rod flow modeling from gradient descent to momentum-based optimizers (Adam, heavy ball, Nesterov, RMSProp, etc.) by working in joint parameter-moment phase spaces, showing improved accuracy in tracking discrete iterates through edge-of-stability regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptive gradient methods like Adam operate at the edge of stability, but existing continuous-time models primarily focus on gradient descent. There's a need to extend these models to momentum methods, which remain underdeveloped despite their practical importance in machine learning.

Method: Extend rod flow modeling to Adam by working in the joint phase space of parameters and first moment (w, m), treating second moment ν as a smooth auxiliary variable. Develop rod flows for eight optimizers: heavy ball momentum, Nesterov momentum, scalar/per-component versions of RMSProp, Adam, and NAdam.

Result: Rod flow tracks discrete iterates through the edge-of-stability regime significantly more accurately than corresponding stable flow for all eight optimizers, as empirically evaluated on representative machine learning architectures.

Conclusion: Rod flow modeling can be successfully extended to momentum-based optimizers, providing a more accurate continuous-time representation of their dynamics through edge-of-stability regimes compared to traditional stable flow approximations.

Abstract: Cohen et al. (arXiv:2207.14484) observed that adaptive gradient methods such as Adam operate at the edge of stability. While there has been significant work on continuous-time modeling of gradient descent at the edge of stability, extending these models to momentum methods remains underdeveloped. In the gradient descent setting, Regis et al. (arXiv:2602.01480) introduced rod flow, which models consecutive iterates as an extended one-dimensional object -- a "rod." Here we extend rod flow to Adam by working in the joint phase space of parameters and first moment $(w, m)$ and treating the second moment $ν$ as a smooth auxiliary variable. We also develop rod flows for heavy ball momentum, Nesterov momentum, and scalar and per-component versions of RMSProp, Adam, and NAdam. For all eight optimizers, we empirically evaluate rod flow on representative machine learning architectures, where it tracks the discrete iterates through the edge-of-stability regime significantly more accurately than the corresponding stable flow.

</details>


### [116] [SHARP: A Self-Evolving Human-Auditable Rubric Policy for Financial Trading Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06822)
*Xiwen Chen,Wenhui Zhu,Songzhu Zheng,Kashif Rasul,Yueyue Deng,Huayu Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SHARP is a neuro-symbolic framework for autonomous financial trading that replaces unconstrained LLM prompt optimization with structured, symbolic policy rules, enabling targeted edits and better attribution in noisy markets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based trading agents use unbounded free-form prompt optimization, which fails in low signal-to-noise environments with delayed rewards because they can't distinguish systematic logic flaws from market variance, leading to policy drift and poor credit assignment.

Method: SHARP replaces unstructured text mutation with a bounded, human-readable rubric of explicit condition-action rules. When trades fail, an attribution agent uses cross-sample reasoning to isolate specific rule failures, enabling targeted atomic policy edits that undergo strict walk-forward validation.

Result: Across three equity sectors and four LLM backbones, SHARP consistently transformed generic initial heuristics into robust strategies, improving compact models' empirical performance by 10-20 percentage points (e.g., GPT-4o-mini).

Conclusion: SHARP demonstrates that LLMs can achieve dynamic, efficient adaptation while significantly enhancing the structural transparency and auditability required by institutional finance, overcoming fundamental credit assignment problems in noisy markets.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed for autonomous financial trading, a domain requiring continuous adaptation to noisy, non-stationary markets. Existing self-improving agents typically address this through unbounded free-form prompt optimization. However, in low signal-to-noise environments with delayed scalar rewards (P\&L), this unstructured approach exacerbates the fundamental credit assignment problem: optimizers cannot reliably distinguish systematic logic flaws from stochastic market variance, inevitably leading to policy drift. To overcome this bottleneck, we introduce the Self-Evolving Human-Auditable Rubric Policy (SHARP), a neuro-symbolic framework that replaces unconstrained text mutation with structured, symbolic policy optimization. SHARP confines the agent's reasoning to a bounded, human-readable rubric of explicit condition-action rules. When sub-optimal trades occur, an attribution agent employs cross-sample reasoning across multiple samples to isolate specific rule failures. This enables targeted, atomic policy edits that are subsequently regularized through strict walk-forward validation. Evaluated across three diverse equity sectors and four LLM backbones, SHARP consistently transforms generic initial heuristics into highly robust strategies, lifting the empirical performance of compact models by 10 to 20 percentage points on average (e.g., GPT-4o-mini). Ultimately, SHARP demonstrates that LLMs can achieve dynamic and efficient adaptation while significantly enhancing the structural transparency and auditability demanded by institutional finance.

</details>


### [117] [A Unified Measure-Theoretic View of Diffusion, Score-Based, and Flow Matching Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06829)
*Aditya Ranganath,Mukesh Singhal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified framework connecting diffusion models, score-based generative models, and flow matching as instances of learning time-dependent vector fields that transport a reference distribution to data distribution via stochastic/deterministic dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The methods are converging methodologically but fragmented notation and competing derivations obscure their shared structure and practical tradeoffs in sampling, stability, and computation.

Method: Survey and present unified framework for continuous-time generative modeling based on transporting reference distribution to data distribution via stochastic/deterministic dynamics governed by continuity and Fokker-Planck equations.

Result: Derived connections showing: (i) reverse-time sampling for diffusion/score-based models as controlled stochastic dynamics, (ii) probability flow ODE yields identical marginals connecting diffusion to normalizing flows, (iii) flow matching as direct velocity field regression, clarifying when it coincides/differs from score-based training.

Conclusion: Provides unified theoretical foundation connecting diffusion models, score-based generative models, and flow matching, with practical implications for sampling schemes, discretization errors, and connections to Schrodinger bridges and entropic optimal transport.

Abstract: We survey continuous-time generative modeling methods based on transporting a simple reference distribution to a data distribution via stochastic or deterministic dynamics. We present a unified framework in which diffusion models, score-based generative models, and flow matching are instances of learning a time-dependent vector field that induces a family of marginals $(ρ_t)_{t \in [0,1]}$ governed by continuity and Fokker-Planck equations. Such a unified theory is timely because these methods are converging methodologically, yet fragmented notation and competing derivations continue to obscure their shared structure and the practical tradeoffs governing sampling, stability, and computation. Within this framework, we (i) derive reverse-time sampling for diffusion and score-based models as controlled stochastic dynamics, (ii) show that the probability flow ODE yields identical marginals and connects diffusion to likelihood-based normalizing flows, and (iii) interpret flow matching as direct regression of the velocity field under a chosen interpolation, clarifying when it coincides with or differs from score-based training. We compare objectives, sampling schemes, and discretization errors under unified notation, discuss connections to Schrodinger bridges and entropic optimal transport, and summarize theoretical guarantees and open problems on approximation, stability, and scalability.

</details>


### [118] [ProtSent: Protein Sentence Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06830)
*Dan Ofer,Oriel Perets,Michal Linial,Nadav Rappoport*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProtSent fine-tunes protein language models with contrastive learning across multiple protein datasets to create better general-purpose protein embeddings that capture functional, evolutionary and structural similarity without task-specific supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protein language models produce per-residue representations but their sequence embeddings aren't explicitly trained to capture functional, evolutionary, or structural similarity between proteins, limiting their usefulness for downstream tasks.

Method: Contrastive fine-tuning framework using MultipleNegativesRankingLoss across five diverse protein-pair datasets: Pfam families, structural hard negatives, AlphaFold DB structural pairs, StringDB protein-protein interactions, and Deep Mutational Scanning data.

Result: ProtSent improves performance on 15 of 23 downstream tasks with ESM-2 150M, achieving +105% on remote homology detection, +17% on variant effect prediction, and +19.9% Recall@1 on SCOPe-40 structural retrieval. Smaller 35M variant improves 16 of 23 tasks with similar gains.

Conclusion: Contrastive fine-tuning restructures protein embedding space to better capture function and structure without task-specific supervision, making PLMs into effective general-purpose protein embedding models.

Abstract: Protein language models (pLMs) produce per-residue representations that capture evolutionary and structural information, yet their mean-pooled sequence embeddings are not explicitly trained to reflect functional, evolutionary or structural similarity between proteins. We present Protein Sentence Transformers (ProtSent), a contrastive fine-tuning framework for adapting PLMs into general-purpose embedding models. ProtSent trains with MultipleNegativesRankingLoss across five protein-pair datasets: Pfam families, structurally derived hard negatives, AlphaFold DB structural pairs, and StringDB protein--protein interactions, and Deep Mutational Scanning data. We evaluate on 23~downstream tasks using frozen embeddings with a k-nearest-neighbor probe to measure embedding neighborhood quality. On ESM-2 150M, ProtSent improves 15 of 23 tasks, with gains of +105% on remote homology detection, +17% on variant effect prediction, and +19.9% Recall@1 on SCOPe-40 structural retrieval. The 35M variant improves 16 of 23 tasks with +40.5% on remote homology and +15.5% Recall@1 on SCOPe-40. Contrastive fine-tuning restructures the embedding space to better capture protein function and structure, without any task-specific supervision. We release the models, public data, and training recipe and code.

</details>


### [119] [Why DDIM Hallucinates More than DDPM: A Theoretical Analysis of Reverse Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06831)
*Muhammad H. Ashiq,Samanyu Arora,Abhinav N. Harish,Ishaan Kharbanda,Hung Yun Tseng,Grigorios G. Chrysos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Theoretical analysis shows DDPM's stochasticity helps avoid hallucinations that trap DDIM samplers in certain regions, with practical implications for improved sampler design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the hallucination phenomena in diffusion models by comparing stochastic (DDPM) and deterministic (DDIM) samplers, particularly why DDPM performs better in avoiding certain types of sampling artifacts.

Method: Theoretical analysis of reverse ODE (DDIM) and SDE (DDPM) dynamics for Gaussian mixture targets, with empirical validation of the theoretical predictions about hallucination rates.

Result: After a critical time τ, DDIM can get stuck on segments between modes while DDPM's stochasticity helps it escape these regions, leading to significantly lower hallucination rates for DDPM. Additional stochastic steps can help DDIM avoid hallucinations.

Conclusion: DDPM's stochastic nature provides a crucial advantage over deterministic DDIM in avoiding hallucinations, and strategic use of stochasticity can improve diffusion samplers to overcome these limitations.

Abstract: We theoretically study the hallucination phenomena in two canonical diffusion samplers: the stochastic Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) and the deterministic Denoising Diffusion Implicit Model (DDIM). We analyze the reverse ODE (DDIM) and SDE (DDPM) for a Gaussian mixture target, proving that after a critical time $τ$, (a) DDIM can become stuck on the segment connecting the two nearest modes and (b) DDPM *stochasticity* helps it become unstuck from this region, thus avoiding hallucination. Our empirical validation verifies that DDPM has a significantly lower hallucination rate than DDIM when this region is entered. Building on our observations, we exhibit how using additional stochastic steps can help DDIM avoid hallucinations and offer new insights on how to design improved samplers.

</details>


### [120] [Attribution-Based Neuron Utility for Plasticity Restoration in Deep Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06834)
*Patrick Elisii,Lucas Beauchemin,Dawer Jamshed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GXD is a new utility measure for adaptive resetting in continual learning that estimates the functional cost of replacing network units, improving reliability when existing reset criteria degrade.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual learning systems need to balance knowledge acquisition with preservation, but deep networks can suffer from loss of plasticity during continued training. Existing adaptive reset interventions use proxy utility measures that can become misaligned with their intended purpose.

Method: Proposes Gradient times Difference from Reference (GXD), a theoretically motivated utility measure based on reference-based gradient attribution that estimates the first-order functional cost of replacing a network unit. This reframes adaptive resetting as an intervention cost estimation problem.

Result: GXD, as a utility measure aligned with the functional cost of resets, makes interventions more reliable in settings where existing reset criteria degrade, providing a practical path toward more robust continual learning systems.

Conclusion: Utility measures that are properly aligned with the functional cost of reset operations can improve the reliability of adaptive reset interventions in continual learning, with GXD offering a theoretically grounded approach to this problem.

Abstract: Continual learning research attempts to conserve two fundamental capabilities: new knowledge acquisition and the preservation of previously acquired knowledge. While knowledge in this case can be measured through performance over an implicit or explicit task space, model plasticity generally concerns adaptability as data distributions evolve. Though much of the literature has focused on catastrophic forgetting, deep networks can also suffer from loss of plasticity, becoming progressively harder to update under continued training. Recent research has identified multiple mechanisms underlying this phenomenon, including neuron saturation, parameter norm growth, and loss of useful curvature directions. Adaptive reset-based interventions, which selectively reinitialize low-utility network parameters, have emerged as practical solutions to restore trainability. Existing utility measures used to guide resets, such as activation magnitude, contribution utility, or gradient-based activity, rely on proxy signals that can become misaligned with the intervention they are meant to guide. In this paper, we introduce gradient times difference from reference (GXD), a theoretically motivated utility measure based on reference-based gradient attribution that estimates the first-order functional cost of replacing a unit. Our results show that utility measures aligned with the functional cost of the reset can make interventions more reliable in settings where existing reset criteria degrade. GXD reframes adaptive resetting as an intervention cost estimation problem, providing a practical path toward more robust continual learning systems.

</details>


### [121] [On Privacy Leakage in Tabular Diffusion Models: Influential Factors, Attacker Knowledge, and Metrics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06835)
*Masoumeh Shafieinejad,D. B. Emerson,Behnoosh Zamanlooy,Elaheh Bassak,Fatemeh Tavakoli,Sara Kodeiri,Marcelo Lotif,Xi He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper examines privacy risks in tabular diffusion models for synthetic data generation, showing that membership inference attacks can succeed even without perfect attacker knowledge, and warns against relying on heuristic privacy metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's growing interest in using tabular diffusion models to generate synthetic proxies for real tabular data to reduce privacy risks and proprietary data exposure, making it essential to understand and measure the privacy risks associated with these models.

Method: The research uses state-of-the-art membership inference attacks on tabular diffusion models in both black-box and white-box settings to quantify privacy leakage, examining factors like training setup, synthesis choices, and attacker knowledge.

Result: The study demonstrates that adversaries can construct successful attacks without perfect knowledge of the training setup, identical data distributions, or massive compute resources, and reveals that heuristic privacy metrics like distance-to-closest record have significant limitations.

Conclusion: Tabular diffusion models pose substantial privacy risks through membership inference attacks, requiring more robust privacy assessments beyond simple heuristic metrics, as attackers don't need complete information about the training setup or data distribution to succeed.

Abstract: Tabular data plays an important role in many fields and industries, including those with elevated privacy considerations and risks. As such, there is a rising interest in generating high-quality synthetic proxies for real tabular data as a means of reducing privacy risk and proprietary data exposure. With tabular diffusion models (TDMs) demonstrating leading performance in synthesizing such data, understanding and measuring the privacy risks associated with these models is imperative. Leveraging state-of-the-art membership inference attacks for TDMs in both black- and white-box settings, this work quantifies the impact of training setup, synthesis choices, and attacker knowledge on privacy leakage. Moreover, the results demonstrate that adversaries need not have perfect knowledge of the training setup, identical data distributions, or massive compute resources to construct successful attacks. Finally, the pitfalls associated with applying heuristic privacy metrics, such as distance-to-closest record, are revealed.

</details>


### [122] [How to Compress KV Cache in RL Post-Training? Shadow Mask Distillation for Memory-Efficient Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06850)
*Rui Zhu,Weiheng Bai,Qiushi Wu,Yang Ren,Haixu Tang,Yuchu Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RL methods for LLMs face a "memory wall" in long-context reasoning tasks due to KV cache footprint during online rollouts, and KV cache compression introduces off-policy bias that's amplified by RL instability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL frameworks like RLHF/RLAIF require online trajectory generation for LLMs, but for long-context reasoning tasks, this rollout phase creates severe memory constraints due to massive KV cache requirements. While KV cache compression can reduce memory overhead, it introduces off-policy bias that existing statistical methods can't effectively correct.

Method: The paper examines the fundamental issue of KV cache compression during RL rollouts, analyzing how even near-lossless compression introduces critical off-policy bias that's amplified by RL optimization instability. It critiques existing approaches like importance reweighting for failing to handle the bias-variance tradeoff effectively.

Result: The analysis reveals that minor approximation errors from KV cache compression are drastically amplified by RL instability due to the mismatch between sparse sampling context and dense learning context. Statistical methods like importance reweighting suffer from high gradient variance and severe sample inefficiency, failing to correct the magnified bias.

Conclusion: Current approaches to addressing the memory wall in RL for LLMs through KV cache compression create fundamental off-policy bias problems that existing statistical correction methods cannot solve effectively, highlighting a critical research gap in making RL scalable for long-context reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a crucial paradigm for unlocking the advanced reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), encompassing frameworks like RLHF and RLAIF. Regardless of the specific optimization algorithm (e.g., PPO, GRPO, or Online DPO), online RL inherently requires an exploratory trajectory generation (rollout) phase. However, for long-context reasoning tasks, this rollout phase imposes a severe ``memory wall'' due to the exorbitant Key-Value (KV) cache footprint. While applying KV cache compression during rollouts mitigates this memory overhead, it induces a critical off-policy bias. Although modern KV compression is often nearly lossless during standard inference, even minuscule approximation errors are drastically amplified by the inherent instability of RL optimization. Specifically, the sampler generates responses under a sparse context, whereas the learner updates parameters using the full, dense context. Existing statistical solutions, such as importance reweighting, struggle to correct this magnified bias, suffering from high gradient variance and severe sample inefficiency.

</details>


### [123] [Benchmarked Yet Not Measured -- Generative AI Should be Evaluated Against Real-World Utility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06856)
*Ishani Mondal,Shweta Bhardwaj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Generative AI shows benchmark-reality disconnect; proposed SCU-GenEval framework shifts evaluation from static benchmarks to longitudinal utility measurement focusing on human outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative AI systems perform well on benchmarks but fail in real-world deployment across 28 cases. The paper identifies a "benchmark utility gap" caused by three evaluation failures: proxy displacement, temporal collapse, and distributional concealment.

Method: Proposes SCU-GenEval, a four-stage evaluation framework: 1) stakeholder-goal mapping, 2) construct-indicator specification, 3) mechanism modeling, and 4) longitudinal utility measurement. Introduces supporting instruments: structured deployment protocols, context-conditioned user simulators, and persona-goal proxy metrics.

Result: Identifies the need to measure "utility" - the change in stakeholder capability through sustained AI interaction - rather than just output properties. Provides concrete framework to operationalize this perspective.

Conclusion: Progress in generative AI must be evaluated through measurable improvements in human outcomes, not benchmark performance alone. Calls for paradigm shift from benchmark-centered transparency to stakeholder, goal, and context-conditioned utility transparency.

Abstract: Generative AI systems achieve impressive performance on standard benchmarks yet fail to deliver real-world utility, a disconnect we identify across 28 deployment cases spanning education, healthcare, software engineering, and law. We argue that this benchmark utility gap arises from three recurring failures in evaluation practice: proxy displacement, temporal collapse, and distributional concealment. Motivated by these observations, we argue that generative AI evaluation requires a paradigm shift from static benchmark-centered transparency toward stakeholder, goal, and context-conditioned utility transparency grounded in human outcome trajectories. Existing evaluations primarily characterize properties of model outputs, while deployment success depends on whether interaction with AI improves stakeholders' ability to achieve their goals over time. The missing construct is therefore utility: the change in a stakeholder's capability induced through sustained interaction with an AI system within a deployment context. To operationalize this perspective, we propose SCU-GenEval, a four-stage evaluation framework consisting of stakeholder-goal mapping, construct-indicator specification, mechanism modeling, and longitudinal utility measurement. To make these stages practically deployable, we introduce three supporting instruments: structured deployment protocols, context-conditioned user simulators, and persona- and goal-conditioned proxy metrics. We conclude with domain-specific calls to action, arguing that progress in generative AI must be evaluated through measurable improvements in human outcomes rather than benchmark performance alone.

</details>


### [124] [Christoffel-DPS: Optimal sensor placement in diffusion posterior sampling for arbitrary distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06861)
*James Rowbottom,Nick Huang,Carola-Bibiane Schönlieb,Ben Adcock*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A distribution-free sensor placement framework using Christoffel functions for optimal sensor placement that works with generative recovery models like diffusion posterior sampling, outperforming classical Gaussian-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical optimal sensor placement (OSP) methods rely on Gaussian assumptions and can't handle complex real-world distributions, while existing sensor-selection methods either need too many sensors or mismatch with modern recovery models like generative diffusion models.

Method: Introduces Christoffel-DPS framework based on Christoffel function, providing mathematical guarantees for optimal sampling and recovery with arbitrary sensors and signal distributions. Develops offline and online variants for generative models.

Result: Christoffel-DPS outperforms Gaussian OSP baselines and existing generative-model placement methods, particularly effective in low sensor budget regimes. The framework is model-agnostic and works with various unconditional DPS and flow-matching models.

Conclusion: Distribution-free sensing using Christoffel functions provides theoretically principled and practically superior sensor placement that matches modern recovery models, addressing the mismatch between classical OSP tools and advanced generative reconstruction techniques.

Abstract: State estimation is a critical task in scientific, engineering and control applications. Since the reliability of reconstructions depends on the number and position of sensors, optimal sensor placement (OSP) is essential in scenarios where measurements are sparse and expensive. Classical OSP approaches rely on Gaussian assumptions and are consequently unable to account for the complex distributions encountered in many real-world systems. Generative-model-based reconstruction using sensor guided diffusion posterior sampling (DPS) has emerged as a promising technique for reconstructing states from highly complex distributions. However, existing sensor-selection methods either require unrealistically many sensors or emulate classical OSP, creating a mismatch between modern recovery models with classical OSP tools motivating the need for fundamentally new ideas towards OSP that match the recent advances made in powerful recovery models. We introduce a distribution-free sensor placement framework based on the Christoffel function: a mathematical formulation of optimal sampling and recovery guarantees for posterior sampling with arbitrary sensors and signal distributions, from which we derive a new OSP strategy with non-asymptotic bounds on the number of sensors needed for recovery. We develop Christoffel-DPS, with offline and online variants, instantiating Christoffel sampling for generative models. Christoffel-DPS outperforms Gaussian OSP baselines and existing generative-model placement methods, validating that distribution-free sensing is both theoretically principled and practically superior. The framework is model-agnostic; we demonstrate its application to a range of unconditional DPS and flow-matching models on structurally non-Gaussian benchmarks, showing the efficacy of Christoffel-DPS in low sensor budget regimes.

</details>


### [125] [Multi-Objective Multi-Agent Bandits: From Learning Efficiency to Fairness Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06864)
*John Wang,Mengfan Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes two algorithms for multi-objective multi-agent multi-armed bandits with fairness constraints, achieving logarithmic regret for efficiency and sublinear regret for fairness, demonstrating trade-offs between efficiency and fairness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing the challenge of multi-objective multi-agent bandits where agents observe heterogeneous reward vectors and communicate over time-varying graphs. The paper aims to tackle both efficient learning (measured by Pareto regret) and fair learning (captured via social welfare) in a distributed multi-agent setting.

Method: Two novel algorithms: 1) Pareto UCB1 Gossip for efficiency, using novel exploration radius to separate statistical uncertainty from consensus error; 2) Simulated NSW UCB Gossip for fairness, integrating preference-based reward simulation, gossip-based utility estimation, and UCB-style exploration.

Result: Pareto UCB1 Gossip achieves O(log T) regret and O(√T) instance-independent rate. Simulated NSW UCB Gossip achieves O(T^(3/4)) instance-independent regret. Experiments show 100% improvement in efficiency setting and 50% improvement in fairness setting over baselines.

Conclusion: The paper successfully addresses efficiency-fairness trade-offs in multi-objective multi-agent bandits, showing that fairness constraints limit information aggregation and slow convergence. The proposed algorithms outperform baselines significantly, providing practical solutions for distributed multi-agent systems with both efficiency and fairness objectives.

Abstract: We study multi-objective multi-agent multi-armed bandits (MO-MA-MAB) under stochastic rewards, where agents observe heterogeneous reward vectors and communicate over time-varying graphs. We formulate this emerging problem setting to address \emph{efficient learning}, measured by Pareto regret, and incorporate \emph{fair learning} as an additional goal, captured via social welfare. To measure efficiency, we formulate Pareto regret and develop \textsc{Pareto UCB1 Gossip}, whose novel exploration radius explicitly separates statistical uncertainty in Pareto-based inference from consensus error. To express the fairness constraint, we formulate a Nash Social Welfare objective over preference-scalarized rewards and propose \textsc{Simulated NSW UCB Gossip}, which integrates preference-based reward simulation, gossip-based utility estimation, and UCB-style exploration. We prove that \textsc{Pareto UCB1 Gossip} achieves \(\mathcal{O}(\log T)\) regret and an instance-independent rate of \(\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{T})\), while \textsc{Simulated NSW UCB Gossip} achieves an instance-independent regret bound of \(\mathcal{O}(T^{3/4})\). This separation reveals the cost of imposing the fairness constraint to our efficiency objective: fairness limits information aggregation and slows convergence. Experiments show that our methods consistently outperform baselines, improving performance by approximately \(100\%\) and \(50\%\) in the efficiency and fairness settings, respectively.

</details>


### [126] [Dataset Watermarking for Closed LLMs with Provable Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06865)
*Pengrun Huang,Kamalika Chaudhuri,Yu-Xiang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First dataset watermarking method for closed LLMs with provable detection that embeds watermarks via increased co-occurrence of random word pairs and detects via statistical analysis of model outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are trained on loosely curated data that may include proprietary datasets or evaluation benchmarks, creating need for dataset watermarking to detect unauthorized training.

Method: Embed dataset-level watermark by increasing co-occurrence frequency of randomly selected word pairs through rephrasing, then detect using statistical test on co-occurrence patterns in model-generated outputs.

Result: Method reliably detects watermark (p<0.01) during fine-tuning stage, remains effective when watermarked data constitutes only ~1% of total fine-tuning tokens, and preserves dataset utility and semantic integrity.

Conclusion: This work presents the first effective dataset watermarking solution for closed LLMs with provable detection that works even with minimal watermarked data presence while maintaining dataset quality.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are pre-trained and post-trained on vast amounts of loosely curated data, raising the possibility that these models may have been trained on proprietary datasets or the same benchmarks used for evaluation. This motivates the need for dataset watermarking: designing datasets such that training on them leaves detectable signatures in the resulting model. Prior work has explored this problem for open models. We introduce the first dataset watermarking method for closed LLMs with provable detection. In particular, we embed a dataset-level watermark signal by increasing the co-occurrence frequency of randomly selected word pairs through rephrasing, and detect it using a statistical test on co-occurrence patterns in model-generated outputs. We evaluate our method with multiple base models and benchmark datasets and show that it reliably detects the watermark ($p <0.01$) in the fine-tuning stage. Notably, our method remains effective in a data mixture setting where the watermarked dataset constitutes only approximately $1\%$ of the total fine-tuning tokens. Furthermore, we show that our method preserves the utility and semantic integrity of the benchmark.

</details>


### [127] [A Finite-Iteration Theory for Asynchronous Categorical Distributional Temporal-Difference Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06866)
*Ege C. Kaya,Abolfazl Hashemi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides finite-iteration guarantees for two categorical temporal-difference learning methods (scalar categorical TD in Cramér geometry and signed-categorical TD in MMD geometry) under practical asynchronous single-state updates driven by Markovian trajectories, bridging a gap between theory and practical implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing theory for distributional policy evaluation largely focuses on synchronous full-state updates under generative models, model-based estimators, or accelerated variants, while practical implementations use asynchronous single-state updates driven by Markovian trajectories. This creates a gap between theory and practice that needs to be addressed.

Method: The authors analyze two categorical policy-evaluation methods: (1) scalar categorical temporal-difference learning in the Cramér geometry, and (2) multivariate signed-categorical temporal-difference learning in the maximum mean discrepancy geometry. Both algorithms are reformulated as asynchronous single-state stochastic-approximation recursions that contract in a statewise supremum norm after suitable isometric embeddings.

Result: The analysis permits finite-iteration guarantees in three scenarios: (1) discounted problems under i.i.d. state sampling, (2) discounted problems under Markovian state sampling, and (3) undiscounted fixed-horizon problems under i.i.d. episodic sampling.

Conclusion: The paper successfully bridges the gap between theoretical analysis and practical implementation of categorical temporal-difference learning by providing finite-iteration convergence guarantees for asynchronous single-state updates under realistic Markovian trajectory sampling, which aligns with how these algorithms are actually deployed in practice.

Abstract: Recent non-asymptotic analyses have substantially advanced the theory of distributional policy evaluation, but they largely concern synchronous full-state updates under a generative model, model-based estimators, accelerated variants, or different approximation architectures. Standard categorical temporal-difference learning is typically used in a different regime. It asynchronously performs a single-state update at each iteration and, in online settings, is driven by a Markovian trajectory. This leaves an important gap between existing finite-iteration theory and the categorical recursions most closely aligned with practical distributional temporal-difference implementations. We bridge this gap for two categorical policy-evaluation methods: scalar categorical temporal-difference learning in the Cramér geometry and multivariate signed-categorical temporal-difference learning in the maximum mean discrepancy geometry. After suitable isometric embeddings, both algorithms take the form of asynchronous single-state stochastic-approximation recursions that contract in a statewise supremum norm. This permits finite-iteration guarantees in discounted problems under both i.i.d. and Markovian state sampling, and in undiscounted fixed-horizon problems under i.i.d. episodic sampling.

</details>


### [128] [When Descent Is Too Stable: Event-Triggered Hamiltonian Learning to Optimize](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06868)
*Yi Wang,Chandrajit Bajaj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SHAPE is a structured adaptive port-Hamiltonian optimizer that improves fixed-budget nonconvex optimization by dynamically balancing local descent, basin exploitation, and triggered exploration when stagnation occurs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed-budget nonconvex optimization often fails because optimizers get stuck refining uninformative local minima after finding nearby stationary points. The key challenge is determining when to continue descending, when to exploit promising basins, and when to trigger movement away from stagnation.

Method: SHAPE lifts optimization to an augmented phase space (q, p) where q is the candidate solution and p carries directional sensitivity. It uses a learned Hamiltonian vector field for structured local descent and implements event-triggered transitions when local equilibria are detected. A controller processes gradient oracle information, and the design preserves passivity-compatible structure while working with various gradient inputs.

Result: Experiments on fixed-budget nonconvex optimization tasks show that SHAPE improves best-so-far performance compared to fixed-policy optimizers. The method demonstrates effectiveness in balancing descent, exploration, and budget allocation.

Conclusion: Adaptive Hamiltonian energy shaping provides a principled mechanism for balancing exploration and exploitation in difficult optimization landscapes, offering a structured approach to event-triggered minima hunting under local information constraints.

Abstract: Fixed-budget nonconvex optimization can fail not because local descent is unstable, but because it is too stable: after reaching a nearby stationary point, an optimizer may spend the remaining evaluations refining an uninformative local minimum. We formulate this failure mode as a control problem over optimizer dynamics, where the learner must decide when to descend, when to exploit a promising basin, and when stagnation should trigger movement elsewhere. We introduce SHAPE, a structured adaptive port-Hamiltonian task-family optimizer for event-triggered minima hunting under local information. Starting from gradient-descent dynamics, SHAPE lifts optimization to an augmented phase space $(q, p)$, where the primal state $q$ represents the candidate solution, the cotangent variable $p$ carries directional sensitivity, and a controller $u$ provides processed information from current gradient oracle. Within each stage, a learned Hamiltonian vector field induces structured local descent; across stages, a fixed event clock in the implementation updates ports and memory when local equilibria are detected, with stage-dependent horizons treated in the analysis as a direct generalization. This design preserves a passivity-compatible structure while allowing the same trained policy to use clean, stochastic, or estimated gradient inputs. Experiments on fixed-budget nonconvex optimization tasks show that SHAPE improves best-so-far performance compared with fixed-policy optimizers. These results suggest that adaptive Hamiltonian energy shaping provides a principled mechanism for balancing descent, exploration, and budget allocation in difficult optimization landscapes.

</details>


### [129] [Continuous First, Discrete Later: VQ-VAEs Without Dimensional Collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06870)
*Xinyu Zhao,Nikita Karagodin,Hamed Hassani,Sinan Hersek,Paul Pu Liang,Yury Polyanskiy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adding an initial autoencoder warm-up phase before VQ-VAE training prevents dimensional collapse, improving representation quality and reducing reconstruction/perceptual loss across image and audio models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While most VQ-VAE improvements focus on codebook size and utilization, the problem of dimensional collapse (where representations occupy only 1-2% of full rank) remains unaddressed, creating a hard loss lower bound that existing techniques can't surpass.

Method: Theoretical analysis combining sequential learning effects from Saxe et al. (2014) with rate-distortion theory, leading to a practical solution: an AE Warm-Up phase that trains the model as a regular (unquantized) autoencoder before introducing vector quantization.

Result: AE Warm-Up successfully restores representation dimension, raising VQGAN codebook effective dimension from 3-5 to 17-19 (reducing rFID by 17-35%) and WavTokenizer dimension from 4 to 17-19 (improving PESQ by 11-14%). Warm-up duration governs final loss and theoretical predictions match experimental results.

Conclusion: Dimensional collapse is a fundamental limitation in VQ-VAEs that can be effectively mitigated through an initial autoencoder warm-up phase, providing theoretical justification and empirical validation for improved representation learning across domains.

Abstract: While many approaches to improve VQ-VAE performance focus on codebook size and utilization, the effect of dimensional collapse, where trained VQ-VAE representations live in an extremely low-dimensional subspace (1-2% of full rank), remains unaddressed. We show theoretically and empirically that dimension collapse causes a hard loss lower bound that various codebook improvement techniques fail to surpass. Our analytic framework extends the sequential learning effect of Saxe et al. [2014] by introducing ideas from rate-distortion theory and explains how the latent collapse is caused by the VQ suppressing lower-variance directions. Our theory justifies a simple solution: a "warm-up phase" that trains the model as an (unquantized) autoencoder before introducing VQ. On both synthetic experiments and large-scale image (VQGAN) and audio (WavTokenizer) VQ-VAEs, we show that AE Warm-Up successfully restores representation dimension, leading to lower reconstruction and perceptual loss at the same training budget. Across codebook sizes $K \in$ {$2^{10}, 2^{14}, 2^{16}$}, AE warm-up raises VQGAN codebook effective dimension from 3-5 to 17-19 and reduces rFID by 17-35%; on WavTokenizer at $K \in$ {$2^{13}, 2^{14}$}, it raises codebook dimension from 4 to 17-19 and improves PESQ by 11-14%. We empirically characterize how warm-up duration governs the achievable final loss. In agreement with experiment, our theoretical analysis predicts downstream performance as a function of warm-up length, enabling an adaptive criterion for switching from AE Warm-up to VQ-VAE training.

</details>


### [130] [On the Divergence of Differential Temporal Difference Learning without Local Clocks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06874)
*David Antrobius,Shangtong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In reinforcement learning, learning rates based on local state-visit counts can converge for average-reward TD learning when global time-based rates diverge, revealing a fundamental difference from discounted RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the relationship between learning rates based on global time steps vs. local state-visit counts in RL. While in discounted RL these two types have equivalent convergence properties, it's unknown whether this holds for average-reward RL, particularly closing an open problem from prior work.

Method: The authors distinguish two learning rate types: global clock rates (α_t depending only on time step t) and local clock rates (α_ν(S_t,t) depending on state visit counts ν(s,t)). They analyze convergence properties of differential temporal difference learning algorithms under these different learning rate schedules.

Result: The key result is a counterexample showing that differential TD learning can converge with local clock learning rates but diverge with global clock learning rates in average-reward RL. This contrasts with discounted RL where convergence properties are equivalent for both rate types.

Conclusion: The correspondence between global and local clock learning rate convergence properties, which holds in discounted RL, breaks down in average-reward RL. This resolves an open problem from previous work and has important implications for learning rate selection in average-reward RL algorithms.
