Abstract: Learning rate is a critical component of reinforcement learning (RL). This work uses global and local clocks to distinguish two types of learning rates. The former is of the standard form $α_t$ that depends only on the time step $t$ (i.e., a global clock). The latter is of the form $α_{ν(S_t, t)}$, where $ν(s, t)$ counts the number of visits to state $s$ until time $t$ (i.e., a local clock). In discounted RL, an RL algorithm that is convergent with a local clock is always also convergent with a global clock, and vice versa. We are not aware of any counterexample. The key contribution of this work is to show that this nice correspondence breaks down in average-reward RL. Specifically, we construct a counterexample showing that although differential temporal difference learning is convergent with a local clock, it can diverge with a global clock. This counterexample closes the open problem in Wan et al. [2021], Blaser et al. [2026].

</details>


### [131] [Temporal Attention for Adaptive Control of Euler-Lagrange Systems with Unobservable Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06877)
*Giansalvo Cirrincione,Adriano Fagiolini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A meta-control architecture using self-attention on motion history for adaptive control of Euler-Lagrange systems with unobservable friction states, outperforming Transformers in short/matched memory regimes but showing limitations in long memory scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptive control of Euler-Lagrange systems is challenging when friction depends on unobservable internal states, making closed-loop behavior non-Markovian and causing standard adaptive laws to lose convergence guarantees.

Method: Proposes a meta-control architecture with computed-torque controller gains generated by a self-attention block processing recent motion history. Head count is selected before training via surrogate analysis of memory-state gradient autocovariance using temporal rank-tracking framework. Architecture is then fixed for RL training under shielded admissibility constraint.

Result: Single-layer attention-only meta-controller outperformed deeper Transformer baseline in short/matched memory regimes (12% and 19% tracking-error reductions, effect sizes d ≈ -1.1 and -2.1, p<0.05). However, in long memory regime, advantage disappeared with 40% of runs showing divergence or policy collapse, revealing static head-count prescription weakness.

Conclusion: The attention-based approach works well for short/matched memory friction but static architecture selection fails for long memory. Future work should integrate rank-tracking into RL loop for dynamic head adjustment during runtime.

Abstract: Adaptive control of Euler-Lagrange systems is challenging when friction is governed by a finite-horizon internal state that is not directly observable from joint measurements. In this setting, the measured closed-loop state is no longer Markovian, and standard certainty-equivalence adaptive laws may lose their convergence guarantees.
  The paper proposes a meta-control architecture in which the gains of a computed-torque controller are generated by a self-attention block processing a short window of recent motion history. The number of attention heads is selected before policy training through a surrogate analysis of the autocovariance of the memory-state gradient along the temporal window. This surrogate is based on a temporal adaptation of an incremental rank-tracking framework previously developed by the authors. The selected head count is then fixed and used as an architectural hyperparameter in a reinforcement-learning stage, where the policy is trained under a shielded admissibility constraint.
  The approach is tested on a 2-DOF manipulator with nonlinear friction and variable payload. In the short and matched memory regimes, the single-layer attention-only meta-controller outperforms a deeper Transformer baseline, with tracking-error reductions of 12 and 19 percentage points, respectively. The reported effect sizes are large, with d approximately -1.1 and -2.1, and Mann-Whitney p < 0.05 in both cases.
  In the long memory regime, however, the advantage disappears. Four out of ten training runs show either divergence or payload-invariant policy collapse, revealing a weakness in the static Phase-1 head-count prescription. This motivates moving rank-tracking inside the reinforcement-learning loop, allowing attention heads to be pruned or grown at runtime instead of fixed before training.

</details>


### [132] [Better Protein Function Prediction by Modeling Survivorship Bias](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06879)
*Zhongmou Chao,Poompol Buathong,Ekaterina Selivanovitch,Susan Daniel,Peter I. Frazier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Evo-PU is a positive-unlabeled learning framework that incorporates evolutionary mutation models to account for survivorship bias in protein sequence data, improving predictions of protein functionality compared to standard methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protein sequence data suffers from survivorship bias - we only observe sequences from surviving organisms, while non-functional mutations are eliminated by natural selection. Standard positive-unlabeled (PU) learning ignores the evolutionary processes that shape sequence observability, leading to suboptimal predictions.

Method: Evo-PU incorporates scientific understanding of nucleotide mutation to model survivorship bias for single-organism sequence data. It differentiates between missing sequences that are evolutionarily accessible but unobserved (likely non-functional) versus those that are evolutionarily unlikely to arise.

Result: On three prediction tasks using single-organism uniform-coverage surveillance data (influenza, RSV mutagenesis studies, and SARS-CoV-2 variant prediction), Evo-PU outperforms standard PU learning, one-class classification, and protein language models. On multi-organism datasets with heterogeneous coverage, opportunities for generalization were identified.

Conclusion: Incorporating evolutionary models of mutation into PU learning frameworks improves protein function prediction by properly accounting for survivorship bias, especially for uniform-coverage surveillance data. This approach better distinguishes between non-functional proteins and evolutionarily inaccessible sequences.

Abstract: Protein sequence data from nature exhibits survivorship bias: we only observe data from those organisms that survive and reproduce, while non-functional protein mutations are eliminated by natural selection. Thus, predicting whether a protein sequence is functional often requires learning from positive examples alone. While positive-unlabeled (PU) learning frameworks offer a generic solution to this problem, existing PU methods ignore the evolutionary processes that shape sequence observability and cause survivorship bias. Consider a sequence that is one mutation away from a commonly-observed protein variant in a well-surveilled organism. If the sequence were functional, it would likely be observed. If it is not observed, this suggests non-functionality. In contrast, sequences that are unlikely to arise through mutation may be missing simply because they never arose. Thus, these two kinds of missing sequences should be treated differently when training models. In this work, we propose Evo-PU, a PU learning framework that uses a scientific understanding of nucleotide mutation to model survivorship bias for well-surveilled single-organism sequence data. On three prediction tasks using single-organism uniform-coverage surveillance data -- predicting results from held-out influenza and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) mutagenesis studies, and predicting future SARS-CoV-2 variants -- Evo-PU outperforms standard PU learning, one-class classification (OCC), and protein language models (PLMs). On prediction tasks from multi-organism ProteinGym datasets with more heterogeneous surveillance coverage, we identify opportunities to generalize our approach.

</details>


### [133] [Don't Retrain, Align: Adapting Autoregressive LMs to Diffusion LMs via Representation Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06885)
*Fred Zhangzhi Peng,Alexis Fox,Anru R. Zhang,Alexander Tong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new method (REPR-ALIGN) that aligns diffusion language model representations with pretrained autoregressive model representations to accelerate DLM training and improve low-data performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion language models show complementary capabilities to autoregressive models, but existing AR-to-DLM conversion methods mainly transfer parameters through continued training with modifications. The authors question whether the internal representation geometry learned by next-token prediction can be explicitly preserved during conversion.

Method: REPR-ALIGN: a representation alignment objective that adapts a bidirectional masked diffusion model to reuse representations from a pretrained AR model of identical architecture. They align hidden states of the DLM to the frozen AR model at every layer using cosine similarity while optimizing the standard masked denoising objective, with no adapters or architectural changes beyond attention mask.

Result: The simple alignment yields up to 4x training acceleration in their setting and is particularly effective in low-data regimes. The method suggests linguistic representations can transfer across generation order.

Conclusion: Representation alignment provides a simple and effective technique for training diffusion language models by preserving semantic structure learned by AR pretraining during DLM training, viewing DLM training as relearning decoding paths rather than relearning language representations.

Abstract: Diffusion language models (DLMs) have recently demonstrated capabilities that complement standard autoregressive (AR) models, particularly in non-sequential generation and bidirectional editing. Although recent work has shown that pretrained autoregressive checkpoints can be converted into diffusion language models, existing recipes primarily transfer parameters through continued denoising training with objective- and attention-level modifications. We instead ask whether the internal representation geometry learned by next-token prediction can be explicitly preserved during AR-to-DLM conversion. We hypothesize that much of the semantic structure learned by AR pretraining can transfer across generation orders, and thus DLM training should be viewed as relearning the decoding path rather than relearning language representations. To investigate this, we introduce REPR-ALIGN, a representation alignment objective that adapts a bidirectional masked diffusion model to reuse representations from a pretrained AR model of identical architecture. Concretely, we align the hidden states of the DLM to the frozen AR model at every layer using cosine similarity, while optimizing the standard masked denoising objective. This simple alignment, with no adapters and no architectural changes beyond the attention mask, yields up to 4x training acceleration in our setting and is particularly effective in low-data regimes. Our results suggest that linguistic representations can transfer across generation order, and that representation alignment provides a simple and effective technique for training diffusion language models. Code is available at https://github.com/pengzhangzhi/Open-dLLM.

</details>


### [134] [Streaming Adversarial Robustness in Fuzzy ARTMAP: Mechanism-Aligned Evaluation, Progressive Training, and Interpretable Diagnostics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06902)
*Shane Cairns,Leonardo Enzo Brito da Silva,Sasha Petrenko,Donald C. Wunsch,Jian Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies adversarial robustness in streaming Fuzzy ARTMAP networks, introduces WB-Softmax white-box attack surrogate, shows defense rankings reverse across protocols, and demonstrates ART's category geometry enables interpretable diagnosis of vulnerabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adversarial robustness has been extensively studied for offline deep networks, but there is limited understanding of robustness in strict single-pass streaming neural learners like Fuzzy ARTMAP, which operate differently from conventional deep networks.

Method: Introduces WB-Softmax, a differentiable white-box attack surrogate aligned with ARTMAP's category-competition and map-field prediction mechanism. Uses formal streaming evaluation principle requiring robustness assessment on final deployed model. Evaluates across four image benchmarks and compares different defense strategies including adversarial training and progressive two-stage selective training.

Result: WB-Softmax achieves 89-100% attack success on vanilla Fuzzy ARTMAP models. Defense rankings can reverse across protocols: offline adversarial training appears strong under transfer attacks but collapses under adaptive white-box evaluation, while progressive two-stage selective training provides strongest overall replay-free robustness. ART's explicit category geometry enables interpretable diagnosis of separation collapse and match-score inversion.

Conclusion: The paper provides a mechanism-aligned, protocol-aware framework for adversarial robustness in streaming prototype-based learners, highlighting the importance of protocol-aware evaluation and showing how ART's architecture enables both vulnerability analysis and robust defense design.

Abstract: Adversarial robustness has been studied extensively for offline deep networks, but less is known about strict single-pass streaming neural learners. This paper studies adversarial robustness in Fuzzy ARTMAP, an Adaptive Resonance Theory architecture based on category competition, complement coding, match tracking, and replay-free prototype updates. We introduce WB-Softmax, a differentiable white-box attack surrogate aligned with ARTMAP's category-competition and map-field prediction mechanism, and formalize a streaming evaluation principle requiring robustness to be assessed on the final deployed model. Across four image benchmarks, WB-Softmax achieves 89-100% attack success on vanilla Fuzzy ARTMAP models. We show that defense rankings can reverse across protocols: offline adversarial training may appear strong under transfer attacks yet collapse under adaptive white-box evaluation, whereas progressive two-stage selective training provides the strongest overall replay-free robustness. We further show that ART's explicit category geometry enables interpretable diagnosis of separation collapse and match-score inversion. These results provide a mechanism-aligned, protocol-aware framework for adversarial robustness in streaming prototype-based learners.

</details>


### [135] [Conservative Flows: A New Paradigm of Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06905)
*Eshed Gal,Md Shahriar Rahim Siddiqui,Moshe Eliasof,Eldad Haber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new generative modeling paradigm using discrete stochastic dynamics initialized from data, not noise, that preserves probability and works with existing flow models


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current generative models mostly rely on transporting from noise to data; the authors propose an alternative approach using stochastic dynamics that leaves the data distribution invariant, starting from data-supported states instead of noise

Method: Developed two probability-preserving sampling mechanisms: 1) corrected Langevin dynamics with Metropolis adjustment, and 2) predictor-corrector flow. Both operate directly on existing pretrained flow model checkpoints without retraining

Result: Validated on synthetic Swiss-roll target, ImageNet-256 and Oxford Flowers-102 datasets; the proposed samplers consistently improved over original generation procedures across all benchmarks

Conclusion: The framework offers a viable alternative to conventional noise-to-data transport methods, enabling better generation quality from existing flow models through probability-preserving discrete stochastic dynamics initialized from data

Abstract: Modern generative modeling is dominated by transport from a noise prior to data. We propose an alternative paradigm in which generation is performed by a discrete stochastic dynamics that leaves the data distribution invariant, initialized from data-supported states rather than from noise. The framework can utilize any pretrained flow model. We develop two probability-preserving sampling mechanisms, a corrected Langevin dynamics with a Metropolis adjustment and a predictor-corrector flow, that operate directly on existing checkpoints. We validate the framework on a synthetic Swiss-roll target, ImageNet-256 and Oxford Flowers-102, where our samplers consistently improve over the original generation procedures.

</details>


### [136] [TraXion: Rethinking Pre-training Frameworks for Mobility and Beyond](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06906)
*Shang-Ling Hsu,Mark Tenzer,Cyrus Shahabi,Khurram Shafique*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TraXion is a pre-training framework designed for multi-entity spatiotemporal event streams (MESES) that addresses three unique structural properties of human mobility data, outperforming task-specific baselines across multiple domains including mobility, security, and healthcare.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing pre-training approaches treat mobility data like language modeling (trajectories as sentences, visits as tokens), but this analogy fails because human mobility has three unique structural properties: tuple-valued events (location, time, activity), persistent user signatures across trajectories, and interdependencies between users due to co-location. These properties define a broader class called MESES that includes various real-world event streams.

Method: The authors propose TraXion, a pre-training framework whose objectives and architecture are jointly designed to satisfy three axioms derived from MESES properties. They formalize the three structural properties as axioms that any MESES pre-training framework should satisfy, then design TraXion specifically to meet these requirements.

Result: TraXion outperforms task-specific baselines on every task across six public mobility datasets, covering anomaly detection, next-POI recommendation, next-visit prediction, and social-link prediction. When applied unchanged to enterprise authentication logs and ICU mortality prediction, TraXion matches or exceeds prior work on both domains, demonstrating cross-domain applicability.

Conclusion: Event streams from diverse domains (mobility, security, healthcare) can be modeled under a single unified framework (TraXion) by properly accounting for their structural properties as MESES, rather than forcing inappropriate analogies from other domains like language modeling. This represents a generalizable approach to pre-training for multi-entity spatiotemporal event streams.

Abstract: Human mobility differs from text and from generic time series in three structural ways: visits are tuple-valued events whose meaning depends on the joint distribution over location, time, and activity; users carry persistent signatures across trajectories; and visits are not independent across users, since co-location at shared places is a primary signal. Existing pre-training recipes for mobility import objectives from language modeling, treating trajectories as sentences and visits as tokens, an analogy that fails against each of the three properties above. These properties define a broader class, multi-entity spatiotemporal event streams (MESES), spanning enterprise authentication logs, electronic health records, and other event-stream domains where entities share infrastructure, schedules, or contexts. We make the properties precise as three axioms that any pre-training framework for MESES should satisfy, and introduce TraXion, whose objectives and architecture are jointly designed to meet them. A single TraXion checkpoint per dataset beats task-specific baselines on every task across six public mobility datasets covering anomaly detection, next-POI recommendation, next-visit prediction, and social-link prediction. The same recipe, applied unchanged to enterprise authentication logs and ICU mortality prediction, matches or exceeds prior work on both, showing that event streams from domains as different as mobility, security, and healthcare can be modeled under a single framework.

</details>


### [137] [Same Signal, Opposite Meaning: Direction-Informed Adaptive Learning for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06908)
*Ziming Li,Jiatan Huang,Xiaoguang Guo,Guilin Wang,Chuxu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive test-time compute methods for LLM agents need dynamic gating because traditional fixed-direction gating (confidence/uncertainty/difficulty-based) fails when the relationship between gating signals and compute benefits reverses across different environments and backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing adaptive compute methods assume a fixed relationship between gating signals (confidence, uncertainty, difficulty) and the value of extra computation. However, this alignment is unstable - the same signal can predict rollout benefit in one setting but rollout harm in another, across different environments and backbones. This makes fixed-direction gates unreliable and can worsen performance by selecting states where extra computation is actually harmful.

Method: The authors propose DIAL (Direction-Informed Adaptive Learning), a sparse gate trained from signal-agnostic counterfactual exploration to learn the utility direction of state features per specific (environment, backbone) combination. This addresses the distinction between compute need (states where rollouts help compare alternatives) and compute suitability (states where context doesn't support useful rollout-based improvement).

Result: Across six environments and three backbones, DIAL yields a stronger overall success-cost trade-off than fixed-direction baselines, demonstrating that learning direction-specific gating signals per environment-backbone combination outperforms traditional static gating approaches.

Conclusion: Fixed-direction gating for adaptive test-time compute in LLM agents is fundamentally limited because the relationship between gating signals and compute value can reverse across different settings. A direction-informed approach like DIAL that learns context-specific utility directions is necessary for reliable and effective adaptive computation.

Abstract: Adaptive test-time compute for LLM agents aims to invoke extra computation only when it improves performance. Existing methods typically use confidence-, uncertainty-, or difficulty-based gates, assuming a fixed direction from the gating signal through compute need to the value of computation. This makes gating a utility-calibration problem: gating signals should align with whether extra computation improves the final outcome over the base policy. We show that this alignment is unstable: the same signal predicts rollout benefit in one setting and rollout harm in another, with reversals across environments and backbones even when the task is fixed. Wrong-direction gates can therefore worsen performance by precisely selecting harmful states. This reversal reflects a deeper distinction between compute need and compute suitability: a high uncertainty signal may indicate decision-difficult states where rollouts help compare alternatives, or intervention-unsuitable states where the current context does not support useful rollout-based improvement. Under this two-source model, fixed-direction gates are unreliable across heterogeneous settings. To address this, we propose DIAL (Direction-Informed Adaptive Learning), a sparse gate trained from signal-agnostic counterfactual exploration to learn the utility direction of state features per (environment, backbone). Across six environments and three backbones, DIAL yields a stronger overall success-cost trade-off than fixed-direction baselines.

</details>


### [138] [Dual-Scale Temporal Fusion Reveals Structured Predictability in Subseasonal-to-Seasonal Temperature Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06911)
*Elnaz Bashir,Jiali Wang,Lin Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dual-scale learning framework separates historical climate context from recent weather evolution, combining them through spatially adaptive fusion to enable stable 30-90 day temperature forecasts, revealing that predictability shifts systematically with season and geography rather than just lead time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Subseasonal-to-seasonal (S2S) temperature forecasts (30-90 days) are critically needed for agriculture, energy planning, and risk management, but their reliability varies substantially across seasons and regions. Current approaches attribute forecast skill primarily to lead time, which doesn't fully explain spatiotemporal predictability patterns.

Method: Developed a dual-scale learning framework that separates calendar-aligned historical climate context from lead-time matched recent weather evolution. These are combined through spatially adaptive fusion to enable stable temperature forecasts across 30-90 day window. Includes topology-aware structural constraints to improve spatial coherence of predicted temperature fields.

Result: The learned fusion weights reveal systematic shifts in balance between temporal scales with season and geography: winter interannual context dominates over high latitudes and complex terrain (where forecast is most difficult), while summer predictions show more balanced temporal contributions. Forecast skill within subseasonal window is primarily determined by this spatially explicit reorganization of predictability rather than simple lead-time decay.

Conclusion: S2S predictability should be reframed as a structured, multi-scale phenomenon rather than just lead-time dependent. The framework provides a more interpretable foundation for improving forecast systems and informing their practical use, with spatial coherence particularly improved over complex terrain through topology-aware constraints.

Abstract: Subseasonal-to-seasonal (S2S) temperature forecasts, spanning several weeks to a few months, are critically needed in agriculture practice, energy planning, and extreme-weather induced risk management, yet their reliability varies substantially across seasons and regions. Forecast skill is often attributed primarily to lead time, but this perspective does not fully explain the spatiotemporal patterns of predictability. Here we show that S2S predictability is organized across interacting temporal components, spatial heterogeneity, and large-scale pattern coherence, and that this structure can be explicitly characterized and exploited. We develop a dual-scale learning framework that separates calendar-aligned historical climate context from lead-time matched recent weather evolution, combining them through spatially adaptive fusion to enable stable temperature forecasts across the 30 to 90-day window. The learned fusion weights reveal that the balance between these two temporal scales shifts systematically with season and geography: during winter, interannual context dominates over high latitudes and complex terrain where forecast is the most difficult, while summer predictions reflect a more balanced temporal contribution across the domain. This spatially explicit reorganization of predictability, rather than simple lead-time decay, emerges as the primary determinant of forecast skill within the subseasonal window. Topology-aware structural constraints further improve spatial coherence of predicted temperature fields, stabilizing large-scale pattern organization particularly over complex terrain. These results reframe S2S predictability as a structured, multi-scale phenomenon, providing a more interpretable foundation for improving forecast systems and informing their use in practice.

</details>


### [139] [LLMs are not (consistently) Bayesian: Quantifying internal (in)consistencies of LLMs' probabilistic beliefs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06915)
*Chacha Chen,Matthew Jörke,Adam Goliński,Masha Fedzechkina,Guillermo Sapiro,Sinead Williamson,Nicholas Foti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs often don't update beliefs in a Bayesian way when processing new evidence, with heuristic updates sometimes outperforming exact Bayesian computation due to misspecified world models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern AI systems operate in complex domains where they need to not only produce correct answers but also properly represent and update uncertain beliefs about the world as new evidence arrives, which is crucial for applications in medicine, science, and law.

Method: Introduces the novel technique of studying LLMs as information processing rules and utilizes the information processing gap to study internal inconsistencies in how LLMs update probabilistic beliefs from evidence. Conducts extensive experiments evaluating multiple approaches LLMs use to incorporate evidence into beliefs.

Result: Some approaches produce (nearly) Bayesian updates, while others seem to use learned heuristics. Surprisingly, non-Bayesian heuristic updates often outperform exact Bayesian computation in downstream task performance, indicating LLMs' probabilistic models of the world are misspecified.

Conclusion: The measure developed can provide diagnostics to identify issues with LLM-powered inferential systems, highlighting the importance of understanding how LLMs process and update information rather than just focusing on final outputs.

Abstract: Modern AI systems are being deployed in complex domains such as medicine, science, and law, where it is important that they not only produce correct answers, but also represent and update uncertain beliefs about the world as new evidence arrives. We introduce the novel technique of studying LLMs as information processing rules and utilize the information processing gap to study the internal (in)consistencies of how LLMs update their probabilistic beliefs from evidence. Our extensive experiments evaluate multiple approaches in which LLMs can incorporate evidence into their beliefs. Some of these approaches produce (nearly) Bayesian updates; others seem to use a learned heuristic. Surprisingly, the non-Bayesian heuristic updates often outperform exact Bayesian computation in terms of downstream task performance -- indicating the LLMs' probabilistic models of the world are misspecified. Lastly, we show how our measure can provide diagnostics to identify issues with LLM-powered inferential systems.

</details>


### [140] [Tyche: One Step Flow for Efficient Probabilistic Weather Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06916)
*Fan Xu,Yuan Gao,Kun Wang,Rui Su,Fenghua Ling,Hao Wu,Wanli Ouyang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tyche is a one-step conditional flow model for efficient probabilistic weather forecasting that maps Gaussian noise directly to future weather states in a single function evaluation, matching state-of-the-art performance while being much more computationally efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current diffusion-based ensemble forecasters have high inference costs that scale with forecast horizon, ensemble size, and denoising steps, making large operational ensembles expensive to run. There's a need for more efficient probabilistic weather forecasting systems that maintain accuracy and calibration.

Method: Tyche uses a one-step conditional flow model with destination-aware average-velocity flow that transports Gaussian noise directly to future weather states (1-NFE). It employs JVP-regularized rectification objective for temporal self-consistency without explicit Jacobians, parameterized by an isotropic Swin-style transformer. Includes rollout-based finetuning with curriculum CRPS calibration supervision.

Result: Experiments on ERA5 at 1.5° and 6-hour resolution show Tyche matches or exceeds forecast skill and calibration of state-of-the-art multi-step generative baselines and the operational ECMWF IFS ensemble, using merely a single NFE.

Conclusion: Tyche enables efficient probabilistic weather forecasting with single-step inference while maintaining competitive accuracy and calibration, addressing computational bottlenecks of existing approaches.

Abstract: Probabilistic weather forecasting requires not only accurate trajectories, but calibrated distributions over plausible atmospheric futures. Recent data-driven systems have achieved remarkable deterministic skill, and diffusion-based ensemble forecasters have substantially improved sample realism and uncertainty quantification. However, their inference cost scales with forecast horizon, ensemble size, and the number of denoising steps required for each transition, making large operational ensembles expensive. To address this, we present Tyche, a one-step conditional flow model for efficient probabilistic weather forecasting. Tyche models the conditional forecast distribution with a destination-aware average-velocity flow that maps Gaussian noise directly to future weather states in a single function evaluation (1-NFE). To make this one-step transport learnable in high-dimensional geophysical fields, we derive a JVP-regularized rectification objective that enforces temporal self-consistency across source and destination flow timesteps without explicitly forming Jacobians. The transport field is parameterized by an isotropic Swin-style transformer that preserves fine-scale spatial structure while remaining scalable on global grids. To improve ensemble reliability under autoregressive forecasting, we further introduce a rollout-based finetuning stage with curriculum CRPS calibration supervision. Experiments on ERA5 at 1.5$^\circ$ and 6-hour resolution show that our Tyche, using merely a single NFE, matches or exceeds the forecast skill and calibration of state-of-the-art multi-step generative baselines and the operational ECMWF IFS ensemble.

</details>


### [141] [Target-Aware Data Augmentation for SAT Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06931)
*Eshed Gal,Uri Ascher,Eldad Haber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A solver-free data generation framework for SAT problems that creates labeled instances by construction, combined with an LPGNN architecture that incorporates constraint violations for improved learning on NP-hard problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning-based approaches to NP-hard problems like SAT are constrained by the high cost of generating labeled training data through solver-in-the-loop methods, which scale poorly with problem size and limit usable supervision.

Method: 1) A target-aware, solver-free data generation framework that produces correctly labeled SAT/UNSAT instances by construction, aligning them with target benchmark properties. 2) A linear-programming-aware graph neural network (LPGNN) that incorporates constraint-violation residuals into message passing.

Result: The approach yields orders-of-magnitude speedups in data generation and demonstrates that benchmark-aligned synthetic data can effectively augment solver-labeled datasets for GNN-based SAT prediction.

Conclusion: Proposes a data-centric paradigm for learning on NP-hard problems where scalable, task-aligned data generation is as critical as model design, enabling more efficient machine learning approaches to combinatorial problems.

Abstract: Learning-based approaches to NP-hard problems have shown increasing promise, but their progress is fundamentally constrained by the high cost of generating labeled training data. In domains such as Boolean satisfiability (SAT), standard pipelines rely on solver-in-the-loop labeling, which scales poorly with problem size and limits the amount of usable supervision. This bottleneck hinders the broader goal of leveraging machine learning to capture structure in hard combinatorial problems. In this work, we propose a target-aware, solver-free data generation framework for SAT that produces correctly labeled SAT and UNSAT instances by construction, eliminating the need for expensive solver calls. Our method aligns generated instances with the structural properties of a target benchmark, making synthetic data effective for downstream learning. We further develop a linear-programming-aware graph neural network (LPGNN) architecture that incorporates constraint-violation residuals into message passing, enabling the model to exploit underlying optimization structure. Together, these contributions support a data-centric paradigm for learning on NP-hard problems, where scalable, task-aligned data generation is as critical as model design. Our approach yields orders-of-magnitude speedups in data generation, demonstrating that benchmark-aligned synthetic data can effectively augment solver-labeled datasets for GNN-based SAT prediction.

</details>


### [142] [Learned Lyapunov Shielding for Adaptive Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06934)
*Giansalvo Cirrincione,Adriano Fagiolini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel adaptive control framework combining structured Lyapunov functions, residual SAC policies, and physics-informed neural networks with a closed-form safety filter for Euler-Lagrange systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the Slotine-Li adaptive controller for Euler-Lagrange systems by learning key components (Lyapunov functions, control policies, and dynamics models) while maintaining safety guarantees and avoiding computationally expensive online optimization.

Method: Three learned components: 1) structured quadratic Lyapunov function with Cholesky parameterization, 2) residual Soft Actor-Critic policy for bounded torque corrections, 3) physics-informed neural network for unmodeled dynamics. Closed-form safety filter derived from Lyapunov constraint without online QP solver.

Result: Proved several theoretical guarantees: global feasibility of filter under drift-decay condition, exponential stability under exact shielding, convergence to KKT point, PAC generalization bound. Experimental results show 41% and 24% tracking error reduction on 2-DOF manipulator under different friction conditions, with successful scaling to 7-DOF industrial robot.

Conclusion: The framework successfully combines learning-based enhancements with formal safety guarantees for Euler-Lagrange systems, demonstrating practical benefits in tracking performance while maintaining theoretical rigor and scalability to industrial robotic systems.

Abstract: We augment the Slotine--Li adaptive controller for Euler--Lagrange systems with three learned components: a structured-quadratic Lyapunov function \(V_ψ\) whose positive-definiteness follows from a Cholesky parameterization, a residual Soft Actor--Critic policy that adds bounded torque corrections to the analytic baseline, and a physics-informed neural network that estimates unmodeled dynamics. A closed-form safety filter, derived from the single affine constraint \(\dot V_ψ+ αV_ψ\le 0\), projects every policy output onto the safe set without requiring an online QP solver. We prove: global feasibility of the filter under a drift-decay condition on the control-degeneracy set; exponential stability under exact shielding, with a robust extension whose margin depends on the PINN approximation error; almost-sure convergence of the three-timescale policy--certificate--multiplier updates to a KKT point; and a PAC generalization bound for the certificate over compacts. On a 2-DOF manipulator with nonlinear friction and variable payload, the learned certificate accounts for most of the empirical gain: tracking error drops by 41\% on nominal friction and 24\% on aggressive friction at the centroid of the training distribution. A 7-DOF scalability study on a Franka Emika Panda confirms clean convergence of the full pipeline at industrial scale, identifies the conditions under which gains over exact model-based baselines should and should not be expected, and documents a warm-start pathology of the learned certificate that has practical implications for deployment.

</details>


### [143] [A Reproducible Optimisation Protocol for Calibrating Prompt-Based Large Language Model Workflows in Evidence Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06937)
*Teo Susnjak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A reproducible calibration workflow for prompt-based LLMs in structured evidence synthesis tasks, separating scientific rules from mutable prompts, optimizing against metrics, and preserving workflows as inspectable artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for reproducible, optimized calibration of prompt-based LLMs in structured scientific tasks like evidence synthesis, where current approaches lack systematic workflow preservation and optimization against explicit metrics.

Method: Separates scientific task rules from mutable prompt harness, optimizes harness against labeled/reference examples and task metrics, preserves workflow as inspectable artifact with specification, metric, settings, and evaluation traces. Uses smaller student LLM for task execution and larger reflection LLM for prompt optimization during calibration.

Result: Demonstrates workflow implementation using DSPy and GEPA tools, validated on title/abstract screening tasks with benchmark data. Shows compilation, artifact round-tripping, and how optimization budget affects smaller student model performance.

Conclusion: Provides a transferable framework for reproducible LLM calibration in structured evidence synthesis, enabling systematic optimization and preservation of prompt-based workflows while demonstrating practical implementation and validation.

Abstract: This methods article presents a reproducible calibration workflow for prompt-based large language models (LLMs) in structured evidence-synthesis tasks. The method separates the rules that define the scientific task from the mutable prompt harness that frames and applies them. It optimises that harness against labelled or reference examples and an explicit task metric, then preserves the calibrated workflow as an inspectable artefact with its specification, metric, settings, and evaluation traces. The example code instantiates the protocol with DSPy and GEPA tools, but the underlying logic can transfer to other prompt-optimisation frameworks that support structured task definitions, metric-guided search, and artefact reuse. Title and abstract screening is the worked validation case because it provides labelled benchmark data and clear evaluation metrics. The demonstrated workflow uses a smaller student LLM for performing the scientific task execution and a larger reflection LLM to steer the prompt optimisation process during calibration. This work shows compilation, artefact round-tripping, and how optimisation budget affects a smaller student model.

</details>


### [144] [A Generalized Singular Value Theory for Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06938)
*Brian Charles Brown,Robert Bridges,David Grimsman,Mauricio Munoz,Sean Warnick*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Most modern neural architectures can be represented with a generalized SVD decomposition where they're left-invertible before the final linear layer, enabling norm preservation between input space and feature space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to develop a mathematical framework that reveals inherent structural properties of neural networks, particularly their invertibility and distance preservation characteristics, which can enable better understanding and applications in areas like adversarial detection and model bias analysis.

Method: The method builds on abstract Generalized Singular Value Decomposition (GSVD) theory, proves that most neural architectures admit a GSVD representation with left-invertible nonlinear portions, provides a data-driven algorithm for estimating this representation from trained models, and proposes a model architecture that naturally facilitates the decomposition.

Result: The results show that neural networks can be represented in a way that is left-invertible before the final linear layer, with norm preservation properties (perturbations in embedding correspond proportionally to changes in input). A proof-of-concept demonstrates the representation can identify adversarial perturbations.

Conclusion: The paper establishes a theoretical foundation for understanding neural network invertibility and distance calibration, with practical implications for adversarial detection and potential applications in model bias analysis and other areas requiring interpretability and robustness.

Abstract: Building on the abstract Generalized Singular Value Decomposition (GSVD) theory of Brown et al. [2025], we prove that most modern neural architectures admit a generalized SVD representation in which they are left-invertible before a final linear layer, with no change in input-output behavior. Furthermore, the left-invertible nonlinear portion of the input-output behavior can be made to be \emph{norm preserving}, meaning that perturbations in the left-invertible ``embedding'' (the activations prior to the final linear layer in this representation) correspond proportionally to changes in the input, i.e., distance in feature space can be calibrated directly to distance in input space. We provide a data-driven algorithm for estimating this representation from trained models and propose a model architecture that naturally facilitates the decomposition. We then provide a proof-of-concept that the learned representation can be used to identify adversarial perturbations to model inputs, and develop the theory necessary for future applications to areas such as model bias and invertibility.

</details>


### [145] [Bias and Uncertainty in LLM-as-a-Judge Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06939)
*James Fiedler*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation using raw outputs (naive estimator) is biased; existing correction methods depend on judge quality and calibration stability, with shared calibration across models potentially causing severe bias including reversed conclusions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation has become standard but uses biased naive estimators (raw judge outputs). Recent correction methods exist but their reliability depends critically on judge quality and calibration stability, with shared calibration across models introducing potentially severe biases that can lead to wrong conclusions.

Method: Study failure modes through analytical results, simulations over judge quality (J) and cross-model calibration instability (ΔJ), and conduct a real-data MMLU-Pro case study demonstrating sign reversal. Propose J and ΔJ as diagnostics for reliability of corrected estimates.

Result: Found that shared calibration across models can introduce severe bias including cases where comparison estimates point in wrong direction with high apparent confidence. Demonstrated sign reversal in MMLU-Pro case study. Proposed J and ΔJ as reliability diagnostics for corrected estimates.

Conclusion: LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation with naive estimators is systematically biased; correction methods depend on judge quality and calibration stability. Shared calibration can be severely biased. Authors propose J and ΔJ diagnostics for when corrected estimates are unreliable and provide reporting guidance for LaaJ evaluation.

Abstract: LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation has become a standard tool for assessing base model performance. However, characterizing performance via the naive estimator, i.e., raw judge outputs, is systematically biased. Recent work has proposed estimators to correct this bias, but their reliability depends critically on judge quality and, for model comparisons, on calibration stability. Sharing calibration across compared models is practically attractive but can introduce severe bias, including cases where the comparison estimate points in the wrong direction with high apparent confidence. We study these failure modes through analytical results, simulations over judge quality ($J$) and cross-model calibration instability ($ΔJ$), and a real-data MMLU-Pro case study with sign reversal. We propose $J$ and $ΔJ$ as diagnostics for when corrected estimates, especially shared-calibration comparisons, are likely unreliable, and provide reporting guidance for LaaJ evaluation.

</details>


### [146] [Causal-Aware Foundation-Model for Bilevel Optimization in Discrete Choice Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06941)
*Shivaram Subramanian,Zhengliang Xue,Markus Ettl,Yingdong Lu,Jayant Kalagnanam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: C3PO: A causal-aware foundation model framework for real-time optimal pricing decisions in discrete choice environments that combines imitation learning, multi-task learning, and in-context learning to optimize prices while respecting business constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a need for optimal decision-making in service pricing where providers must select optimal assortments while dealing with heterogeneous users making personalized acceptance/rejection choices based on their preferences. Traditional approaches may not effectively handle the bilevel decision problem or incorporate behavioral economics insights for new products.

Method: Proposes C3PO (Constrained Triple-Head Price Optimization) network that integrates three learning components: imitation learning of prices, multi-task learning of revenue responses, and in-context learning of price elasticity. Uses frontier model prompting to retrieve enhanced elasticity priors from behavioral economics literature for new products. Trained on simulated data from multiple classical discrete choice models with customer segments and counterfactual action/outcome pairs.

Result: Demonstrates strong in-context learning performance across simulated, synthetic, and real-world datasets. The trained model consistently improves pricing KPIs, with gains increasing as customer price sensitivity increases. Successfully deployed in real-world applications including healthcare, tender pricing, airline ancillary pricing, achieving substantial gains across multiple products, markets, and divisions.

Conclusion: C3PO provides an effective framework for real-time optimal pricing decisions by combining causal awareness with foundation model capabilities, behavioral economics insights, and constraint adherence, demonstrating practical value across diverse real-world applications.

Abstract: We introduce a causal aware foundation-model framework for real time optimal decision making in discrete choice environments. We propose a constrained triple-head price optimization (C3PO) network to solve a bilevel decision problem in which a service provider selects an optimal assortment while heterogeneous users make personalized acceptance or rejection choices optimizing their own personalized preferences. C3PO integrates imitation learning of prices, multi-task learning of revenue responses, and in context learning of price elasticity to generate pricing recommendations while adhering to business constraints. During inference, frontier model prompting retrieves an enhanced elasticity prior for new products from behavioral economics literature, improving pricing effectiveness. We demonstrate strong in context learning performance using simulated, synthetic, and real-world datasets. C3PO is trained on simulated data generated from multiple classical discrete choice models in economics. The model is trained on data comprising simulated customer segments and counterfactual action and outcome pairs and evaluated on randomly generated choice environments with no access to the underlying preference structure. The trained model consistently improves the pricing KPIs, with gains increasing as customer price sensitivity increases. We also deploy the tuned foundation model for optimal pricing in real-world applications such as healthcare, tender pricing, airline ancillary pricing, and other domains, achieving substantial gains across multiple products, markets, and divisions.

</details>


### [147] [ProtoSSL: Interpretable Prototype Learning from Unlabeled Time-Series Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06943)
*Steven Song,Sahil Sethi,Brett Beaulieu-Jones,Robert L. Grossman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProtoSSL: A framework for learning interpretable, projection-based prototypes from unlabeled time-series data and adapting them to downstream tasks, improving label efficiency and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current prototype networks in time-series domains require label supervision, tying prototypes to specific tasks and needing large labeled datasets. There's a need for interpretable methods that can learn from unlabeled data and adapt efficiently to downstream tasks with limited labels.

Method: Separates motif discovery from label alignment. First learns a reusable prototype bank using self-supervised objective applied to prototype activations, then aligns prototypes to downstream tasks through efficient assignment procedure. Evaluated on six ECG datasets and audio classification.

Result: ProtoSSL improves label efficiency, outperforming supervised prototype baselines in low-data regimes with as few as 256 labeled examples; with fine-tuning, outperforms supervised baselines at full dataset scale. Human evaluation shows ProtoSSL produces prototypes and explanations judged more favorably than supervised approaches.

Conclusion: ProtoSSL enables learning generalizable prototypes from unlabeled data before downstream label space is known, and subsequent assignment of interpretable prototypes to new time-series tasks, addressing limitations of supervised prototype methods.

Abstract: In time-series domains where both predictive performance and interpretability are essential, deep neural networks achieve strong results but provide limited insight into how their predictions are made. Projection-based prototype networks address this limitation by grounding predictions in similarity to representative training examples, enabling case-based explanations and global prototype inspection. However, existing approaches rely on label supervision, tying prototypes to a specific task and requiring large labeled datasets. We introduce ProtoSSL, a novel framework for learning interpretable, projection-based prototypes from unlabeled time-series data and adapting them to downstream tasks. Our key idea is to separate motif discovery from label alignment. ProtoSSL first learns a reusable prototype bank using a self-supervised objective applied directly to prototype activations, and then aligns these prototypes to downstream tasks through an efficient assignment procedure. Across six electrocardiography (ECG) datasets, ProtoSSL improves label efficiency, outperforming supervised prototype baselines in low-data regimes with as few as 256 labeled examples; with fine-tuning, ProtoSSL outperforms supervised prototype baselines at full dataset scale. In a human evaluation study, ProtoSSL produces prototypes and prototype-based explanations that are judged more favorably than those learned with direct label supervision. We further show that the framework extends to audio classification. Thus, ProtoSSL enables both learning generalizable prototypes from unlabeled data before the downstream label space is known, and subsequent assignment of interpretable, projection-grounded prototypes to new time-series tasks.

</details>


### [148] [Adaptive Memory Decay for Log-Linear Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06946)
*Yaxita Amin,Helen Zichen Li,Mengfan Zhang,Samet Ayhan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Input-dependent decay for log-linear attention improves memory efficiency by learning per-token, per-level decay parameters instead of using fixed uniform weights, maintaining log-linear complexity while enhancing long-range memory performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current sequence models face a tradeoff between memory capacity and computational efficiency. Log-linear attention uses fixed decay parameters (λ) that are independent of input content, assigning uniform weights across hierarchy levels, which introduces unnecessary rigidity and limits adaptability to different input patterns.

Method: Proposes learning the decay parameter λ directly from input via a lightweight two-layer MLP, producing per-token, per-level decay that adapts to content rather than position. Uses softplus activation to let each Fenwick tree level scale independently, avoiding inter-level competition that softmax introduces. Preserves log-linear complexity exactly with negligible parameter overhead.

Result: Input-dependent decay consistently outperforms the baseline log-linear attention on associative recall, selective copying, and language modeling tasks. Shows largest gains in long-range memory settings where baseline λ degrades or collapses entirely.

Conclusion: Adaptive, input-dependent decay parameters overcome the rigidity of fixed uniform weights in log-linear attention, providing better memory efficiency while maintaining computational benefits, especially for long-range memory tasks where the baseline approach fails.

Abstract: Sequence models face a fundamental tradeoff between memory capacity and computational efficiency. Transformers achieve expressive context modeling at quadratic cost, while linear attention and state-space models run in linear time by compressing context into a fixed-size hidden state, inherently limiting recall. Log-linear attention navigates this tradeoff by organizing memory across a Fenwick tree hierarchy, growing its hidden state logarithmically with sequence length at log-linear compute cost. However, its memory decay parameter λ is fixed and independent of the input, assigning uniform weights across all hierarchy levels regardless of the content, which introduces unnecessary rigidity. We propose learning λ directly from the input via a lightweight two-layer MLP, producing per-token, per-level decay that adapts to content rather than position. A softplus activation lets each Fenwick tree level scale independently, avoiding the inter-level competition that softmax introduces. This modification preserves log-linear complexity exactly and adds negligible parameter overhead. We evaluate on associative recall, selective copying, and language modeling, finding that input-dependent decay consistently outperforms the baseline, with the largest gains in long-range memory settings where baseline λ degrades or collapses entirely.

</details>


### [149] [Rollback-Free Stable Brick Structures Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06947)
*Chenhui Xu,Ziyue Bai,Fuxun Yu,Heng Huang,Jinjun Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A reinforcement learning approach that enables rollback-free generation of physically stable brick structures by shifting physical validity enforcement from inference-time correction to training-time policy optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While autoregressive models have advanced 3D generation, creating physically stable brick structures remains challenging due to strict gravity and interconnectivity requirements. Existing approaches rely on external physical simulators during inference for rejection sampling and brick-by-brick rollbacks, which severely bottlenecks efficiency.

Method: Proposes a reinforcement learning paradigm that shifts physical validity enforcement from test-time correction to training-time policy optimization. The model utilizes assembly-level rewards that optimize for collision avoidance, global connectivity, structural interlocking, and shape conformity, allowing the model to internalize physical priors.

Result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art generation quality while accelerating inference speed by orders of magnitude compared to existing methods. It enables the first rollback-free generation of stable brick structures without external physical simulators during inference.

Conclusion: This reinforcement learning paradigm successfully internalizes physical priors, allowing efficient generation of physically stable brick structures without test-time correction, representing a significant advancement in 3D brick structure generation technology.

Abstract: While autoregressive models have advanced 3D generation, creating physically stable brick structures remains a challenge due to the strict requirements of gravity and interconnectivity. Existing approaches rely on external physical simulators during inference to perform rejection sampling and brick-by-brick rollbacks, which severely bottlenecks efficiency. To address this, we propose a reinforcement learning paradigm that shifts physical validity enforcement from test-time correction to training-time policy optimization. By utilizing assembly-level rewards, the model optimizes for collision avoidance, global connectivity, structural interlocking, and shape conformity. This paradigm allows the model to internalize physical priors, enabling the first rollback-free generation of stable brick structures. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art generation quality while accelerating inference speed by orders of magnitude. Our code and dataset are available at https://github.com/miniHuiHui/STABLE. Our models are available at https://huggingface.co/miniHui/STABLE.

</details>


### [150] [Kurtosis-Guided Denoising Score Matching for Tabular Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06955)
*Victor Livernoche,Jie Zan,Reihaneh Rabbany*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Kurtosis-based noise scaling (K-DSM) improves anomaly detection by adaptively setting per-feature noise levels based on distribution shape, achieving SOTA performance without complex multi-scale training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Denoising score matching (DSM) can detect anomalies via score magnitude, but choosing the right perturbation scale is challenging: too little noise yields unstable estimates in sparse regions, too much erases local structure. Hyperparameter tuning is difficult when anomalies are unknown and no validation set exists.

Method: Proposes K-DSM: kurtosis-based noise scaling that sets per-feature noise levels from the shape of each marginal distribution. Uses lightweight EMA-teacher filtering to remove low-density training points before each gradient step for contaminated data settings.

Result: K-DSM achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard tabular anomaly detection benchmarks in semi-supervised setting. With EMA-teacher filtering, it also performs strongly in fully unsupervised (contaminated) settings.

Conclusion: Carefully trained single-scale models can be strong anomaly detectors without needing multi-scale training. Simple, data-adaptive noise scaling enables robust anomaly detection while reducing hyperparameter tuning reliance.

Abstract: Denoising score matching (DSM) provides a way to learn data distributions by training a neural network to recover the score function, defined as the gradient of the log density, from noise-corrupted samples. Once trained, the score magnitude at a test point reflects how consistent that point is with the learned distribution, making it a natural anomaly signal. The key practical challenge is selecting the perturbation scale: too little noise yields unstable score estimates in sparse regions, while too much erases local structure and weakens anomaly sensitivity. This is compounded by the difficulty of hyperparameter tuning when anomalies are unknown and no validation set is available. We introduce kurtosis-based noise scaling (K-DSM), a per-feature scheme that sets noise levels from the shape of each marginal distribution, improving coverage of low-density regions and precision in high-density regions without extra model complexity. Contrary to prior claims that multi-scale or noise-conditioned training is necessary, we find that a carefully trained single-scale model is already a strong anomaly detector. On standard tabular anomaly detection benchmarks, K-DSM achieves state-of-the-art performance in the semi-supervised setting. When combined with a lightweight EMA-teacher filtering rule that removes low-density training points before each gradient step, it also achieves strong performance in the fully unsupervised (contaminated) setting, suggesting that simple, data-adaptive noise scaling enables robust anomaly detection while reducing reliance on hyperparameter tuning.

</details>


### [151] [$f$-Divergence Regularized RLHF: Two Tales of Sampling and Unified Analyses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06977)
*Di Wu,Chengshuai Shi,Jing Yang,Cong Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Theoretical framework for online RLHF with general f-divergence regularization, introducing two algorithms with provable efficiency and performance bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLHF approaches mostly use reverse KL regularization, with recent empirical exploration of alternative divergences, but lacking unified theoretical understanding of general f-divergence regularization.

Method: Develop comprehensive theoretical framework for online RLHF with general f-divergence regularization. Propose two algorithms: one extends classical optimism principle with exploration bonus, another exploits sensitivity of optimal policy to reward perturbations under f-divergence regularization.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows O(log T) regret and O(1/T) sub-optimality gap are achievable, establishing provable efficiency of both algorithms and providing first performance bounds for online RLHF under general f-divergence regularization.

Conclusion: The work fills the theoretical gap by providing unified understanding of f-divergence regularization in RLHF, with algorithms achieving strong theoretical guarantees, advancing the theoretical foundations of reinforcement learning from human feedback.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) has become a cornerstone technique for post-training large language models. While most existing approaches rely on the reverse KL-regularization, recent empirical studies have begun exploring alternative divergences (e.g., forward KL, chi-squared) as regularizers in RLHF. However, a unified theoretical understanding of general $f$-divergence regularization remains under-explored. To fill this gap, this work develops a comprehensive theoretical framework for online RLHF with a general $f$-divergence regularized objective. Rather than treating each possible divergence function individually, we adopt a holistic perspective across the entire function class and propose two algorithms based on distinct sampling principles. The first extends the classical optimism principle with a carefully designed exploration bonus, while the second introduces a new method that exploits the sensitivity of the optimal policy to reward perturbations under $f$-divergence regularization. Theoretical analysis shows that $O(\log T)$ regret and $O(1/T)$ sub-optimality gap are achievable, establishing provable efficiency of both algorithms and, to the best of our knowledge, the first performance bounds for online RLHF under general $f$-divergence regularization.

</details>


### [152] [PLOT: Progressive Localization via Optimal Transport in Neural Causal Abstraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06979)
*Jonathn Chang,Arya Datla,Ziv Goldfeld*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PLOT (Progressive Localization via Optimal Transport) is a framework that efficiently localizes causal variables in neural networks for causal abstraction, using optimal transport to find correspondences between abstract variables and neural sites, enabling faster and more accurate causal analysis compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods like Distributed Alignment Search (DAS) require computationally expensive searches over candidate neural sites to find intervention handles for causal abstraction, as the relevant neural sites are unknown beforehand. This computational burden limits scalability of causal interpretability research.

Method: PLOT uses optimal transport to fit couplings between abstract variables and candidate neural sites, creating global soft correspondences that can be calibrated into intervention handles. It operates progressively: starting from coarse sites (tokens, timesteps, layers) and moving to finer supports (coordinate groups, PCA spans), optionally guiding DAS based on localized signals.

Result: PLOT handles are extremely fast and competitive in accuracy. PLOT-guided DAS achieves DAS-level accuracy at a fraction of the runtime. In experiments of increasing complexity, transport-only PLOT handles work well, and when combined with DAS guidance, provide efficient localization for causal abstraction research at scale.

Conclusion: PLOT provides an efficient transport-based framework for localizing causal variables in neural networks, significantly reducing the computational cost of causal abstraction while maintaining or improving accuracy, making large-scale mechanistic interpretability more feasible.

Abstract: Causal abstraction offers a principled framework for mechanistic interpretability, aligning a high-level causal model with the low-level computation realized by a neural network through counterfactual intervention analysis. Existing methods such as distributed alignment search (DAS) learn expressive subspace interventions, but the relevant neural site is unknown a priori, so finding a handle requires a computationally burdensome search over candidate sites. We introduce PLOT (Progressive Localization via Optimal Transport), a transport-based framework that localizes causal variables from the output effect geometry of abstract and neural interventions. PLOT fits an optimal transport coupling between abstract variables and candidate neural sites, yielding a global soft correspondence that can be calibrated into intervention handles. In simple settings, a single coupling over individual neurons suffices. In larger models, PLOT is applied progressively, moving from coarse sites such as tokens, timesteps, or layers to finer supports such as coordinate groups or PCA spans, and optionally guiding DAS based on the localized signal. Across experiments of increasing complexity, transport-only PLOT handles are exceedingly fast and competitive on accuracy, while PLOT-guided DAS reaches DAS-level accuracy at a fraction of full DAS runtime, providing an efficient localization engine for causal abstraction research at scale.

</details>


### [153] [FastOmniTMAE: Parallel Clause Learning for Scalable and Hardware-Efficient Tsetlin Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06982)
*Ahmed K. Kadhim,Lei Jiao,Rishad Shafik,Ole-Christoffer Granmo,Mayur Kishor Shende*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FastOmniTMAE accelerates Omni TM-AE training 5x via parallel two-stage evaluation/update process while maintaining embedding quality, with FPGA implementation enabling efficient hardware deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing embedding models face trade-offs: deep architectures (BERT) are powerful but complex, while simpler models (Word2Vec) lack interpretability. TM offers logic-based learning, but Omni TM-AE training is too slow for practical applications.

Method: Propose FastOmniTMAE that replaces sequential training dependencies with parallel two-stage process (evaluation and update). Also implement it as reusable accelerator on SoC-FPGA platforms to address inefficient TM training on conventional GPUs.

Result: Achieves 5x faster training in classification while maintaining comparable embedding quality under Spearman/Kendall similarity measures. FPGA implementation achieves similarity scores of 0.669 (resource-constrained FPGA) and 0.696 (UltraScale+ SoC) with small hardware footprint.

Conclusion: FastOmniTMAE enables efficient logic-based embedding training with significant speed improvements and demonstrates viability of FPGA acceleration for Tsetlin Machine-based approaches.

Abstract: Embedding models in natural language processing (NLP) increasingly rely on deep architectures such as BERT, while simpler models such as Word2Vec provide efficient representations but limited interpretability. The Tsetlin Machine (TM) offers an alternative logic-based learning paradigm. Omni TM Autoencoder (Omni TM-AE) applies this paradigm to static embedding by exploiting automaton state distributions within a single clause layer, but its training process remains slow. In this work, we propose FastOmniTMAE, a reformulation of Omni TM-AE that replaces sequential training dependencies with a two-stage parallel process: evaluation and update. Using a Single-Run Multi-Environment Benchmark covering classification, similarity, and clustering, FastOmniTMAE achieves up to 5$\times$ faster training in classification while maintaining comparable embedding quality under both Spearman and Kendall similarity measures. To address the limited efficiency of TM training on conventional GPUs, we further implement FastOmniTMAE as a reusable accelerator on SoC-FPGA platforms. The Multi-Hardware Benchmark shows that FastOmniTMAE achieves similarity scores of 0.669 on a resource-constrained FPGA and 0.696 on an UltraScale+ SoC, demonstrating efficient logic-based embedding training with a small hardware footprint.

</details>


### [154] [Response Time Enhances Alignment with Heterogeneous Preferences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06987)
*Federico Echenique,Alireza Fallah,Baihe Huang,Michael I. Jordan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Using response time data alongside binary choices can fix preference distortion in LLM alignment when labelers are anonymous/highly heterogeneous, enabling consistent estimation of true population-average preferences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard LLM alignment assumes all labelers share the same preferences, ignoring real-world heterogeneity. Anonymous labeling and reliance on binary choice data alone distort learned policies and make true population-average preferences unidentifiable.

Method: Augment preference datasets with user response times as a secondary signal. Model each decision using a Drift-Diffusion Model (DDM) to introduce a novel, consistent estimator that corrects distortions from choice-only labels.

Result: The estimator asymptotically converges to true average preference even when each anonymous labeler contributes only one choice. Empirically outperforms standard baselines across synthetic and real-world datasets, eliminating bias floor.

Conclusion: Response time data (essentially free to collect, requiring no user tracking) enables accurate estimation of population-average preferences without needing user identifiers or repeated elicitations, promising significant improvements for future data-collection pipelines.

Abstract: Aligning large language models (LLMs) to human preferences typically relies on aggregating pooled feedback into a single reward model. However, this standard approach assumes that all labelers share the same underlying preferences, ignoring the fact that real-world labelers are highly heterogeneous and usually anonymous. Consequently, relying solely on binary choice data fundamentally distorts the learned policy, making the true population-average preference unidentifiable. To overcome this critical limitation, we demonstrate that augmenting preference datasets with a simple, secondary signal -- the user's response time -- can restore the identifiability of the population's average preference. By modeling each decision as a Drift-Diffusion Model (DDM), we introduce a novel, consistent estimator of heterogeneous preferences that successfully corrects the distortions of standard choice-only labels. We prove that our estimator asymptotically converges to the true average preference even in extreme cases where each anonymous labeler contributes only a single choice. Empirically, across both synthetic and real-world datasets, our method consistently outperforms standard baselines that otherwise fail and plateau at a bias floor. Because response times are essentially free to record and require zero user tracking or identification, our results bring promises and open up new opportunities for future data-collection pipelines to improve the social benefit without requiring user-level identifiers or repeated elicitations.

</details>


### [155] [Why Does Agentic Safety Fail to Generalize Across Tasks?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06992)
*Yonatan Slutzky,Yotam Alexander,Tomer Slor,Yoav Nagel,Nadav Cohen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Safety generalization is inherently harder than task generalization due to more complex relationships between tasks and their safe execution, not just training limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agents in multi-task settings must generalize to unseen tasks, but evidence shows safety often fails to generalize even when task execution does. There's concern that current safety approaches may be fundamentally insufficient.

Method: Combined theoretical analysis of linear-quadratic control with H∞-robustness (proving higher Lipschitz constants for safety mappings) with empirical demonstrations using neural network agents in simulated quadcopter navigation and LLM agents in CRM tasks.

Result: Theoretical proof shows mapping from task specification to optimal controller has higher Lipschitz constant with safety requirements than without. Empirical results in both simulated and LLM environments confirm that safety generalization failures are inherent, not just training artifacts.

Conclusion: Failures of agentic safety to generalize reflect an inherent property of safety complexity, suggesting current safety enhancement efforts are insufficient and fundamentally different approaches are needed.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly deployed in multi-task settings, where the task to perform is specified at test time, and the agent must generalize to unseen tasks. A major concern in such settings is safety: often, an agent must not only execute unseen tasks, but do so while avoiding risks and handling ones that materialize. Empirical evidence suggests that even when the ability to execute generalizes to unseen tasks, the ability to do so safely frequently does not. This paper provides theory and experiments indicating that failures of agentic safety to generalize across tasks are not merely due to limitations of training methods, but reflect an inherent property of safety itself: the relationship between a task and its safe execution is more complex than the relationship between a task and its execution alone. Theoretically, we analyze linear-quadratic control with $H_{\infty}$-robustness, and prove that the mapping from task specification to an optimal controller has higher Lipschitz constant with safety requirements than without, yielding a Lipschitz bound of independent interest. Empirically, we demonstrate our conclusions in simulated quadcopter navigation with a neural network agent and in CRM with an LLM agent.
