Our findings suggest that current efforts to enhance agentic safety may be insufficient, and point to a need for fundamentally different approaches.

</details>


### [156] [Echo: KV-Cache-Free Associative Recall with Spectral Koopman Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06997)
*Anupama Sridhar,Alexander Johansen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Echo introduces Spectral Koopman Attention (SKA) to replace attention layers, enabling associative recall in constant memory without KV caches, achieving 100% retrieval accuracy on long sequences where pure SSMs fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers' KV caches grow linearly with sequence length, creating memory bottlenecks for long reasoning traces. State-space models offer constant memory but suffer from a memory cliff where retrieval accuracy collapses when query-fact gaps exceed the effective horizon of recurrent states.

Method: Echo uses Spectral Koopman Attention (SKA) as a drop-in replacement for attention layers that augments SSM blocks. It fits a spectral linear system to key/value history via kernel ridge regression and retrieves through learned power-iterated filters, maintaining O(r²) streaming state with small projection rank r.

Result: On Multi-Query Associative Recall benchmark, pure Mamba-2 SSM fails (∼3% accuracy), while SKA-augmented models achieve 100% retrieval accuracy across all configurations, including 4,096 token gaps with 32 KV pairs. SKA outperforms both pure SSM and SSM+Attention hybrids on five additional transfer benchmarks while maintaining constant inference memory.

Conclusion: SKA effectively addresses the memory cliff problem in SSMs, enabling reliable associative recall without KV cache memory bottlenecks. The spectral operator (not prefix masking) drives the retrieval improvements, making Echo a promising architecture for long-context reasoning with constant memory requirements.

Abstract: Long chain-of-thought reasoning and agentic tool-calling produce traces spanning tens of thousands of tokens, yet Transformer KV caches grow linearly with sequence length, creating a memory bottleneck on commodity hardware. State-space models offer constant-memory recurrence but suffer a memory cliff: retrieval accuracy collapses once the gap between a stored fact and its query exceeds the effective horizon of the recurrent state. We introduce Echo, a KV-cache-free associative recall architecture built around Spectral Koopman Attention (SKA); a drop-in replacement for attention layers that augments SSM blocks with a closed-form dynamical operator whose sufficient statistics are accumulated in constant memory with no KV cache. Echo fits a spectral linear system to the key and value history via kernel ridge regression and retrieves through a learned power-iterated filter, all from $O(r^{2})$ streaming state where $r$ is a small projection rank. On the Multi-Query Associative Recall benchmark, a pure Mamba-2 SSM fails to exceed chance accuracy (${\sim}3\%$) across all gap lengths and KV-pair counts, while at the 50M parameter scale SKA-augmented models achieve $100\%$ retrieval accuracy on every configuration tested, including distractor gaps of $4{,}096$ tokens with $32$ KV pairs. Across five additional transfer benchmarks including needle-in-a-haystack, tool-trace, and multi-hop retrieval, SKA consistently outperforms both pure SSM and SSM+Attention hybrids while maintaining constant inference memory. Ablations confirm that the spectral operator, not the prefix masking strategy, drives the retrieval gain.

</details>


### [157] [Inductive Power Grid Cascading Failure Analysis with GRU-Gated Graph Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07010)
*Tianxin Zhou,Xiang Li,Haibing Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A transfer learning approach using GRU-gated Graph Attention Networks that can identify vulnerable transmission lines in unseen power grids without retraining, outperforming traditional baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for identifying vulnerable transmission lines in power grids are trained and evaluated on single grids, making knowledge transfer to unseen grids an unsolved problem in cascading failure prevention.

Method: Train a single Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)-gated Graph Attention Network on combined cascading failure data from limited training grids, then apply it directly to any unseen grid without retraining; GRU gates control node information retention/discard at each cascade iteration.

Result: The model successfully transfers zero-shot to multiple new grids across inter-time and inter-domain settings, and consistently identifies more vulnerable lines than established structural and electrical baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed transfer learning approach enables effective identification of vulnerable transmission lines in unseen power grids, addressing the key challenge of knowledge transfer in cascading failure prediction.

Abstract: Identifying vulnerable transmission lines in power grids before a cascading failure occurs is challenging: existing methods can learn inter-line failure correlations from cascade data, but they are trained and evaluated on a single grid, and transferring the learned knowledge to an unseen grid remains an open problem. We address this by training a single Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)-gated Graph Attention Network on combined cascading failure data from limited training grids and applying it directly to any unseen grid without retraining. A GRU gate controls what information each node retains or discards at each cascade iteration. Empirical evaluation shows that the model transfers zero-shot to multiple new grids spanning inter-time and inter-domain settings. Using information extracted from the trained model, we consistently identify more vulnerable lines than established structural and electrical baselines.

</details>


### [158] [Dual-Agent Co-Training for Health Coaching via Implicit Adversarial Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07011)
*Da Long,Lingyi Fu,Diya Michelle Rao,Jasmine Ruales Carrera,Yang Bai,Shandian Zhe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dual-agent framework for AI health coaches that interactively co-trains both the coach agent and client simulator through adversarial optimization, outperforming one-sided training approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivational interviewing health coaching is effective but limited by scarcity of human coaches and high costs. Existing AI methods train only one side (coach OR client), limiting exploration and efficiency.

Method: Proposed dual-agent framework co-trains both health coach agent and client simulator interactively. Coach optimized with DPO using Pareto-dominant responses from multi-dimensional LLM judge; client trained adversarially by reversing preferences, creating implicit adversarial dynamic.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the method effectively improves coaching quality across several important dimensions compared to existing approaches.

Conclusion: Interactive co-training of both coach and client agents through adversarial optimization is an effective approach for developing better AI health coaches, with the framework interpretable as a stochastic game.

Abstract: Motivational-interviewing-based health coaching is an effective approach for improving mental health and promoting healthy behavior change. However, the scarcity of trained human coaches and the high cost of coaching services make such support inaccessible to many people who could benefit from it. This motivates the development of AI health coaches that can provide scalable and affordable support. Existing methods typically optimize only one side of the interaction: they either train a dialogue agent against a fixed client environment or train a client simulator against a fixed assistant. This one-sided setup can limit exploration of the interaction space and may be inefficient at developing the capabilities required by the target agent and pushing its performance boundaries. In this paper, we propose a dual-agent framework that interactively co-trains both the health coach agent and the client simulator. The coach is optimized with DPO using Pareto-dominant response pairs identified by a multi-dimensional LLM judge. In turn, the client is trained adversarially by reversing these preferences, inducing an implicit adversarial training dynamic. We further show that this co-training process admits a natural stochastic-game interpretation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method effectively improves coaching quality across several important dimensions.

</details>


### [159] [FlashMol: High-Quality Molecule Generation in as Few as Four Steps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07020)
*Xinyuan Wei,Zian Li,Shaoheng Yan,Cai Zhou,Muhan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlashMol is an ultra-fast generative model that produces high-quality molecular conformations in just 4 steps using distribution matching distillation and strategic timestep respacing, achieving 250× acceleration over traditional diffusion models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical diffusion-based models for generating 3D molecular conformations require hundreds of steps (like GeoLDM), making large-scale computational drug discovery screening impractical. Recent few-step approaches (12-50 steps) sacrifice sample stability and quality.

Method: Adapts distribution matching distillation (DMD) with reverse KL-divergence minimization to molecular domain. Uses strategic timestep respacing to provide better initialization for effective distillation. Adds Jensen-Shannon divergence regularization to mitigate mode-seeking behavior and improve diversity.

Result: FlashMol matches or surpasses original 1000-step teacher models, achieves up to 250× acceleration in sampling speed while maintaining high molecular quality, and operates in as few as 4 steps. Validated on QM9 and GEOM-DRUG datasets.

Conclusion: FlashMol enables practical large-scale in silico drug screening by combining ultra-fast generation (4 steps) with high-quality molecular conformation generation, solving the speed-stability trade-off in molecular generative models.

Abstract: Generating chemically valid 3D molecular conformations is critical for computational drug discovery. Classical diffusion-based models like GeoLDM perform well but require hundreds of steps, making large-scale in silico screening impractical. Recent efforts on few-step molecular generation have accelerated this process to 12-50 steps, but they often largely sacrifice sample stability. In this work, we present FlashMol, an ultra-fast molecule generative model producing high-quality molecular conformations in as few as 4 steps. To achieve this, we adapt distribution matching distillation (DMD) - a reverse KL-divergence minimization objective - to the molecular domain for effective distillation. Considering the local minimization behavior of DMD, we respace the molecule generation timesteps, providing the generator with much better initialization and enables effective distillation. Additionally, to mitigate the mode-seeking behavior of DMD and improve diversity, we further regularize it with a Jensen-Shannon divergence term, which incorporates the mean-seeking behavior of the forward KL divergence. Extensive experiments on QM9 and GEOM-DRUG datasets demonstrate that FlashMol matches and even surpasses the original 1000-step teacher, achieving up to 250$\times$ acceleration in sampling speed while maintaining high molecular quality.

</details>


### [160] [Self Driving Datasets: From 20 Million Papers to Nuanced Biomedical Knowledge at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07022)
*Haydn Jones,Yimeng Zeng,Alden Rose,Li S. Yifei,Yining Huang,Kaiwen Wu,Jiaming Liang,Maggie Ziyu Huan,Yoseph Barash,Cesar de la Fuente-Nunez,Osbert Bastani,Zachary Ives,Mark Yatskar,Jacob R. Gardner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PubMed can be autonomously converted into large, nuanced structured datasets using LLM-based entity tagging and multi-agent research systems, outperforming curated databases in size, accuracy, and context preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manually curated biomedical databases are expensive, lag behind literature, discard experimental context, and obscure nuances needed for data assessment.

Method: Three components: (1) LLM-based entity tagging pipeline using 9 ontologies to tag 4.5B entities across PubMed; (2) hybrid sparse-dense retrieval for entity-filtered queries; (3) Starling multi-agent system that designs retrieval filters, induces extraction schemas, and produces structured records with supporting passages.

Result: Produced ~6.3M records across six biomedical tasks (blood-brain barrier, bioavailability, toxicity, gene-disease, localization, reactions), with frontier-model rejection rates of 0.6-7.7% vs. 7.3-16.5% in curated databases. Creates largest public datasets for some properties with preserved experimental nuance.

Conclusion: PubMed can be effectively transformed into structured datasets superior to curated alternatives, establishing foundation for AI-driven therapeutic design with preserved experimental context and nuance.

Abstract: Manually curated biomedical repositories -- spanning bioactivity, genomics, and chemistry -- are expensive to maintain, lag behind primary literature, and discard experimental context, obscuring nuances needed to assess data correctness and coverage. We show that PubMed itself can be autonomously and cost-effectively turned into structured datasets that are larger, more nuanced, and more accurate than the curated databases they replace. We present three coupled contributions: (1) an LLM-based entity-tagging pipeline, grounded in nine biomedical ontologies, that tags 4.5B entities across 19 categories in a 22.5M-paper, 2.5T-token PubMed corpus; (2) hybrid sparse-dense retrieval supporting entity-filtered semantic queries over the tagged corpus; and (3) Starling, a multi-agent deep research system that, given only a natural-language task description, designs precision- and recall-targeted retrieval filters, induces an extraction schema, and emits structured records with nuance-rich fields and supporting passages. Across six tasks -- blood-brain barrier permeability, oral bioavailability, acute toxicity (LD50), gene-disease associations, protein subcellular localization, and chemical reactions -- Starling produces ~6.3M records (91K-3M per task); several are, to our knowledge, the largest public datasets for their property. Frontier-model rejection of our extractions is 0.6-7.7% across tasks, far below error rates we measure on widely used curated counterparts (e.g., 16.5% on BBB_Martins, 7.3% on Bioavailability_Ma). Beyond scale and accuracy, the supporting passages carry nuance tabular databases discard -- e.g., oral bioavailability may depend on fed vs. fasted state. Together, the corpus, retrieval, and agent establish a foundation for AI-driven therapeutic design. Code and datasets: https://github.com/starling-labs/starling.

</details>


### [161] [Delulu: A Verified Multi-Lingual Benchmark for Code Hallucination Detection in Fill-in-the-Middle Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07024)
*Mahdi Erfanian,Nelson Daniel Troncoso,Aashna Garg,Amabel Gale,Xiaoyu Liu,Pareesa Ameneh Golnari,Shengyu Fu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Delulu is a verified benchmark for evaluating hallucinations in code generation LLMs during Fill-in-the-Middle tasks, covering 7 languages and 4 hallucination types, showing current models struggle significantly with this problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs for code generation frequently produce plausible but incorrect completions (hallucinations) in FIM tasks that pass superficial review but cause runtime errors. There's a need for a systematic benchmark to evaluate and quantify this problem across models.

Method: Created Delulu benchmark with 1,951 FIM samples across 7 languages and 4 hallucination types. Used adversarial pipeline: frontier LLM generates plausible hallucinations, 4 judge models evaluate them, embedding-based clustering mines harder examples, self-contained Docker containers verify golden completions compile while hallucinated variants produce expected runtime errors, and final human-expert review removes biased/trivial samples.

Result: Evaluated 11 open-weight FIM models from 5 families (0.5B-32B parameters). Strongest model reaches only 84.5% pass@1, no family exceeds 0.77 Edit Similarity, and every family produces hallucination-aligned completions on non-trivial share of samples, showing difficulty is task-intrinsic rather than family-specific.

Conclusion: Current code generation LLMs struggle significantly with hallucinations in FIM tasks, and Delulu provides a rigorous benchmark to measure and address this problem. The benchmark reveals fundamental challenges that affect all model families, indicating room for substantial improvement in hallucination-resistant code generation.

Abstract: Large Language Models for code generation frequently produce hallucinations in Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM) tasks -- plausible but incorrect completions such as invented API methods, invalid parameters, undefined variables, or non-existent imports. These failures pass superficial review yet introduce runtime errors. We introduce Delulu, a verified multi-lingual benchmark of 1,951 FIM samples across 7 languages and 4 hallucination types. Samples are curated through an adversarial pipeline: a frontier LLM generates plausible hallucinations, four diverse judge models evaluate them, embedding-based clustering mines progressively harder examples, self-contained Docker containers verify that golden completions compile while hallucinated variants produce the expected runtime error, and a final human-expert review removes any remaining biased or trivially decidable samples. We evaluate 11 open-weight FIM models from five families spanning 0.5B-32B parameters: a six-point Qwen2.5-Coder scaling slate, plus a cross-family slate (CodeLlama, DeepSeek-Coder-V2, StarCoder2). The strongest model reaches only 84.5% pass@1, no family exceeds 0.77 Edit Similarity, and every family produces hallucination-aligned completions on a non-trivial share of samples, confirming that the difficulty exposed by Delulu is task-intrinsic rather than family-specific. We release the benchmark, containers, and evaluation framework at https://github.com/microsoft/delulu.

</details>


### [162] [A Systematic Investigation of The RL-Jailbreaker in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07032)
*Montaser Mohammedalamen,Kevin Roice,Reginald McLean,Alyssa Lefaivre Škopac*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RL-based jailbreaking successfully compromises all targeted models and safeguards; environment formalization (dense rewards and extended episodes) is the primary driver of success, providing tools for both improving attacks and hardening defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As generative models evolve from next-token predictors to autonomous systems, adversarial jailbreaking remains a major safety threat. While RL frames jailbreaking as multi-step attacks, there's incomplete mechanistic understanding of why this framework succeeds, creating a gap in systematic analysis of RL jailbreaking.

Method: Presents the first systematic decomposition of RL jailbreaking by deconstructing the framework into problem formalization (reward function, action space, episode length) and algorithmic measures (RL algorithm, training data, reward-shaping) to identify structural determinants of adversarial success.

Result: RL-jailbreaker successfully compromised all targeted models and safeguards. Analysis reveals that environment formalization, specifically dense rewards and extended episode lengths, is the primary driver of jailbreaking success.

Conclusion: This work provides a tool for improving RL-jailbreaker efficiency and ultimately hardening generative models resistant to RL-based attacks. The systematic decomposition offers insights into both offensive capabilities and defensive strategies against adversarial manipulation.

Abstract: The evolution of generative models from next-token predictors to autonomous engines of complex systems necessitates rigorous safety hardening. Adversarial jailbreaking, the strategic manipulation of models to elicit harmful output, remains a primary threat to safe deployment. While Reinforcement Learning (RL) frames jailbreaking as a multi-step attack through sequential optimization, a mechanistic understanding of why the framework succeeds remains incomplete. To fill this gap, we present the first systematic decomposition of RL jailbreaking. We deconstruct the framework into problem formalization (reward function, action space, episode length), and algorithmic measures (RL algorithm, training data, reward-shaping) to identify the structural determinants of adversarial success. Our results reveal that the RL-jailbreaker successfully compromised all targeted models and safeguards. Through this first-of-its-kind analysis, we demonstrate that environment formalization, specifically dense rewards and extended episode lengths, is the primary driver of jailbreaking success. This work provides a tool for improving RL-jailbreaker efficiency and, ultimately, harden generative models resistant to RL-based attacks.

</details>


### [163] [PACEvolve++: Improving Test-time Learning for Evolutionary Search Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07039)
*Minghao Yan,Bo Peng,Benjamin Coleman,Ziqi Chen,Zhouhang Xie,Shuo Chen,Zhankui He,Noveen Sachdeva,Weili Wang,Ed H. Chi,Shivaram Venkataraman,Wang-Cheng Kang,Derek Zhiyuan Cheng,Beidou Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PACEvolve++ is a reinforcement learning framework for evolutionary search that adapts search policies at test time by using a trainable advisor model to generate hypotheses and a frontier model to execute them, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-driven evolutionary search systems use fixed, prompt-elicited policies that can't adapt during search, which is problematic for expensive real-world tasks where learning task-specific search dynamics is crucial for progress.

Method: PACEvolve++ decouples strategy from implementation: a trainable advisor model generates, assesses, and selects hypotheses, while a stronger frontier model translates them into executable candidates. It uses phase-adaptive training with group-relative feedback early and best-of-k frontier contribution later to handle non-stationary feedback.

Result: Across three domains (expert-parallel load balancing, sequential recommendation, and protein fitness extrapolation), PACEvolve++ outperforms state-of-the-art evolutionary search frameworks with frontier models, achieving faster convergence and stabilizing test-time training.

Conclusion: PACEvolve++ successfully enables test-time policy adaptation in evolutionary search agents through advisor-model reinforcement learning, demonstrating superior performance and adaptability compared to fixed-policy approaches in practical engineering and research tasks.

Abstract: Large language models have become drivers of evolutionary search, but most systems rely on a fixed, prompt-elicited policy to sample next candidates. This limits adaptation in practical engineering and research tasks, where evaluations are expensive, and progress depends on learning task-specific search dynamics. We introduce PACEvolve++, an advisor-model reinforcement learning framework for test-time policy adaptation in evolutionary search agents. PACEvolve++ decouples strategic search decisions from implementation: a trainable advisor generates, assesses, and selects hypotheses, while a stronger frontier model translates selected hypotheses into executable candidates. To train the advisor under non-stationary feedback, we propose a phase-adaptive approach that adapts its optimization strategy to different phases of the evolutionary process. Early in evolution, it uses group-relative feedback to learn broad search preferences; later, as reward gaps compress, it emphasizes best-of-$k$ frontier contribution to support stable refinement. Across expert-parallel load balancing, sequential recommendation, and protein fitness extrapolation, PACEvolve++ outperforms the state-of-the-art evolutionary search framework with frontier models, achieving faster convergence and stabilizing test-time training during evolutionary search.

</details>


### [164] [CommFuse: Hiding Tail Latency via Communication Decomposition and Fusion for Distributed LLM Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24013)
*Rezaul Karim,Austin Wen,Wang Zongzuo,Weiwei Zhang,Yang Liu,Walid Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CommFuse introduces a novel communication-computation overlap technique using decomposed P2P communication to eliminate tail latency in distributed LLM training, achieving better efficiency than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rapid growth of large language models requires partitioning workloads across accelerators, but parallelization strategies suffer from substantial communication overhead that hinders computational efficiency. While communication-computation overlap helps, existing solutions have tail latency problems that limit effectiveness.

Method: Proposes CommFuse which replaces conventional collective operations (reduce-scatter and all-gather) with decomposed peer-to-peer (P2P) communication. It schedules partitioned computations to enable fine-grained overlap, providing an exact algorithm for reducing communication overhead and eliminating tail latency. Compatible with data-parallel training and tensor-level parallelism strategies like TPSP and UP.

Result: Experimental evaluations show the technique consistently achieves lower latency, superior Model FLOPS Utilization (MFU), and high throughput compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: CommFuse effectively mitigates communication bottlenecks in tensor parallelism and data parallelism for distributed LLM training and inference by eliminating tail latency through fine-grained overlap of decomposed P2P communication with computation.

Abstract: The rapid growth in the size of large language models has necessitated the partitioning of computational workloads across accelerators such as GPUs, TPUs, and NPUs. However, these parallelization strategies incur substantial data communication overhead significantly hindering computational efficiency. While communication-computation overlap presents a promising direction, existing data slicing based solutions suffer from tail latency. To overcome this limitation, this research introduces a novel communication-computation overlap technique to eliminate this tail latency in state of the art overlap methods for distributed LLM training. The aim of this technique is to effectively mitigate communication bottleneck of tensor parallelism and data parallelism for distributed training and inference. In particular, we propose a novel method termed CommFuse that replaces conventional collective operations of reduce-scatter and all-gather with decomposed peer-to-peer (P2P) communication and schedules partitioned computations to enable fine-grained overlap. Our method provides an exact algorithm for reducing communication overhead that eliminates tail latency. Moreover, it presents a versatile solution compatible with data-parallel training and various tensor-level parallelism strategies, including TPSP and UP. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that our technique consistently achieves lower latency, superior Model FLOPS Utilization (MFU), and high throughput.

</details>


### [165] [Unlocking High-Fidelity Molecular Generation from Mass Spectra via Dual-Stream Line Graph Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07048)
*Xujun Che,Xiuxia Du,Depeng Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DualLGD is a new molecular generation method that uses dual-stream diffusion with atom-level and bond-level reasoning streams connected via bidirectional cross-attention, achieving 3x state-of-the-art performance on mass spectrometry benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph diffusion methods for de novo molecular generation from tandem mass spectra have a fundamental architectural bottleneck: they process atoms and bonds in a single computation stream, creating circular dependency problems where atom-level reasoning depends on bond information and vice versa.

Method: Dual-stream Line Graph Diffusion (DualLGD) reformulates molecular graph denoising as alternating solution of two coupled subproblems: atom-level reasoning and bond-level reasoning in separate representation spaces. The line graph provides natural bond space representation, and incidence-constrained bidirectional cross-attention synchronizes the streams at every layer.

Result: On NPLIB1 and MassSpecGym benchmarks, DualLGD achieves top-1 accuracy of 34.37% and 23.89% respectively, approximately 3x the previous state of the art. Ablation studies show the architecture itself (not pre-training) is the primary source of improvement.

Conclusion: The dual-stream architecture with explicit atom-bond separation and synchronization via line graphs and constrained cross-attention is more effective than single-stream approaches for molecular generation from mass spectra, addressing the fundamental circular dependency problem in molecular structure inference.

Abstract: De novo molecular generation from tandem mass spectra is a challenging inverse problem whose core difficulty lies in the circular dependency between atom-level and bond-level reasoning: determining a bond's type requires knowing its endpoint atoms' chemical environment, yet an atom's environment is in turn defined by its incident bonds. Existing graph diffusion methods process atoms and bonds within a single computation stream, where atom-bond information synchronization can only occur implicitly across layers. We argue that this single-stream paradigm, rather than the choice of any particular aggregation kernel, is a key architectural bottleneck. We propose DualLGD (Dual-stream Line Graph Diffusion), which reformulates molecular graph denoising as the alternating solution of two coupled subproblems: atom-level reasoning and bond-level reasoning, each operating in its own dedicated representation space. The line graph provides a natural mathematical construction for the bond space, in which bond angles, dihedrals, conjugation chains, and rings correspond to local topological motifs between bonds. Incidence-constrained bidirectional cross-attention synchronizes the two streams at every layer, ensuring that each atom attends only to its incident bonds and vice versa, respecting the fundamental chemical principle that an atom's environment is determined by its bonding context. On the NPLIB1 and MassSpecGym benchmarks, DualLGD achieves top-1 accuracy of 34.37\% and 23.89\%, approximately $3\times$ the previous state of the art. Ablation studies confirm the architecture as the primary source of improvement: DualLGD without any pre-training already surpasses the previous best fully pretrained model.

</details>


### [166] [Towards Differentially Private Reinforcement Learning with General Function Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07049)
*Yi He,Xingyu Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First theoretical guarantees for differentially private online RL with general function approximation, achieving regret scaling of Õ(K^{3/5}) while maintaining privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work on differentially private reinforcement learning was limited to tabular and linear settings. The paper aims to extend privacy guarantees to RL with general function approximation, which is more practical for complex problems where linear models are insufficient.

Method: Combines batched policy update scheme with exponential mechanism for differential privacy. Uses a novel regret analysis framework that works with general function approximation rather than restrictive assumptions.

Result: Achieves Õ(K^{3/5}) regret under differential privacy for model-free RL with general function approximation, matching state-of-the-art bounds for the linear case. Establishes first batch update regret bound using standard complexity measure (coverability). Also uncovers gaps in prior private RL results with linear function approximation.

Conclusion: The work provides the first theoretical foundation for differentially private RL beyond linear settings, demonstrates feasibility of maintaining privacy while achieving good regret bounds with general function approximation, and clarifies the landscape of private RL theory.

Abstract: We present the first theoretical guarantees for differentially private online reinforcement learning (RL) with general function approximation, extending beyond prior work restricted to tabular and linear settings. Our approach combines a batched policy update scheme with the exponential mechanism, together with a novel regret analysis. We show that, even under general function approximation, the regret in the model-free setting under differential privacy matches the state of the art for the linear case, scaling as $\widetilde{O}(K^{3/5})$, where $K$ denotes the number of episodes. As an important by-product, we also establish the first regret bound for online RL with batch update that depends on the standard complexity measure of coverability, complementing existing results based on a newly introduced Eluder-Condition class. In addition, we uncover fundamental gaps in recent results for private RL with linear function approximation, thereby clarifying its landscape.

</details>


### [167] [Integrating Causal DAGs in Deep RL: Activating Minimal Markovian States with Multi-Order Exposure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07057)
*Jiamin Xu,Jacqueline Maasch,Kyra Gan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel causal RL approach (MOSE) that constructs provably minimal Markovian states from longitudinal causal graphs, but shows minimal states alone don't improve neural network performance - requiring controlled redundancy via multi-order historical state exposure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world RL applications often lack well-defined Markovian states from raw observed variables. While causal RL has gained interest, existing work assumes Markovian states are provided and focuses on using causality for faster learning. There's a fundamental gap: how to construct MDP states from longitudinal causal graphs that provably satisfy the Markov property.

Method: Proposes a procedure to construct a provably minimal state representation from longitudinal causal graphs. Observes minimal representation alone fails to improve neural network performance, leading to MOSE (Multi-Order State Exposure) which feeds multi-order historical state constructions into the same Q-function.

Result: MOSE consistently outperforms both minimal state construction and single-window policies on common benchmarks and synthetic datasets. Including minimal representation alongside MOSE further improves performance.

Conclusion: Establishes a core principle for causal deep RL: minimal sufficiency is not enough, and controlled redundancy through multi-order historical state exposure is necessary to unlock the benefit of causal state information.

Abstract: Online reinforcement learning (RL) relies on the Markov property for guaranteed performance, but real-world applications often lack well-defined states given raw observed variables. While causal RL has attracted growing interest, existing work typically assumes Markovian states are provided and focuses on using causality to accelerate learning, leaving a fundamental gap: \emph{given a longitudinal causal graph over observed variables, how does one construct MDP states that provably satisfy the Markov property?} We address this by providing a procedure that constructs a provably minimal state representation. In deep RL, we observe that the minimal representation alone empirically fails to improve performance, indicating that neural networks cannot directly exploit Markovian minimality. To address this, we propose \textbf{MOSE} (Multi-Order State Exposure), which feeds multi-order historical state constructions into the same $Q$-function. MOSE consistently outperforms both the minimal state construction and single-window policies on common benchmarks and synthetic datasets. Including the minimal representation alongside MOSE can further improve performance. Our results establish a core principle for causal deep RL: minimal sufficiency is not enough, and \emph{controlled redundancy} is necessary to unlock the benefit of causal state information.

</details>


### [168] [Dr. Post-Training: A Data Regularization Perspective on LLM Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07063)
*Pingbang Hu,Xueshen Liu,Z. Morley Mao,Jiaqi W. Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dr. Post-Training reframes general training data as a data-induced regularizer to prevent overfitting to scarce target data, outperforming data selection methods with efficient system optimizations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM post-training faces a critical challenge: effectively using scarce, high-fidelity target data alongside abundant but imperfect general training data. Existing data selection methods are limited, and there's need for better approaches to balance these data sources.

Method: The framework treats general training data as a data-induced regularizer. At each training step, it constructs a feasible set of model update directions using general data, then projects the target data update direction onto that set. This creates a family of methods offering flexible bias-variance tradeoffs, with efficient system optimizations for LLM-scale use.

Result: Extensive experiments across SFT, RLHF, and RLVR show consistent outperformance of state-of-the-art data selection baselines. System benchmarks confirm the efficiency of the proposed methods with minimal overhead.

Conclusion: The Dr. Post-Training framework provides a more principled and flexible approach than data selection methods, reconceptualizing general data as regularization rather than a selection pool, enabling better bias-variance tradeoffs for LLM post-training.

Abstract: Data selection methods address a critical challenge in LLM post-training: effectively leveraging scarce, high-fidelity target data alongside abundant but imperfectly aligned general training data. In this work, we move beyond the data-selection framing and introduce Dr. Post-Training (Data-Regularized Post-Training), a novel framework that reconceptualizes general training data as a data-induced regularizer that prevents overfitting to the scarce target objective, rather than serving as a pool for selection. Specifically, our framework proposes that at each training step, construct a feasible set of model update directions using the general training data, and project the model update direction specified by the scarce target data onto that feasible set. Standard training and existing data selection methods arise as special cases with different choices of the data-induced regularizer, and these methods correspond to different points on a bias--variance spectrum with different regularization strength. Building on this view, we propose a family of methods offering a richer design space and more flexible bias--variance tradeoffs. For practical LLM-scale use, we introduce careful system optimizations that realize these methods with minimal overhead. Extensive experiments across SFT, RLHF, and RLVR show that our methods consistently outperform state-of-the-art data selection baselines, and system benchmarks confirm their efficiency.

</details>


### [169] [PolarAdamW: Disentangling Spectral Control and Schur Gauge-Equivariance in Matrix Optimisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07067)
*Haozhou Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PolarAdamW is a new optimizer that separates Muon's spectral control from its gauge-equivariance, outperforming both Muon and AdamW on standard transformers but being outperformed by Muon on SO(3)-equivariant tasks where gauge-equivariance matters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to separate the two distinct effects in Muon's matrix-level update: spectral control via polar mapping and Schur gauge-equivariance. The motivation is to understand which component drives performance improvements in different contexts and to create an optimizer that retains spectral control while breaking gauge-equivariance to work better with AdamW's preconditioning.

Method: The authors develop PolarAdamW, a controlled hybrid optimizer that applies Muon's Newton-Schulz polar map to AdamW's preconditioned direction rather than to raw momentum. This preserves Muon's polar spectral-norm control but breaks the gauge-equivariance, since AdamW's coordinatewise preconditioner is basis-dependent. The method is proved to maintain comparable per-iteration wall-time to Muon.

Result: On DeiT-Tiny transformers trained from scratch, where multiplicity-basis freedom is trivial, PolarAdamW outperforms Muon by +1.93 pp and AdamW by +9.5 pp. Under the 300-epoch DeiT-style recipe, it remains ahead of Muon by +1.37 pp and AdamW by +5.80 pp. However, on SO(3)-equivariant 3D point-cloud regression with non-trivial multiplicity-basis freedom, Muon outperforms PolarAdamW at all capacities, with the gap widening with increased capacity.

Conclusion: The double dissociation experiment separates spectral control from Schur gauge-equivariance. Spectral control composes well with AdamW preconditioning on standard transformers, while gauge-equivariance becomes crucial when multiplicity-basis freedom is structurally non-trivial (e.g., in equivariant tasks). Both matrix-polar optimizers outperform AdamW across all settings.

Abstract: Muon's matrix-level update couples two distinct effects: spectral control via a polar map, and equivariance under orthogonal changes of multiplicity-space basis (Schur gauge-equivariance). We separate them with PolarAdamW, a controlled hybrid that preserves Muon's polar spectral-norm control but breaks the gauge-equivariance, since AdamW's coordinatewise preconditioner is basis-dependent. Algorithmically, PolarAdamW applies Muon's Newton-Schulz polar map to AdamW's preconditioned direction rather than to raw momentum, at per-iteration wall-time comparable to Muon. We prove that Muon's polar step is Schur gauge-equivariant on multiplicity matrices while AdamW's coordinatewise step is not.
  On DeiT-Tiny trained from scratch on four independently sampled 100-class subsets of ImageNet-1k, where multiplicity-basis freedom is trivial, PolarAdamW outperforms Muon by +1.93 pp in test accuracy on average and AdamW by +9.5 pp; under the 300-epoch DeiT-style recipe, it remains ahead of Muon by +1.37 pp and AdamW by +5.80 pp on average. On SO(3)-equivariant 3D point-cloud regression, where multiplicity-basis freedom is non-trivial, the ordering reverses: Muon outperforms PolarAdamW at every audited capacity, and the gap widens with capacity. Both matrix-polar optimisers continue to outperform AdamW. This double dissociation separates spectral control from Schur gauge-equivariance: the first composes well with AdamW preconditioning on standard transformers, while the second becomes consequential when multiplicity-basis freedom is structurally non-trivial.

</details>


### [170] [Less Random, More Private: What is the Optimal Subsampling Scheme for DP-SGD?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07072)
*Andy Dong,Ayfer Özgür*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Balanced Iteration Subsampling (BIS) outperforms Poisson subsampling for DP-SGD by eliminating participation variance, achieving better privacy amplification and reducing required noise by up to 9.6%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Poisson subsampling introduces substantial participation variance in DP-SGD, where samples appear in very different numbers of training iterations. This variance is not just a practical artifact but fundamentally limits privacy amplification.

Method: Proposes Balanced Iteration Subsampling (BIS), a structured scheme where each sample participates in exactly a fixed number of iterations. Develops asymptotic theory showing BIS achieves stronger privacy amplification than Poisson subsampling. Introduces a practical near-exact Monte Carlo accountant for BIS to provide finite-noise guarantees.

Result: BIS consistently outperforms Poisson subsampling across 60+ DP-SGD configurations, especially in low-noise regimes relevant for high-utility training. It reduces required noise multiplier by up to 9.6%. Proves BIS is optimal at both extremes of noise spectrum (σ→0 and σ→∞).

Conclusion: Structured participation (BIS) can be both more practical and more private than unstructured randomness (Poisson subsampling). Overturns the common intuition that more sampling randomness necessarily yields stronger privacy amplification in DP-SGD.

Abstract: Poisson subsampling is the default sampling scheme in differentially private machine learning, largely because its unstructured randomness yields tractable privacy amplification analyses. Yet this same randomness introduces substantial participation variance: each sample appears in very different numbers of training iterations. In this work, we show that this variance is not merely a practical artifact to be tolerated, but a fundamental source of suboptimal privacy amplification. We prove that Balanced Iteration Subsampling (BIS), a structured scheme in which each sample participates in exactly a fixed number of iterations, achieves stronger privacy amplification than Poisson subsampling and is optimal at both extremes of the noise spectrum ($σ\to 0$ and $σ\to \infty$). Our analysis reveals that the privacy-noise tradeoff is governed not by maximizing randomness, but by eliminating participation variance while preserving uniform marginal participation across iterations. To translate this asymptotic theory into finite-noise guarantees, we introduce a practical near-exact Monte Carlo accountant for BIS, which removes the analytical slack of existing RDP and composition-based PLD analyses. Evaluations across more than 60 practical DP-SGD configurations show that BIS consistently outperforms Poisson subsampling in the low-noise regimes most relevant for high-utility private training, reducing the required noise multiplier by up to $9.6\%$. These results overturn the common intuition that more sampling randomness necessarily yields stronger privacy amplification: in DP-SGD, structured participation can be both more practical and more private. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/dong-xin-ao-andy/bis-mc-accountant.

</details>


### [171] [ModelLens: Finding the Best for Your Task from Myriads of Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07075)
*Rui Cai,Weijie Jacky Mo,Xiaofei Wen,Qiyao Ma,Wenhui Zhu,Xiwen Chen,Muhao Chen,Zhe Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ModelLens learns from public leaderboard data to recommend the best pretrained models for new datasets without requiring expensive forward passes, outperforming existing AutoML and model routing approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the growing challenge of selecting appropriate pretrained models from hundreds of thousands available, especially for new datasets and models that haven't been benchmarked together. Existing methods like AutoML, transferability estimation, and model routing are insufficient because they either work with small predefined pools, require expensive computational overhead, or presuppose known candidate models.

Method: ModelLens creates a unified framework that learns from scattered public leaderboard interactions to create a performance-aware latent space over model-dataset-metric tuples. It captures implicit patterns of model capabilities across diverse evaluation settings, allowing it to rank unseen models on unseen datasets without running candidates on the target dataset.

Result: The system was evaluated on a comprehensive benchmark of 1.62 million evaluation records spanning 47K models and 9.6K datasets. ModelLens outperformed baselines that rely only on metadata or require running each candidate on the target dataset. When used as a Top-K recommendation pool, it improved multiple representative routing methods by up to 81% across diverse QA benchmarks, and demonstrated generalization to both text and vision-language tasks in case studies.

Conclusion: ModelLens provides an effective solution for model recommendation in real-world settings where models and datasets continuously emerge without prior benchmarking. By leveraging implicit patterns in public leaderboard data, it enables efficient model selection without expensive computational overhead, significantly advancing practical model deployment capabilities.

Abstract: The open-source model ecosystem now contains hundreds of thousands of pretrained models, yet picking the best model for a new dataset is increasingly infeasible: new models and unbenchmarked datasets emerge continuously, leaving practitioners with no prior records on either side. Existing approaches handle only fragments of this in-the-wild setting: AutoML and transferability estimation select models from small predefined pools or require expensive per-model forward passes on the target dataset, while model routing presupposes a given candidate pool. We introduce ModelLens, a unified framework for model recommendation in the wild. Our key insight is that public leaderboard interactions, though scattered and noisy, collectively trace out an implicit atlas of model capabilities across heterogeneous evaluation settings, a signal rich enough to learn from directly. By learning a performance-aware latent space over model--dataset--metric tuples, ModelLens ranks unseen models on unseen datasets without running candidates on the target dataset. On a new benchmark of 1.62M evaluation records spanning 47K models and 9.6K datasets, ModelLens surpasses baselines that either rely on metadata alone or require running each candidate on the target dataset. Its recommended Top-K pools further improve multiple representative routing methods by up to 81% across diverse QA benchmarks. Case studies on recently released benchmarks further confirm generalization to both text and vision-language tasks.

</details>


### [172] [Test-Time Compositional Generalization in Diffusion Models via Concept Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07078)
*Zekun Wang,Anant Gupta,Tianyi Zhu,Christopher J. MacLellan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pretrained diffusion models can discover and compose query-specific concepts from their time-indexed score geometry without predefined concept libraries, enabling compositional generalization through gradient ascent and product-of-experts integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current compositional generation methods in diffusion models assume relevant concepts or conditioning signals are already available. The paper investigates whether pretrained diffusion models can instead discover query-specific concepts from their learned time-indexed scores and compose them at test time, without predefined concept libraries.

Method: Given a single out-of-distribution query, the method performs gradient ascent on the score function s_θ(x_t,t) at multiple noising timesteps to recover local density modes, maps these modes into clean-space Gaussians, greedily selects relevant prototypes with a submodular likelihood objective, and combines them into a product-of-experts (PoE) teacher model with analytic score. This teacher can be sampled directly via classifier-free guidance or used to generate samples for training new class embeddings and low-rank adapters.

Result: On held-out composition benchmarks built from ColorMNIST and CelebA, both the analytic PoE sampler and the low-rank adapted model outperform query-only and nearest trained-class baselines. The approach shows competitive compositional generation performance without requiring predefined concept libraries.

Conclusion: The time-indexed score geometry of diffusion models contains reusable density-mode concepts that support test-time compositional generation without predefined concept libraries, enabling models to discover and compose relevant concepts from their learned representations for novel configurations.

Abstract: Compositional generalization requires models to produce novel configurations from familiar parts. In diffusion models, prior compositional generation methods typically assume that the relevant concepts or conditioning signals are already available. We instead ask whether a pretrained diffusion model can discover query-specific concepts from the time-indexed scores it learns for the noisy marginals $p_t(x_t)$ and compose them at test time. Given a single out-of-distribution query, our method performs gradient ascent on $s_θ(x_t,t) \approx \nabla_{x_t}\log p_t(x_t)$ at multiple noising timesteps to recover local density modes, maps these modes into clean-space Gaussians, greedily selects relevant prototypes with a submodular likelihood objective, and combines them into a product-of-experts (PoE) teacher model with an analytic score. This teacher model can be sampled directly through classifier-free guidance or used to generate a sample pool for training a new class embedding and low-rank adapter. On held-out composition benchmarks built from ColorMNIST and CelebA, both the analytic PoE sampler and the low-rank adapted model outperform query-only and nearest trained-class baselines. These results suggest that the time-indexed score geometry of the diffusion model contains reusable density-mode concepts that support test-time compositional generation without a predefined concept library.

</details>


### [173] [Actor-Critic with Active Importance Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07094)
*Majid Molaei,Gabor Paczolay,Matteo Papini,Alberto Maria Metelli,Marcello Restelli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AISAC is an Actor-Critic algorithm that optimizes behavior policy using importance sampling to reduce gradient variance while maintaining unbiased estimates, improving learning speed and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the problem of high variance in policy gradient estimation in Actor-Critic methods, which leads to slow learning, poor sample efficiency, and training instability in reinforcement learning.

Method: AISAC extends Actor-Critic by optimizing the behavior policy to minimize gradient variance using importance sampling principles. For continuous action spaces, it employs Gaussian behavior policies optimized through cross-entropy minimization while preserving unbiased gradient estimates.

Result: Experiments on Inverted Pendulum and Half Cheetah tasks show improved learning speed, sample efficiency, and training stability compared to standard Actor-Critic methods. The method improves both target policy updates and critic estimation accuracy across different hyperparameter settings.

Conclusion: AISAC accelerates convergence and stabilizes reinforcement learning training, making it promising for real-world applications. Future work includes integration with advanced algorithms like Soft Actor-Critic and TD3 for more complex environments.

Abstract: This paper introduces the Active-Importance-Sampling Actor-Critic (AISAC) algorithm, an extension of the Actor-Critic framework for reducing variance in policy gradient estimation. AISAC optimizes the behavior policy to minimize gradient variance while preserving unbiased gradient estimates. Using importance sampling principles, the algorithm adapts the behavior policy toward efficient data collection distributions aligned with target policy gradients.
  For continuous action spaces, AISAC employs Gaussian behavior policies optimized through cross-entropy minimization. We provide theoretical analysis demonstrating variance reduction and unbiasedness. Experiments on Inverted Pendulum and Half Cheetah tasks show improved learning speed, sample efficiency, and training stability compared to standard Actor-Critic methods.
  Results indicate that optimizing the behavior policy improves both target policy updates and critic estimation accuracy across different hyperparameter settings. AISAC accelerates convergence and stabilizes reinforcement learning training, making it promising for real-world applications. Future work includes integration with advanced algorithms such as Soft Actor-Critic and TD3 for more complex environments.

</details>


### [174] [Query-efficient model evaluation using cached responses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07096)
*Hayden Helm,Ben Johnson,Carey Priebe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A method called DKPS (Data Kernel Perspective Space) that uses cached model responses to predict benchmark performance with fewer queries, saving evaluation costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating new models on benchmarks is expensive. Existing evaluation frameworks often cache responses from previously-evaluated models, creating an opportunity to leverage this cached information to reduce the number of queries needed for accurate evaluation of new models.

Method: Introduces DKPS (Data Kernel Perspective Space), a method for quantifying relationships between models in black-box settings. Uses cached model responses to predict benchmark performance with fewer queries. Also proposes an offline method for selecting optimal query sets that maximize goodness-of-fit on reference models.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows DKPS methods are query-efficient under certain conditions. Empirical results demonstrate DKPS achieves same mean absolute error as baselines with substantially reduced query budget. The offline query selection method improves prediction accuracy over random selection.

Conclusion: DKPS-based methods provide effective query-efficient approaches for predicting benchmark performance by leveraging cached model responses, offering practical cost savings for model evaluation while maintaining accuracy.

Abstract: Evaluating a new model on an existing benchmark is often necessary to understand its behavior before deployment. For modern evaluation frameworks, generating and evaluating a response for all queries can be prohibitively expensive. In practice, responses from previously-evaluated models are often cached -- creating a potential opportunity to use this additional information to decrease the number of queries required to accurately evaluate a new model. In this paper, we introduce an approach for predicting benchmark performance that leverages cached model responses based on the Data Kernel Perspective Space (DKPS), a method for quantifying the relationship between models in the black-box setting. Theoretically, we show that DKPS-based methods are query-efficient under certain conditions. Empirically, we demonstrate that DKPS-based methods achieve the same mean absolute error as baselines with a substantially decreased query budget. We conclude by proposing an offline method for selecting a set of queries that maximizes the goodness-of-fit on reference models, improving prediction accuracy over random query selection.

</details>


### [175] [CarCrashNet: A Large-Scale Dataset and Hierarchical Neural Solver for Data-Driven Structural Crash Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07098)
*Mohamed Elrefaie,Dule Shu,Matthew Klenk,Faez Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CarCrashNet is a public high-fidelity open-source benchmark dataset for data-driven structural crash simulation, containing over 14,000 component-scale and 825 full-vehicle crash simulations with validation against commercial solvers and experimental data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Crash simulation is essential for vehicle development but challenging due to nonlinear mechanics, large deformations, material plasticity, and complex interactions. There's a need for standardized, high-quality datasets to advance data-driven approaches in crashworthiness modeling.

Method: Developed CarCrashNet benchmark combining component-scale (bumper-beam pole-impact) and full-vehicle simulations in multi-modal format. Validated open-source OpenRadioss workflow against experimental data and Ansys LS-DYNA. Introduced CrashSolver ML model for full-vehicle crash prediction from high-resolution FE data.

Result: Created comprehensive dataset with 14,000+ bumper-beam simulations and 825 full-vehicle crashes from three vehicle models (Dodge Neon, Toyota Yaris, Chevrolet Silverado). Established benchmark reliability through validation against commercial and experimental data. Performed extensive benchmarking of CrashSolver against state-of-the-art geometric deep learning and transformer models.

Conclusion: CarCrashNet serves as a foundation for reproducible research in structural simulation, crashworthiness modeling, and AI-driven virtual crash testing, advancing the field through open-source, validated benchmark data and ML approaches.

Abstract: Crash simulation is a cornerstone of modern vehicle development because it reduces the need for costly physical prototypes, accelerates safety-driven design iteration, and increasingly supports virtual testing workflows. At the same time, modeling structural crash mechanics remains exceptionally challenging: the response is governed by nonlinear contact, large deformation, material plasticity, failure, and complex multi-body interactions evolving over space and time on high-resolution finite-element meshes. In this work, we introduce \textsc{CarCrashNet}, a public high-fidelity open-source benchmark for data-driven structural crash simulation. \textsc{CarCrashNet} combines component-scale and full-vehicle simulations in a multi-modal format, including more than 14{,}000 bumper-beam pole-impact simulations with varying geometry, materials, and boundary conditions, together with 825 full-vehicle crash simulations built from three industry-standard vehicle models of increasing structural complexity: Dodge Neon, Toyota Yaris, and Chevrolet Silverado. To establish the reliability of the benchmark, we validate our open-source finite-element workflow based on OpenRadioss against both experimental crash data and the commercial solver Ansys LS-DYNA. We also introduce \textsc{CrashSolver}, a machine-learning model designed for full-vehicle crash prediction from high-resolution finite-element crash data. We further perform extensive benchmarking across the released datasets and evaluate \textsc{CrashSolver} against state-of-the-art geometric deep learning and transformer-based neural solvers. Our results position \textsc{CarCrashNet} as a foundation for reproducible research in structural simulation, crashworthiness modeling, and AI-driven virtual crash testing. The dataset is available at https://github.com/Mohamedelrefaie/CarCrashNet.

</details>


### [176] [Almost Sure Convergence Rates of Stochastic Approximation and Reinforcement Learning via a Poisson-Moreau Drift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07104)
*Xinyu Liu,Zixuan Xie,Shangtong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Established almost sure convergence rates for stochastic approximation algorithms under Markovian noise, achieving rates close to optimal for both power-law and harmonic learning rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing the fundamental theoretical challenge of establishing almost sure convergence rates for stochastic approximation and reinforcement learning algorithms under Markovian noise, which is crucial for algorithms like Q-learning and linear TD learning.

Method: Developed a novel Lyapunov drift construction that combines Poisson-equation based correction for Markovian noise with Moreau-envelope smoothing for contractive mappings.

Result: For power-law learning rates O(n^{-η}) with η∈(1/2,1), obtained almost sure convergence rate arbitrarily close to o(n^{1-2η}). For harmonic learning rates O(n^{-1}), obtained rate arbitrarily close to o(n^{-1}), approaching the optimal O(n^{-1}loglogn) given by law of iterated logarithm.

Conclusion: The paper makes significant progress on a fundamental theoretical challenge, providing strong convergence rate guarantees for stochastic approximation algorithms under Markovian noise with both power-law and harmonic learning rates, with the harmonic rate result being particularly strong as it approaches optimality.

Abstract: Establishing almost sure convergence rates for stochastic approximation and reinforcement learning under Markovian noise is a fundamental theoretical challenge. We make progress towards this challenge for a class of stochastic approximation algorithms whose expected updates are contractive, a setting that arises in many reinforcement learning algorithms such as $Q$-learning and linear temporal difference learning. Specifically, for a power-law learning rate $O(n^{-η})$ with $η\in (1/2, 1)$, we obtain an almost sure convergence rate arbitrarily close to $o(n^{1 - 2η})$. For a harmonic learning rate $O(n^{-1})$, we obtain an almost sure convergence rate arbitrarily close to $o(n^{-1})$, which we argue is a strong result because it is close to the optimal rate $O(n^{-1}\log\log n)$ given by the law of the iterated logarithm (for a special case of i.i.d. noise). Key to our analysis is a novel Lyapunov drift construction that applies a Poisson-equation based correction for Markovian noise to the well-established Moreau-envelope smoothing for the contractive mapping.

</details>


### [177] [Theoretical Limits of Language Model Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07105)
*Lucas Monteiro Paes,Natalie Mackraz,Barry-John Theobald,Federico Danieli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper establishes information-theoretic limits for language model alignment under KL-divergence constraints, showing that best-of-N sampling nearly achieves theoretical optimum while common RL methods remain suboptimal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While RL and best-of-N alignment are widely used, the fundamental limits of reward improvement under KL budgets remain poorly understood. The paper aims to characterize information-theoretic limits of KL-regularized alignment.

Method: Derived theoretical bounds on maximum expected reward gain for fixed KL-divergence budget, providing closed-form expression governed by Jeffreys divergence. Developed practical estimator from base model samples. Extended analysis to proxy reward setting and reward ensembling.

Result: Best-of-N alignment closely approaches theoretical limit, while PPO and GRPO remain substantially suboptimal. The analysis explains empirical phenomena, shows reward ensembling mitigates reward hacking, and reveals need for algorithmic improvements.

Conclusion: Current alignment methods (especially RL-based) fail to achieve theoretical optimality, suggesting algorithmic improvements are needed for optimal alignment without high inference costs. Best-of-N is near-optimal but computationally expensive.

Abstract: Language model (LM) alignment improves model outputs to reflect human preferences while preserving the capabilities of the base model. The most common alignment approaches are (i) reinforcement learning, which maximizes the expected reward under a KL-divergence constraint, and (ii) best-of-$N$ alignment, which selects the highest-reward output among $N$ independent samples. Despite their widespread use, the fundamental limits of reward improvement under a KL budget remain poorly understood. We characterize the information-theoretic limits of KL-regularized alignment by deriving the maximum achievable expected reward gain for a fixed KL-divergence budget. Our first result provides a closed-form expression for the optimal reward improvement, governed by a Jeffreys divergence term rather than the $\sqrt{\texttt{KL}}$ used in prior analyses. We further reformulate this expression as a covariance under the base model, yielding a practical estimator that predicts achievable alignment gains from base model samples alone. We extend our analysis to the proxy reward setting, showing that the gap between ideal and proxy alignment (reward hacking) grows with the magnitude of reward error and when the KL penalty factor decreases. We then prove that reward ensembling mitigates reward hacking, providing a theoretical justification for this technique used in practice. Empirically, we compute the KL-reward Pareto frontier for two tasks for LMs, safety and summarization, and show that best-of-$N$ closely approaches the theoretical limit, while PPO and GRPO remain substantially suboptimal. Our theoretical results shed light on several empirically observed phenomena in the alignment literature and suggest that algorithmic improvements are needed to achieve optimal alignment without high inference costs.

</details>


### [178] [Solving Max-Cut to Global Optimality via Feasibility-Preserving Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07113)
*Hao Chen,Chendi Qian,Christopher Morris,Andrea Lodi,Can Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A graph neural network trained in self-supervised fashion serves as a fast neural proxy for SDP solvers in exact Max-Cut solving, reducing bounding costs by up to 10.6× compared to state-of-the-art SDP solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exact combinatorial optimization using branch-and-bound relies on expensive SDP relaxations. Solving these repeatedly for Max-Cut problems is computationally prohibitive, creating a need for faster alternatives to traditional SDP solvers.

Method: Propose a Max-Cut-specific graph neural network with O(n² + ne) complexity that predicts both primal- and dual-feasible SDP solutions without requiring solved SDP labels (self-supervised training). The primal solutions yield feasible Max-Cut solutions via Goemans-Williamson algorithm.

Result: The neural architecture reduces bounding costs in exact Max-Cut solving by up to 10.6× compared to using state-of-the-art SDP solver Mosek, while maintaining validity-preserving properties.

Conclusion: Learned, validity-preserving neural surrogates can substantially accelerate exact optimization over structured convex relaxations, demonstrating significant potential for combinatorial optimization problems beyond Max-Cut.

Abstract: Exact solution of hard combinatorial optimization problems often relies on strong convex relaxations, but solving these relaxations repeatedly inside a branch-and-bound algorithm can be prohibitively expensive. Hence, we consider this challenge for Max-Cut, where branch and bound commonly uses semidefinite programming (SDP) relaxations to bound subproblems. We propose a Max-Cut-specific graph neural network that serves as a principled, lightweight neural proxy for these SDP solvers and can be plugged directly into an exact branch-and-bound framework. The proposed architecture has update steps of complexity $\mathcal{O}(n^2 + ne)$, and predicts both primal- and dual-feasible SDP solutions. The primal SDP solutions yield feasible Max-Cut solutions via the Goemans--Williamson algorithm. In addition, it is trained in a self-supervised fashion without requiring solved SDP relaxations as labels. Empirically, we show that our architecture can substantially reduce the cost of bounding in exact Max-Cut solving by up to $10.6 \times$ compared with using the state-of-the-art SDP solver Mosek. Our work highlights the potential of learned, validity-preserving surrogates for accelerating exact optimization over structured convex relaxations.

</details>


### [179] [Where to Spend Rollouts: Hit-Utility Optimal Rollout Allocation for Group-Based RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07114)
*Tao Wang,Shuo Li,Yan Sun,Dongsheng Ding,Edgar Dobriban*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HORA is a rollout allocation method that adaptively distributes compute in group-based RL by maximizing "hit utility" (probability of finding correct solutions), outperforming uniform allocation in mathematical reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current group-based RL methods like GRPO allocate fixed, uniform rollouts per prompt, which is inefficient. They waste compute on prompts where solutions are already found while under-exploring prompts where more samples could reveal correct trajectories.

Method: Introduced "hit utility" - the posterior probability that at least one rollout in an additional allocation will be correct. Proposed HORA (Hit-Utility Optimal Rollout Allocation), a learning-free policy that maximizes total posterior hit utility within allocation batches while keeping downstream reward evaluation unchanged.

Result: Across 4 mathematical reasoning benchmarks and 3 model scales, HORA preserved comparable Pass@1 and improved Pass@K over compute-matched GRPO in 10 of 12 configurations. It's drop-in compatible with other group-based estimators like RLOO, and uniform prior performed competitively against learned alternatives.

Conclusion: HORA provides an efficient, adaptive rollout allocation strategy for RLVR that improves exploration efficiency in group-based policy optimization, particularly benefiting multi-sample evaluation metrics while maintaining single-sample performance.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a central paradigm for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models. Group-based policy optimization methods, such as GRPO, typically allocate a fixed number of rollouts to every prompt. This uniform allocation can be inefficient: it over-allocates compute to prompts whose sampled groups are already saturated while under-exploring prompts for which additional samples may reveal useful correct trajectories. To address this limitation, we introduce hit utility, the posterior probability that at least one rollout in a proposed additional allocation for a prompt will be correct. Building on this notion, we propose Hit-Utility Optimal Rollout Allocation (HORA), a learning-free rollout allocation policy that maximizes total posterior hit utility within each allocation batch. HORA adaptively reallocates rollout budgets while leaving the downstream reward evaluation and group-based advantage estimator unchanged. Across four mathematical reasoning benchmarks and three model scales, HORA preserves comparable Pass@1 and improves Pass@K over compute-matched GRPO in ten of twelve model--benchmark configurations, with one tie and one saturated exception. It is also drop-in compatible with other group-based estimators such as RLOO. Ablation studies indicate that the uniform prior used by HORA is competitive with five prompt-conditioned learned-prior alternatives.

</details>


### [180] [Conformal-Style Quantile Analyses for Stochastic Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07115)
*Chengyu Du,Mengfan Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A conformal-style bandit algorithm (ACP-UCB1) that optimizes for upper-tail performance rather than mean rewards, achieving logarithmic regret for upper-quantile objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical bandit algorithms optimize for mean rewards, but many real-world problems require good upper-tail performance (e.g., safety-critical applications). The upper endpoint of prediction intervals ranks arms differently than their means, creating a mismatch with traditional bandit objectives.

Method: ACP-UCB1 combines adaptive conformal estimation of the upper endpoint F_j^{-1}(1-α/2) with UCB-type optimism bonuses. Key innovations include handling recomputed conformity scores from evolving empirical quantile estimates and adaptive level selection, controlled through reward-quantile concentration, perturbation arguments for score quantiles, and deterministic level localization.

Result: ACP-UCB1 achieves logarithmic upper-quantile regret with per-arm contribution O(log n / Δ_j^{ACP}). The algorithm outperforms UCB1 on upper-tail objectives, as validated through metric-specific regret decompositions and numerical experiments.

Conclusion: The paper successfully bridges conformal prediction with bandit algorithms to address upper-tail optimization problems, providing theoretical guarantees and practical improvements over mean-focused approaches.

Abstract: Stochastic bandit algorithms are usually analyzed under a mean-reward criterion, yet many problems favor arms with strong upper-tail performance, which we study herein. For a fixed miscoverage level \(α\), the natural upper-tail target of arm \(j\) is the upper endpoint \(F_j^{-1}(1-α/2)\) of a central prediction interval. This target can rank arms differently from their means, creating a central mismatch with the classical bandit objective. To this end, we propose ACP-UCB1, a conformal-style policy that combines an adaptive conformal estimate of the upper endpoint with a UCB-type optimism bonus. The technical challenge is that the conformity scores used by ACP-UCB1 are recomputed from evolving empirical quantile estimates and evaluated at an adaptive level. We control this endpoint through reward-quantile concentration, a perturbation argument for recomputed score quantiles, and deterministic localization of the adaptive level. ACP-UCB1 achieves logarithmic upper-quantile regret with per-arm contribution \(O(\nicefrac{\log n}{Δ_j^{\mathrm{ACP}}})\). We also provide metric-specific regret decompositions comparing ACP-UCB1 with UCB1 and use numerical experiments to validate performance and improvement.

</details>


### [181] [Stabilized neural Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman solvers: Error analysis and applications in model-based reinforcement learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07116)
*Minseok Kim,Yeongjong Kim,Namkyeong Cho,Yeoneung Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical analysis of physics-informed neural solvers for continuous-time reinforcement learning with Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equations, providing error bounds and demonstrating performance on high-dimensional benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physics-informed neural solvers are promising for model-based reinforcement learning in continuous time, but practical implementations exist in a hybrid regime between classical grid methods and continuous-PDE PINNs that lacks theoretical understanding.

Method: Develops error theory for hybrid neural solvers where value functions are represented by neural networks, finite-difference HJB policy-evaluation operators use network queries at shifted points, and residuals are minimized by random continuous collocation. Provides population L² stability estimate, finite-sample collocation certificate, and conditional multi-step propagation through greedy policy improvement.

Result: Proved stability bound separating residual error, initial/exterior-collar mismatch, policy mismatch, and model-identification error with explicit gradient amplification factor for learned dynamics. Experiments on LQR (up to 64D), Allen-Cahn control, pendulum, Hopper, and 3D quadrotor show predicted error trends and compare favorably against model-based and model-free RL baselines.
