Conclusion: The hybrid neural solver regime preserves finite-difference policy-evaluation structure while avoiding grid-based unknowns, with proven stability that avoids inverse-viscosity blow-up, making it theoretically sound and practically effective for high-dimensional continuous-time control problems.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural solvers offer a promising route to model-based reinforcement learning in continuous time, where optimal feedback synthesis is governed by Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman (HJB) equations. Practical implementations often occupy a regime that is neither a classical grid method nor a continuous-PDE PINN: the value function is represented by a neural network, finite-difference HJB policy-evaluation operators are evaluated by network queries at shifted points, and residuals are minimized by random continuous collocation. This regime preserves the stabilized finite-difference policy-evaluation structure while avoiding grid-based value unknowns. We develop an error theory for this hybrid regime. Interpreting finite differences as shift operators acting on neural networks, we prove a population $L^2$ stability estimate for one policy-evaluation step with learned dynamics. The bound separates residual error, initial and exterior-collar mismatch, policy mismatch, and model-identification error, with an explicit gradient amplification factor for learned dynamics, while the underlying linear evaluation stability remains free of hidden inverse-viscosity blow-up. We further give a finite-sample collocation certificate and a conditional multi-step propagation result through greedy policy improvement. Experiments on compact-control LQR upto 64 dimensions, Allen--Cahn control, pendulum, Hopper, and 3D quadrotor benchmarks compare against representative model-based and model-free RL baselines, demonstrating the predicted residual, policy-mismatch, and learned-model error trends.

</details>


### [182] [When Symbol Names Should Not Matter: A Logistic Theory of Fresh-Symbol Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07120)
*Wenjie Guan,Jelena Bradic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformers are analyzed for abstract reasoning using template tasks with disjoint train/test vocabularies, showing how token collisions affect classification margins and when prompting improves generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether transformers reason with abstract symbols rather than concrete token names, using template tasks as a clean testbed where models must learn classification rules invariant to symbol renaming.

Method: Analyze regularized kernel logistic classification in the transformer-kernel regime, decomposing learned predictors into ideal template-level classifiers and finite-sample perturbations from token overlaps. Use colored collision graphs to encode token overlaps and prove margin-transfer guarantees.

Result: Vocabulary size controls average collision rates, but collision geometry determines whether ideal classification margins are preserved. The perturbation framework applies to abstraction-augmented inputs, providing a margin-versus-collision criterion for when prompting improves fresh-symbol generalization.

Conclusion: Template-based analysis extends to logistic classification with refined diversity conditions, offering a general framework for understanding when transformers can abstract away from specific token names to achieve symbol-invariant reasoning.

Abstract: Template tasks have emerged as a clean testbed for asking whether transformers reason with abstract symbols rather than concrete token names. We study the fixed-label classification version of this problem, where train and test examples share latent templates but may use disjoint vocabularies. Unlike next-token prediction, the model need not emit unseen symbols; it must learn a decision rule invariant to symbol renaming. We analyze regularized kernel logistic classification in the transformer-kernel regime. Our main result decomposes the learned predictor into an ideal template-level classifier and a finite-sample perturbation caused by accidental token overlaps in the training data. We encode these overlaps by a colored collision graph and prove high-probability margin-transfer guarantees for fresh-symbol classification. This perspective extends template-based analyses to logistic classification and refines scalar diversity conditions: vocabulary size controls the average rate of collisions, but collision geometry controls whether the ideal classification margin is preserved. More broadly, the same perturbation framework applies to abstraction-augmented inputs, yielding a general margin-versus-collision criterion for identifying when prompting strategies improve fresh-symbol generalization. Synthetic template experiments illustrate the predicted roles of regularization, sample size, and transformer-kernel structure.

</details>


### [183] [Convergence and Emergence of In-Context Reinforcement Learning with Chain of Thought](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07123)
*Zixuan Xie,Xinyu Liu,Rohan Chandra,Shangtong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper provides the first theoretical analysis showing how Chain-of-Thought reasoning enhances in-context reinforcement learning by demonstrating equivalence to temporal difference learning and proving convergence properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While empirical studies show that Chain-of-Thought (CoT) generation amplifies in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) capabilities, there is no theoretical understanding of how CoT interacts with ICRL mechanisms.

Method: The analysis is conducted in a policy evaluation setup with linear Transformers. The authors prove that with specific Transformer parameters, CoT generation is equivalent to repeatedly executing temporal difference learning updates, and provide finite sample convergence analysis.

Result: The paper proves geometric reduction of policy evaluation error with CoT length until saturating at a statistical floor determined by context length, and shows the desired Transformer parameters are global minimizers of the pretraining loss.

Conclusion: This work provides the first theoretical foundation for understanding how Chain-of-Thought reasoning emerges and enhances in-context reinforcement learning capabilities in Transformers.

Abstract: In-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) refers to the ability of RL agents to adapt to new tasks at inference time without parameter updates by conditioning on additional context. Recent empirical studies further demonstrate that Chain-of-Thought (CoT) generation can amplify this ICRL capability. This paper is the first to provide a theoretical understanding on how CoT interacts with ICRL. We conduct our analysis in a policy evaluation setup with linear Transformer. We prove that with specific Transformer parameters, the CoT generation process is equivalent to repeatedly executing temporal difference learning updates. Additionally, we provide finite sample convergence analysis showing that the policy evaluation error decreases geometrically with CoT length and eventually saturates at a statistical floor determined by the context length. We also prove that the desired Transformer parameters are a global minimizer of the pretraining loss, providing a theoretical understanding on the empirical emergence of those parameters.

</details>


### [184] [The Position Curse: LLMs Struggle to Locate the Last Few Items in a List](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07127)
*Zhanqi Zhang,Hua-Dong Xiong,Robert C. Wilson,Mikio Aoi,Marcelo G. Mattar,Li Ji-An*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs perform poorly at retrieving items at specific positions in sequences (especially backward positions), which the authors call the "Position Curse," but this capability can be improved through targeted fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from an observation that modern LLMs can handle needle-in-haystack problems with near-perfect accuracy but fail at simple positional retrieval tasks in short sequences. As LLM coding agents increasingly work with large codebases where precise indexing is essential for code understanding and editing, position-based retrieval becomes a critical capability.

Method: The authors: 1) Characterized the "Position Curse" by evaluating two complementary queries (retrieve item given position, and retrieve position given item), 2) Tested positions specified as forward/backward offsets from anchors (list endpoints or other items), 3) Evaluated both open-source and closed-source models, 4) Created PosBench, a position-focused training dataset, 5) Used LoRA fine-tuning to improve position retrieval capabilities, and 6) Tested generalization on PyIndex, a code-understanding benchmark.

Result: Key findings: 1) Backward retrieval substantially lags forward retrieval across all models tested, 2) LoRA fine-tuning on PosBench improves both forward and backward retrieval, 3) The fine-tuned models generalize to the PyIndex code-understanding benchmark, 4) Despite improvements, absolute performance remains far from saturated, indicating significant room for enhancement.

Conclusion: Position-based retrieval is a key capability gap in current LLMs that impacts practical applications like code understanding and editing. While the "Position Curse" is a significant limitation, it can be partially addressed through targeted training. The findings suggest that position-based retrieval should become an important consideration for future pretraining objectives and model design.

Abstract: Modern large language models (LLMs) can find a needle in a haystack (locating a single relevant fact buried among hundreds of thousands of irrelevant tokens) with near-saturated accuracy, yet fail to retrieve the last few items in a short list. We call this failure the Position Curse. For instance, even in a two-line code snippet, Claude Opus 4.6 misidentifies the second-to-last line most of the time. To characterize this failure, we evaluated two complementary queries: given a position in a sequence (of letters or words), retrieve the corresponding item; and given an item, return its position. Each position is specified as a forward or backward offset from an anchor, either an endpoint of the list (its start or end) or another item in the list. Across both open-source and frontier closed-source models, backward retrieval substantially lags forward retrieval. To test whether this capability can be rescued by post-training, we constructed PosBench, a position-focused training dataset. LoRA fine-tuning improves both forward and backward retrieval and generalizes to a held-out code-understanding benchmark (PyIndex), yet absolute performance remains far from saturated. As LLM coding agents increasingly operate over large codebases where precise indexing becomes essential for code understanding and editing, position-based retrieval emerges as a key capability for future pretraining objectives and model design.

</details>


### [185] [Simple KNN-Based Outlier Detection Achieves Robust Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07130)
*Tianle Jiang,Yufa Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Simple K-Nearest-Neighbor (KNN) distance-based outlier removal performs as well as more complex algorithms for robust k-Means clustering, providing constant-factor approximation guarantees while being computationally efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current robust k-Means approaches often require adding extra centers or removing more outliers than necessary. There's limited theoretical understanding of how classic outlier detection heuristics perform for robust clustering, despite the close connection between outlier detection and robust clustering.

Method: The proposed method simply removes points with large K-Nearest-Neighbor distances, without introducing additional centers or discarding extra outliers beyond the specified number z. This connects outlier detection techniques directly to robust clustering.

Result: Theoretically, under reasonable assumptions about optimal cluster sizes, the method achieves constant-factor approximation guarantees comparable to prior work. Empirically, it outperforms or matches more sophisticated algorithms on real-world datasets in terms of both clustering cost and runtime.

Conclusion: Simple KNN-based outlier detection heuristics are surprisingly effective for robust k-Means clustering, bridging techniques from outlier detection and clustering, and offering an efficient, theoretically-grounded approach.

Abstract: Being robust to the presence of outliers is crucial for applying clustering algorithms in practice. In the $\textit{robust $k$-Means}$ problem (i.e., $k$-Means with outliers), the goal is to remove $z$ outliers and minimize the $k$-Means cost on the remaining points. Despite the close connection between robust $k$-Means and outlier detection, both theoretical and empirical understanding of the effectiveness of $\textit{classic outlier detection heuristics}$ for robust $k$-Means remains limited. In this paper, we prove that under a practical assumption on the optimal cluster sizes, simply removing points with large $K$-Nearest-Neighbor distances achieves performance comparable to prior work in terms of approximation guarantees: it yields a constant-factor reduction from robust $k$-Means to standard $k$-Means, without introducing additional centers or discarding extra outliers, as is commonly required by existing approaches. Empirically, experiments on real-world datasets show that our method outperforms or matches several more sophisticated algorithms in terms of clustering cost and runtime. These results demonstrate that simple KNN-based heuristics can be surprisingly effective for robust clustering, highlighting new opportunities to bridge techniques from outlier detection and clustering.

</details>


### [186] [GAD in the Wild: Benchmarking Graph Anomaly Detection under Realistic Deployment Challenges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07133)
*Jingjing Zhou,Shiyu Huang,Qing Qing,Zuquan Yuan,Huafei Huang,Ziqi Xu,Mingliang Hou,Xikun Zhang,Renqiang Luo,Ivan Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive benchmark for Graph Anomaly Detection that systematically evaluates models under three real-world challenges: million-scale graphs, extreme anomaly scarcity, and missing node attributes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GAD benchmarks are limited to small-scale, curated graphs with balanced anomaly ratios, creating a substantial gap between academic evaluation and real-world deployment in applications like financial fraud detection and social platform governance.

Method: Created a multi-dimensional benchmark using five diverse graphs, including two native industrial-scale datasets with over 3.7 million nodes. Generated controlled benchmark variants and evaluated nine representative GAD models across the three deployment challenges.

Result: Evaluation revealed three major limitations: (1) most GNN-based methods fail to scale to million-node graphs due to memory constraints; (2) detection performance drops sharply under realistic anomaly ratios (0.1%), often resulting in zero recall; (3) reconstruction-based models are highly sensitive to attribute imputation strategies.

Conclusion: Strong performance in laboratory settings doesn't guarantee robustness in production environments. The benchmark serves as a diagnostic testbed to promote development of robust, scalable GAD systems for large-scale, imperfect real-world graphs.

Abstract: Graph Anomaly Detection (GAD) is a critical task in graph machine learning with vital applications in financial fraud detection and social platform governance. However, existing GAD benchmarks are often restricted to small-scale, curated graphs with relatively balanced anomaly ratios, leaving a substantial gap between academic evaluation and real-world deployment. To bridge this gap, we present a multi-dimensional benchmark that systematically evaluates GAD models under three deployment-relevant challenges: million-scale graphs, extreme anomaly scarcity, and missing node attributes. We derive a family of controlled benchmark variants from five diverse graphs, including two native industrial-scale datasets with over 3.7 million nodes. Our extensive evaluation of nine representative GAD models reveals three major limitations: (1) most GNN-based methods fail to scale to million-node graphs due to prohibitive memory requirements; (2) detection performance drops sharply under realistic anomaly ratios (e.g., 0.1\%), often resulting in zero recall; and (3) reconstruction-based models are highly sensitive to attribute imputation strategies. Our findings suggest that strong performance in laboratory settings does not guarantee robustness in production environments. We release this benchmark and empirical evaluation as a diagnostic testbed to promote the development of robust and scalable GAD systems for large-scale, imperfect graphs encountered in practice. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Benchmark_GAD-E7A3.

</details>


### [187] [Adaptive Negative Reinforcement for LLM Reasoning:Dynamically Balancing Correction and Diversity in RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07137)
*Yash Ingle,Jaival Chauhan,Ankit Yadav,Sudhakar Mishra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Adaptive Negative Sample Reinforcement (A-NSR) and Confidence-Weighted Negative Reinforcement (CW-NSR) to improve negative sample reinforcement learning for LLMs, introducing time-dependent scheduling and confidence-based penalty weighting for better training dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Negative Sample Reinforcement (NSR) techniques for improving LLM reasoning abilities use fixed penalties throughout training and treat all incorrect responses equally, which limits their effectiveness and training dynamics.

Method: Two extensions to NSR: 1) A-NSR with time-dependent scheduling functions that heavily correct errors early and shift to more subtle updates later, and 2) CW-NSR with confidence-weighted penalties based on normalized sequence likelihood, giving larger penalties for confident wrong paths and smaller penalties for uncertain exploratory errors.

Result: The methods were evaluated on challenging reasoning datasets (MATH, AIME 2025, AMC23) using Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B architecture, with formal analysis showing how these mechanisms govern token-level updates and provide defense against overfitting.

Conclusion: The proposed adaptive and confidence-weighted negative reinforcement approaches address limitations of fixed-penalty NSR, enabling more sophisticated training dynamics that balance error correction with exploration while preventing overfitting.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a highly effective method for improving the reasoning abilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). Recent research shows that Negative Sample Reinforcement (NSR) -- which focuses on penalizing incorrect steps rather than simply rewarding correct ones -- can match or even exceed the performance of more complex frameworks like PPO and GRPO across the entire Pass@k spectrum. However, current NSR techniques usually apply a fixed penalty throughout the training process and treat every incorrect response with the same weight.
  To address these limitations, we propose two extensions to the NSR framework: Adaptive Negative Sample Reinforcement. Rather than using a fixed update rule, A-NSR uses time-dependent scheduling functions. In the initial training phases, the system focuses heavily on correcting errors to stabilize the model. As training continues, it shifts toward more subtle and controlled updates. We also introduce Confidence-Weighted Negative Reinforcement, which operates on the principle that different mistakes carry different levels of importance. CW-NSR assigns specific penalty weights based on the model's normalized sequence likelihood. If the model is highly confident in a wrong path, it receives a larger penalty and for uncertain errors -- where the model is effectively exploring -- are penalized less strictly. Our formal analysis shows how these mechanisms govern token-level updates, allowing the model to leverage prior-guided probability redistribution while providing a natural defense against overfitting. We evaluated these methods on difficult reasoning datasets, including MATH, AIME 2025, and AMC23, using the Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B architecture.

</details>


### [188] [Regret-Oracle Complexity Tradeoffs in Agnostic Online Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07155)
*Idan Attias,Steve Hanneke,Arvind Ramaswami*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a new agnostic online learning algorithm that significantly improves oracle efficiency from doubly-exponential to polynomial complexity by using weak-consistency oracles and dynamic pruning of non-realizable label sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agnostic online learning approaches using oracle-efficient methods suffer from doubly-exponential oracle complexity (O(T^{2^{O(d_LD)}})), making them computationally impractical even with oracle access to ERM. There's a need for more efficient algorithms that use weaker primitives while maintaining near-optimal regret bounds.

Method: The authors propose an adaptive agnostic-to-realizable reduction that dynamically prunes non-realizable label sequences on the fly. Instead of using computationally intensive SOA or ERM oracles, they rely on a weak-consistency oracle that only checks if a dataset is realizable. They use VC dimension (d_VC) to bound the number of actively maintained paths, achieving polynomial complexity.

Result: The algorithm reduces oracle query complexity from doubly-exponential to polynomial O(T^{d_VC+1}) while preserving near-optimal expected regret. It also achieves memory reduction over standard reductions. The paper provides a formal tradeoff analysis between query budgets and regret, with lower bounds showing that any learner restricted to Q = o(√T) queries must suffer Ω(T/Q) regret.

Conclusion: The work significantly advances oracle-efficient online learning by introducing dynamic pruning techniques that achieve polynomial query complexity with weak-consistency oracles, while maintaining strong regret guarantees and formally characterizing the regret-oracle complexity tradeoff.

Abstract: Agnostic online learning is classically solved via a reduction to the realizable setting, utilizing Littlestone's Standard Optimal Algorithm (SOA) as a base learner. However, the SOA is computationally intractable to execute even for a single round. To overcome this barrier, recent work in oracle-efficient online learning replaces the SOA with a realizable base learner that accesses the concept class exclusively through an offline empirical risk minimization (ERM) oracle. While such agnostic learners achieve near-optimal expected regret, they suffer from a doubly-exponential oracle complexity of $O\big(T^{2^{O(d_\mathrm{LD})}}\big)$, where $d_\mathrm{LD}$ is the Littlestone dimension and $T$ is the number of rounds. In this work, we significantly improve this oracle complexity while relying on an even weaker primitive: a weak-consistency oracle, which merely decides whether a given labeled dataset is realizable. At the core of our approach is an adaptive and dynamic agnostic-to-realizable reduction that actively prunes non-realizable label sequences on the fly. By using the VC dimension ($d_\mathrm{VC}$) to bound the number of dynamically maintained active paths, our algorithm reduces the total query complexity down to $O(T^{d_\mathrm{VC}+1})$ while perfectly preserving near-optimal expected regret. Crucially, this dynamic pruning also yields a memory reduction over the standard reduction. Furthermore, we formally quantify the regret--oracle complexity tradeoff, providing upper bounds that smoothly interpolate between restricted query budgets and attainable expected regret. We complement these with lower bounds proving that any learner restricted to $Q = o(\sqrt{T})$ queries must suffer an expected regret of $Ω(T/Q)$.

</details>


### [189] [Learned Lagrangian Models of PDEs via Euler-Lagrange Residual Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07157)
*Lyra Zhornyak,Eric Forgoston,M. Ani Hsieh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A method that uses learned continuous Lagrangians to forecast PDE-governed system dynamics by minimizing the Euler-Lagrange residual via mesh-free optimization on local space-time patches, enabling stable long-range predictions with linear scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a method for stable long-range predictions of PDE-governed systems that can directly use learned continuous Lagrangians while maintaining conservative structure. Existing approaches suffer from coupling between model error and integration error, and global coupling in fixed discretizations that complicates learning and slows computation.

Method: Develops an optimization-based integrator that minimizes the squared Euler-Lagrange residual through a mesh-free near-symplectic construction on local space-time patches. Uses Jacobi iteration for linear scaling with domain size, decouples model error from integration error, and works with any learned network structure without retraining for varying dynamics or boundary conditions.

Result: The method achieves error comparable to classical symplectic methods while generalizing to spatially varying dynamics and arbitrary boundary conditions without retraining. Validated on learned representations of a double pendulum, 1D wave equation, and 2D wave equation with stable long-range predictions.

Conclusion: The optimization-based approach bypasses global coupling issues of traditional methods, enables direct use of learned continuous Lagrangians for PDE forecasting, maintains conservative structure for stability, and scales efficiently while being compatible with existing physics-guided ML methods.

Abstract: We present the first method to directly use a learned continuous Lagrangian to forecast the dynamics of systems governed by partial differential equations, exploiting the inherent conservative structure to achieve stable long-range predictions. We develop an optimization-based integrator that minimizes the squared Euler--Lagrange residual via a mesh-free near-symplectic construction on local space-time patches. Different from integrators for analytical models, integrators for learned models should decouple model error (phase error) from integration error (conservation error). By relying on optimization rather than time-stepping, we bypass the global coupling inherent to fixed discretizations, which slows time- and space-stepping and complicates learning. Our method scales linearly with domain size via Jacobi iteration, and places no structural requirements on the learned network, allowing it to be coupled with existing physics-guided machine learning (ML) methods. We validate our approach on a learned representation of a double pendulum, a one-dimensional wave equation, and a two-dimensional wave equation. Our method achieves error comparable to classical symplectic methods while generalizing to spatially varying dynamics and arbitrary boundary conditions without retraining.

</details>


### [190] [Neurosymbolic Imitation Learning with Human Guidance: A Privileged Information Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07166)
*Nikhilesh Prabhakar,Varun Balaji,Athresh Karanam,Kristian Kersting,Sriraam Natarajan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A neurosymbolic approach for imitation learning that combines neural networks' ability to handle high-dimensional data with symbolic methods' generalization capabilities, leveraging gaze data as privileged information during training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current imitation learning approaches have limitations: pure neural methods require large samples and suffer from overfitting despite handling high-dimensional data well, while pure symbolic methods generalize well but don't handle high-dimensional data effectively. There's a need for an approach that achieves both benefits.

Method: A neurosymbolic approach that integrates neural and symbolic learning techniques. The key innovation is exploiting additional privileged information (specifically gaze data) that is only available during training but not during deployment. This combines the strengths of both paradigms.

Result: Empirical evaluations demonstrate that the proposed approach is effective, efficient, and has strong generalization capability. It successfully handles high-dimensional data while achieving good generalization, overcoming the limitations of pure neural or pure symbolic methods.

Conclusion: The neurosymbolic approach achieves the "best of both worlds" - combining neural networks' ability to process high-dimensional data with symbolic methods' generalization power. The use of privileged information (gaze data) during training provides a key advantage for learning effective policies for complex environments.

Abstract: Imitation learning is widely used for learning to act in complex environments. While pure neural-based methods handle high dimensional data effectively, they suffer from the requirement of large number of samples and are prone to overfitting. Pure symbolic approaches, while generalize well, do not handle high-dimensional data effectively. We propose a neurosymbolic approach that achieves the best of both worlds, i.e, handling high-dimensional data while achieving generalization. The key advantage of our approach is that it can effectively exploit additional privileged information that is available only during training (in our case, gaze data). Our empirical evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness, efficiency and the generalization capability of our proposed approach.

</details>


### [191] [Cost-Ordered Feasibility for Multi-Armed Bandits with Cost Subsidy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07171)
*Ishank Juneja,Carlee Joe-Wong,Osman Yağan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel algorithm called Cost-Ordered Feasibility (COF) is proposed for multi-armed bandits with cost-subsidy, achieving better theoretical regret bounds and improved empirical performance compared to existing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many real-world applications require minimizing cost while maintaining a minimum reward constraint, rather than simply maximizing reward as in classic MAB problems. The paper addresses the challenge where the reward constraint is relative to the unknown best reward and arm costs are known, which has practical relevance in resource-constrained decision-making scenarios.

Method: The authors first derive instance-dependent lower bounds for the MAB-CS problem. They then propose the Cost-Ordered Feasibility (COF) algorithm that intelligently combines samples from all arms to gauge the feasibility of cheaper arms, systematically exploring arm quality relative to cost constraints.

Result: COF achieves improved instance-dependent upper bounds on both cumulative cost and quality regret relative to the cheapest feasible arm. Empirical validation on MovieLens, Goodreads, and synthetic datasets shows COF outperforms existing baselines with better practical performance.

Conclusion: The paper provides both stronger theoretical guarantees and superior empirical results for the MAB-CS problem, demonstrating that COF effectively addresses the cost minimization problem with quality constraints and offers practical value for real-world applications.

Abstract: The classic multi-armed bandit (MAB) problem tackles the challenge of accruing maximum reward while making decisions under uncertainty. However, in applications, often the goal is to minimize cost subject to a constraint on the minimum permissible reward, an objective captured by multi-armed bandits with cost-subsidy (MAB-CS). Of interest to this paper is the setting where the quality (reward) constraint is specified relative to the unknown best reward and the cost of each arm is known. We characterize the expected sub-optimal samples required by any policy by proving instance-dependent lower bounds that offer new insight into the problem and are a strict generalization of prior bounds. Then, we propose an algorithm called Cost-Ordered Feasibility (COF) that leverages our insight and intelligently combine samples from all arms to gauge the feasibility of a cheap arm. Thereafter, we analyze COF to establish instance-dependent upper bounds on its expected cumulative cost and quality regret, i.e., relative to the cheapest feasible arm. Finally, we empirically validate the merits of COF, comparing it to baselines from the literature through extensive simulation experiments on the MovieLens and Goodreads datasets as well as representative synthetic instances. Not only does our paper develop qualitatively better theoretical regret upper bounds, but COF also convincingly demonstrates improved empirical performance.

</details>


### [192] [Learning Multi-Relational Graph Representations for DNA Methylation-Based Biological Age Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07175)
*Qing Qing,Xikun Zhang,Zhongyuan Zhang,Jiarui Liu,Xingtong Yu,Xiaotao Shen,Ziqi Xu,Qixin Zhang,Zhe Wang,Renqiang Luo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RelAge-GNN is a graph neural network framework that uses multi-relational graphs (co-methylation, genomic co-localization, gene associations) to predict biological age from DNA methylation data, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most existing DNA methylation-based aging clocks treat CpG sites as independent features, ignoring the complex biological relationships among them. This limits their ability to capture the full biological complexity of aging.

Method: Constructs three complementary graphs capturing different biological relationships among CpG sites: (1) co-methylation patterns, (2) genomic co-localization, and (3) gene-level associations. Uses independent GNN branches for each graph and a learnable gating mechanism to fuse representations.

Result: Achieves competitive accuracy and stronger correlation with chronological age compared to state-of-the-art methods. Shows improved sensitivity in detecting age acceleration across diverse disease cohorts. Provides interpretable insights through post hoc analysis of relational structure contributions.

Conclusion: RelAge-GNN effectively models biological relationships among CpG sites for improved age prediction, offers better disease characterization potential, and provides biologically meaningful interpretability for aging research.

Abstract: Aging clocks aim to estimate biological age, a measure of physiological state distinct from chronological age, from observable biomarkers, and are widely used for health assessment and disease analysis. DNA methylation is a particularly informative biomarker due to its stability and strong association with aging, and recent learning-based approaches have improved predictive performance. However, most existing methods treat CpG sites as independent features, overlooking the complex and heterogeneous biological relationships among them. We propose RelAge-GNN, a multi-relational graph neural network framework for DNA methylation-based age prediction. Our method constructs three complementary graphs capturing co-methylation patterns, genomic co-localization, and gene-level associations among CpG sites. Each graph is modeled by an independent GNN branch, and a learnable gating mechanism adaptively fuses the resulting representations. Experiments on large-scale datasets show that RelAge-GNN achieves competitive accuracy and stronger correlation with chronological age compared to state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, the model exhibits improved sensitivity in detecting age acceleration across diverse disease cohorts, highlighting its potential utility for disease characterization. Finally, through post hoc interpretability analyses, we quantify the contributions of different relational structures and CpG sites, providing biologically meaningful insights and suggesting potential directions for aging-related research. Our code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/RelAge-GNN-F1E3/.

</details>


### [193] [HyperEyes: Dual-Grained Efficiency-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Parallel Multimodal Search Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07177)
*Guankai Li,Jiabin Chen,Yi Xu,Xichen Zhang,Yuan Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HyperEyes is a parallel multimodal search agent that enables concurrent search across multiple entities using visual grounding and retrieval fusion, trained with efficiency-aware reinforcement learning to reduce tool-call rounds while improving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal search agents process entities sequentially, causing redundant interaction rounds when queries decompose into independent sub-retrievals. This inefficiency stems from issuing one tool call per entity rather than searching concurrently.

Method: Two-stage training: 1) Parallel-Amenable Data Synthesis Pipeline for cold-start supervision with efficiency-oriented trajectories via Progressive Rejection Sampling; 2) Dual-Grained Efficiency-Aware RL with TRACE (trajectory-level reward that tightens reference) and On-Policy Distillation (token-level corrective signals). Also introduces IMEB benchmark for joint evaluation of search capability and efficiency.

Result: HyperEyes-30B surpasses strongest comparable open-source agent by 9.9% in accuracy with 5.3x fewer tool-call rounds on average across six benchmarks. Validated on new IMEB benchmark for both capability and efficiency.

Conclusion: Effective multimodal agents should search wider rather than longer. HyperEyes demonstrates that fusing visual grounding and retrieval into atomic parallel actions, combined with efficiency-first training objectives, enables significant improvements in both accuracy and inference efficiency compared to sequential approaches.

Abstract: Existing multimodal search agents process target entities sequentially, issuing one tool call per entity and accumulating redundant interaction rounds whenever a query decomposes into independent sub-retrievals. We argue that effective multimodal agents should search wider rather than longer: dispatching multiple grounded queries concurrently within a round. To this end, we present HyperEyes, a parallel multimodal search agent that fuses visual grounding and retrieval into a single atomic action, enabling concurrent search across multiple entities while treating inference efficiency as a first-class training objective. HyperEyes is trained in two stages. For cold-start supervision, we develop a Parallel-Amenable Data Synthesis Pipeline covering visual multi-entity and textual multi-constraint queries, curating efficiency-oriented trajectories via Progressive Rejection Sampling. Building on this, our central contribution, a Dual-Grained Efficiency-Aware Reinforcement Learning framework, operates at two levels. At the macro level, we propose TRACE (Tool-use Reference-Adaptive Cost Efficiency), a trajectory-level reward whose reference is monotonically tightened during training to suppress superfluous tool calls without restricting genuine multi-hop search. At the micro level, we adapt On-Policy Distillation to inject dense token-level corrective signals from an external teacher on failed rollouts, mitigating the credit-assignment deficiency of sparse outcome rewards. Since existing benchmarks evaluate accuracy as the sole metric, omitting inference cost, we introduce IMEB, a human-curated benchmark of 300 instances that jointly evaluates search capability and efficiency. Across six benchmarks, HyperEyes-30B surpasses the strongest comparable open-source agent by 9.9% in accuracy with 5.3x fewer tool-call rounds on average.

</details>


### [194] [Star Elastic: Many-in-One Reasoning LLMs with Efficient Budget Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07182)
*Ali Taghibakhshi,Ruisi Cai,Saurav Muralidharan,Sharath Turuvekere Sreenivas,Aditya Vavre,Ameya Sunil Mahabaleshwarkar,Bilal Kartal,Sheldon Liang,Marcin Chochowski,Zijia Chen,Akhiad Bercovich,Ran Zilberstein,Ran El-Yaniv,Yonatan Geifman,Daniel Korzekwa,Yoshi Suhara,Oluwatobi Olabiyi,Ashwath Aithal,Nima Tajbakhsh,Pavlo Molchanov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Star Elastic is a post-training method that creates multiple nested submodels from a parent LLM in a single training run, enabling elastic computation control during inference and significantly reducing training costs compared to training separate models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training families of LLMs separately is prohibitively expensive and inefficient. Static architectures allocate constant resources regardless of token difficulty, limiting efficiency. There's a need for elastic computation control that can adapt to the varying demands of different reasoning phases.

Method: A novel LLM post-training method that adds N nested submodels to a parent reasoning model via a single post-training job. Uses an end-to-end trainable router, curriculum-based knowledge distillation, and supports nesting along SSM, embedding channel, MoE, and FFN axes. Also extends to quantized regimes via Quantization-Aware Distillation (QAD).

Result: Generated 23B and 12B variants from Nemotron Nano v3 (30B/3.6A) with 160B training tokens. Nested models match or outperform independently trained baselines, achieving 360x reduction vs pretraining from scratch and 7x reduction over state-of-the-art compression. Achieves up to 16% higher accuracy and 1.9x lower latency via dynamic per-phase model selection.

Conclusion: Star Elastic enables efficient training of model families with significant compute savings while providing elastic inference capabilities that advance the accuracy-latency Pareto frontier. The method successfully adapts to quantized regimes and offers practical deployment benefits through smaller footprints and flexible computation allocation.

Abstract: Training a family of large language models (LLMs), either from scratch or via iterative compression, is prohibitively expensive and inefficient, requiring separate training runs for each model in the family. In this paper, we introduce Star Elastic, a novel LLM post-training method that adds N nested submodels to a given parent reasoning model using the compute of one run (N-fold savings) via a single post-training job. Beyond reducing training costs, Star Elastic also addresses a fundamental limitation of efficient reasoning: the rigidity of static architectures, which forces the allocation of constant resources regardless of token difficulty. By unlocking elastic budget control, Star Elastic enables a novel inference scheme that uses different submodels for each reasoning phase (thinking and answering). Star Elastic supports (1) nesting along the SSM, embedding channel, MoE, and FFN axes, (2) learning nested submodels via an end-to-end trainable router, and (3) curriculum-based knowledge distillation. Building on the Nemotron Elastic framework, we apply Star Elastic to the NVIDIA Nemotron Nano models, with a particular focus on hybrid Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures: from Nemotron Nano v3 (30B/3.6A), we generate 23B (2.8A) and 12B (2.0A) variants with 160B training tokens. All nested models match or outperform independently trained baselines of comparable size and achieve a 360x reduction versus pretraining from scratch and a 7x reduction over state-of-the-art compression. Crucially, elastic budget control advances the accuracy-latency Pareto frontier, achieving up to 16% higher accuracy and 1.9x lower latency via dynamic per-phase model selection. We further extend Star Elastic to quantized regimes via Quantization-Aware Distillation (QAD), producing nested NVFP4 and FP8 elastic checkpoints that preserve zero-shot slicing while delivering smaller deployment footprints.

</details>


### [195] [Coupling Models for One-Step Discrete Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07193)
*Fred Zhangzhi Peng,Avishek Joey Bose,Anru R. Zhang,Alexander Tong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Coupling Models enable one-step discrete generation by learning direct coupling between discrete sequences and Gaussian latents, outperforming existing one-step baselines across text, DNA, and image domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current discrete generative modeling often relies on sequential methods like autoregressive decoding or iterative refinement, which are slow. Recent distillation methods compress multi-step samplers but aren't purpose-built. There's a need for efficient one-step discrete generation that avoids complex continuous flows and hand-specified couplings.

Method: Coupling Models learn a direct coupling between discrete sequences and Gaussian latents, then train a purpose-built decoder to invert this coupling for one-step generation. This avoids both sequential generation and complex continuous flows over the simplex, while also eliminating hand-specified data-to-noise couplings.

Result: Coupling Models improve state-of-the-art one-step baselines significantly: reduces LM1B text-generation perplexity by 33% at lowest-perplexity point, reduces Fly Brain enhancer-design FBD by 18%, and reduces MNIST-Binary FID by 46%.

Conclusion: Effective one-step discrete generation depends critically on how data and noise are coupled before decoding. Coupling Models demonstrate this principle and offer efficient single-step generation across diverse discrete domains including text, DNA sequences, and binary images.

Abstract: Generative modeling over discrete structures underpins applications across deep learning, from biological sequence design and code generation to large language models, yet generation often remains sequential, relying on autoregressive decoding or iterative refinement. In this work, we introduce Coupling Models(Coupling Models), a one-step discrete generative model that learns a direct coupling between discrete sequences and Gaussian latents. Unlike recent distillation methods that compress a pretrained multi-step sampler into a few steps, Coupling Model trains a purpose-built decoder to invert this coupling and generate samples in a single step. The model also avoids complex continuous flows over the simplex and hand-specified data-to-noise couplings. Empirically,Coupling Model improves the strongest one-step baselines in each domain: it reduces LM1B text-generation perplexity by 33% at its lowest-perplexity operating point, Fly Brain enhancer-design FBD by 18%, and MNIST-Binary FID by 46%. These results suggest that effective one-step discrete generation depends strongly on how data and noise are coupled before decoding. Code is available at https://github.com/pengzhangzhi/Coupling-Models.

</details>


### [196] [Arrow: A Foundation Model for Causal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07204)
*Ryan Thompson,He Zhao,Daniel M. Steinberg,Edwin V. Bonilla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Arrow is a foundation model for zero-shot causal discovery that uses a transformer-based architecture to predict skeleton edge probabilities and node order scores from observational tabular data, achieving competitive performance with lower computational cost than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for fast, accurate, and reusable causal discovery models that can work on new datasets without retraining, overcoming the computational expense and dataset-specific nature of traditional causal discovery methods.

Method: Arrow factorizes DAGs into an undirected skeleton and topological order, uses transformer-based architecture to contextualize variables, predicts skeleton edge probabilities and node order scores, and is trained end-to-end on diverse synthetic datasets using a differentiable directed edge composite likelihood.

Result: Arrow matches or outperforms existing causal discovery methods across in-distribution, out-of-distribution synthetic, semi-synthetic, and real datasets at substantially lower inference cost than competitive alternatives.

Conclusion: Large-scale pretraining on diverse synthetic data can yield zero-shot causal discovery models that are fast, accurate, and reusable on new datasets, demonstrating the viability of foundation models for causal discovery.

Abstract: We introduce Arrow, a foundation model for zero-shot causal discovery on observational tabular data. Arrow factorizes a directed acyclic graph into an undirected skeleton and a topological order, guaranteeing acyclicity by construction. Given a new dataset, it uses a transformer-based architecture to contextualize variables within and across observations, then predicts skeleton edge probabilities and node order scores that together define a graph. Arrow is trained in a supervised fashion on synthetic datasets with ground-truth graphs, using an end-to-end differentiable directed edge composite likelihood induced by the skeleton-order factorization. The training distribution spans diverse graph families, functional forms, noise models, and dataset shapes. Across in- and out-of-distribution synthetic, semi-synthetic, and real datasets, Arrow matches or outperforms existing causal discovery methods at substantially lower inference cost than competitive alternatives. Our results demonstrate that large-scale pretraining on diverse synthetic data can yield zero-shot causal discovery models that are fast, accurate, and reusable on new datasets.

</details>


### [197] [FAME: Forecasting Academic Impact via Continuous-Time Manifold Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07208)
*Jianrong Ding,Jianyuan Zhong,Zhengyan Shi,Qiang Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FAME is a spatiotemporal framework that projects scientific papers into dynamic latent spaces to forecast academic impact, outperforming LLM evaluators by capturing knowledge evolution trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used to evaluate research ideas but struggle to assess long-term impact since true impact takes years to emerge. There's a need for verifiable proxy tasks to evaluate scientific judgment capabilities.

Method: Proposed FAME (Forecasting Academic Impact via Continuous-Time Manifold Evolution) - a spatiotemporal framework that projects papers into dynamic latent space informed by textual features and verified knowledge-flow graphs. It learns geometric constraints aligning impactful manuscripts with forward momentum of their fields.

Result: On 3,200 arXiv papers across three fast-evolving subfields, FAME consistently and substantially outperforms state-of-the-art LLM evaluators in prospective multidimensional impact forecasting. Integrating FAME's dynamic geometric signals into LLMs significantly improves their forecasting performance.

Conclusion: Manuscript impact forecasting serves as a useful, measurable proxy benchmark for scientific evaluation. FAME provides a strong, trajectory-aware foundation for automated scientific evaluation, demonstrating that dynamic modeling of scientific evolution beats static text-based approaches.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used to brainstorm and evaluate research ideas, yet assessing such judgments is fundamentally difficult because the true impact of a new idea may take years to emerge. We address this challenge by using the impact forecasting of human-authored manuscripts as a verifiable proxy task. In a prospective forecasting study, we find that frontier LLMs fail to reliably distinguish high-impact papers from ordinary publications, suggesting that static text-based judging is insufficient for scientific evaluation. To address this limitation, we propose $\textbf{FAME}$ ($\underline{\text{F}}$orecasting $\underline{\text{A}}$cademic Impact via Continuous-Time $\underline{\text{M}}$anifold $\underline{\text{E}}$volution), a spatiotemporal framework for modeling the dynamic trajectories of scientific topics. FAME projects papers into a dynamic latent space informed by textual features and a verified knowledge-flow graph, learning geometric constraints that align impactful manuscripts with the forward momentum of their fields. Experiments on 3,200 arXiv papers across three fast-evolving subfields show that FAME consistently and substantially outperforms state-of-the-art LLM evaluators in prospective multidimensional impact forecasting. Furthermore, integrating FAME's dynamic geometric signals into LLMs significantly improves their forecasting performance. These results support manuscript impact forecasting as a useful, measurable proxy benchmark and position FAME as a strong, trajectory-aware foundation for automated scientific evaluation.

</details>


### [198] [HARMONY: Bridging the Personalization-Generalization Gap by Mitigating Representation Skew in Heterogeneous Split Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07211)
*Jiseok Youn,You Rim Choi,Goodsol Lee,Sangtae Ha,Hyung-Sin Kim,Saewoong Bahk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HARMONY is a hybrid split federated learning framework that enables heterogeneous client architectures while maintaining accurate OOD prediction by using meta-learning simulation and contrastive feature alignment to address representation skew.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile devices face diverse resource constraints and non-IID data distributions, needing both fast local inference for in-distribution classes and remote support for out-of-distribution classes. Existing hybrid SFL suffers from representation skew with heterogeneous client architectures, causing degraded server-side OOD prediction.

Method: HARMONY uses meta-learning to simulate diverse client extractors across different parameters and architectures, learning to personalize effectively. To mitigate representation skew, it implements server-side contrastive learning to align extracted features without compromising client personalization or requiring raw label sharing.

Result: HARMONY achieves test accuracy improvements up to 43.0% without OOD and 28.3% with OOD compared to state-of-the-art methods across multiple datasets and model families, while maintaining acceptable latency.

Conclusion: HARMONY successfully addresses the representation skew problem in hybrid SFL with heterogeneous client architectures, enabling better OOD prediction through meta-learning-based personalization and contrastive feature alignment, making it suitable for real-world mobile deployments with diverse hardware constraints.

Abstract: Mobile devices face diverse resource constraints and non-IID data class distributions, requiring fast on-device inference for local in-distribution (ID) classes and on-demand remote support for client-specific out-of-distribution (OOD) classes. Hybrid split federated learning (Hybrid SFL) couples personalized client-side front ends (supporting early exit) with a generalized server-side backend for fallback inference, balancing accuracy and cost. However, under client architectural heterogeneity, the existing hybrid SFL suffers from representation skew, where features from customized extractors fail to align in the shared space, leading to a sharp degradation in the server model responsible for OOD prediction. We propose HARMONY, the first hybrid SFL framework to support heterogeneous client architectures. HARMONY modifies meta-learning to simulate diverse extractors across parameters and architectures, and to learn to personalize. To mitigate representation skew, HARMONY conducts server-side contrastive learning to align extracted features, neither sacrificing clients' personalization nor sharing raw labels. Compared to the state of the art across multiple datasets and model families, HARMONY improves test accuracy by up to 43.0%/28.3% without/with OOD, respectively, while maintaining acceptable latency.

</details>


### [199] [Same Brain, Different Prediction: How Preprocessing Choices Undermine EEG Decoding Reliability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07212)
*Dengzhe Hou,Zihao Wu,Lingyu Jiang,Zirui Li,Fangzhou Lin,Kazunori D. Yamada*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EEG deep learning models show unstable predictions across preprocessing pipelines; the paper introduces tools to measure, decompose, and reduce this instability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EEG is crucial for brain-computer interfaces and clinical neuroscience, but current deep learning models are typically trained and evaluated under a single, unreported preprocessing pipeline, hiding potential instability in predictions.

Method: Formalize preprocessing choices as counterfactual intervention space; use Walsh-Hadamard decomposition of 2^7 pipeline space; introduce Preprocessing Uncertainty (PU) metric; study Normalized Adaptive PGI (NA-PGI) regularization.

Result: EEG predictions are surprisingly unstable - up to 42% of trial-level predictions flip when preprocessing changes; preprocessing sensitivity is near-additive in practice; PU captures complementary instability to model confidence; NA-PGI shows promise as mitigation.

Conclusion: Preprocessing pipeline choices significantly impact EEG prediction stability, and the proposed tools enable systematic measurement, decomposition, and reduction of this instability for more reliable EEG analysis.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) is a cornerstone of brain-computer interfaces and clinical neuroscience, yet deep learning models are typically trained and evaluated under a single, unreported preprocessing pipeline. We formalize preprocessing choices as a counterfactual intervention space and show that EEG predictions are surprisingly unstable under this space: across six datasets spanning four paradigms, up to 42% of trial-level predictions flip when only the preprocessing changes, a variability that standard uncertainty methods do not explicitly quantify because they condition on a fixed preprocessing pipeline. We provide three tools to make this instability measurable, decomposable, and reducible. First, a Walsh-Hadamard decomposition of the 2^7 pipeline space reveals that sensitivity is near-additive in practice under the binary intervention design, enabling efficient step-by-step optimization. Second, we introduce Preprocessing Uncertainty (PU), a per-trial diagnostic that captures a dimension of instability complementary to model-based confidence. Third, we study Normalized Adaptive PGI (NA-PGI), a graph-structured regularizer that exploits the compositional structure of preprocessing interventions as one mitigation strategy with clear scope conditions.

</details>


### [200] [Improved Model-based Reinforcement Learning with Smooth Kernels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07218)
*Kun Long,Yuqiang Li,Xianyi Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Kernel smoothing model-based RL with Bernstein-style exploration bonus achieves improved horizon-dependent regret bounds for Lipschitz continuous MDPs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical RL theory focuses on low-rank MDPs with restrictive structural assumptions. Kernel smoothing offers an alternative that leverages MDP smoothness rather than low-rank structure, but existing approaches may not achieve optimal regret bounds.

Method: Proposes a new kernel smoothing model-based approach for online RL in finite-horizon settings under Lipschitz continuity. Incorporates a Bernstein-style exploration bonus into the kernel smoothing framework to balance exploration-exploitation trade-off.

Result: Achieves a regret bound that improves upon state-of-the-art in its dependence on the horizon. The analysis reveals a delicate synergy between Bernstein-style bonuses and kernel smoothing, with a new tight Bernstein-type concentration inequality for martingales as a technical contribution.

Conclusion: The kernel smoothing approach with Bernstein-style exploration offers a theoretically sound alternative to low-rank MDP assumptions, achieving improved regret bounds by leveraging smoothness properties through careful integration of concentration techniques with non-parametric estimation.

Abstract: For continuous state-action space scenarios, classical reinforcement learning (RL) theory predominantly focuses on low-rank Markov decision processes (MDPs), which provide sample-efficient guarantees at the expense of restrictive structural assumptions. Kernel smoothing model-based approaches offer a promising alternative paradigm that instead leverages the smoothness of the MDP and employs non-parametric kernel smoothing estimates of transition dynamics. This paper proposes a new kernel-smoothing model-based approach for online reinforcement learning in finite-horizon settings under Lipschitz continuity assumptions on the MDP. By incorporating a Bernstein-style exploration bonus into the kernel smoothing framework, our method achieves a regret bound which improves upon the state-of-the-art regret bound in its dependence on the horizon. The theoretical advancement relies on a delicate analysis of the synergy between Bernstein-style bonuses and kernel smoothing, where a new tight Bernstein-type concentration inequality for martingales may be of independent interest.

</details>


### [201] [Experience Sharing in Mutual Reinforcement Learning for Heterogeneous Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07244)
*Xiaoze Liu,Dhananjay Ram,Yuting Zhang,Zhaoyang Zhang,Wei Xia,Stefano Soatto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mutual Reinforcement Learning enables concurrent RL post-training for heterogeneous LLMs through typed experience exchange while maintaining separate parameters, objectives, and tokenizers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of enabling experience sharing across heterogeneous LLMs that have different tokenizers, parameters, and objectives during reinforcement learning post-training. There's a need to make experience-sharing design operational across different model families.

Method: The framework combines Shared Experience Exchange (SEE), Multi-Worker Resource Allocation (MWRA), and Tokenizer Heterogeneity Layer (THL) that retokenizes text and aligns token-level traces across incompatible vocabularies. Three specific implementations are tested on top of GRPO: Peer Rollout Pooling (PRP) for data-level sharing, Cross-Policy GRPO Advantage Sharing (XGRPO) for value-level sharing, and Success-Gated Transfer (SGT) for outcome-level sharing.

Result: A contextual-bandit analysis reveals structural positions on a stability-support trade-off: PRP incurs density-ratio variance and THL residual costs, XGRPO preserves on-policy actor support while changing scalar baselines, and SGT provides a rescue-set score direction toward verified peer successes. In the evaluated regime, outcome-level sharing (SGT) occupies the most favorable point on this trade-off.

Conclusion: Mutual Reinforcement Learning provides a practical framework for heterogeneous LLM experience exchange, with outcome-level success transfer emerging as the most favorable approach in the evaluated regime due to its balanced stability-support trade-off characteristics.

Abstract: We introduce Mutual Reinforcement Learning, a framework for concurrent RL post-training in which heterogeneous LLM policies exchange typed experience while keeping separate parameters, objectives, and tokenizers. The framework combines a Shared Experience Exchange (SEE), Multi-Worker Resource Allocation (MWRA), and a Tokenizer Heterogeneity Layer (THL) that retokenizes text and aligns token-level traces across incompatible vocabularies. This substrate makes the experience-sharing design question operational across model families. We instantiate three controlled probes on top of GRPO: data-level rollout sharing via Peer Rollout Pooling (PRP), value-level advantage sharing via Cross-Policy GRPO Advantage Sharing (XGRPO), and outcome-level success transfer via Success-Gated Transfer (SGT). A contextual-bandit analysis characterizes their structural positions on a stability-support trade-off: PRP pays density-ratio variance and THL residual costs, XGRPO preserves on-policy actor support while changing scalar baselines, and SGT supplies a rescue-set score direction toward verified peer successes. In the evaluated regime, outcome-level sharing occupies the favorable point of this trade-off.

</details>


### [202] [On the Robustness of Distribution Support under Diffusion Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07220)
*Ruijia Cao,Yuchen Wu,Nisha Chadramoorthy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Theoretical analysis showing diffusion guidance ensures samples stay close to target support, explaining why it produces high-quality results


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion guidance empirically produces controllable, high-fidelity samples, but its theoretical properties remain largely unexplored. It's not well understood why it consistently produces high-quality samples.

Method: Establish a "robustness of support" property showing that with exact score functions, guided diffusion processes almost always generate samples close to the target support. Analysis covers DDIM, DDPM, and various discretization schemes using exponential integrators.

Result: Demonstrated that guided diffusion processes maintain proximity to target support, preventing generation of structurally implausible samples that could adversely affect downstream tasks.

Conclusion: The work provides rigorous theoretical foundation explaining why diffusion guidance produces physically meaningful and structurally plausible samples by ensuring robustness of support.

Abstract: Diffusion guidance is a powerful technique that enables controllable and high-fidelity sample generation with diffusion models. At a high level, it modifies the score function by incorporating a guidance term that steers the generative process toward a desired condition. Despite its empirical success, the theoretical properties of diffusion guidance remain largely unexplored, and it is not well understood why it consistently produces high-quality samples.
  In this work, we explain the effectiveness of diffusion guidance by establishing a \emph{robustness of support} property. Specifically, we show that, given exact access to the score functions, guided diffusion processes almost always generate samples that remain close to the target support. This property is particularly desirable, as samples that lie off the support are often structurally implausible and may adversely affect downstream tasks. Our analysis covers both Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) and Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM), and applies to a wide range of discretization schemes induced by exponential integrators. Our results provide a rigorous foundation for understanding why diffusion guidance produces physically meaningful and structurally plausible samples.

</details>


### [203] [Bifurcation Models: Learning Set-Valued Solution Maps with Weight-Tied Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07277)
*Caleb Jore,Jialin Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes bifurcation models - weight-tied dynamical systems where different initializations converge to different stable equilibria, allowing models to represent solution sets rather than arbitrarily selected single solutions, with applications to frustrated Ising models and Allen-Cahn equations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard supervised learning forces models to predict single solutions for problems with multiple correct answers, requiring arbitrary selection of one solution as the target label. This arbitrary selector can be discontinuous and harder to learn than the actual solution set itself.

Method: Proposes bifurcation models - weight-tied dynamical systems where different initializations can converge to different stable equilibria. The models represent an attractor landscape rather than forcing convergence to a single chosen branch. Theoretically proves that broad set-valued maps with locally Lipschitz branches can be represented by regular equilibrium dynamics.

Result: 1) Bifurcation models can discover multiple valid equilibria without explicit branch labels. 2) Experiments on frustrated Ising models show these dynamics outperform single-branch supervision. 3) Allen-Cahn experiments reveal that diversity isn't automatic - it can be explicitly encouraged but comes with an accuracy-diversity tradeoff. 4) Theoretical proof shows induced selectors from these models are almost everywhere regular, unlike manual selectors which can be arbitrarily irregular.

Conclusion: Bifurcation models provide a principled approach to learning multiple correct solutions simultaneously, avoiding arbitrary branch selection in supervised learning. They offer theoretical guarantees about regularity while demonstrating practical advantages in scientific and combinatorial problems with multiple valid solutions.

Abstract: Many scientific and combinatorial problems admit multiple correct solutions, not a single label. Standard supervised learning resolves this ambiguity by choosing one solution as the target, but this hidden selector can be arbitrary, discontinuous, and harder to learn than the underlying solution set. We study bifurcation models, a weight-tied dynamical view in which different initializations can converge to different stable equilibria, so the model represents an attractor landscape rather than one chosen branch. We prove that broad set-valued maps with locally Lipschitz branches can be represented by regular equilibrium dynamics and that the induced selectors are almost everywhere regular, while manual selectors can be arbitrarily irregular. Experiments on frustrated Ising models show that such dynamics can discover multiple valid equilibria without branch labels and outperform single-branch supervision. Allen--Cahn experiments further show that diversity is not automatic: it can be encouraged explicitly, but with an accuracy--diversity tradeoff.

</details>


### [204] [Don't Learn the Shape: Forecasting Periodic Time Series by Rank-1 Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07222)
*Takato Honda*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FLAIR is an extremely simple yet effective time series forecasting method that outperforms complex models for periodic data, using just 28 parameters for hourly forecasts and achieving comparable results to state-of-the-art models like PatchTST.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper challenges the assumption that more parameters and complex models are needed for accurate time series forecasting. The authors discovered that hourly electricity series reshaped as matrices are approximately rank-1, suggesting that simple approaches should work well, and question whether all the flexibility in modern forecasting models is actually beneficial or just estimation noise.

Method: FLAIR reshapes periodic time series into matrices (24 rows for hourly, columns as days), treats them as approximately rank-1 problems, and uses a simple approach: take the average of the last 2 cycles as a frozen baseline. The method involves one SVD per period candidate, GCV-averaged Ridge regression, and requires no per-task tuning, pre-training, or GPU computation.

Result: On the GIFT-Eval benchmark with 97 configurations, FLAIR matches PatchTST's performance (relMASE 0.838 vs 0.849), uses only 28 parameters for hourly forecasting (57 for weekly), trains in 22 minutes on a single CPU core, and outperforms 8 alternative methods including Fourier, EWMA, James-Stein, and rank-r SVD approaches.

Conclusion: For high-rank-1, many-cycle periodic time series, simpler methods with fewer parameters can outperform complex models because extra flexibility often translates to estimation noise rather than improved forecasting. The frozen baseline approach (averaging last 2 cycles) is surprisingly effective and robust.

Abstract: How few parameters do we really need to forecast a periodic time series? An hourly electricity series, reshaped as a 24-row matrix with one column per day, is approximately rank-1: a daily shape modulated by a daily level (median centered rank-1 energy 0.82 on GIFT-Eval). Should we learn the shape? Smoothing, shrinkage, and low-rank fits all seem like obvious upgrades over the simple average of the last K=2 cycles. On all 97 GIFT-Eval configurations, we tested 8 such alternatives (e.g., Fourier, EWMA, James-Stein, rank-r SVD): none significantly beats the frozen baseline under Holm correction; two are significantly worse. The resulting method, FLAIR, is (a) Effective: matches PatchTST on aggregate GIFT-Eval (relMASE 0.838 vs 0.849); (b) Compact: 28 scalars for hourly, 57 for weekly; (c) Fast: 22 minutes on one CPU core of a MacBook Pro; (d) Closed-form & Hands-Off: one SVD per period candidate, GCV-averaged Ridge, no GPU, no pre-training, no per-task tuning. In the high-rank-1, many-cycle regime, extra flexibility is estimation noise.

</details>


### [205] [Predictive but Not Plannable: RC-aux for Latent World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07278)
*Wenyuan Li,Guang Li,Keisuke Maeda,Takahiro Ogawa,Miki Haseyama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RC-aux is a lightweight correction for latent world models that improves planning by adding multi-horizon prediction and budget-conditioned reachability supervision, making latent spaces better aligned with long-horizon search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent world models often achieve accurate short-horizon prediction but create latent spaces poorly aligned with planning, specifically suffering from spatiotemporal mismatch - local predictive training doesn't translate well to long-horizon goal-directed search where Euclidean distance doesn't reflect what's reachable within finite action budgets.

Method: RC-aux adds planning-aligned supervision on two axes: 1) Time axis: multi-horizon open-loop prediction trains beyond one-step consistency; 2) Space axis: budget-conditioned reachability supervision with temporal hard negatives encourages the latent space to distinguish states reachable within current planning horizon vs eventually reachable states. Keeps world-model backbone unchanged.

Result: Across goal-conditioned pixel-control tasks and a LIBERO-Goal extension, RC-aux improves LeWM-style planning with modest additional cost, demonstrating that planning success depends not just on predictive accuracy but also on whether representations encode the temporal and geometric structure needed for search.

Conclusion: RC-aux effectively corrects spatiotemporal mismatch in latent world models by adding reachability-oriented supervision, enabling better planning performance without major architectural changes. The reachability signal learned can also be leveraged by reachability-aware planners at test time.

Abstract: A latent world model may achieve accurate short-horizon prediction while still inducing a latent space that is poorly aligned with planning. A key issue is spatiotemporal mismatch: these models are often trained with local predictive supervision, but deployed for long-horizon goal-directed search in latent spaces where Euclidean distance may not reflect what is reachable within a finite action budget. We present the Reachability-Correction auxiliary objective (RC-aux), a lightweight correction for this mismatch in reconstruction-free latent world models. RC-aux keeps the world-model backbone unchanged and adds planning-aligned supervision along two axes. Along the time axis, multi-horizon open-loop prediction trains the model beyond one-step consistency. Along the space axis, budget-conditioned reachability supervision, together with temporal hard negatives, encourages the latent space to distinguish states that are eventually reachable from those reachable within the current planning horizon. At test time, the learned reachability signal can also be used by a reachability-aware planner to favor trajectories that are both goal-directed and attainable under the available budget. We instantiate RC-aux on LeWorldModel and evaluate it under both continuation-training and matched-from-scratch settings. Across goal-conditioned pixel-control tasks and a LIBERO-Goal extension, RC-aux improves LeWM-style planning with modest additional cost. These results suggest that planning with latent world models depends not only on predictive accuracy, but also on whether the learned representation encodes the temporal and geometric structure required by downstream search. The code is available at https://github.com/Guang000/RC-aux.

</details>


### [206] [Modulated learning for private and distributed regression with just a single sample per client device](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07233)
*Praneeth Vepakomma,Amirhossein Reisizadeh,Samuel Horváth,Munther Dahleh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated learning framework for clients with only one data sample each, using private data transformations instead of local model updates to enable collaborative learning while preserving privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many real-world applications involve learning from numerous devices where each device has only a single sample (e.g., fitness trackers, body-worn sensors, usage aggregators). Standard federated learning fails in this setup because local updates based on one sample are unhelpful, especially in early rounds, and privacy noise further degrades utility.

Method: Clients apply a single, carefully calibrated noisy perturbation to transform their single sample, then share post-processed representations with the server. The server aggregates these representations to compute an unbiased gradient update that matches the non-private centralized gradient in expectation while preserving data privacy.

Result: The approach enables devices with extremely limited data (single sample) to collaborate and learn accurate, privacy-preserving models without requiring large local datasets. It differs from traditional private federated learning where communication involves model coefficients rather than privately transformed data samples.

Conclusion: The proposed method successfully addresses the challenge of federated learning with one sample per client by transforming private data samples into secure representations that enable effective collaborative learning while maintaining individual privacy, expanding federated learning to applications with data-sparse devices.

Abstract: This work focuses on the question of learning from a large number of devices with each device holding only a single sample of data. Several real-world applications exist to this one sample per client setup up including learning from fitness trackers, data/app usage aggregators, body-worn sensing devices, and daily event monitors to name a few. When a client has only one sample, the standard federated learning paradigm breaks down as a local update based on that single point is far from being useful, especially in the earlier rounds for estimation of the model coefficients. This utility is further weakened by the privacy-inducing noise applied at every round. This work caters to this problem to enable such clients to collaboratively contribute to effectively learn a global model without leaking the privacy of their data.
