The proposed approach injects a single, carefully calibrated noisy perturbation to transform the sample at each client, followed by a post-processed representation which is shared with the server. These representations aggregated at the server are processed to obtain an unbiased gradient update that in expectation matches the non-private centralized gradient while preserving data privacy. This approach is different than traditional private federated learning, where the communication payloads involve model coefficients as opposed to privately transformed data samples. This method enables devices with extremely limited data to collaborate and learn accurate, privacy-preserving models without requiring large local datasets or sacrificing individual privacy.

</details>


### [207] [Mask2Cause: Causal Discovery via Adjacency Constrained Causal Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07280)
*Omar Muhammad,Pasupuleti Dhruv Shivkant,Deepak N. Subramani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mask2Cause is an end-to-end deep learning framework for causal discovery in time series that recovers causal graphs directly during forecasting, using novel mechanisms like Inverted Variable Embedding and Adjacency-Constrained Masked Attention with state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current neural methods for causal discovery in time series have limitations: component-wise architectures fail to capture shared system dynamics, and decoupled post-hoc graph extraction risks overfitting to spurious correlations.

Method: Proposes Mask2Cause framework with Inverted Variable Embedding and Adjacency-Constrained Masked Attention mechanism, trained with homoscedastic or heteroscedastic objectives to capture causal influences in both mean and variance.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art causal discovery on diverse benchmarks (synthetic chaotic dynamics to realistic biological simulations) with significantly reduced parameter complexity compared to baselines. Inferred causal structures reduce forecasting model parameter count by >70% while maintaining accuracy.

Conclusion: Mask2Cause provides an effective end-to-end solution for causal discovery in time series that addresses limitations of existing approaches while offering practical benefits for model efficiency through discovered causal structure.

Abstract: Leveraging deep learning for causal discovery in time series remains challenging because existing neural methods predominantly rely on component-wise architectures that fail to capture shared system dynamics or employ decoupled post-hoc graph extraction that risks overfitting to spurious correlations. We propose $\textbf{Mask2Cause}$, an end-to-end framework that recovers the underlying causal graph directly during the forecasting forward pass. Our approach introduces an Inverted Variable Embedding and an Adjacency-Constrained Masked Attention mechanism, trained with homoscedastic or heteroscedastic objectives to capture causal influences in both mean and variance. Empirical results on diverse benchmarks, from synthetic chaotic dynamics to realistic biological simulations, demonstrate state-of-the-art causal discovery with significantly reduced parameter complexity compared to standard baselines. We further show that inferred causal structures can be used to reduce parameter count of forecasting models by more than 70% on average while maintaining predictive accuracy.

</details>


### [208] [Sample Complexity of Stochastic Optimization with Integer Variables](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07239)
*Hongyu Cheng,Yinghao Zheng,Marco Molinaro,Amitabh Basu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Integer stochastic optimization has varying sample complexity compared to continuous problems: same for Lipschitz objectives in ℓ∞ ball, smaller for ℓ₂ ball in certain regimes, but significantly larger for strongly convex smooth objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the sample complexity differences between stochastic integer optimization and continuous optimization problems, determining when integer constraints increase or decrease statistical complexity compared to their continuous counterparts.

Method: Establish sample complexity results through theoretical analysis across three scenarios: 1) Lipschitz objectives over ℓ∞ ball subsets, 2) Lipschitz objectives over ℓ₂ ball subsets, 3) Strongly convex smooth objectives.

Result: 1) For Lipschitz objectives in ℓ∞ ball, integer and continuous optimization have identical statistical complexity. 2) For ℓ₂ ball, integer optimization can require strictly smaller samples than continuous in certain regimes. 3) For strongly convex smooth objectives, integer optimization needs Ω(1/ε²) samples vs O(1/ε) for continuous.

Conclusion: Integer optimization's sample complexity relative to continuous optimization depends fundamentally on problem structure - sometimes equal, sometimes smaller, sometimes significantly larger, with strongly convex smooth objectives showing the worst-case quadratic increase in sample requirements.

Abstract: We establish sample complexity results for stochastic optimization over the integers, especially with a view to understand the complexity with respect to the corresponding continuous optimization problem. We show that integer optimization can sometimes require strictly more samples and sometimes strictly smaller number of samples, depending on the structure of the objective and constraints. 1. For Lipschitz objectives over subsets of the $\ell_\infty$ ball, the statistical complexity of general stochastic mixed-integer, nonlinear, nonconvex optimization is exactly the same as stochastic linear optimization with just bound constraints. 2. For Lipschitz objectives over subsets of the $\ell_2$ ball, we show that integer optimization can require strictly *smaller* sample size compared to the continuous setting in a certain regime. To get to this result, we also establish tight sample complexity results for nonconvex continuous stochastic optimization which, to the best of our knowledge, do not appear in prior work. 3. For strongly convex, smooth objectives, integer optimization has high statistical complexity compared to the continuous setting. In particular, we show that integer optimization requires $Ω(1/ε^2)$ samples to report an $ε$-approximate solution, compared to the well-known $O(1/ε)$ sample complexity from the continuous optimization literature.

</details>


### [209] [Generative Modeling with Flux Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07319)
*Peter Pao-Huang,Xiaojie Qiu,Stefano Ermon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flux Matching generalizes score-based models to non-conservative vector fields, allowing flexible design of generative dynamics beyond score matching constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Score-based generative models require matching the data score (gradient of log density), which limits them to conservative vector fields. This constraint prevents incorporating structural priors, optimizing sampling efficiency, or encoding directed dependencies.

Method: Flux Matching imposes a weaker condition: instead of requiring vector fields to equal the data score, it only requires that the stationary distribution of the learned vector field matches the data distribution. This allows learning any vector field whose stationary distribution is the data, not just conservative ones.

Result: Flux Matching performs strongly on high-dimensional image datasets and enables applications that score matching cannot: faster sampling, interpretable mechanistic models, and dynamics encoding directed variable dependencies. The approach turns vector field design into a flexible choice.

Conclusion: Flux Matching opens a new dimension in generative modeling by generalizing beyond conservative vector fields, transforming vector field design from a fixed target into an optimization/design choice with practical benefits.

Abstract: We introduce Flux Matching, a new paradigm for generative modeling that generalizes existing score-based models to a broader family of vector fields that need not be conservative. Rather than requiring the model to equal the data score, the Flux Matching objective imposes a weaker condition that admits infinitely many vector fields whose stationary distribution is the data. This flexibility enables a class of generative models that cannot be learned under score matching, in which inductive biases, structural priors, and properties of the dynamics can be directly imposed or optimized. We show that Flux Matching performs strongly on high-dimensional image datasets and, more importantly, that our added freedom unlocks a range of applications including faster sampling, interpretable and mechanistic models, and dynamics that encode directed dependencies between variables. More broadly, Flux Matching opens a new dimension in generative modeling by turning the vector field itself into a design choice rather than a fixed target. Code is available at https://github.com/peterpaohuang/flux_matching.

</details>


### [210] [SparseRL-Sync: Lossless Weight Synchronization with ~100x Less Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07330)
*Lucas Hu,Ranchi Zhao,Isaac Zhu,Zach Zhang,Hscos Zhang,Hugh Yin,Jason Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SparseRL-Sync: A lossless sparse weight synchronization method for large-scale RL that reduces communication by 100x by only transmitting changed parameters (typically 99%+ sparse updates).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In large-scale RL with Trainer-Rollout architecture, weight synchronization becomes a major bottleneck as model sizes grow, especially in bandwidth-constrained environments like cross-datacenter deployments, heterogeneous clusters, and online RL systems.

Method: Observes that parameter changes in large-model RL training are highly sparse (99%+). Proposes SparseRL-Sync which replaces full-weight transfers with lossless sparse update payloads containing only indices and values of changed parameters, which can be exactly reconstructed on the inference side.

Result: Reduces per-update communication volume from S to approximately S/X (e.g., 100x reduction with 99% sparsity). Combined with bucketing, also reduces launch and control-plane overhead, improving scalability and efficiency in bandwidth-limited asynchronous RL settings.

Conclusion: SparseRL-Sync effectively addresses the communication bottleneck in large-scale RL systems by exploiting the sparsity of parameter updates, enabling significant throughput improvements and latency reduction in bandwidth-constrained deployments while maintaining 100% fidelity.

Abstract: In large-scale reinforcement learning (RL) systems with decoupled Trainer-Rollout execution, the Trainer must regularly synchronize policy weights to the Rollout side to limit policy staleness. When inter-node bandwidth is abundant, such synchronization is usually only a small fraction of end-to-end cost. As model size grows, however, the communication demand rises rapidly. In bandwidth-constrained or network-variable deployments -- for example, cross-datacenter or cross-cluster settings, heterogeneous resource pools, and online RL -- weight synchronization can become a dominant bottleneck for throughput and tail latency. We observe that, in mainstream large-model RL training, the locations where parameters actually change are highly sparse at the element level (often 99%+ sparsity). Building on this observation, we propose and implement SparseRL-Sync, which replaces full-weight transfers with a lossless sparse update payload (indices and values) that can be exactly reconstructed on the inference side, thereby preserving 100% fidelity. Under a simplified cost model, sparse synchronization reduces the per-update communication volume from S to approximately S/X; with 99% sparsity (X ~ 100), this yields about a 100x reduction in transmitted data. Combined with appropriate bucketing, SparseRL-Sync also reduces launch and control-plane overhead, significantly improving scalability and end-to-end efficiency in bandwidth-limited and highly asynchronous RL settings.

</details>


### [211] [When Are Experts Misrouted? Counterfactual Routing Analysis in Mixture-of-Experts Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07260)
*Youngsik Yoon,Siwei Wang,Wei Chen,Jungseul Ok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Standard top-k routing in MoE models works well on confident tokens but fails on fragile reasoning tokens where better equal-compute alternatives exist but aren't selected, and simple router updates alone can improve reasoning performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While Mixture-of-Experts models use routing to select which experts process each token, there's little direct evaluation of whether these routing decisions are actually optimal or whether better routing decisions exist within the same computational budget.

Method: Researchers evaluate routing quality by comparing standard routes against sampled equal-compute alternatives, scoring each by next-token probability in verified reasoning trajectories. They analyze multiple models (Qwen3-30B-A3B, GPT-OSS-20B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite, OLMoE-1B-7B) and perform minimal router-only updates while keeping experts frozen.

Result: Standard routers perform well on confident tokens but are uninformative on fragile reasoning tokens, where lower-loss equal-compute routes consistently exist but aren't selected. Simple router-only updates improved performance on reasoning benchmarks (AIME 2024+2025 and HMMT 2025) for both Qwen3-30B-A3B and GPT-OSS-20B.

Conclusion: Current top-k routing training has fundamental limitations - it only scores executed routes and uses aggregate statistics for load balancing, missing better routing alternatives that exist within the model. The performance gap is partly due to router-reachable misallocation rather than just expert capacity limitations.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models route each token to a small subset of experts, but whether the routes selected by a trained top-$k$ router are good ones is rarely evaluated directly. Holding the model fixed, we compare each standard route against sampled equal-compute alternatives for the same token and score each by the next-token probability it assigns to the realized token in a verified reasoning trajectory. The result is sharply token-conditional: the standard router is well-aligned with route utility on confident tokens but uninformative on the fragile tokens that drive hard reasoning, where lower-loss equal-compute routes consistently exist inside the frozen model but are not selected. The same pattern holds across Qwen3-30B-A3B, GPT-OSS-20B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite, and OLMoE-1B-7B, and follows structurally from how standard top-$k$ training evaluates routing decisions: the language modeling loss scores only the executed route, and load balancing depends only on aggregate routing statistics. A minimal router-only update to the final-layer router, leaving every expert and every other router frozen, is sufficient to shift pass@K on AIME 2024+2025 and HMMT 2025 for both Qwen3-30B-A3B and GPT-OSS-20B, suggesting that at least part of the failure reflects router-reachable misallocation rather than expert capacity alone.

</details>


### [212] [Rethinking Importance Sampling in LLM Policy Optimization: A Cumulative Token Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07331)
*Yuheng Zhang,Chenlu Ye,Shuowei Jin,Changlong Yu,Wei Xiong,Saurabh Sahu,Nan Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CTPO introduces cumulative token IS ratios for unbiased prefix correction with lower variance than full sequence ratios, achieving state-of-the-art performance in mathematical reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL methods for LLM post-training face a bias-variance dilemma: token-level IS ratios introduce bias by ignoring prefix state mismatches, while full sequence ratios suffer from high variance due to multiplicative accumulation of per-token ratios.

Method: CTPO uses cumulative token IS ratios (product of per-token ratios up to position t) for unbiased prefix correction with lower variance, combined with position-adaptive clipping that scales log-space clip bounds according to √t growth of cumulative log-ratios.

Result: CTPO achieves best average performance across model scales on challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks compared to strong GRPO and GSPO baselines in tool-integrated reasoning settings.

Conclusion: Cumulative token IS ratios provide a theoretically principled solution to the bias-variance dilemma in off-policy policy-gradient estimation for LLM post-training, enabling more stable and effective optimization.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning, including reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), has emerged as a powerful approach for LLM post-training. Central to these approaches is the design of the importance sampling (IS) ratio used in off-policy policy-gradient estimation. Existing methods face a fundamental bias-variance dilemma: token-level IS ratios, as adopted by PPO (Schulman et al., 2017) and GRPO (Shao et al., 2024), introduce bias by ignoring prefix state distribution mismatch; full sequence ratios provide exact trajectory-level correction but suffer from high variance due to the multiplicative accumulation of per-token ratios, while GSPO (Zheng et al., 2025) improves numerical stability via length normalization at the cost of deviating from the exact full-sequence IS correction. In this work, we identify the cumulative token IS ratio, the product of per-token ratios up to position $t$, as a theoretically principled solution to this dilemma. We prove that, under the token-level policy-gradient formulation, this ratio provides an unbiased prefix correction for each token-level gradient term and has strictly lower variance than the full sequence ratio. Building on this insight, we propose CTPO (Cumulative Token Policy Optimization), which combines the cumulative token IS ratio with position-adaptive clipping that scales log-space clip bounds according to the natural $\sqrt{t}$ growth of the cumulative log-ratio. This yields more consistent regularization across token positions. We implement and evaluate CTPO in the tool-integrated reasoning setting on several challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks, achieving the best average performance across both model scales compared with strong GRPO and GSPO baselines. Code will be available at https://github.com/horizon-llm/CTPO.

</details>


### [213] [PerCaM-Health: Personalized Dynamic Causal Graphs for Healthcare Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07267)
*Elahe Khatibi,Ziyu Wang,Saba A. Farahani,Di Huang,Hung Cao,Ramesh Jain,Amir M. Rahmani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PerCaM-Health: A framework for learning personalized dynamic causal graphs from health data by combining population-level models with patient-specific adaptations, enabling better patient-level intervention reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing temporal causal discovery methods fail in healthcare settings because cohort-level models lack personalization, while per-patient models are unreliable due to short, noisy, non-stationary individual trajectories. This creates a gap between population-level causal modeling and the patient-specific, time-varying mechanisms needed for intervention reasoning.

Method: PerCaM-Health learns a knowledge-guided population temporal graph, then conservatively adapts and evolves it using patient-specific temporal evidence and rolling-window updates, producing interpretable and auditable graph sequences. Couples these graphs with temporal structural equations.

Result: Experiments on semi-synthetic dynamic health benchmark show PerCaM-Health improves graph recovery, dynamic edge tracking, and intervention direction accuracy compared to cohort-level, per-patient, and non-personalized temporal baselines.

Conclusion: Jointly modeling personalization and temporal evolution yields more reliable causal structure and intervention reasoning for healthcare applications.

Abstract: Personalized healthcare decisions require reasoning about how physiological and behavioral variables influence an individual patient over time. Existing temporal causal discovery methods are poorly matched to this setting: cohort-level models provide stable but non-personalized structures, while per-patient discovery is unreliable because individual trajectories are short, noisy, irregular, and non-stationary. This creates a fundamental gap between population-level causal modeling and the patient-specific, time-varying mechanisms needed for intervention reasoning. We introduce PerCaM-Health, a framework for learning personalized dynamic causal graphs from longitudinal health data. The framework learns a knowledge-guided population temporal graph, then conservatively adapts and evolves it using patient-specific temporal evidence and rolling-window updates, producing interpretable and auditable graph sequences. By coupling these graphs with temporal structural equations, the framework enables patient-level counterfactual queries, such as estimating short-horizon outcome changes under hypothetical behavioral interventions. Experiments on a semi-synthetic dynamic health benchmark show that PerCaM-Health improves graph recovery, dynamic edge tracking, and intervention direction accuracy compared to cohort-level, per-patient, and non-personalized temporal baselines. These results demonstrate that jointly modeling personalization and temporal evolution yields more reliable causal structure and intervention reasoning.

</details>


### [214] [Mage: Multi-Axis Evaluation of LLM-Generated Executable Game Scenes Beyond Compile-Pass Rate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07342)
*Hugh Xuechen Liu,Kıvanç Tatar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Compile-pass rate is misleading for LLM code generation in multi-component domain-specific tasks; a four-axis evaluation protocol reveals runtime success can be anti-correlated with functional correctness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of LLM code generation relies heavily on compile-pass rate, but this can be misleading for complex, multi-component domain-specific artifacts like game scene synthesis, where functional correctness and structural fidelity matter more than just successful compilation.

Method: Developed a four-axis evaluation protocol called 'Mage' (compile success, runtime success, structural fidelity, mechanism adherence) and applied it to 858 generation attempts across four open-weight LLMs (7B-30B), 26 hand-crafted Unity goal pattern playable concepts, and two automatically extracted IR granularity levels. Compared direct NL-to-C# generation with structural IR conditioning approaches.

Result: Direct NL-to-C# generation achieved highest runtime-pass rate (43% mean) but produced structurally vacuous scenes (mechanism F1 ≈ 0.12). Structural IR conditioning halved runtime rate but recovered domain-faithful structure (F1 up to 1.00). Behavior-only and full-scene IR granularities were statistically indistinguishable (McNemar p = 1.0), indicating input-level granularity saturation.

Conclusion: Compile rate is anti-correlated with functional correctness for multi-component domain-specific code generation, highlighting the need for multi-axis evaluation to detect divergence between compile success and actual functional/structural correctness. The benchmark and evaluation framework are released for independent verification.

Abstract: Compile-pass rate is the dominant evaluation signal for LLM code generation, yet for multi-component domain-specific artifacts it can be actively misleading. We demonstrate this on executable game scene synthesis with a four-axis evaluation protocol (named `Mage') -- compile success, runtime success, structural fidelity, and mechanism adherence -- applied to 858 generation attempts across four open-weight LLMs (7B--30B), 26~hand-crafted Unity goal pattern playable concepts, and two automatically extracted IR granularity levels. Direct NL-to-C\# generation achieves the highest runtime-pass rate (43\% mean) yet produces structurally vacuous scenes (mechanism $F_1 \approx 0.12$). Structural IR conditioning halves the runtime rate but recovers domain-faithful structure ($F_1$ up to 1.00). Within IR conditioning, behavior-only and full-scene granularity are statistically indistinguishable (McNemar $p = 1.0$), indicating input-level granularity saturation. These results show that compile rate is anti-correlated with functional correctness in this domain and that multi-axis evaluation is necessary to detect the divergence. We release the benchmark, replay logs, and per-record metrics for independent verification.

</details>


### [215] [bispectrum: Selective $G$-Bispectra Made Practical](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07270)
*Johan Mathe,Adele Myers,Simon Mataigne,Nina Miolane*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents bispectrum, an open-source PyTorch library for efficient computation of selective G-bispectra, which are complete invariants under group transformations, and demonstrates their effectiveness as pooling layers in deep learning architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many ML tasks are invariant under group transformations (e.g., translations, rotations), and G-bispectra provide principled complete invariants for such tasks. However, practical deployment has been hampered by high computational costs and fragmented implementations across different groups.

Method: Developed bispectrum, an open-source PyTorch library implementing selective G-bispectra for seven different group actions. For finite groups, selectivity reduces cost from O(|G|²) to O(|G|). For planar rotations, uses disk bispectrum. For spherical 3D rotations, introduced augmented selective bispectrum reducing cost from O(L³) to Θ(L²). Implemented various compute optimizations and made library differentiable for integration into ML pipelines.

Result: The library delivers near-exact G-invariance with selective G-bispectra computed in sub-millisecond time on GPU. Evaluation on three benchmark datasets shows G-bispectra consistently outperform alternatives (norm pooling, gated pooling, Fourier-ELU pooling, max pooling, and non-equivariant baselines) in the low-data, moderate-capacity regime.

Conclusion: Selective G-bispectra provide an efficient, practical solution for incorporating group invariance into machine learning pipelines, overcoming previous computational limitations while maintaining principled mathematical properties and delivering superior performance in relevant regimes.

Abstract: Many machine learning tasks are invariant under the action of a group $G$ of transformations: signal classification can be invariant under translations, image classification under 2D rotations, and spherical-image classification under 3D rotations. The $G$-bispectrum is a principled complete invariant of a signal (retaining all all signal's information up to the group action) with proven benefits in machine learning and as a pooling layer in deep networks. However, its deployment has been hampered by high computational cost and a patchwork of group-specific implementations. We present bispectrum, an open-source, fully unit-tested PyTorch library that implements selective $G$-bispectra for seven different group actions, as differentiable modules that can be directly incorporated into machine learning pipelines and deep learning architectures. For finite groups $G$, selectivity reduces the computational cost from $O(|G|^2)$ to $O(|G|)$. For planar rotations, we leverage the disk bispectrum. For spherical 3D rotations, we introduce an augmented selective bispectrum at band-limit $L$ which reduces the cost from $O(L^3)$ to $Θ(L^2)$ coefficients. We profile the entire library (for which we implemented various compute optimizations), showing that it delivers near-exact $G$-invariance with its selective $G$-bispectra computed in sub-millisecond time on GPU (up to commonly used bandlimits). We evaluate the benefits of incorporating $G$-bispectra as pooling layers into deep learning architectures on three classical benchmark datasets --comparing against norm pooling, gated pooling, Fourier-ELU pooling, max pooling, and (non-equivariant) data-augmented convolutional baselines. Results show that $G$-bispectra consistently outperform alternatives in the low-data, moderate-capacity regime.

</details>


### [216] [MISA: Mixture of Indexer Sparse Attention for Long-Context LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07363)
*Ruijie Zhou,Fanxu Meng,Yufei Xu,Tongxuan Liu,Guangming Lu,Muhan Zhang,Wenjie Pei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MISA is a drop-in replacement for DeepSeek Sparse Attention's indexer that reduces computational cost by routing queries to only a few active heads from a larger pool, using mixture-of-experts principles while maintaining performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The DeepSeek Sparse Attention indexer uses many query heads (e.g., 64) that share the same selected token set, making this multi-head design the dominant computational cost on long contexts. There's a need to reduce this overhead while maintaining the indexer's expressive power.

Method: Proposes MISA (Mixture of Indexer Sparse Attention) which treats indexer heads as a pool of mixture-of-experts. Uses a lightweight router with block-level statistics to pick query-dependent subsets of only a few active heads. Only these routed heads run the heavy token-level scoring. Also introduces a hierarchical variant that keeps an enlarged candidate set from the routed pass and re-ranks it with the original DSA indexer.

Result: With only 8 active heads and no additional training, MISA matches dense DSA indexer on LongBench across DeepSeek-V3.2 and GLM-5 while running with 8x and 4x fewer indexer heads respectively. Outperforms HISA on average. Preserves fully green Needle-in-a-Haystack heatmaps up to 128K-token contexts and recovers >92% of tokens selected by DSA indexer per layer. TileLang kernel delivers ~3.82x speedup over DSA's original indexer kernel on H200 GPU.

Conclusion: MISA provides an efficient drop-in replacement for DSA indexers that significantly reduces computational costs while maintaining or improving performance through intelligent routing of queries to specialized attention heads in a mixture-of-experts framework.

Abstract: DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA) sets the state of the art for fine-grained inference-time sparse attention by introducing a learned token-wise indexer that scores every prefix token and selects the most relevant ones for the main attention. To remain expressive, the indexer uses many query heads (for example, 64 on DeepSeek-V3.2) that share the same selected token set; this multi-head design is precisely what makes the indexer the dominant cost on long contexts. We propose MISA (Mixture of Indexer Sparse Attention), a drop-in replacement for the DSA indexer that treats its indexer heads as a pool of mixture-of-experts. A lightweight router uses cheap block-level statistics to pick a query-dependent subset of only a few active heads, and only those heads run the heavy token-level scoring. This preserves the diversity of the original indexer pool while reducing the per-query cost from scoring every prefix token with every head to scoring it with only a handful of routed heads, plus a negligible router term computed on a small set of pooled keys. We further introduce a hierarchical variant of MISA that uses the routed pass to keep an enlarged candidate set and then re-ranks it with the original DSA indexer to recover the final selected tokens almost exactly. With only eight active heads and no additional training, MISA matches the dense DSA indexer on LongBench across DeepSeek-V3.2 and GLM-5 while running with eight and four times fewer indexer heads respectively, and outperforms HISA on average. It also preserves fully green Needle-in-a-Haystack heatmaps up to a 128K-token context and recovers more than 92% of the tokens selected by the DSA indexer per layer. Our TileLang kernel delivers roughly a 3.82 times speedup over DSA's original indexer kernel on a single NVIDIA H200 GPU.

</details>


### [217] [Unsolvability Ceiling in Multi-LLM Routing: An Empirical Study of Evaluation Artifacts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07395)
*Saloni Garg,Amit Sagtani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multi-LLM routing systems have inflated unsolvability estimates due to evaluation artifacts, causing routers to learn suboptimal strategies with significant opportunity costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior research attributes routing headroom to an "unsolvability ceiling" - queries that no model in a pool can solve. However, there's a need to investigate whether reported unsolvability is genuine or stems from evaluation artifacts that inflate headroom estimates.

Method: Large-scale study with 206,000 query-model pairs across six benchmarks using Gemma 4 and Llama 3.1 families. Evaluated with both LLM-as-a-judge and exact-match metrics. Introduced decomposition framework to attribute failures to specific artifacts, and used dual-judge validation and exact-match grounding. Analyzed router training signals with random-feature and shuffled-label controls.

Result: Substantial portion of reported unsolvability stems from evaluation artifacts: (1) systematic judge biases favoring verbosity over correctness, (2) truncation under fixed generation budgets, and (3) output format mismatches. Through dual-judge validation and exact-match grounding, measured unsolvability was reduced across tasks. Standard routers collapse to majority-class prediction (~79% smallest-tier optimal), incurring 13-17 percentage point opportunity cost.

Conclusion: Existing routing headroom estimates are substantially inflated due to evaluation artifacts. Provides actionable recommendations: dual-judge validation, exact-match anchoring, and cost-sensitive objectives. Emphasizes the need for reliable evaluation protocols in multi-LLM systems.

Abstract: Efficient routing across multiple LLMs enables cost-quality tradeoffs by directing queries to the cheapest capable model. Prior work attributes routing headroom to an "unsolvability ceiling", queries no model in the pool can solve. We present a large-scale study of multi-tier LLM routing with 206,000 query-model pairs across six benchmarks (MMLU, MedQA, HumanEval, MBPP, Alpaca, ShareGPT) using the Gemma 4 and Llama 3.1 families. Evaluating with both LLM-as-a-judge and exact-match metrics, we show that a substantial portion of reported unsolvability stems from evaluation artifacts: (i) systematic judge biases favoring verbosity over correctness, (ii) truncation under fixed generation budgets, and (iii) output format mismatches. Through dual-judge validation and exact-match grounding, we reduce measured unsolvability across tasks. We introduce a decomposition framework attributing failures to these artifacts, revealing consistent patterns across domains and model families. These artifacts also distort router training signals: standard routers collapse to majority-class prediction (~79% smallest-tier optimal), confirmed via random-feature and shuffled-label controls, incurring a 13-17 percentage point opportunity cost. We provide actionable recommendations including dual-judge validation, exact-match anchoring, and cost-sensitive objectives. Our findings suggest existing routing headroom estimates are substantially inflated, underscoring the need for reliable evaluation protocols in multi-LLM systems.

</details>


### [218] [Rubric-based On-policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07396)
*Junfeng Fang,Zhepei Hong,Mao Zheng,Mingyang Song,Gengsheng Li,Houcheng Jiang,Dan Zhang,Haiyun Guo,Xiang Wang,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ROPD introduces a rubric-based on-policy distillation framework that uses structured semantic rubrics instead of teacher logits, enabling black-box distillation from teacher responses only while achieving superior performance to logit-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current on-policy distillation (OPD) methods rely on teacher logits, which limits their application to white-box scenarios where internal model outputs are accessible. The authors aim to create a black-box compatible alternative that works with proprietary and open-source LLMs.

Method: ROPD framework induces prompt-specific rubrics by analyzing teacher-student response contrasts, then uses these rubrics to score student rollouts for on-policy optimization, eliminating the need for teacher logits.

Result: ROPD outperforms advanced logit-based OPD methods across most scenarios and achieves up to 10x gain in sample efficiency, demonstrating that rubric-based approaches can be more effective than logit-based distillation.

Conclusion: Rubric-based OPD serves as a flexible, black-box-compatible alternative to logit-based OPD, offering a simple yet strong baseline for scalable distillation across different types of language models.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) is a powerful paradigm for model alignment, yet its reliance on teacher logits restricts its application to white-box scenarios. We contend that structured semantic rubrics can serve as a scalable alternative to teacher logits, enabling OPD using only teacher-generated responses. To prove it, we introduce ROPD, a simple yet foundational framework for rubric-based OPD. Specifically, ROPD induces prompt-specific rubrics from teacher-student contrasts, and then utilizes these rubrics to score the student rollouts for on-policy optimization. Empirically, ROPD outperforms the advanced logit-based OPD methods across most scenarios, and achieving up to a 10x gain in sample efficiency. These results position rubric-based OPD as a flexible, black-box-compatible alternative to the prevailing logit-based OPD, offering a simple yet strong baseline for scalable distillation across proprietary and open-source LLMs. Code is available at https://github.com/Peregrine123/ROPD_official.

</details>


### [219] [Tracking Large-scale Shared Bikes with Inertial Motion Learning in GNSS Blocked Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07412)
*Feng Liu,Kejia Li,Zhiwei Yang,Chunwei Yang,Qun Li,Guobin Wu,Qiang Ni,Ruipeng Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel inertial tracking framework for bicycles that combines mechanical constraints with mixture-of-experts modeling to improve localization accuracy in GNSS-denied environments like urban canyons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNSS fails in complex riding environments like urban canyons, while pure inertial navigation suffers from cumulative drifts and poor robustness. Alternative sensors (visual, LiDAR) aren't suitable for large-scale deployment on bikes.

Method: Integrates bicycle mechanical constraints with a mixture-of-experts model. Uses multiple expert modules to capture shared representations with gating mechanism for multi-task learning and uncertainty-aware trajectory estimation. Leverages the mechanical transmission between pedal and rear wheel to convert rider's periodic pedaling behaviors into wheel speed for dynamic calibration.

Result: System improves accuracy of baselines by at least 12%, with wheel speed errors below 0.5 m/s at 95-percentile. Validated with real-world riding data from shared bikes on DiDi ride-hailing platform.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses limitations of existing bike tracking methods by combining mechanical constraints with advanced ML modeling, offering a practical solution for GNSS-denied urban environments suitable for large-scale deployment on shared bike platforms.

Abstract: Although Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) provide a general solution for bike tracking outdoors, there still exist complex riding environments where only inertial navigation systems work, such as urban canyons. Despite decades of research, localization using only low-cost inertial sensors still faces challenges such as cumulative drifts and poor robustness caused by filtering methods. Furthermore, sensors such as visual and LiDAR could provide reliable measurements, but they are not suitable for large-scale deployment. In this paper, we propose an inertial tracking framework that integrates bicycle mechanical constraints with a mixture-of-experts model. Specifically, we leverage multiple expert modules to capture shared representations and weight them through the gating mechanism, thus improving multi-task learning performance and enabling uncertainty-aware trajectory estimation. Furthermore, based on the mechanical transmission between the pedal and the rear wheel of a bike, we explore the intrinsic relationship between the rider's periodic pedalling behaviors and acceleration variations, and convert such patterns into bike's wheel speed for dynamic calibration. Experiments with real-world riding data from shared bikes of the DiDi ride-hailing platform demonstrate that our system improves the accuracy of baselines by at least 12%, with wheel speed errors below 0.5 m/s at 95-percentile.

</details>


### [220] [The Convergence Gap: Instruction-Tuned Language Models Stabilize Later in the Forward Pass](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07282)
*Yifan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Instruction-tuned models take longer to converge to final predictions than pretrained models, with late MLP layers being key controllers of this prediction delay.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how post-training (instruction tuning) affects model prediction dynamics - specifically when checkpoints commit to their final outputs during forward passes.

Method: Introduced "convergence gap" diagnostic using model-diffing across six paired pretrained/instruction-tuned checkpoints. Used endpoint-matched readouts, same-history checks, fixed-history template replay, and matched-prefix interventions to isolate late MLP windows as leverage points.

Result: Instruction-tuned models remain farther from final predictions later into the stack. Late MLP windows are the strongest tested bidirectional control: grafting IT late layers into PT hosts increases KL divergence by +0.34 nats, while PT-late swaps into IT hosts reduce it by -0.51 nats.

Conclusion: Instruction-tuned models exhibit delayed prediction convergence, with late MLP computation being a key controller of this post-training signature under matched history conditions.

Abstract: Final outputs hide when a checkpoint commits to its next-token prediction. We introduce the convergence gap, a model-diffing diagnostic that decodes each layer's next-token distribution and measures its distance to the model's own final distribution. Across six paired pretrained and instruction-tuned checkpoints in native prompting regimes, instruction-tuned checkpoints remain farther from their final predictions later into the stack. The effect persists under endpoint-matched raw and tuned readouts, endpoint-free same-history checks, and fixed-history template replay. Matched-prefix interventions identify late MLP windows as the largest tested leverage point: late IT grafts into PT hosts increase late KL by +0.34 nats, while PT-late swaps into IT hosts reduce it by -0.51 nats; matched random late perturbations give only +0.003 versus +0.327 for the true late graft. A preselected Gemma case study provides behavior-facing plausibility for the same late swap, without serving as a benchmark claim. These results identify a robust predictiondynamics signature of post-training: released instruction-following checkpoints tend to settle later, and late MLP computation is the strongest tested bidirectional handle on that delay under matched histories.

</details>


### [221] [Instruction Tuning Changes How Upstream State Conditions Late Readout: A Cross-Patching Diagnostic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07284)
*Yifan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates how early and late layers cooperate to produce behavioral differences in instruction-tuned vs pretrained models, showing that late-layer effects depend on earlier-layer states and are not self-contained.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While prior work has identified model-internal handles on behavior in middle-to-late layers, it's unclear how earlier computation and late layers cooperate to produce next-token differences between pretrained base and instruction-tuned models.

Method: The authors introduce first-divergence cross-patching: at the first token where pretrained base and instruction-tuned checkpoints disagree, they cross each model's earlier-layer state with each model's late stack, testing across five dense families (4B-32B).

Result: Instruction-tuned late stacks show a +1.68 logit interaction effect, being positive in every family tested. Sparse features in final MLP layers mediate the effect, suggesting a handoff from earlier state to final-layer feature activation to token margin.

Conclusion: Late-layer behavioral differences depend on earlier-layer states; studies localizing effects to late layers should test whether they survive under other checkpoints' upstream states before treating late stacks as self-contained modules.

Abstract: Recent interpretability work has identified model-internal handles on post-trained behavior, including refusal directions, assistant/persona axes, and sparse chat-tuning features. These results localize where behaviors can be read out or controlled, often in middle-to-late layers. We ask how earlier computation and the late stack cooperate to turn those differences into next-token margins. To test this, we introduce first-divergence cross-patching: at the first token where pretrained base (PT) and instruction-tuned (IT) checkpoints disagree, we cross each model's earlier-layer state with each model's late stack. The diagnostic separates training recipes: same-base instruction-following descendants show late effects that depend on their own earlier-layer state, while OpenMath2 math-domain SFT and controlled code/biomed CPT controls with verified domain learning do not; for OpenMath2, the late effect is already largely portable from base earlier-layer state. Across five dense families (4B-32B), the IT late stack adds +0.76 logits from PT upstream and +2.44 from IT upstream, giving a +1.68 interaction that is positive in every family. Thus the late stack has a real PT-upstream effect, but its larger effect in the IT checkpoint appears only when it reads its own post-trained upstream state. Sparse features in final MLP layers partially mediate the effect and are driven by upstream patches, supporting a handoff from earlier state to final-layer feature activation to IT-token margin. Forced-token scoring shows that the local token choice can change later exact-answer success. Operationally, paired-checkpoint studies that localize a difference to late layers should test whether it survives under the other checkpoint's upstream state before treating the late stack as self-contained.

</details>


### [222] [Physical Simulators as Do-Operators: Causal Discovery under Latent Confounders for AI-for-Science](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07467)
*Tsuyoshi Okita*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CFM-SD is a new method that uses physics-based simulators as do-operators to handle latent confounders and real interventional data in scientific applications where existing methods fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing causal discovery methods assume causal sufficiency (no latent confounders) and use fast virtual interventions, but in real scientific applications like molecular design and materials science, latent confounders are ubiquitous and real physics-based simulations take hours to days per data point.

Method: CFM-SD (Causal Flow Matching with Simulation Data) uses first-principles physical simulators as do-operators in Pearl's interventional calculus to simultaneously handle latent confounders and real interventional data. Theoretically identifies d-variable causal structure with O(d) single-variable interventions.

Result: On synthetic data, CFM-SD achieves average F1=0.800 vs F1=0.127-0.562 for baselines. On real scientific data, achieves 57-58% bias reduction in molecular toxicity prediction and battery electrolyte optimization.

Conclusion: CFM-SD successfully addresses key limitations of existing methods for scientific applications by handling latent confounders and real, expensive interventions, demonstrating practical value beyond synthetic benchmarks.

Abstract: Existing interventional causal discovery methods -- IGSP, DCDI, ENCO -- assume causal sufficiency (no latent confounders) and rely on virtual interventions in synthetic simulators. In AI-for-Science settings such as molecular design and materials science, latent confounders are ubiquitous and real interventions (e.g., physics-based simulations) require hours to days per data point. We propose CFM-SD (Causal Flow Matching with Simulation Data), which uses first-principles physical simulators as do-operators in Pearl's interventional calculus to simultaneously handle latent confounders and real interventional data. Theoretically, $d$-variable causal structure is identifiable with $O(d)$ single-variable interventions -- the minimum under physical realizability constraints. In Intrinsic Evaluation on synthetic data ($γ=0.2$--$0.8$), CFM-SD achieves average F1$=0.800$ vs. F1$=0.127$--$0.562$ for all baselines. In Extrinsic Evaluation on real scientific data, CFM-SD achieves 57--58\% bias reduction in molecular toxicity prediction and battery electrolyte optimization, demonstrating practical value beyond synthetic benchmarks.

</details>


### [223] [Pretraining Induces a Reusable Spectral Basis for Downstream Task Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07302)
*Junjie Yu,Yue Wang,Zihan Deng,Yan Zhu,Wenxiao Ma,Quanying Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Finetuning happens in a low-dimensional subspace; stable directions encode transferable structure from pretraining, not irrelevant noise, and can be leveraged for parameter-efficient adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why certain parameter directions remain stable during finetuning and whether these stable directions encode task-relevant structure or irrelevant noise, which is central to understanding how pretrained knowledge transfers to downstream tasks.

Method: Systematic spectral analysis across vision and language models to analyze singular vectors of pretrained weight matrices; propose parameter-efficient method that freezes pretrained singular vectors and optimizes only leading spectral coefficients.

Result: Leading singular vectors remain highly stable under finetuning and are shared across unrelated downstream tasks; larger pretraining datasets yield greater spectral stability; parameter-efficient method achieves competitive GLUE performance with only 0.2% trainable parameters.

Conclusion: Stable directions encode transferable structure rather than irrelevant noise: successful pretraining discovers reusable spectral bases that downstream tasks inherit and operate within, linking pretraining scale to geometric transferability.

Abstract: Finetuning pretrained models occurs in a low-dimensional subspace of the full parameter space. Prior work has focused on characterizing this optimization subspace, but largely ignored the complementary question: why do certain directions remain unexplored during finetuning? Are these stable directions irrelevant to downstream tasks, or do they already encode task-relevant structure that requires no further adjustment? Answering this question is central to understanding how pretrained knowledge transfers. Through systematic spectral analysis across vision and language models, we show that the leading singular vectors of pretrained weight matrices remain highly stable under finetuning and are shared across unrelated downstream tasks, revealing that pretraining establishes a reusable spectral coordinate system. Models pretrained on larger datasets exhibit greater spectral stability under distribution shift or task change, directly linking pretraining scale to geometric transferability. Motivated by these findings, we propose a parameter-efficient method that freezes pretrained singular vectors and optimizes only leading spectral coefficients, achieving competitive performance on GLUE with 0.2% trainable parameters. Our results reveal that the stable directions encode transferable structure rather than irrelevant noise: successful pretraining discovers spectral bases that downstream tasks inherit and operate within.

</details>


### [224] [SHRED: Retain-Set-Free Unlearning via Self-Distillation with Logit Demotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07482)
*Zizhao Hu,Ameya Godbole,Johnny Tian-Zheng Wei,Mohammad Rostami,Jesse Thomason,Robin Jia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SHRED is a retain-set-free unlearning method for LLMs that identifies high-information tokens in forget set instances and selectively demotes only those memorized tokens while preserving general language competence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current machine unlearning methods for LLMs require a retain set of curated examples to preserve model utility, creating extra data dependency and complicating deployment. There's a need for retain-set-free methods that can selectively remove memorized content without costly retraining.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) Selection - perform forward pass on forget set instances, identify bottom (lowest probability, highest Shannon information) tokens as forget positions, keep remaining as benign anchors; (2) Training - construct modified KL targets that demote memorized token's logit at forget positions while preserving original distribution at benign positions, then train via single top KL self-distillation objective.

Result: SHRED establishes new Pareto-optimal trade-off between forget efficacy and model utility across four standard unlearning benchmarks, outperforming retain-set-dependent methods. It shows robustness against relearning and membership-inference attacks, and maintains stable utility after many sequential unlearning runs.

Conclusion: SHRED successfully addresses the retain-set dependency problem in LLM unlearning by exploiting the insight that memorized knowledge concentrates in high-information tokens, enabling selective forgetting while preserving general language competence without additional data requirements.

Abstract: Machine unlearning for large language models (LLMs) aims to selectively remove memorized content such as private data, copyrighted text, or hazardous knowledge, without costly full retraining. Most existing methods require a retain set of curated examples to prevent catastrophic degradation of general model utility, creating an extra data dependency that complicates deployment. We propose SHRED (Self-distillation via High-surprisal-only Retain-set-free Entropy Demotion), a retain-set-free unlearning method built on a key insight: not all tokens within a forget set instance carry memorized information equally. High-information tokens concentrate the model's memorized knowledge, while low-information tokens reflect general language competence. SHRED operates in two stages. (1) Selection: We perform a forward pass on a forget set instance, collect per-token autoregressive probabilities, and select the bottom (lowest probability, highest Shannon information) as forget positions; the remaining positions are retained as benign anchors. (2) Training: We construct modified KL targets that demote the memorized token's logit at forget positions while preserving the original distribution at benign positions. The model is then trained via a single top KL self-distillation objective that simultaneously drives forgetting and utility preservation. We evaluate SHRED across four standard unlearning benchmarks and demonstrate that it establishes a new Pareto-optimal trade-off between forget efficacy and model utility, outperforming retain-set-dependent methods. Our analysis shows that SHRED is robust against relearning attacks and membership-inference attacks, and it maintains stable utility even after many sequential unlearning runs.

</details>


### [225] [Latent Order Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07304)
*Emil Carlsson,Newton Mwai,Fredrik D. Johansson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Latent Order Bandits (LOB) is a new bandit algorithm that requires only knowledge of partial action preference orders per latent state, allowing reward scale variations across instances while maintaining preference structure, improving robustness over traditional latent bandits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional latent bandit algorithms are sample-inefficient for personalization and require accurate posterior distributions of rewards and latent states, which are hard to obtain. They also assume identical reward distributions for all instances of the same latent state, which is overly restrictive for real-world scenarios where preferences may be shared but reward scales differ.

Method: Proposes Latent Order Bandits (LOB) that relax assumptions by requiring only prior knowledge of partial order of action preferences in each state. This allows instances of the same state to vary in reward distributions while maintaining shared preference orders. Provides an upper-confidence bound procedure applicable to both total and partial latent orders, and a posterior-sampling algorithm for improved empirical performance.

Result: Proves an upper bound on regret for the LOB algorithm. Empirical experiments show both UCB and posterior-sampling variants are competitive with full-prior latent bandits when same-state instances share reward parameters, and preferable when reward scales differ between instances with the same latent state.

Conclusion: LOB provides a more flexible and practical approach to latent bandits by requiring only partial preference order knowledge rather than full reward distributions, making it more robust to variations in reward scales across instances while maintaining effective exploration-exploitation tradeoffs.

Abstract: Bandit algorithms solve diverse sequential decision-making problems, but are often too sample-inefficient for from-scratch personalization. To substantially reduce exploration times, latent bandit algorithms exploit cross-instance structure implied by discrete latent states, provided that the posterior distribution of rewards and latent states is known and accurate. However, obtaining an accurate model of this structure is difficult, and a small number of latent states may be insufficient to characterize the reward distributions in all problem instances. We propose latent order bandits (LOB), relaxing the assumptions of latent bandits to require only prior knowledge of a partial order of action preferences in each state. This allows instances of the same state to vary in reward distributions, as long as the partial order of actions is shared. For example, groups of users on a streaming service may agree on which movie genres are the best but rate experiences on different scales. We give an upper-confidence bound procedure for the LOB problem, applicable to both total and partial latent orders, and give an upper bound on its regret. To improve empirical performance, we propose a posterior-sampling algorithm and show, in a suite of experiments, that both are competitive with full-prior latent bandits when same-state instances share reward parameters, and preferable to them when reward scales differ between instances with the same latent state.

</details>


### [226] [Does Your Neural Network Extrapolate? Feature Engineering as Identifiability Bias for OOD Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07483)
*Leonel Aguilar,Jan Nagler,Christoph Hoelscher,Nino Antulov-Fantulin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep neural networks can learn OOD-relevant representations only when provided with implicit structural commitments (feature/label maps, model classes) that bridge ID training to OOD extrapolation, making the same architecture perform vastly differently out-of-distribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why deep neural networks succeed or fail at learning out-of-distribution (OOD)-relevant representations from in-distribution (ID) training data, since OOD extrapolation is non-identifiable from ID data alone.

Method: Theoretical analysis decoupling feature learning from DGP identifiability, plus empirical studies including a 264-run positional-encoding study across Transformer, Mamba, and S4D, and three natural-science applications (chemistry, exoplanets, DNA).

Result: The same architecture at same ID loss can differ by ~520x in OOD performance depending on representation; correct features are necessary but not sufficient - model class must express target and training data must cover relevant representation space.

Conclusion: OOD generalization requires implicit structural commitments (φ, ψ, M) that bridge ID training to OOD extrapolation; when these commitments match the true DGP and are identifiable, OOD error vanishes (e.g., Fourier coordinates for periodic functions).

Abstract: Successful deep neural networks discover salient features of data. We show when and why they fail to learn out-of-distribution (OOD)-relevant representations from an in-distribution (ID) training window. This requires decoupling feature learning from data-generating-process (DGP) identifiability. From a single training window, OOD extrapolation is non-identifiable: infinitely many DGPs are $\varepsilon$-observationally equivalent on the training data but diverge arbitrarily outside it, and no in-distribution criterion alone reliably breaks the tie. A structural commitment, the feature map, label map, and model class $(\varphi, ψ, \mathcal{M})$, dictates the assumed DGP and governs OOD generalization while leaving ID performance essentially unchanged. When architecture, pretraining, augmentation, input formats, or domain knowledge implicitly inject the missing commitment, the model succeeds. When it cannot infer OOD-relevant structure from ID evidence, it fails. Changing only the representation can make the same architecture, at the same in-distribution loss, differ by ${\sim}520\times$ out of distribution. When the commitment is correct and identifiable, OOD error vanishes. For example, Fourier coordinates turn periodic extrapolation into interpolation on $\mathbb{S}^1$. The same mechanism predicts outcomes in three natural-science settings (mass-action chemistry; Kepler's-third-law exoplanet prediction, $n=2{,}362$; and cross-species coding-DNA detection) and in a 264-run positional-encoding study across Transformer, Mamba, and S4D. Finally, a controlled study shows: correct features are necessary but not sufficient. The model class must express the target, and the transformed training data must cover the relevant representation space.

</details>


### [227] [Excluding the Target Domain Improves Extrapolation: Deconfounded Hierarchical Physics Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07485)
*Tsuyoshi Okita*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DHG uses deconfounded hierarchical gating to remove temperature confounding effects in physics-constrained generative models, achieving 46% better extrapolation performance on battery temperature benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physics-constrained deep generative models struggle with out-of-distribution extrapolation because they apply physical constraints as static regularization, ignoring hierarchical structure of physical laws and confounding variable problems like temperature effects.

Method: Proposes Deconfounded Hierarchical Gate (DHG) that: 1) identifies when and how strongly temperature confounding contaminates each constraint level, 2) uses counterfactual estimation with do-operator and backdoor adjustment to remove confounding, 3) applies Coarse-to-Fine physical constraints progressively rather than uniform regularization.

Result: DHG achieves RMSE = 0.215 on lithium-ion battery temperature extrapolation benchmark (trained at 24°C, evaluated at 4.0-43.0°C), a 46% improvement over unconstrained baseline (0.397). Counterintuitively, excluding target-domain data from pretraining outperforms including it by 39% in extrapolation (0.224 vs 0.324 RMSE) because it learns more domain-agnostic physical patterns.

Conclusion: DHG successfully addresses confounding variable problems in physics-constrained generative models by deconfounding hierarchical constraints, leading to significantly improved out-of-distribution extrapolation performance. The method reveals that withholding target-domain data during pretraining can actually improve transfer learning by forcing models to learn more fundamental physical patterns.

Abstract: Extrapolation to out-of-distribution conditions is a fundamental challenge for physics-constrained deep generative models. Existing methods apply physical constraints as a single static regularization term uniformly across the generation process, and address neither the hierarchical structure of physical laws and the confounding variable problem. We propose the Deconfounded Hierarchical Gate (DHG), which serves as a diagnostic and control mechanism: it identifies when and how strongly temperature confounding contaminates each constraint level, so that hierarchical gates reflect intrinsic physical inconsistency rather than spurious temperature effects. DHG combines counterfactual estimation via the do-operator with backdoor adjustment to remove confounding, then applies Coarse-to-Fine physical constraints progressively. We report a counter-intuitive finding in pretraining: excluding the target-domain data from pretraining outperforms including it by 39% in extrapolation performance (RMSE 0.224 vs. 0.324). This occurs because FNO learns domain-agnostic physical patterns that transfer more effectively when the target domain is withheld. On a lithium-ion battery temperature extrapolation benchmark (trained at 24 degrees Celsius, evaluated at 4.0--43.0 degrees Celsius), our method achieves RMSE = 0.215, a 46% improvement over the unconstrained baseline (Pure CFM: 0.397).

</details>


### [228] [Why Self-Inconsistency Arises in GNN Explanations and How to Exploit It](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07527)
*Wenxin Tai,Yaqian Liu,Ting Zhong,Fan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Self-Denoising (SD) is a training-free post-processing method that improves explanation consistency in Self-Interpretable Graph Neural Networks by identifying and calibrating self-inconsistent edges caused by context perturbation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-Interpretable Graph Neural Networks (SI-GNNs) produce explanations that can be self-inconsistent - when the model is reapplied to its own explanatory graph subset, it may generate different explanations. This inconsistency undermines the reliability of explanations, but the underlying causes remain poorly understood.

Method: 1) Identify re-explanation-induced context perturbation as the direct cause of score variation; 2) Introduce latent signal assignment hypothesis to explain why only some edges are sensitive to this perturbation; 3) Analyze how conciseness regularization affects latent signal assignment; 4) Propose Self-Denoising (SD) - a model-agnostic, training-free post-processing strategy that calibrates explanations with only one additional forward pass.

Result: Experiments across representative SI-GNN frameworks, backbone architectures, and benchmark datasets support the latent signal assignment hypothesis. Self-Denoising consistently improves explanation quality while adding only about 4-6% computational overhead in practice.

Conclusion: Self-inconsistent edges do not provide stable evidence for model predictions. Self-Denoising offers an efficient, model-agnostic solution to calibrate explanations by addressing context perturbation issues, enhancing the reliability of SI-GNN explanations with minimal computational cost.

Abstract: Recent work has observed that explanations produced by Self-Interpretable Graph Neural Networks (SI-GNNs) can be self-inconsistent: when the model is reapplied to its own explanatory graph subset, it may produce a different explanation. However, why self-inconsistency arises remains poorly understood. In this work, we first identify re-explanation-induced context perturbation as the direct cause of score variation. We then introduce a latent signal assignment hypothesis to explain why only some edges are sensitive to this perturbation, and analyze how conciseness regularization affects latent signal assignment. Given that self-inconsistent edges do not provide stable evidence for the model's prediction, we propose Self-Denoising (SD), a model-agnostic and training-free post-processing strategy that calibrates explanations with only one additional forward pass. Experiments across representative SI-GNN frameworks, backbone architectures, and benchmark datasets support our hypothesis and show that SD consistently improves explanation quality while adding only about 4--6\% computational overhead in practice.

</details>


### [229] [ProteinJEPA: Latent prediction complements protein language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07554)
*Dan Ofer,Dafna Shahaf,Michal Linial*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adding masked-position latent prediction (JEPA) to standard masked language modeling (MLM) outperforms pure MLM on many protein prediction tasks under matched computational budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protein language models primarily use masked language modeling (MLM) to predict amino acids. The authors investigate whether adding latent-space prediction objectives (JEPA) can complement token-level MLM predictions under the same computational constraints.

Method: Proposed "masked-position MLM+JEPA" which predicts latent targets only at masked positions while retaining the standard MLM cross-entropy loss. Compared this against: 1) pure MLM-only, 2) all-position MLM+JEPA, and 3) JEPA-only. Evaluated on pretrained (ESM2-35M/150M) and random-init models across 16 downstream tasks under matched wall-clock budgets.

Result: Masked-position MLM+JEPA outperformed pure MLM: 10 wins/3 losses/3 ties on ESM2-35M and 11/2/3 on ESM2-150M. Gains were consistent across 11 of 16 tasks including stability, fitness prediction, variant effect, disorder, homology, enzyme classification, and fold retrieval. All-position MLM+JEPA matched MLM-only but didn't show the same gains. JEPA-only collapsed in nearly all experiments.

Conclusion: JEPA combined with MLM (specifically at masked positions) is competitive and can outperform pure MLM in both pretraining and continued training, even under matched computational budgets. The specific design of where to apply latent prediction (masked positions only) is crucial for success.

Abstract: Protein language models are trained primarily with masked language modeling (MLM), which predicts amino-acid identities at masked positions. We ask whether latent-space prediction can complement these token-level objectives under matched wall-clock budget. Across pretrained and random-init protein sequence encoders at 35--150M parameters, we find that the best protein-JEPA design is not all-position latent prediction but a variant: predicting latent targets only at masked positions, and retaining the MLM cross-entropy. We call this recipe masked-position MLM+JEPA. On a 16-task downstream suite (15 frozen linear probes plus SCOPe-40 zero-shot fold retrieval), under matched wall-clock budgets, this recipe wins more tasks than it loses against MLM-only continuation: 10 wins / 3 losses / 3 ties (hereafter W/L/T) on pretrained ESM2-35M, 11/2/3 on ESM2-150M while results in pretraining from scratch are mixed (6/8/2). Gains are seen for multiple models on 11 of 16 tasks, including stability, \b{eta}β\b{eta}-lactamase fitness, variant effect, intrinsic disorder, remote homology, enzyme classification, and SCOPe-40 fold retrieval. Tasks with more losses than wins are Fluorescence (TAPE) and Peptide-HLA Binding. All-position MLM+JEPA matches MLM-only overall but does not reproduce the masked-position gains. JEPA-only (no MLM) collapses in nearly every experiment. We conclude that JEPA, when combined with MLM, is competitive and can outperform pure MLM in pretraining and continued training, even under matched wall-clock budgets.

</details>


### [230] [Beyond Linear Attention: Softmax Transformers Implement In-Context Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07333)
*Zixuan Xie,Xinyu Liu,Claire Chen,Shuze Daniel Liu,Rohan Chandra,Shangtong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First theoretical analysis of in-context reinforcement learning using practical softmax attention Transformers (not simplified linear attention), showing they implement weighted softmax temporal difference learning for policy evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing theoretical analyses of in-context RL rely on unrealistic linear attention simplifications, while practical systems use softmax attention. There's a gap in understanding how real Transformers with softmax attention achieve in-context RL.

Method: Theoretical analysis showing that Transformers with softmax attention can be mapped to iterative updates of a new weighted softmax TD learning algorithm. This algorithm generalizes both linear and tabular TD as special cases and performs policy evaluation in kernel space.

Result: Proved three key results: 1) Softmax attention Transformers implement weighted softmax TD learning, 2) Policy evaluation error decays with layer count under certain contraction conditions, 3) Identified parameters are global minimizers of pretraining loss, explaining their emergence empirically.

Conclusion: This work provides the first theoretical foundation for understanding how practical softmax attention Transformers achieve in-context RL, establishing connections between Transformer forward passes and reinforcement learning algorithms while explaining parameter emergence through optimization theory.

Abstract: In-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) studies agents that, after pretraining, adapt to new tasks by conditioning on additional context without parameter updates. Existing theoretical analyses of ICRL largely rely on linear attention, which replaces the softmax function in the standard attention with an identity mapping. This paper provides the first theoretical understanding of ICRL without making the unrealistic linear attention simplification. In particular, we consider the standard softmax attention used in practice. We show that, with certain parameters, the layerwise forward pass of a Transformer with such softmax attention is equivalent to iterative updates of a weighted softmax temporal difference (TD) learning algorithm. Here, weighted softmax TD is a new RL algorithm that performs policy evaluation in kernel space and adopts both linear TD and tabular TD as special cases. We also prove that under a certain contraction condition, the policy evaluation error decays as the number of layers grows, with the identified parameters above. Finally, we prove that those parameters are a global minimizer of a pretraining loss, explaining their emergence in our numerical experiments.

</details>


### [231] [Ensemble Distributionally Robust Bayesian Optimisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07565)
*Tigran Ramazyan,Denis Derkach*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel ensemble distributionally robust Bayesian optimization algorithm for zeroth-order optimization under context distributional uncertainty that is computationally tractable, handles continuous contexts, and provides improved theoretical regret bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bayesian optimization (BO) is commonly used for zeroth-order optimization under context distributional uncertainty, but real-world data is complex and noisy. While using ensemble surrogate models helps mitigate weaknesses of individual models, there's a need for robust algorithms that remain computationally tractable while handling continuous contexts.

Method: Proposed Ensemble Distributionally Robust Bayesian Optimization algorithm that employs ensemble surrogate models to address distributional uncertainty while maintaining computational tractability and supporting continuous context variables.

Result: Theoretical sublinear regret bounds that improve current state-of-the-art results, with empirical behavior aligning with theoretical guarantees. The algorithm remains computationally tractable while managing continuous context.

Conclusion: The proposed ensemble distributionally robust Bayesian optimization algorithm successfully addresses context distributional uncertainty with improved theoretical guarantees, computational efficiency, and empirical validation, advancing the state-of-the-art in robust zeroth-order optimization.

Abstract: We study zeroth-order optimisation under context distributional uncertainty, a setting commonly tackled using Bayesian optimisation (BO). A prevailing strategy to make BO more robust to the complex and noisy nature of data is to employ an ensemble as the surrogate model, thereby mitigating the weaknesses of any single model. In this study, we propose a novel algorithm for Ensemble Distributionally Robust Bayesian Optimisation that remains computationally tractable while managing continuous context. We obtain theoretical sublinear regret bounds, improving current state-of-the-art results. We show that our method's empirical behaviour aligns with its theoretical guarantees.

</details>


### [232] [CellScientist: Dual-Space Hierarchical Orchestration for Closed-Loop Refinement of Virtual Cell Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07335)
*Mengran Li,Bo Li,Jiaying Wang,Wenbin Xing,Yixuan Dong,Chengyang Zhang,Hongliang Zhang,Yuzhong Peng,Jinlin Wu,Bob Zhang,Bingo Wing-Kuen Ling,Fuji Yang,Zhen Lei,Jiebo Luo,Zelin Zang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CellScientist is a dual-space hierarchical framework that couples high-level hypothesis space with low-level executable implementation to solve refinement-routing problems in Virtual Cell Modeling, enabling targeted model revision through structured feedback loops.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-assisted modeling workflows face a refinement-routing problem where prediction discrepancies observed through executable implementations can originate from different levels (modeling assumption, representation design, implementation, or task constraint). Without structured feedback propagation across these levels, iterative refinement may fix code while failing to address the underlying faulty assumptions.

Method: CellScientist uses a dual-space hierarchical framework that couples a high-level hypothesis space with a low-level executable implementation space. It represents modeling decisions as structured states, realizes them as admissible programs under task and interface constraints, and routes execution discrepancies back to targeted hypothesis or implementation updates, creating a closed Hypothesis -> Implementation -> Hypothesis loop.
