### [258] [On the Tradeoffs of On-Device Generative Models in Federated Predictive Maintenance Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07860)
*Usevalad Milasheuski,Piero Baraldi,Enrico Zio,Stefano Savazzi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated generative models (VAEs, GANs, DMs) for anomaly detection in industrial IoT predictive maintenance, analyzing performance/communication trade-offs and proposing partial federation taxonomy for model personalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FL preserves data privacy in IoT environments but discriminative models dominate; generative models offer new opportunities for unsupervised anomaly detection in time series for predictive maintenance in critical industrial infrastructures.

Method: Comprehensive analysis of VAEs, GANs, and DMs in federated PdM contexts, comparing performance and communication overhead under full and partial federation setups (where only subsets of model components are shared), with experiments on real-world time series dataset.

Result: Distinct trade-offs in model utility, stability, and scalability in heterogeneous, bandwidth-constrained FL settings. Full federation improves GAN stability vs. independent local training, but GANs remain less robust than VAEs and DMs. Partial federation (especially decoder sharing) outperforms full federation for DMs in bandwidth-constrained, non-IID settings.

Conclusion: Proposes novel taxonomy for federated generative models formalizing partial component sharing as principled mechanism for model personalization, highlighting different federation strategies' suitability depending on model type and deployment constraints.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for preserving client data ownership and control over distributed Internet of Things (IoT) environments. While discriminative models dominate most FL use cases, recent advances in generative models -- such as Variational Autoencoders (VAE), Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN), and Diffusion Models (DM) -- offer new opportunities for unsupervised anomaly detection in time series analysis, with relevant applications in predictive maintenance (PdM) in critical industrial infrastructures. In this work, we present a comprehensive analysis of VAEs, GANs, and DMs in the context of federated PdM. We analyze their performance and communication overhead under both full and partial federation setups, where only subsets of model components are shared. Building on this analysis, the paper proposes a novel taxonomy for federated generative models that formalizes partial component sharing as a principled mechanism for model personalization. Our experiments over a real-world time series dataset reveal distinct trade-offs in model utility, stability, and scalability, especially in heterogeneous and bandwidth-constrained FL settings. For the evaluated GAN-based configurations, full federation improves training stability relative to independent local training, although the model remains less robust than the VAE- and DDPM-based alternatives. For DMs, however, partial federation -- especially decoder sharing -- can outperform full federation in bandwidth-constrained, non-IID settings.

</details>


### [259] [GameGen-Verifier: Parallel Keypoint-Based Verification for LLM-Generated Games via Runtime State Injection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07442)
*Chaobo Jia,Ruipeng Wan,Ting Sun,Weihao Tan,Borui Wan,Yuxuan Tong,Guangming Sheng,Hong Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GameGen-Verifier is an automated verification system for LLM-generated games that decomposes specifications into verifiable keypoints and tests them independently, achieving 92.2% accuracy and 16.6x speedup over agent-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based game generation lacks reliable automated verification because game correctness requires evaluating long-horizon interactions involving state updates, interaction rules, and phase transitions. Existing agent-based verification approaches are problematic due to being reachability-bound, time-consuming, coverage-limited, and sensitive to agent gameplay ability.

Method: GameGen-Verifier decomposes game specifications into verifiable keypoints and grounds them into independent verification units. Each unit patches the game runtime to a concrete target state, executes bounded interactions, and judges outcomes against keypoint assertions. GGV-Harness provides concurrency management, runtime isolation, and fault recovery.

Result: On the VeriGame dataset of 100 games across seven genres, GameGen-Verifier achieves up to 92.2% accuracy against human judgments, compared to 58.8% for coverage-enforced Agent-as-a-Verifier baselines, while reducing wall-clock time by up to 16.6x.

Conclusion: GameGen-Verifier provides a more effective and efficient automated verification paradigm for LLM-generated games by decomposing verification into targeted, bounded units rather than relying on open-ended gameplay, addressing core limitations of existing approaches.

Abstract: LLM-based game generation promises to turn natural-language specifications into executable games, but progress is limited by the lack of reliable automated verification. Unlike conventional code generation, game correctness is defined over long-horizon interaction: a game may appear correct while violating core mechanics such as state updates, interaction rules, and phase transitions. Existing Agent-as-a-Verifier approaches collapse verification into open-ended gameplay, making verdicts reachability-bound, time-consuming, coverage-limited, and sensitive to the agent's gameplay ability.
  We present GameGen-Verifier, an automated verification paradigm for LLM-generated games that decomposes a specification into verifiable keypoints and grounds them into independent verification units. Each unit patches the game runtime into a concrete target state, executes a bounded interaction, and judges the outcome against the keypoint assertion. We implement GGV-Harness, a scalable agentic harness providing concurrency management, runtime isolation, and fault recovery.
  On VeriGame, our dataset of 100 games across seven genres, GameGen-Verifier achieves up to 92.2% accuracy against human judgments versus 58.8% for the coverage-enforced Agent-as-a-Verifier baseline, while reducing wall-clock time by up to 16.6x.

</details>


### [260] [KL for a KL: On-Policy Distillation with Control Variate Baseline](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07865)
*Minjae Oh,Sangjun Song,Gyubin Choi,Yunho Choi,Yohan Jo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: vOPD introduces a control variate baseline (value function) to stabilize On-Policy Distillation by reducing gradient variance while maintaining an unbiased single-sample estimator, matching expensive full-vocabulary baselines at lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-Policy Distillation (OPD) is unstable in practice due to high gradient variance from single-sample Monte Carlo estimators, and existing stabilization methods are either computationally expensive (full-vocabulary reverse KL) or biased (top-k approximations).

Method: Propose vOPD which frames OPD as policy-gradient RL and introduces a control variate baseline (value function). The value function has closed form as per-token negative reverse KL divergence between student and teacher, requiring no extra computation. It subtracts this as a detached baseline to reduce variance while keeping gradient unbiased, with optional top-k approximation for efficiency.

Result: vOPD consistently outperforms vanilla OPD and matches the performance of expensive full-vocabulary baselines across mathematical and scientific reasoning benchmarks, while offering efficient stabilization through principled RL variance reduction.

Conclusion: vOPD provides an efficient, effective stabilization method for On-Policy Distillation by applying control variate baselines from RL theory, balancing computational efficiency with unbiased variance reduction for practical large language model training.

Abstract: On-Policy Distillation (OPD) has emerged as a dominant post-training paradigm for large language models, especially for reasoning domains. However, OPD remains unstable in practice due to the high gradient variance of its single-sample Monte Carlo estimator, and recipes for stable training are still immature. We propose vOPD (On-Policy Distillation with a control variate baseline), which casts OPD as policy-gradient RL and stabilizes it by introducing a control variate baseline-canonically a value function -- from the RL literature. We show that the OPD value function admits a closed form as the per-token negative reverse KL divergence between the student and the teacher, available directly from the already-computed forward pass with no additional critic or inference. Existing stabilization methods either compute the full token-level reverse KL over the entire vocabulary, adding significant overhead, or restrict it to a top-k support, biasing the objective. vOPD instead preserves the lightweight single-sample estimator, subtracting the value function as a detached baseline to keep the gradient unbiased while reducing variance. Furthermore, we show that a top-k approximation of the baseline further lowers cost without compromising performance. Across mathematical and scientific reasoning benchmarks, vOPD consistently outperforms vanilla OPD and matches the most expensive full-vocabulary baseline, offering an efficient stabilization of On-Policy Distillation through principled RL variance reduction.

</details>


### [261] [VNN-LIB 2.0: Rigorous Foundations for Neural Network Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07451)
*Ann Roy,Allen Antony,Andrea Gimelli,Matthew L. Daggitt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VNN-LIB 2.0 provides rigorous theoretical foundations for neural network verification, addressing serious shortcomings in Version 1.0 through formal syntax, semantics, type systems, and Agda mechanization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VNN-LIB 1.0 has serious shortcomings as a formal foundation: lacks precise syntax/semantics/type system, limited expressivity, relies on externally defined ONNX models with informal and evolving semantics, making it unlike established standards like SMT-LIB.

Method: Introduced the notion of "network theory" to abstractly characterize the minimal semantic interface required from neural network models. Built formal syntax for a more expressive query language, developed a type system over numeric domains from network theory, created formal semantics, and mechanized the entire standard in Agda theorem prover.

Result: Developed VNN-LIB 2.0 with robust theoretical foundations that are independent of specific ONNX versions while remaining compatible with evolving model representations. Provides a formal, self-contained standard for trustworthy neural network verification.

Conclusion: VNN-LIB 2.0 addresses the foundational problems of Version 1.0 by providing rigorous theoretical underpinnings through network theory abstraction, formal language constructs, and Agda mechanization, establishing a trustworthy standard for neural network verification ecosystem.

Abstract: Neural network verification is an active and rapidly maturing research area, with a growing ecosystem of solvers and tools. The VNN-LIB standard was introduced to support interoperability in this ecosystem, but Version~1.0 has several serious short-comings as a formal foundation: it lacks a precise syntax, semantics, and type system, offers limited expressivity, and relies on externally defined ONNX models whose semantics are informal and constantly evolving. The latter distinguishes VNN-LIB from established standards such as SMT-LIB, where queries are self-contained and have fixed semantics.
  In this paper we address these challenges by developing the theoretical foundations of VNN-LIB~2.0. Our key contribution is the introduction of the notion of a \emph{network theory}, which abstractly characterises the minimal semantic interface required from a neural network model format. This abstraction enables VNN-LIB to be defined independently of any specific ONNX version while remaining compatible with evolving model representations. Building on this foundation, we present a formal syntax for a more expressive query language, a type system for it over the numeric domains provided by the network theory, and finally a formal semantics. To ensure internal consistency, the standard is mechanised in the Agda theorem prover. VNN-LIB~2.0 therefore provides robust and rigorous foundations for trustworthy neural network verification.

</details>


### [262] [Trajectory as the Teacher: Few-Step Discrete Flow Matching via Energy-Navigated Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07924)
*Amin Karimi Monsefi,Dominic Culver,Nikhil Bhendawade,Manuel R. Ciosici,Yizhe Zhang,Irina Belousova*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TS-DFM improves discrete flow matching for text generation by replacing blind stochastic jumps with guided navigation during training, allowing a small student model to outperform a much larger teacher while being significantly faster.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses a key limitation in distillation for discrete flow matching. When student models underperform, conventional wisdom blames insufficient capacity, but the authors argue the trajectory is actually the bottleneck. Each training trajectory is built through blind stochastic jumps without quality evaluation, causing early bad decisions to propagate through subsequent steps, yet students must still imitate these flawed results.

Method: TS-DFM (Trajectory-Shaped Discrete Flow Matching) replaces blind jumps with guided navigation using a lightweight "energy compass" that evaluates candidate continuations at each midpoint, selecting the most coherent ones. This shaping occurs only during training, so inference cost remains unchanged while training quality improves dramatically.

Result: On 170M-parameter language modeling, the shaped student at just 8 steps achieves 32% lower perplexity than the 1,024-step teacher while being 128x faster. Gains are consistent across source distributions and three evaluators of increasing scale. TS-DFM achieves the best perplexity of any discrete-generation baseline, outperforming methods trained on 6x more data or using 5x larger models.

Conclusion: The trajectory, not the student capacity, is the key bottleneck in discrete flow matching distillation. By replacing blind stochastic jumps with guided navigation during training, TS-DFM enables small models to dramatically outperform their teachers while maintaining efficient inference, challenging the conventional wisdom about model capacity limitations in this domain.

Abstract: Discrete flow matching generates text by iteratively transforming noise tokens into coherent language, but may require hundreds of forward passes. Distillation uses the multi-step trajectory to train a student to reproduce the process in a few steps. When the student underperforms, the usual explanation is insufficient capacity. We argue the opposite: the trajectory is the bottleneck, not the student. Each training trajectory is built through a chain of blind stochastic jumps with no evaluation of sequence quality; a single bad decision at an early midpoint propagates through subsequent steps, yet the student must imitate the result. Trajectory-Shaped Discrete Flow Matching (TS-DFM) replaces these blind jumps with guided navigation: a lightweight energy compass evaluates candidate continuations at each midpoint, selecting the most coherent. All shaping is training-only; inference cost is unchanged. On 170M-parameter language modeling, the shaped student at 8 steps achieves 32% lower perplexity than the 1,024-step teacher while being 128x faster, with gains consistent across source distributions and three evaluators of increasing scale. TS-DFM achieves the best perplexity of any discrete-generation baseline we compare against, including methods trained on 6x more data or using 5x larger models.

</details>


### [263] [Inference-Time Attribute Distribution Alignment for Unconditional Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07456)
*Hao Luan,See-Kiong Ng,Chun Kai Ling*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A plug-and-play method for inference-time attribute distributional alignment in unconditional diffusion models using optimal control theory to match population-level attribute distributions without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inference-time controllable generation is crucial for real-world applications, but most existing techniques focus on individual samples rather than ensuring sample populations follow specific attribute distributions (like demographic balance or semantic proportions).

Method: Formalizes inference-time attribute distributional alignment as an optimal control problem over reverse diffusion processes. Treats the diffusion process as a dynamical system and augments it with time-dependent additive perturbations as control. Solves for perturbations using optimal-control-based algorithm optimizing differentiable distribution-matching objective while penalizing control effort to preserve data fidelity.

Result: Experiments in image generation demonstrate the proposed approach can better align attribute distributions to diverse and flexible test-time targets compared to baselines, without requiring retraining or finetuning of pretrained diffusion models.

Conclusion: The optimal-control-based approach provides an effective plug-and-play solution for inference-time attribute distributional alignment in unconditional diffusion models, enabling population-level distribution control while maintaining data quality.

Abstract: Inference-time controllable generation is essential for real-world applications of unconditional diffusion models. However, most existing techniques focus on individual samples, struggling in applications that require the sample population to follow specific attribute distributions (e.g., demographic balance or semantic proportions). We formalize this setting as the inference-time attribute distributional alignment problem for pretrained unconditional diffusion models. To address this, we cast inference-time attribute distributional alignment as an optimal control problem over the reverse diffusion process, viewing the process as the rollout of a dynamical system and augmenting it with additive, time-dependent perturbations as control. We solve for the perturbations using an optimal-control-based algorithm to optimize a differentiable distribution-matching objective while penalizing control effort to preserve data fidelity. Experiment results in image generation demonstrate that our proposed plug-and-play approach can better align attribute distributions to diverse and flexible test-time targets compared to baselines, without retraining or finetuning the pretrained diffusion model.

</details>


### [264] [INO-SGD: Addressing Utility Imbalance under Individualized Differential Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07930)
*Xiao Tian,Jue Fan,Rachael Hwee Ling Sim,Bryan Kian Hsiang Low*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces INO-SGD, a differentially private algorithm that addresses utility imbalance in individualized DP where data owners set their own privacy requirements, ensuring better performance for more private data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As data owners gain more control over their data and set individualized privacy requirements (IDP), existing approaches create utility imbalance - data with stronger privacy requirements become underrepresented in trained models, leading to poorer performance on similar data during deployment. This is particularly problematic for sensitive subsets like stigmatized disease data where stronger privacy is needed.

Method: The authors propose INO-SGD algorithm that strategically down-weights data within each training batch across all iterations to improve performance on more private data. The algorithm is specially designed to satisfy individualized DP requirements while addressing the utility imbalance problem.

Result: The paper demonstrates empirical feasibility of the INO-SGD approach, showing it can effectively handle individualized DP requirements while mitigating the utility imbalance problem that affects existing methods.

Conclusion: INO-SGD provides a practical solution to the utility imbalance problem in individualized DP settings, enabling fair representation and performance for data with varying privacy requirements while maintaining proper privacy guarantees.

Abstract: Differential privacy (DP) is widely employed in machine learning to protect confidential or sensitive training data from being revealed. As data owners gain greater control over their data due to personal data ownership, they are more likely to set their own privacy requirements, necessitating individualized DP (IDP) to fulfil such requests. In particular, owners of data from more sensitive subsets, such as positive cases of stigmatized diseases, likely set stronger privacy requirements, as leakage of such data could incur more serious societal impact. However, existing IDP algorithms induce a critical utility imbalance problem: Data from owners with stronger privacy requirements may be severely underrepresented in the trained model, resulting in poorer performance on similar data from subsequent users during deployment. In this paper, we analyze this problem and propose the INO-SGD algorithm, which strategically down-weights data within each batch to improve performance on the more private data across all iterations. Notably, our algorithm is specially designed to satisfy IDP, while existing techniques addressing utility imbalance neither satisfy IDP nor can be easily adapted to do so. Lastly, we demonstrate the empirical feasibility of our approach.

</details>


### [265] [Estimation of Motor Unit Parameters from Surface Electromyograms using an Informed Autoencoder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07458)
*Kaja Balzereit,Malte Mechtenberg,Axel Schneider*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An informed autoencoder is developed to simultaneously estimate multiple subject-specific motor unit parameters (innervation zone centers and conduction velocities) from non-invasive EMG recordings, combining data-driven ML with physical constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motor unit parameters can improve neuromechanical models for movement and force prediction, but non-invasive determination is challenging due to subject-specific variations and changes with muscle contraction. Existing methods rely on white-box modeling requiring substantial manual effort.

Method: Developed an informed autoencoder that reconstructs surface EMG recordings while learning motor unit parameters in its latent space. The model adheres to physical laws relating parameters to EMG signals, addressing an inverse problem with nonlinear loss function.

Result: On synthetic data: innervation zone centers estimated with mean absolute error of 2.5989 mm, and conduction velocities estimated with mean absolute error of 0.1697 m/s. Results demonstrate plausibility of the approach.

Conclusion: This novel approach enables simultaneous estimation of several motor unit parameters while reducing manual modeling effort through integration of data-driven machine learning, showing promise for improving neuromechanical models non-invasively.

Abstract: Motor unit parameters such as the innervation zone centre or the conduction velocity of the electrical potential harbour the potential to improve the fidelity of neuromechanical models used for movement and force prediction. Determining these parameters in a non-invasive way is challenging, as they are subject-specific and may vary with muscle contraction. Existing work on the estimation of motor unit parameters mainly relies on white-box modelling and therefore requires substantial manual modelling effort. This work targets the simultaneous estimation of multiple subject-specific motor unit parameters from electromyography (EMG) recordings measured non-invasively at the skin surface. This results in an inverse problem with a nonlinear loss function. To address this problem, an informed autoencoder is developed. This autoencoder reconstructs the surface EMG recordings while learning the parameters in its latent space and adhering to physical laws that relate the parameters to the EMG signals. In experiments on synthetic data, innervation zone centres are estimated with a mean absolute error of 2.5989 $\mathrm{mm}$, and conduction velocities of the electric potential are estimated with a mean absolute error of 0.1697 $\mathrm{m}\mathrm{s}^{-1}$. These results demonstrate the plausibility of this novel approach, which enables the simultaneous estimation of several motor unit parameters while reducing manual modelling effort through the integration of data-driven machine learning.

</details>


### [266] [It Just Takes Two: Scaling Amortized Inference to Large Sets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07972)
*Antoine Wehenkel,Michael Kagan,Lukas Heinrich,Chris Pollard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural posterior estimation for set-based inference made scalable through decoupled representation learning and posterior modeling, enabling efficient training for large deployment sets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural posterior estimation is widely used for amortized inference in scientific applications where conditioning variables are sets of observations. However, optimal inference requires joint treatment of the entire set, forcing training at deployment set sizes where memory and compute become prohibitive due to the combinatorial complexity of handling large observation sets.

Method: A decoupled approach that separates representation learning from posterior modeling. A mean-pool Deep Set encoder is trained on sets of size at most two, creating an encoder that generalizes to arbitrary set sizes. The inference head is then finetuned on pre-aggregated embeddings, making training cost essentially independent of deployment set size N.

Result: The approach matches or outperforms standard baselines across diverse benchmarks (scalar, image, multi-view 3D, molecular, and high-dimensional conditional generation) with N in the thousands, while requiring only a fraction of the computational resources compared to traditional methods.

Conclusion: The proposed decoupling strategy provides a theoretically grounded, computationally efficient solution for neural posterior estimation with large observation sets, addressing a major scalability bottleneck while maintaining or improving performance across various application domains.

Abstract: Neural posterior estimation has emerged as a powerful tool for amortized inference, with growing adoption across scientific and applied domains. In many of these applications, the conditioning variable is a set of observations whose elements depend not only on the target but also on unknown factors shared across the set. Optimal inference therefore requires treating the set jointly, which in turn requires training the estimator at the deployment set size -- a regime where memory and compute quickly become prohibitive. We introduce a simple, theoretically grounded strategy that decouples representation learning from posterior modeling. Our method trains a mean-pool Deep Set on sets of size at most two, producing an encoder that generalizes to arbitrary set sizes. The inference head is then finetuned on pre-aggregated embeddings, making training cost essentially independent of the deployment set size N. Across scalar, image, multi-view 3D, molecular, and high-dimensional conditional generation benchmarks with N in the thousands, our approach matches or outperforms standard baselines at a fraction of the compute.

</details>


### [267] [Learning Minimal-Deviation Corrections for Multi-Dimensional Mismodelling in HEP Simulations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07460)
*Matthias Schott,Lucie Flek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A neural network method for correcting Monte Carlo simulations in high-energy physics when only 1D distributions are available, preserving multidimensional correlations while improving agreement with target data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monte Carlo simulations in high-energy physics often fail to match observed data, especially in complex scenarios. Traditional correction methods are limited because 1D reweighting ignores correlations, while full multidimensional approaches require large target datasets that aren't typically available.

Method: The proposed method uses neural networks to learn a transformation of simulated events that reproduces available 1D target distributions while remaining close to the original simulation (minimal-deviation principle). This preserves the global correlation structure of the baseline model while enabling targeted corrections of mismodelled features.

Result: Controlled studies with simulated pseudo-data show that the method improves agreement with target distributions while maintaining consistent multidimensional structure. The approach works effectively under the constraints of limited 1D information.

Conclusion: This neural network-based method provides a scalable solution for enhancing Monte Carlo modelling in complex, high-dimensional analyses where traditional techniques are insufficient, especially when only 1D distributions are available for correction.

Abstract: Accurate Monte Carlo (MC) modelling in high-energy physics is challenging, particularly in complex scenarios where simulations fail to reproduce observed data. In practice, experimental information is often limited to one-dimensional (1D) distributions, while mismodelling arises in a multidimensional feature space. This restricts traditional correction methods, as one-dimensional reweighting ignores correlations and fully multidimensional approaches require large target datasets. We propose a neural network-based method that operates under these constraints by learning a transformation of simulated events that reproduces the available 1D target distributions while remaining close to the original simulation. This minimal-deviation principle preserves the global correlation structure of the baseline model while enabling targeted corrections of mismodelled features. Using controlled studies with simulated pseudo-data, we show that the method improves agreement with target distributions and maintains a consistent multidimensional structure. The approach is designed for complex, high-dimensional analyses where traditional techniques are insufficient, providing a scalable way to enhance MC modelling under limited information.

</details>


### [268] [Where's the Plan? Locating Latent Planning in Language Models with Lightweight Mechanistic Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07984)
*Nicole Ma,Nick Rui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Language models can internally represent future constraints like rhyming words during text generation, but only one specific model (Gemma-3-27B) causally uses this information, showing a unique "handoff" mechanism where causal control shifts from rhyme word to line boundary during generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how language models internally represent and use forward-looking constraints during text generation, specifically examining whether they form internal representations of structurally-constrained future tokens and whether these representations causally drive generation.

Method: Used rhyming-couplet completion as a clean test of forward-looking constraints. Applied two lightweight methods: linear probing to detect future-rhyme information in hidden states, and activation patching to test causal influence. Tested Qwen3, Gemma-3, and Llama-3 families across more than ten model scales.

Result: Future-rhyme information is linearly decodable at line boundaries in all models, with signal strengthening with model scale. However, only Gemma-3-27B causally relies on this encoding, showing a "handoff" where causal driver migrates from rhyme word to line boundary around layer 30. Other models condition on rhyme word throughout generation, with near-zero causal effect at line boundary despite strong probe signal. The Gemma-3-27B handoff was localized to five attention heads through path patching.

Conclusion: Language models internally encode forward-looking constraints like rhyme information, but only certain models (like Gemma-3-27B) causally use this information in a sophisticated way with a migration of causal control. Strong linear probe signals don't necessarily indicate causal usage, revealing a dissociation between information encoding and causal mechanism usage in most models.

Abstract: We study planning site formation in language models -- where internal representations of structurally-constrained future tokens form during the forward pass, and whether they causally drive generation. Using rhyming-couplet completion as a clean test of forward-looking constraint, we apply two lightweight methods (linear probing and activation patching) across Qwen3, Gemma-3, and Llama-3 at more than ten scales. Probing shows that future-rhyme information is linearly decodable at the line boundary, with signal that strengthens with scale in all three families. Activation patching reveals that only Gemma-3-27B causally relies on this encoding, exhibiting a handoff in which the causal driver migrates from the rhyme word to the line boundary around layer 30. Every other model we test conditions on the rhyme word throughout generation, with near-zero causal effect at the line boundary despite strong probe signal. We localize the Gemma-3-27B handoff to five attention heads through two-stage path patching that recover ~90% of the rhyme-routing capacity at the newline.

</details>


### [269] [Approximation Error Upper and Lower Bounds for Hölder Class with Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07463)
*Xin He,Yuling Jiao,Xiliang Lu,Jerry Zhijian Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformers can approximate Hölder functions with O(ε^{-d₀/α}) blocks upper bound, require Ω(ε^{-d₀/(4α)}) blocks lower bound, with extensions to regression tasks showing empirical effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish precise approximation error bounds for Transformers on Hölder functions, understanding their expressive power and theoretical limits, with practical implications for real-world regression tasks.

Method: Mathematical analysis of standard Transformer architecture (Softmax, ReLU, residual connections) to derive approximation upper bounds via network construction, and lower bounds using VC-dimension theory.

Result: Upper bound: O(ε^{-d₀/α}) blocks sufficient for ε-approximation of d₀-dimensional α-smooth Hölder functions. Lower bound: Ω(ε^{-d₀/(4α)}) blocks necessary for same accuracy. Results extend to regression with excess risk rates.

Conclusion: Transformers have quantifiable approximation power for Hölder functions with tight bounds, providing theoretical justification for their empirical success and practical utility in regression tasks.

Abstract: We explore the expressive power of Transformers by establishing precise approximation error upper and lower bounds for Hölder class. Specifically, a new approximation upper bound is derived for the standard Transformer architecture equipped with Softmax operators, ReLU activation functions, and residual connections. We prove that a Transformer network composed of at most $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-{d_{0}}/α})$ blocks can approximate any bounded Hölder function with $d_{0}$-dimensional input and smoothness $α\in(0,1]$ under any accuracy $\varepsilon>0$. In the case of approximation lower bounds, leveraging the VC-dimension upper bound, we are the first to rigorously prove that Transformers demand for at least $Ω(\varepsilon^{-{d_{0}}/({4α})})$ blocks to achieve the $\varepsilon$ approximation accuracy. As a final step, we extend the derived results for standard Transformers to a general regression task and establish the corresponding excess risk rates demonstrating Transformers' empirical effectiveness in real-world settings.

</details>


### [270] [Graph-Structured Hyperdimensional Computing for Data-Efficient and Explainable Process-Structure-Property Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07999)
*Jingzhan Ge,Ajeeth Vellore,Ajinkya Palwe,Ahsan Khan,David Gorsich,Matthew P. Castanier,SeungYeon Kang,Farhad Imani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PSP-HDC is a graph-structured hyperdimensional computing framework that encodes process-structure-property relationships to predict and explain fabrication outcomes for complex 3D microstructures, outperforming conventional methods in sparse, heterogeneous data regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome limitations in predicting process-structure-property (PSP) relationships for complex 3D microfabrication. Conventional models fail in sparse, heterogeneous, interaction-dominated data regimes due to statistical underdetermination, spurious correlations, poor transfer, and unstable explanations, while mechanistic approaches require calibrated submodels unavailable during early development.

Method: PSP-HDC uses graph-structured hyperdimensional computing to encode directed PSP graphs as internal priors. It employs a trainable scalar-to-hypervector encoder for parameter-specific embeddings on a fixed hyperdimensional basis, then composes sample representations through graph-aligned binding and bundling along PSP dependencies. Prediction uses associative-memory retrieval against class prototypes.

Result: On sheet-resistance regime prediction for 3D microfabrication, PSP-HDC achieves 0.910 ± 0.077 accuracy over 1000 random splits and 0.896 accuracy under process-fold generalization, outperforming strong baselines.

Conclusion: PSP-HDC provides a robust framework for PSP prediction in sparse, heterogeneous data regimes, offering both accurate predictions and intrinsic, multi-level explanations through unified prototype memories that support decision making and attribution.

Abstract: Multiphoton photoreduction enables high-fidelity fabrication of complex 3D microstructures, yet reliable process-structure-property (PSP) prediction remains difficult because the available data are sparse, heterogeneous, and interaction-dominated. In this regime, conventional feature-vector models are statistically underdetermined, making them prone to spurious correlations, poor regime transfer, and unstable post hoc explanations, whereas mechanistic pipelines depend on calibrated submodels that are rarely available during early process development. We present PSP-HDC, a graph-structured hyperdimensional computing framework that encodes a directed PSP graph as an internal prior for representation, inference, and explanation. A trainable scalar-to-hypervector encoder learns parameter-specific embeddings on a fixed hyperdimensional basis to accommodate heterogeneous scales and noise. Sample representations are then composed through graph-aligned binding and bundling along directed PSP dependencies, and prediction is performed by associative-memory retrieval against class prototypes. Because the same prototype memories support both decision making and attribution, PSP-HDC provides intrinsic explanations at the parameter, group, and within-group levels, while memory alignment and separation quantify prototype formation during training. On sheet-resistance regime prediction for the 3D platform, PSP-HDC achieves an accuracy of 0.910 +/- 0.077 over 1000 random splits and 0.896 under process-fold generalization, outperforming strong baselines.

</details>


### [271] [Position: Mechanistic Interpretability Must Disclose Identification Assumptions for Causal Claims](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08012)
*Zezheng Lin,Fengming Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper audits mechanistic interpretability papers for proper causal identification assumptions and finds they're consistently missing, proposing a disclosure norm for causal claims.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mechanistic interpretability research increasingly makes causal claims (circuits, mediators, etc.) but these require explicit identification assumptions that aren't being properly addressed in published work.

Method: Conducted a purposive audit of 10 papers across four methodological strands, plus a two-human-coder audit on n=30 papers, analyzing how causal claims are made and whether proper identification assumptions are stated.

Result: Found no dedicated identification-assumptions sections in the audited papers; validation metrics (faithfulness, completeness, etc.) are commonly reported as causal support without stating the assumptions that make them identifying. The pattern holds across multiple coders.

Conclusion: Proposes a disclosure norm requiring researchers to: state whether claims are causal, name identification strategies, enumerate assumptions, stress at least one assumption, and explain how conclusions shift if assumptions fail. Emphasizes that validation is not the same as identification.

Abstract: Mechanistic interpretability papers increasingly use causal vocabulary: circuits, mediators, causal abstraction, monosemanticity. Such claims require explicit identification assumptions. A purposive audit of 10 papers across four methodological strands finds no dedicated identification-assumptions section and a recurring pattern: validation metrics such as faithfulness, completeness, monosemanticity, alignment, or ablation effects are reported as causal support without stating the assumptions that make them identifying. A two-human-coder audit on $n=30$ reproduces the direction of the main finding: dedicated identification sections are absent, and validation-metric substitution is common, though exact Dim B/D counts are coding-rule sensitive. The paper proposes a disclosure norm: state whether the claim is causal, name the identification strategy, enumerate assumptions, stress at least one, and explain how conclusions shift if assumptions fail. Validation is not identification.

</details>


### [272] [Uncovering Hidden Systematics in Neural Network Models for High Energy Physics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07470)
*Lucie Flek,Philipp Alexander Jungs,Akbar Karimi,Timo Saala,Alexander Schmid,Matthias Schott,Philipp Soldin,Christopher Wiebusch,Ulrich Willemsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural networks in high-energy physics analyses can be systematically "fooled" by subtle input perturbations within experimental uncertainty bounds, revealing hidden sensitivity to systematic variations that traditional uncertainty estimation methods may underestimate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Systematic uncertainty estimation in neural network-based models for high-energy physics analyses remains challenging. Traditional methods using control regions or nominal feature variations may underestimate true model uncertainty, potentially leaving unaccounted biases that could impact physics results.

Method: The paper draws inspiration from adversarial-attack studies in machine learning to explore how subtle perturbations within experimental uncertainty bounds can affect neural network outputs. Using representative HEP tasks (event classification, object identification) across various network architectures, the researchers test how networks can be systematically "fooled" while keeping input distributions nearly unchanged.

Result: The study demonstrates that neural networks can be systematically "fooled" at significant rates within allowed uncertainty envelopes. Networks show substantial sensitivity to realistic experimental variations that traditional uncertainty estimation methods might miss, revealing hidden vulnerabilities to systematic perturbations.

Conclusion: The paper introduces a quantitative framework to probe and measure neural networks' hidden sensitivity to realistic experimental variations, providing a practical approach to evaluate and control systematic uncertainties in physics analyses, addressing a critical challenge in using neural networks for high-energy physics research.

Abstract: Neural networks (NNs) are inherently multidimensional classifiers that learn complex, non-linear relationships among input observables. While their flexibility enables unprecedented performance in high-energy physics (HEP) analyses, it also makes them sensitive to small variations in their inputs. Consequently, the propagation and estimation of systematic uncertainties in NN-based models remain an open challenge. There are indications that uncertainties derived in control regions or from nominal variations of input features can underestimate the true model uncertainty, potentially leaving biases unaccounted for. Inspired by insights from adversarial-attack studies in machine learning, we explore how subtle perturbations, fully consistent with the experimental uncertainties on the input observables, can lead to substantial changes in NN outputs, while keeping the one-dimensional and correlated input distributions nearly unchanged. Using a set of representative HEP tasks, including event classification and object identification, and testing across a variety of network architectures, we demonstrate that networks can be systematically "fooled" at significant rates within the allowed uncertainty envelopes. Building on this observation, we introduce a quantitative framework to probe and measure the hidden sensitivity of neural networks to realistic experimental variations, providing a practical path to evaluate and control their systematic uncertainty in physics analyses.

</details>


### [273] [Beyond Pairs: Your Language Model is Secretly Optimizing a Preference Graph](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08037)
*Ning Liu,Chuanneng Sun,Kristina Klinkner,Shervin Malmasi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphDPO generalizes DPO to work with directed acyclic preference graphs from multiple rollouts per prompt, handling rich preference structures more effectively than pairwise comparisons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard DPO only uses pairwise comparisons, but in practice training data often has multiple rollouts per prompt with rich preference structures. Collapsing this into independent pairs discards transitivity, introduces redundant/conflicting supervision, and can cause unstable optimization.

Method: Proposes GraphDPO that operates over directed acyclic preference graphs induced by rollout rankings, encodes dominance relations as edges, and optimizes a graph-structured Plackett-Luce-inspired objective that aggregates supervision over graph neighborhoods while enforcing transitivity. Uses equivalence-class construction for discrete/sparse signals and optional ground-truth anchoring with annealed scheduling.

Result: Superior performance on reasoning and program synthesis tasks compared to pairwise and listwise alignment objectives, with linear per-prompt complexity via efficient log-sum-exp aggregation.

Conclusion: Graph-structured preference modeling offers a scalable and robust alternative to pairwise and listwise alignment objectives, better exploiting rich preference structures in practical training data.

Abstract: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) aligns language models using pairwise preference comparisons, offering a simple and effective alternative to Reinforcement Learning (RL) from human feedback. However, in many practical settings, training data consists of multiple rollouts per prompt, inducing rich preference structure that pairwise DPO fails to exploit. Collapsing such data into independent pairs discards transitivity, introduces redundant or conflicting supervision, and can lead to unstable optimization. We propose Graph Direct Preference Optimization (GraphDPO), a principled generalization of DPO that operates over directed acyclic preference graphs induced by rollout rankings. GraphDPO encodes dominance relations as edges and optimizes a graph-structured Plackett--Luce-inspired objective that aggregates supervision over graph neighborhoods, enforcing transitivity while recovering standard DPO as a special case. To handle discrete or sparse signals, we introduce an equivalence-class construction where responses with identical preferences form graph layers, and intra-layer edges contribute zero loss, preventing spurious gradients. Despite leveraging full graph structure, GraphDPO maintains linear per-prompt complexity via efficient log-sum-exp aggregation. We further incorporate optional ground-truth anchoring by inserting verified solutions as dominant nodes and applying an annealed schedule that stabilizes early training while gradually relaxing oracle supervision. Experiments on reasoning and program synthesis tasks demonstrate superior performance, suggesting that graph-structured preference modeling is a scalable and robust alternative to pairwise and listwise alignment objectives.

</details>


### [274] [Transfer Learning Across Fast- and Full-Simulation Domains in High-Energy Physics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07471)
*Matthias Schott,Lucie Flek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transfer learning from fast-simulated to fully simulated datasets in high-energy physics reduces needed training data by ~2x while improving performance across multiple LHC analysis tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine-learning models in HEP rely on simulated data, but full simulation is computationally expensive while fast simulation lacks realism. There's a need to bridge this gap and enable knowledge transfer between different simulation types and experiments.

Method: Systematically studied transfer learning between fast-simulated and fully simulated datasets using three LHC tasks: signal-background classification, quark-gluon jet tagging, and missing transverse energy reconstruction. Used DNNs, GNNs, and transformers, pretrained on ATLAS-like fast simulation and adapted to CMS-like fast simulation and ATLAS Open Data full simulation.

Result: Pretrained models consistently outperformed independently trained baselines across all tasks and required significantly less target-domain training data (typically reducing needed statistics by about a factor of two). Fast simulation learned robust, reusable representations.

Conclusion: Fast simulation can be used to learn transferable representations that improve performance on full simulation tasks, motivating the publication of trained models as reusable scientific assets beyond just large foundation models.

Abstract: Machine-learning models in high-energy physics are often trained on simulated data, where fully simulated samples are computationally expensive while fast simulation provides large statistics at reduced realism. In this work, we systematically study transfer learning between fast-simulated and fully simulated datasets in a realistic LHC environment. We consider three representative tasks, signal-background classification, quark-gluon jet tagging, and missing transverse energy reconstruction, using dense neural networks, graph neural networks, and transformer-based architectures. Models are pretrained on ATLAS-like fast simulation and adapted to CMS-like fast simulation and to fully simulated ATLAS Open Data. Across all tasks, pretrained models consistently outperform independently trained baselines and require significantly less target-domain training data, typically reducing the needed statistics by about a factor of two. These results demonstrate that fast simulation can be used to learn robust, reusable representations and motivate publishing trained models as reusable scientific assets beyond large foundation models.

</details>


### [275] [NPMixer: Hierarchical Neighboring Patch Mixing for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07476)
*Jung Min Choi,Vijaya Krishna Yalavarthi,Lars Schmidt-Thieme*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NPMixer: A hierarchical multivariate time series forecasting model using learnable wavelet transforms and MLP-based patching for capturing local-global dependencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multivariate time series forecasting is challenging due to the complexity of local temporal dynamics and global dependencies across multiple variables. Existing methods struggle to effectively capture both local and global patterns simultaneously.

Method: Proposes NPMixer with Learnable Stationary Wavelet Transform to adaptively decompose signals into trend/detail components. Uses Neighboring Mixer Block with hierarchical MLP layers on non-overlapping patches to capture local temporal dynamics and multi-scale dependencies. Applies Channel-Mixing Encoder to high-frequency components for channel correlation learning while preserving global trend stability.

Result: Extensive experiments on 7 benchmark datasets show NPMixer consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models, achieving better performance in 20 out of 28 (71.4%) evaluated experimental setups for MSE.

Conclusion: NPMixer provides an effective hierarchical architecture for multivariate time series forecasting that successfully captures both local temporal dynamics and global dependencies through its novel learnable wavelet transform and patching mixer approach.

Abstract: Multivariate time series forecasting remains a challenge due to the complexity of local temporal dynamics and global dependencies across multiple variables.
  In this paper, we propose \textbf{N}eighboring \textbf{P}atching \textbf{Mixer} (\textbf{NPMixer}), a hierarchical architecture featuring a Learnable Stationary Wavelet Transform that adaptively learns filter coefficients to decompose signals into trend and detail components in a data-dependent manner.
  Our framework introduces a Neighboring Mixer Block that captures local temporal dynamics through a series of hierarchical MLP layers operating on non-overlapping patches.
  Specifically, the mixer block utilizes MLPs to learn temporal patterns within and across these patches, expanding the receptive field to capture multi-scale dependencies.
  A Channel-Mixing Encoder is applied to high-frequency components to learn channel correlations while preserving the stability of the underlying global trend.
  Extensive experiments on seven benchmark datasets demonstrate that NPMixer consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models, achieving better performance in 20 out of 28 ($71.4\%$) evaluated experimental setups for MSE.

</details>


### [276] [ExpThink: Experience-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Chain-of-Thought Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07501)
*Tingcheng Bian,Yuzhe Zhang,Jing Jin,Jinchang Luo,MingQuan Cheng,Haiwei Wang,Wenyuan Jiang,Miaohui Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ExpThink is an RL framework that compresses chain-of-thought reasoning by automatically learning optimal solution length based on both model capability dynamics and problem difficulty, achieving significant length reduction (up to 77%) while maintaining or improving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large reasoning models using chain-of-thought reasoning suffer from excessive token consumption and high inference latency. Existing RL approaches for CoT compression use uniform, static length penalties that fail to account for model capability changes over time and variations in problem difficulty.

Method: ExpThink uses two complementary RL mechanisms: 1) Experience-guided reward shaping that tracks shortest correct solutions per problem and applies a three-tier reward system that tightens automatically as models improve, creating a self-evolving curriculum. 2) Difficulty-adaptive advantage that uses correct-count normalization instead of standard deviation normalization, producing difficulty-scaled gradients that emphasize learning on hard problems while suppressing gradients on easy ones.

Result: On multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks, ExpThink reduces average response length by up to 77% while simultaneously improving accuracy. It achieves up to 3× higher accuracy-efficiency ratio (accuracy divided by average token count) than vanilla baselines and outperforms existing RL-based compression methods on both metrics.

Conclusion: ExpThink successfully addresses the limitations of existing CoT compression methods by dynamically adapting to both model capability evolution and problem difficulty variation, creating an efficient RL framework that prioritizes accuracy first while achieving substantial compression benefits without manual intervention.

Abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) achieve strong performance via extended chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, yet suffer from excessive token consumption and high inference latency. Existing reinforcement learning (RL) approaches for CoT compression rely on uniform, static length penalties that neglect model capability dynamics and problem-level difficulty variation. We propose \textbf{ExpThink}\xspace, an RL framework that addresses both dimensions through two complementary mechanisms. First, \emph{experience-guided reward shaping} tracks the shortest correct solution found so far for each problem and applies a three-tier reward: full credit for concise correct responses, discounted credit for verbose correct ones, and zero for incorrect ones. The threshold tightens automatically with model improvement, forming a self-evolving curriculum that requires no manual scheduling. Second, \emph{difficulty-adaptive advantage} replaces standard deviation normalization with correct-count normalization, yielding monotonically difficulty-scaled gradients that amplify learning on hard problems to preserve accuracy while suppressing gradients on easy ones to encourage brevity. Together, these mechanisms enforce an accuracy-first, compression-second training objective. Experiments on multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that \textbf{ExpThink}\xspace reduces average response length by up to 77\% while simultaneously improving accuracy, achieving up to $3\times$ higher accuracy-efficiency ratio (accuracy divided by average token count) than the vanilla baseline and outperforming existing RL-based compression methods on both metrics.

</details>


### [277] [Tessellations of Semi-Discrete Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07513)
*Emile Pierret,Johannes Hertrich,Samuel Hurault,Julie Delon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Semi-discrete Flow Matching analysis reveals terminal assignment regions have open, simply connected geometry homeomorphic to balls, but differ significantly from optimal transport Laguerre cells in shape and properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the theoretical geometry of Flow Matching in the semi-discrete setting where a Gaussian source is transported to a finite target distribution, which represents the theoretical foundation for Flow Matching in generative modeling with finite datasets.

Method: Analyzes the exact Flow Matching velocity field available in closed form in semi-discrete regime, investigates terminal assignment regions (preimages of target atoms under terminal flow), and examines their geometric properties including openness, simple connectedness, and homeomorphism to unit balls.

Result: Shows terminal assignment regions are open, simply connected, and under additional assumptions homeomorphic to unit balls. However, these regions differ sharply from Laguerre cells in semi-discrete optimal transport - they can be non-convex, have curved boundaries, and exhibit different boundedness and adjacency patterns, as demonstrated by a planar four-point example.

Conclusion: The study clarifies the intrinsic geometry induced by exact semi-discrete Flow Matching objective independent of neural approximation effects, revealing fundamental geometric differences between Flow Matching and optimal transport assignment patterns that inform theoretical understanding of generative modeling approaches.

Abstract: We study Flow Matching in a semi-discrete setting where a Gaussian source is transported toward a discrete target supported on finitely many points. This semi-discrete regime is the theoretical setting behind the use of Flow Matching for generative modeling, where the target distribution is represented by a finite dataset. In this semi-discrete regime, the exact Flow Matching velocity field is available in closed form, which makes it possible to analyze the geometry induced by the terminal flow map independently of optimization and approximation effects. We investigate the terminal assignment regions, namely the preimages of the target atoms under the terminal flow. We show that these regions are open, simply connected and, under an additional assumption, homeomorphic to the unit ball. At the same time, a planar four-point example shows that these cells can differ sharply from Laguerre cells arising in semi-discrete optimal transport: they may be non-convex, have curved boundaries, and exhibit different boundedness and adjacency patterns. These results clarify the geometry intrinsically induced by the exact semi-discrete Flow Matching objective before neural approximation enters the picture.

</details>


### [278] [SGD for Variational Inference: Tackling Unbounded Variance via Preconditioning and Dynamic Batching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07531)
*Hippolyte Labarrière,Cesare Molinari,Silvia Villa,Lorenzo Rosasco*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BBVI with SGD suffers from unbounded gradient variance violating standard assumptions; paper proves ELBO solution existence for elliptic distributions and provides convergence guarantees for minibatch PSGD with dynamic batching/preconditioning under weaker BG condition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Black-Box Variational Inference typically uses SGD to optimize ELBO, but stochastic gradients in BBVI have unbounded variance that violates standard SGD assumptions. Instead, they satisfy the weaker Blum-Gladyshev condition with quadratic variance growth. There's a gap between stochastic optimization theory and practical BBVI instances that needs addressing.

Method: Focuses on elliptic location-scale family of parameterized distributions. Proves existence of ELBO solution (previously assumed). Establishes comprehensive convergence guarantees for Minibatch Projected SGD with dynamic batching and preconditioning under the BG condition.

Result: 1. Proves fundamental existence of ELBO solution for the considered family. 2. Provides rigorous finite-time and asymptotic convergence guarantees for PSGD with dynamic batching/preconditioning under BG condition. Shows dynamic batching with preconditioning enables guarantees even in complex settings.

Conclusion: Bridges gap between stochastic optimization theory and practical BBVI. Theoretical framework demonstrates dynamic batching with preconditioning systematically enables rigorous convergence guarantees under realistic conditions (BG condition rather than standard assumptions). Numerical results validate theoretical findings for modern inference tasks.

Abstract: Black-Box Variational Inference (BBVI) typically relies on Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) to optimize the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO). However, the stochastic gradients in BBVI inherently exhibit unbounded variance, violating standard assumptions and instead satisfying the weaker Blum-Gladyshev (BG) condition, where variance grows quadratically with distance from the optimum. In this paper, we bridge the gap between stochastic optimization theory and the practical instances of BBVI. Focusing on the broad elliptic location-scale family of parameterized distributions, we offer two main contributions. First, we prove the existence of an ELBO solution, a foundational property usually assumed a priori in the literature. Second, we establish comprehensive convergence guarantees spanning finite-time and asymptotic regimes for Minibatch Projected SGD (PSGD) equipped with dynamic batching and preconditioning under the BG condition. Our theoretical framework demonstrates that dynamic batching combined with preconditioning systematically enables rigorous guarantees even in complex settings. We illustrate our theoretical findings with numerical results, highlighting the efficacy of our approach for modern inference tasks.

</details>


### [279] [On the Invariance and Generality of Neural Scaling Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07546)
*Xing Han,Ziyin Liu,Suchi Saria,Paul Pu Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Researchers develop generalizable neural scaling laws that can be transferred from well-resourced source domains to new domains, using information-theoretic principles about how scaling properties change under data transformations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural scaling laws are expensive to obtain for new model-task pairs because they require compute-intensive sweeps that defeat the purpose of using scaling laws to economize compute resources. The paper aims to develop scaling laws that can be fit once on a source domain and reliably transported to new domains where full sweeps are infeasible.

Method: The paper identifies that scaling laws are preserved under bijective (information-preserving) data transformations and change predictably under non-bijective transformations that lower information resolution (ρ). They establish a single axis (information resolution) along which scaling laws can be transported between domains using information-theoretic principles.

Result: The approach was validated across language, vision, and speech domains. Two cross-domain applications demonstrated: 1) predicting scaling for language models trained on electronic health records from laws fit on general text, and 2) predicting time-series classification scaling under varying noise injection levels, recovering data-scaling exponents within 3% error.

Conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the challenge of developing generalizable scaling laws by identifying information-theoretic invariants that enable reliable transportation of scaling laws from well-resourced source domains to new target domains, significantly reducing the compute cost needed to establish scaling relationships.

Abstract: Neural scaling laws establish a predictable relationship between model performance and data or compute, offering crucial guidance for resource allocation in new domains and tasks. Yet such laws are most needed precisely where they are hardest to obtain: fitting one for a new model task pair demands expensive sweeps that typically exhaust the very compute budget the law is meant to economize. This paper poses the research question of how to develop generalizable scaling laws: laws fit once on a well-resourced source domain and reliably transported to new domains where running a full sweep is infeasible, which requires a fundamental understanding of when and why scaling properties change. We address this by identifying the right invariants: scaling laws are preserved under bijective (information-preserving) transformations of the data and modified in predictable, information-theoretically grounded ways under non-bijective transformations that lower its information resolution $ρ$: a single axis along which a law fit in one domain can be transported to another. We validate this across language, vision, and speech, and demonstrate two cross-domain applications: predicting scaling for language models trained on electronic health records from laws fit on general text, and predicting time-series classification scaling under varying levels of noise injection, recovering the data-scaling exponents to within $3\%$ error.

</details>


### [280] [Disagreement-Regularized Importance Sampling for Adversarial Label Corruption](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07551)
*Csongor Horváth,Ida-Maria Sintorn,Prashant Singh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DR-IS is a disagreement-regularized importance sampling method that uses loss rank disagreement across proxy ensembles to robustly select clean examples under label corruption, outperforming magnitude-based methods like EL2N and providing theoretical guarantees on contamination control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Importance Sampling (IS) fails under label corruption because it prioritizes high-norm examples for variance reduction, which are often adversarial outliers. There's a need for robust sub-sampling methods that can handle targeted attacks on benchmark datasets while maintaining theoretical guarantees.

Method: DR-IS uses loss rank-disagreement across independent proxy ensembles to select examples. It leverages the observation that bulk corrupted examples have bounded empirical rank disagreement, while boundary-clean examples have bounded below disagreement. The method ensures separation when there's a positive structural expectation gap and sufficient boundary-clean examples.

Result: Theoretical: Finite-sample concentration bounds show O(√(log(N/δ)/K)) convergence rate with probability 1-δ. When conditions are met, the bounds certify strict separation and control contamination rate. Empirical: DR-IS remains robust under targeted high-norm attacks that break magnitude-based methods like EL2N on benchmark datasets.

Conclusion: DR-IS provides a theoretically-grounded, robust alternative to standard IS for corrupted label settings, offering explicit finite-sample concentration certificates and contamination bounds. It complements training-dynamics approaches like AUM, especially in loss-aligned regimes, with proven robustness against targeted attacks.

Abstract: Standard Importance Sampling (IS) collapses under label corruption because high-norm examples, prioritized for variance reduction, are often adversarial outliers. We formalize this misalignment using an $\varepsilon$-contamination model and propose Disagreement-Regularized Importance Sampling (DR-IS), a sub-sampling method based on loss rank-disagreement across independent proxy ensemble. We prove finite-sample concentration bounds showing that the empirical rank disagreement of bulk corrupted examples is bounded above, and that of boundary-clean examples bounded below, both at rate $O(\sqrt{\log(N/δ)/K})$ with probability $1-δ$; when the structural expectation gap $Δ'$ between the two groups is positive and the boundary-clean set is at least as large as the selected subset, these bounds certify strict separation and control the contamination rate of the selected subset. Empirically, DR-IS remains robust under targeted high-norm attacks that break magnitude-based methods such as the Error $L_2$-norm (EL2N) on benchmark datasets. DR-IS complements training-dynamics approaches like Area Under the Margin ranking (AUM), offering improved robustness in the loss-aligned regime alongside explicit finite-sample concentration certificates and a contamination bound limiting noise leakage from the statistical tail of corrupted points.

</details>


### [281] [Beyond Distribution Estimation: Simplex Anchored Structural Inference Towards Universal Semi-Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07557)
*Yaxin Hou,Jun Ma,Hanyang Li,Bo Han,Jie Yu,Yuheng Jia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new method called SAGE for Universal Semi-supervised Learning that addresses the challenge of unknown, arbitrary distributions in unlabeled data by using structural inference instead of pseudo-labeling, achieving state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world semi-supervised learning faces critical challenges when labeled data is scarce and unlabeled data follows unknown, arbitrary distributions, which existing methods struggle with because they rely on unrealistic assumptions about uniform distributions or require sufficient labeled data to estimate distributions.

Method: Proposes Simplex Anchored Graph-state Equipartition (SAGE) which: 1) uses representation-level structural inference to capture high-order inter-sample dependencies, 2) employs simplex equiangular tight frame vectors to guide inter-class representation separation, 3) introduces distribution-agnostic weighting to prioritize reliable pseudo-labels, and 4) uses an auxiliary branch to isolate potentially erroneous pseudo-labels.

Result: SAGE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on five standard benchmarks with an average accuracy gain of 8.52%, demonstrating superior performance in the Universal Semi-supervised Learning setting.

Conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the under-explored Universal Semi-supervised Learning paradigm by shifting focus from pseudo-labeling to representation-level structural inference, proving that inter-sample relations are more reliable than pseudo-labels and that structural consensus guided by SAGE effectively overcomes representation confusion in challenging realistic scenarios.

Abstract: Semi-supervised learning faces significant challenges in realistic scenarios where labeled data is scarce and unlabeled data follows unknown, arbitrary distributions. We formalize this critical yet under-explored paradigm as Universal Semi-supervised Learning (UniSSL). Existing methods typically leverage unlabeled data via pseudo-labeling. However, they often rely on the idealized assumption of a uniform unlabeled data distribution or require sufficient labeled data to estimate it. In the UniSSL setting, such dependencies lead to numerous erroneous pseudo-labels, thereby triggering representation confusion. Fortunately, we observe that inter-sample relations captured by representations are more reliable than pseudo-labels. Leveraging this insight, we shift our focus to representation-level structural inference to bypass distribution estimation. Accordingly, we propose Simplex Anchored Graph-state Equipartition (SAGE), which captures high-order inter-sample dependencies to establish structural consensus for guiding representation learning. Meanwhile, to mitigate representation confusion, we employ vectors that satisfy a simplex equiangular tight frame to serve as a coordinate frame for guiding inter-class representation separation. Finally, we introduce a weighting strategy based on distribution-agnostic metrics to prioritize reliable pseudo-labels and an auxiliary branch to isolate potentially erroneous pseudo-labels. Evaluations on five standard benchmarks show that SAGE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, with an average accuracy gain of \textbf{8.52\%}.

</details>


### [282] [Bilevel Graph Structure Learning, Revisited: Inner-Channel Origins of the Reported Gain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07577)
*Minkyoung Kim,Beakcheol Jang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper shows that much of the performance gain in bilevel graph structure learning comes from training dynamics in the inner loop rather than graph rewiring itself, introducing frozen-φ as a diagnostic control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current understanding attributes performance improvements in bilevel graph structure learning primarily to the learned/rewired graph structure, but the authors suspect this attribution may be overstated and that training-dynamics effects play a more significant role.

Method: Introduced frozen-φ, a control method that freezes the graph structure while retaining the inner-loop training schedule, allowing decomposition of bilevel gain into inner channel (training dynamics) and graph channel (graph rewiring). Applied to spatio-temporal flow forecasting and node classification benchmarks.

Result: On spatio-temporal flow forecasting, the inner channel (training dynamics) accounted for 78-101% of the performance gain; on node classification, it accounted for 37-44%. Classical spectral diagnostics were shown to dissociate from task gain. A three-precondition framework predicted the sign of bilevel gain on all six benchmarks.

Conclusion: The attribution of bilevel graph structure learning improvements to graph rewiring is often overstated, with training dynamics playing a substantial role. Frozen-φ serves as a standardized diagnostic, and a three-precondition framework can predict when bilevel learning will be beneficial.

Abstract: Bilevel graph structure learning is widely understood to improve graph neural networks by jointly optimizing model parameters and a learned graph structure, with the resulting performance gain attributed to the rewired adjacency. We find that this attribution may be overstated: training-dynamics effects in the inner loop, rather than the rewiring itself, capture a substantial share of the gain. To establish this, we introduce frozen-$φ$, a control that freezes the graph while retaining the inner-loop training schedule. This decomposes the bilevel gain into an inner channel of $T$-step training dynamics with implicit gradient regularization and a graph channel of the graph rewiring itself. On spatio-temporal flow forecasting the inner channel matches or exceeds the full bilevel pipeline, accounting for 78-101% of the gain; on node classification it accounts for 37-44% under a Bernoulli edge-level parameterization. We also verify that classical spectral diagnostics can dissociate from task gain. We propose frozen-$φ$ as a standardized diagnostic for bilevel graph structure learning, with graph distillation as a method-agnostic complement. A three-precondition framework further predicts the sign of the bilevel gain on all six benchmarks.

</details>


### [283] [Optimal Recourse Summaries via Bi-Objective Decision Tree Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07598)
*Ioannis Chatzis,Jason Liartis,Athanasios Voulodimos,Giorgos Stamou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SOGAR is a method that creates recourse summaries by learning optimal decision trees, finding the complete Pareto front to balance recourse effectiveness and cost, enabling post-hoc trade-off selection without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Actionable Recourse is useful at instance level but unsuitable for global auditing and bias detection. Aggregating local actions is costly and inconsistent, while existing recourse summary methods don't adequately address the fundamental trade-off between recourse effectiveness and cost.

Method: SOGAR formulates recourse summary learning as an optimal decision tree learning problem. It finds the complete Pareto front - all solutions where improving one objective (effectiveness) necessarily worsens the other (cost). Uses shallow axis-parallel decision trees and sparse leaf actions to produce stable, low-cost summaries.

Result: SOGAR produces stable, low-cost, and effective recourse summaries that outperform existing approaches across both effectiveness and cost metrics. The method enables post-hoc selection of desired trade-offs without retraining.

Conclusion: SOGAR addresses limitations of existing recourse methods by providing a principled approach to creating recourse summaries that properly balance effectiveness and cost through Pareto optimal solutions, enabling better global auditing and bias detection.

Abstract: Actionable Recourse provides individuals with actions they can take to change an unfavorable classifier outcome.
