While useful at the instance level, it is ill-suited for global auditing and bias detection, since aggregating local actions is costly and often inconsistent. Recourse Summaries address this limitation by partitioning the population and assigning one shared action per subgroup, enabling comparison across subgroups. Designing summaries involves a fundamental trade-off between recourse effectiveness and recourse cost, which existing methods do not adequately address. We introduce Summaries of Optimal and Global Actionable Recourse (SOGAR), which formulates recourse summary learning as an optimal decision tree learning problem and finds the Pareto front -- the complete set of solutions where improving one objective necessarily worsens the other. SOGAR enables post-hoc selection of the desired trade-off without retraining. Using shallow axis-parallel decision trees and sparse leaf actions, SOGAR produces stable, low-cost, and effective recourse summaries that outperform existing approaches across effectiveness and cost metrics.

</details>


### [284] [Learning Large-Scale Modular Addition with an Auxiliary Modulus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07648)
*Hanato Kikuchi,Ryosuke Masuya,Kazuhiko Kawamoto,Hiroshi Kera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A covariate-shift-free method for learning modular addition by using an auxiliary modulus during training to reduce problem difficulty while preserving input distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for learning modular addition create covariate shift between training and test distributions when they modify input sequences to reduce zeros, making learning challenging for large moduli, many summands, and small datasets.

Method: Introduces an auxiliary modulus Kq during training that reduces wrap-around frequency and problem difficulty while maintaining the same input distribution across training and testing, eliminating the covariate shift issue.

Result: Significantly improves scalability and sample efficiency: achieves 97.0% τ-accuracy at N=64, q=974269 with only 100K samples, while the sparse method achieves only 9.5% with same data and 93.9% even with 1M samples.

Conclusion: The proposed covariate-shift-free method enables effective learning of modular addition across challenging scenarios with large input lengths, large moduli, and limited training data where previous methods fail.

Abstract: Learning parity functions, more general modular addition, is a challenging machine learning task due to its input sensitivity. A recent study substantially scaled modular addition learning in both the number of summands and the modulus. Its key idea is to increase zeros in training sequences, reducing the effective number of summands and thus controlling training difficulty; however, this induces covariate shift between training and test input distributions. This study theoretically and empirically analyzes this side effect and proposes a covariate-shift-free method for modular addition. Specifically, we introduce an auxiliary modulus $Kq$ during training, which reduces wrap-around frequency and problem difficulty while preserving the same input distribution across training and testing. Experiments show strong scalability and sample efficiency: even for large input length $N$, large modulus $q$, and small datasets -- where the sparse method fails to learn -- our method achieves equal or better match accuracy and relaxed $τ$-accuracy. For example, at $N=64$ and $q=974269$, our method trained on 100K samples achieves $97.0\%$ $τ$-accuracy at $τ=0.05$, while the sparse method achieves only $9.5\%$ with the same data size and $93.9\%$ even when extended to 1M samples.

</details>


### [285] [Stochastic Transition-Map Distillation for Fast Probabilistic Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07661)
*George Rapakoulias,Peter Garud,Lingjiong Zhu,Panagiotis Tsiotras*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STMD is a teacher-free distillation method that accelerates diffusion model inference by distilling the full stochastic transition map, enabling few-step sampling while preserving stochasticity for downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models achieve excellent generation quality but suffer from expensive inference costs. Current distillation methods often require complex setups like pretrained teachers, bi-level optimization, or trajectory simulation.

Method: Proposes Stochastic Transition-Map Distillation (STMD) that distills the full transition map of the sampling SDE using a conditional Mean Flow model. Unlike score-based models that only model posterior means, STMD captures the full stochastic transitions, enabling efficient few-step sampling without complex training requirements.

Result: Theoretical convergence bounds in Wasserstein distance provide strong foundation. Validated on image generation tasks across MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CelebA datasets, showing STMD accelerates inference while preserving stochasticity for downstream tasks like diffusion posterior sampling and inverse problems.

Conclusion: STMD offers an efficient, scalable approach for diffusion model acceleration that maintains stochastic generation properties crucial for various applications, with theoretical guarantees and practical validation across multiple datasets.

Abstract: Diffusion models achieve strong generation quality, diversity, and distribution coverage, but their performance often comes with expensive inference. In this work, we propose Stochastic Transition-Map Distillation (STMD), a teacher-free framework for accelerating diffusion model inference while preserving probabilistic sample generation. In contrast to score-based diffusion models, whose denoising parametrization models the mean of the posterior distribution, STMD distills the full transition map associated with the sampling stochastic differential equation (SDE). We parameterize these SDE transitions with a conditional Mean Flow model, yielding a one- or few-step stochastic sampler that retains the transition structure of the underlying diffusion process. This perspective is especially useful for downstream tasks that require stochastic inference, such as diffusion posterior sampling, inverse problems, and energy-based fine-tuning. Compared to recent distillation methods, STMD requires no pretrained teacher, bi-level optimization, or trajectory simulation and caching, enabling efficient and scalable training. We derive convergence bounds for our method in the Wasserstein distance, providing a strong theoretical foundation for our approach, and validate STMD on various image generation examples on the MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CelebA datasets.

</details>


### [286] [Direction-Preserving Number Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07662)
*Bardia Zadeh,George A. Constantinides*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how well low-precision number formats can represent vector directions, showing that standard formats like two's complement and floating-point are suboptimal, and provides optimized scalar alphabets that explain NVIDIA's successful E2M1 format.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-precision formats are widely used for efficiency in machine learning, but their ability to accurately represent vector directions (crucial for vector operations) needs better theoretical understanding and optimization.

Method: Introduced a geometric framework for analyzing directional coverage of product-structured codes, analytically quantified suboptimality gaps compared to spherical codes, proved standard formats are suboptimal with quantified gaps, and numerically optimized scalar alphabets across dimensions.

Result: Standard formats (two's complement, fixed-point, floating-point) are provably suboptimal for directional representation. Optimized scalar alphabets were found, and interestingly, NVIDIA's E2M1 format closely approximates the optimized alphabet for 4 bits, explaining its strong performance in low-precision ML workloads.

Conclusion: There's significant potential to develop new scalar number formats beyond current standards, with analytical justification for why certain formats perform well. The work provides both theoretical framework, optimization results, and formal verification via Lean proofs.

Abstract: Low-precision number formats are widely used in modern machine learning systems due to their efficiency. Accurate direction representation is key to the accuracy of vector operations. This work precisely explores the extent to which the direction of a vector can be represented by selecting its scalar elements from a common finite alphabet of a given size. This is standard practice in machine learning, where low-precision significands may be narrow-width floating-point or integer values. A geometric framework is introduced for analyzing the directional coverage of such product-structured codes.
  This work analytically quantifies the suboptimality gap between such product-structured codes and spherical codes for the vector as a whole, in both low and asymptotically high dimensions. Furthermore, within the product code class, it is proven that the standard formats of two's complement, fixed-point, and floating-point are suboptimal, again with quantified gap, pointing to the potential to develop new scalar number formats.
  Such scalar alphabets are numerically optimized across multiple block dimensions for directional coverage, including the dimension used in NVIDIA's NVFP4 format. Experimental results are presented comparing the performance of standard formats and the optimized alphabet. We find that for four bits, NVIDIA's choice of E2M1 closely approximates the optimized alphabet, providing a geometric explanation for its strong performance in low-precision machine learning workloads and an analytical understanding of the link between that superiority and block size.
  We provide open-source formal proofs in Lean for the theorems in this work, along with the experimental code and the optimized alphabets obtained.

</details>


### [287] [Structured Coupling for Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07676)
*Xavier Sumba,Carles Balsells-Rodas,Yingzhen Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCFM combines flow matching with structured latent variables to learn interpretable representations while maintaining generative quality, bridging gap between scalable but unstructured flow matching and structured but lower-quality latent models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard flow matching scales well but lacks interpretable latent structure, while latent-variable models capture structure but sacrifice generative quality. There's a need to bridge this gap between scalability and interpretability.

Method: Proposes Structured Coupling for Flow Matching (SCFM) that introduces structured latent variables and exogenous noise into the source distribution. Uses a shared time-dependent recognition network for both latent variable model variational inference and flow velocity estimation, jointly learning a structured prior and continuous transport map.

Result: SCFM facilitates unsupervised latent representation learning for clustering, disentanglement, and downstream tasks while remaining competitive with flow matching in sample quality. Shows that meaningful structure can be learned without sacrificing generative fidelity.

Conclusion: SCFM successfully bridges the gap between flow matching and latent variable models, creating a structurally informed yet unconditional, simulation-free flow model that maintains both interpretable latent structure and high generative quality.

Abstract: Standard flow matching scales well but typically relies on an unstructured source distribution, limiting its ability to learn interpretable latent structure. Latent-variable models, by contrast, capture structure but often sacrifice generative quality. We bridge this gap by proposing Structured Coupling for Flow Matching (SCFM), a cooperative framework that augments flow matching with structured latent representation learning. By introducing structured latent variables and exogenous noise into the source, SCFM jointly learns a structured prior (via latent variable modeling) and a continuous transport map (via flow matching). It uses a shared time-dependent recognition network for both latent variable model variational inference and intermediate-time flow velocity estimation. This yields a structurally informed yet unconditional, simulation-free flow model, where the latent variable model can also assist flow sampling. Empirically, SCFM facilitates unsupervised latent representation learning for clustering, disentanglement and downstream tasks, while remaining competitive with flow matching in sample quality, showing that meaningful structure can be learned without sacrificing generative fidelity.

</details>


### [288] [The Coupling Tax: How Shared Token Budgets Undermine Visible Chain-of-Thought Under Fixed Output Limits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07686)
*Wenhua Nie,Junlin Liu,Jianan Wu,Zijie Meng,Yilong Fan,Zhang Zijian,Haoran Zheng,Jyh-Shing Roger Jang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chain-of-thought reasoning can hurt accuracy due to the "coupling tax" - when reasoning traces and answers share token budgets, long traces can crowd out answers, making non-thinking mode better at limited budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While chain-of-thought reasoning is typically viewed as monotonically improving language model accuracy by allowing longer thinking, the authors identify a countervailing effect: the coupling tax where reasoning traces and final answers compete for the same output token budget.

Method: The authors study the coupling tax across multiple benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH-500, BIG-Bench Hard tasks) using Qwen models at different scales. They derive a truncation-waste decomposition formula to predict performance crossovers and demonstrate the phenomenon with Qwen models and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B. They propose split-budget generation as a mitigation.

Result: Non-thinking mode matches or outperforms thinking mode on GSM8K and MATH-500 at budgets up to 2048 tokens. Harder tasks shift the crossover to larger budgets. The proposed split-budget generation with IRIS achieves 74.0% accuracy on full MATH-500, with enhanced variants reaching up to 83.6%.

Conclusion: Test-time reasoning should be evaluated as a budget-allocation problem, not just whether longer traces are available. The coupling tax shows that reasoning traces can negatively impact accuracy when competing with answers for limited token budgets, requiring careful resource allocation strategies.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought reasoning is often treated as a monotone way to improve language-model accuracy by letting a model think longer. We identify a countervailing effect, the coupling tax: when reasoning traces and final answers share one output-token budget, long traces can crowd out the answer they are meant to support. Across GSM8K, MATH-500, and five BIG-Bench Hard tasks with Qwen3 models at three scales, non-thinking mode matches or outperforms thinking mode on GSM8K and MATH-500 at every budget up to 2048 tokens, while harder tasks shift the crossover to larger budgets. We derive a truncation-waste decomposition, $\mathrm{Acc}_{\mathrm{think}}(b)=α_c F_L(b)+α_t(1-F_L(b))$, that predicts this crossover from chain-length and accuracy statistics and explains inverse scaling within the Qwen family. A DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B replication shows the same pattern under a different thinking interface. As a mitigation, split-budget generation decouples reasoning and answer budgets; on full MATH-500, IRIS reaches 74.0% accuracy, a strengthened extraction variant reaches 78.8%, and a fixed non-oracle SC+IRIS gate reaches 83.6%. The results show that test-time reasoning should be evaluated as a budget-allocation problem, not only as a question of whether longer traces are available.

</details>


### [289] [Gradient Starvation in Binary-Reward GRPO: Why Group-Mean Centering Fails and Why the Simplest Fix Works](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07689)
*Wenhua Nie,Jianan Wu,Junlin Liu,Ziwei Li,Zheng Lin,Zhang Zijian,Yilong Fan,Haoran Zheng,Jyh-Shing Roger Jang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRPO's group-mean-centered advantage fails with binary rewards due to gradient starvation, but Sign advantage (A=2r-1) dramatically improves performance by enabling pass@G failure descent and search compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has a failure mode with binary rewards where the group-mean-centered advantage becomes zero when all responses in a group are correct or all are wrong, causing gradient starvation and no learning signal. This occurs more frequently than predicted by i.i.d. Bernoulli assumptions.

Method: The authors propose using a fixed-reference Sign advantage (A=2r-1) instead of group-mean-centered advantage. This approach performs "pass@G failure descent" by increasing the probability that at least one sample in the group succeeds. They empirically test this on GSM8K with Qwen3.5-9B and Llama-3.1-8B models.

Result: Sign advantage achieves 73.8% accuracy on GSM8K versus 28.4% for standard normalized group-mean DrGRPO at group size 4, a 45.4 point gain (p<0.0001). The improvement is directionally consistent across models. Pass@k analysis shows main benefit is search compression rather than capacity expansion.

Conclusion: The Sign advantage effectively addresses gradient starvation in GRPO with binary rewards by enabling learning even when group outcomes are homogeneous, leading to substantial performance improvements through better search compression rather than model capacity enhancement.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is a standard algorithm for reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards, but its group-mean-centered advantage can fail under binary rewards. The failure mode is gradient starvation: when every response in a group is correct or every response is wrong, the centered advantage is exactly zero and the policy receives no learning signal. We prove that the true degeneracy rate always exceeds the i.i.d. Bernoulli prediction by Jensen's inequality, and observe a 0.69 degeneracy rate at group size four in logged Qwen3.5-9B GSM8K training. We then show that the fixed-reference Sign advantage, $A=2r-1$, performs pass@$G$ failure descent by increasing the probability that at least one sample in the group succeeds. On the full GSM8K test set across seven seeds, Sign reaches 73.8% accuracy versus 28.4% for standard normalized group-mean DrGRPO at group size four, a 45.4 point gain with $p<0.0001$. The effect is directionally consistent on Llama-3.1-8B and positive but underpowered on a MATH-500 transfer check. Pass@$k$ analysis indicates that the main benefit is search compression rather than large capacity expansion, aligning the empirical gains with recent RLVR ceiling observations.

</details>


### [290] [Fortifying Time Series: DTW-Certified Robust Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07690)
*Shijie Liu,Tansu Alpcan,Christopher Leckie,Sarah Erfani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First DTW-certified robust defense for time-series anomaly detection using randomized smoothing and lower-bound transformation from ℓₚ-norm to DTW distance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing time-series anomaly detection defenses are heuristic or only provide ℓₚ-norm certified robustness, which fails to capture temporal structure. Small temporal distortions can significantly alter ℓₚ-norm measures, making it unsuitable for time-series data. DTW is more appropriate but lacks certifiable robustness guarantees.

Method: Adapted randomized smoothing paradigm to DTW metric by bridging ℓₚ-norm to DTW distance through a lower-bound transformation. This provides the first DTW-certified robust defense for time-series anomaly detection.

Result: Extensive experiments show significantly improved performance, with up to 18.7% improvement in F1-score under DTW-based adversarial attacks compared to traditional certified models.

Conclusion: The proposed method provides the first certifiable robustness in DTW metric for time-series anomaly detection, addressing the critical need for robust defenses in high-stakes applications where temporal structure preservation is essential.

Abstract: Time-series anomaly detection is critical for ensuring safety in high-stakes applications, where robustness is a fundamental requirement rather than a mere performance metric. Addressing the vulnerability of these systems to adversarial manipulation is therefore essential. Existing defenses are largely heuristic or provide certified robustness only under $\ell_p$-norm constraints, which are incompatible with time-series data. In particular, $\ell_p$-norm fails to capture the intrinsic temporal structure in time series, causing small temporal distortions to significantly alter the $\ell_p$-norm measures. Instead, the similarity metric \emph{Dynamic Time Warping} (DTW) is more suitable and widely adopted in the time-series domain, as DTW accounts for temporal alignment and remains robust to temporal variations. To date, however, there has been no certifiable robustness result in this metric that provides guarantees. In this work, we introduce the first \emph{DTW-certified robust defense} in time-series anomaly detection by adapting the randomized smoothing paradigm. We develop this certificate by bridging the $\ell_p$-norm to DTW distance through a lower-bound transformation. Extensive experiments across various datasets and models validate the effectiveness and practicality of our theoretical approach. Results demonstrate significantly improved performance, e.g., up to 18.7\% in F1-score under DTW-based adversarial attacks compared to traditional certified models.

</details>


### [291] [Toward Better Geometric Representations for Molecule Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07693)
*Shaoheng Yan,Zian Li,Cai Zhou,Qiaojing Huang,Kai Liu,Muhan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LENSEs is a framework that improves representation-conditioned molecule generation by better exploiting pretrained molecular encoders through three mechanisms: representation head extraction, molecule perceptual loss, and node-level representation alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current representation-conditioned molecule generation methods are limited by the quality of representation spaces from pretrained encoders like UniMol, which produce non-smooth representations that aren't fully exploited during generative training.

Method: Proposes LENSEs framework with three complementary mechanisms: (1) representation head for multi-level representation extraction trained simultaneously during generative tasks; (2) molecule perceptual loss for optimizing generator in semantic-informative representation space; (3) node-level representation alignment (REPA) loss to align generator's hidden states with encoder representations.

Result: Achieves 97.28% validity and 98.51% molecule stability on GEOM-DRUG dataset, surpassing existing methods. Demonstrates 4.6x Lipschitz constant reduction and better performance on QM9 probing tasks, showing smoother, more informative refined representations.

Conclusion: LENSEs effectively improves representation-conditioned molecule generation by better exploiting pretrained molecular encoders, and establishes generative training with alignment objectives as a potential pretraining paradigm for molecular encoders.

Abstract: Geometric representation-conditioned molecule generation provides an effective paradigm that decouples molecule representation modeling from structure generation. By decoupling molecule generation into two stages-first generating a meaningful molecule representation, and then generating a 3D molecule conditioned on this representation-the efficiency and quality of the generation process can be significantly enhanced. However, its effectiveness is fundamentally limited by the quality of the representation space: pretrained molecular encoders, such as UniMol, produce representations that are non-smooth and not fully exploited during the generative training process. In this work, we propose LENSEs, a framework that better exploits the potential of molecule representations in representation-conditioned generation methods. In particular, LENSEs introduces three complementary mechanisms: (1) a representation head, simultaneously trained during generative tasks, that extracts multi-level representations from the pretrained encoder; (2) a molecule perceptual loss that optimizes the generator in a semantic-informative representation space; and (3) a node-level representation alignment (REPA) loss that explicitly aligns the generator's hidden states with encoder representations, reducing the semantic gap between pretraining and generation. We demonstrate the effectiveness of these improvements through extensive molecule generation tasks. Specifically, on the challenging molecule generation dataset GEOM-DRUG, LENSEs achieves 97.28% validity and 98.51% molecule stability, surpassing existing advanced methods. Further analyses through Lipschitz constant reduction (4.6x) and QM9 probing tasks also demonstrate the smoother, more informative refined representations, establishing generative training with alignment objectives as a potential pretraining paradigm for molecular encoders.

</details>


### [292] [Future Validity is the Missing Statistic: From Impossibility to $Φ$-Estimation for Grammar-Faithful Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07698)
*Wenhua Nie,Zijie Meng,Kun Zou,Zheng Lin,Ziwei Li,Haoran Zheng,Jyh-Shing Roger Jang,Hao Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Grammar-constrained generation with speculative decoding actually samples from a locally projected distribution rather than the intended grammar-conditional distribution. The paper identifies the future-validity function as the missing correction and proposes oracle and approximate decoders to bridge this gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current grammar-constrained generation approaches using speculative decoding don't actually sample from the intended grammar-conditional distribution that users expect. Instead, they sample from a locally projected distribution, creating a significant fidelity gap.

Method: The paper identifies the future-validity function Φ_t(y) as the missing correction statistic. They show that the target distribution is a Doob transform of the base model with h=Φ, while local masking corresponds to setting h to one. They propose FVO-Spec oracle decoder with exact Φ, and bound total-variation error with approximate Φ. They evaluate estimator hierarchies on tractable grammars (Dyck and finite JSON).

Result: Shows any speculative decoder with local mask access samples from μ^proj rather than μ*. Demonstrates total-variation gap up to 0.996 on Dyck grammars. The proposed OneStep estimator reduces Dyck TV by 14% with under 1% throughput overhead, exact dynamic programming reduces it by 97%, and finite-language correction closes JSON gaps to numerical precision.

Conclusion: The paper establishes that current speculative decoding approaches for grammar-constrained generation don't achieve the intended sampling distribution, identifies the mathematical reason (missing future-validity function), and provides practical solutions that significantly improve fidelity with minimal throughput overhead.

Abstract: Grammar-constrained generation is often combined with local vocabulary masking and speculative decoding, but the resulting sampling law is not the grammar-conditional distribution users usually intend. We show that any speculative decoder with local mask access, Leviathan rejection, and rollback soundness samples from the locally projected distribution $μ^{\mathrm{proj}}$ rather than the grammar-conditional distribution $μ^\star$. This extends the GAD impossibility result to speculative decoding; on Dyck grammars with Qwen3-8B, the total-variation gap can reach 0.996. We identify the future-validity function $Φ_t(y)=\Pr_p[\mathrm{valid\ completion}\mid y]$ as the missing correction statistic. The target distribution is a Doob transform of the base model with $h=Φ$, while local masking corresponds to setting $h$ to one. With exact $Φ$, our oracle decoder FVO-Spec samples exactly from $μ^\star$; with approximate $Φ$, we bound the resulting total-variation error. Because exact future validity is hard for general context-free grammars, we evaluate estimator hierarchies on tractable Dyck and finite JSON languages. OneStep reduces Dyck TV by 14% with under 1% throughput overhead, exact dynamic programming reduces it by 97%, and finite-language correction closes JSON gaps to numerical precision. All fidelity claims are scoped to enumerable grammars and token tries.

</details>


### [293] [Bayesian Fine-tuning in Projected Subspaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07706)
*Viktar Dubovik,Patryk Marszałek,Jacek Tabor,Tomasz Kuśmierczyk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Parameter-efficient Bayesian fine-tuning framework achieves uncertainty quantification in low-dimensional spaces while maintaining computational efficiency, overcoming limitations of both standard LoRA (no uncertainty) and Bayesian LoRA variants (parameter inefficiency).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard LoRA lacks uncertainty quantification, leading to overconfident and poorly calibrated models. Bayesian LoRA variants address this but significantly increase trainable parameters, undermining the original efficiency gains, and suffer from training instability.

Method: Proposes a novel framework for parameter-efficient Bayesian fine-tuning that demonstrates effective uncertainty quantification can be achieved in very low-dimensional parameter spaces through appropriate projection of weight space and modeling weight covariances as low-rank matrices.

Result: Achieves strong performance with improved calibration and generalization while maintaining computational efficiency. Empirical findings show weight covariances exhibit low ranks and uncertainty can be effectively modeled in low-dimensional space.

Conclusion: Effective uncertainty quantification in parameter-efficient fine-tuning is achievable without sacrificing computational efficiency by leveraging low-dimensional projections and low-rank weight covariances, providing a practical solution to the calibration problems of standard LoRA.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) enables parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large models by decomposing weight updates into low-rank matrices, significantly reducing storage and computational overhead. While effective, standard LoRA lacks mechanisms for uncertainty quantification, leading to overconfident and poorly calibrated models. Bayesian variants of LoRA address this limitation, but at the cost of a significantly increased number of trainable parameters, partially offsetting the original efficiency gains. Additionally, these models are harder to train and may suffer from unstable convergence. In this work, we propose a novel framework for parameter-efficient Bayesian fine-tuning, demonstrating that effective uncertainty quantification can be achieved in very low-dimensional parameter spaces. The proposed method achieves strong performance with improved calibration and generalization while maintaining computational efficiency. Our empirical findings show that, with the appropriate projection of the weight space uncertainty can be effectively modeled in a low-dimensional space, and weight covariances exhibit low ranks.

</details>


### [294] [Robust and Reliable AI for Predictive Quality in Semiconductor Materials Manufacturing with MLOps and Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07752)
*Min Gao,Julia Maria Perathoner,Anton Ludwig Bonin,Steven Eulig,Gianni Klesse*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper benchmarks MLOps retraining strategies for semiconductor manufacturing using 5 years of real data, finding that fixed retraining every 5 batches without hyperparameter tuning performs best, and implements conformal prediction for uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Semiconductor manufacturing faces unique challenges for ML deployment due to evolving process conditions, equipment degradation, and raw material variability that cause model performance deterioration over time. There's a need for optimal retraining strategies to maintain model accuracy and computational efficiency.

Method: The study benchmarks various MLOps retraining strategies using 5 years of real manufacturing data. It evaluates different retraining frequencies and hyperparameter optimization strategies using control limit normalized residuals as performance metric. Implements conformal prediction for uncertainty quantification with statistical guarantees.

Result: Fixed retraining every five production batches without hyperparameter retuning achieves superior performance across all drift conditions while significantly reducing computational overhead compared to strategies with hyperparameter optimization. This approach maintains model accuracy during both abrupt process changes and gradual equipment degradation.

Conclusion: The findings provide practical guidelines for implementing robust MLOps strategies in manufacturing where computational efficiency and reliable uncertainty quantification are critical. The approach enables predictive quality control by identifying when prediction intervals fall within acceptable control limits.

Abstract: Semiconductor materials manufacturing presents unique challenges for machine learning deployment due to evolving process conditions, equipment degradation, and raw material variability that can cause model performance deterioration over time. This study benchmarks machine learning operations (MLOps) retraining strategies using five years of real manufacturing data to identify optimal retraining approaches for quality prediction. We evaluate various retraining frequencies and hyperparameter optimization strategies using control limit normalized residuals as key performance metric. Results demonstrate that a fixed retraining cadence every five production batches without hyperparameter retuning achieves superior performance across all drift conditions while significantly reducing computational overhead compared to strategies incorporating hyperparameter optimization. This approach effectively maintains model accuracy during both abrupt process changes and gradual equipment degradation patterns. To address the critical need for uncertainty quantification in manufacturing decision-making, we implement conformal prediction to generate prediction confidence intervals with strong statistical guarantees. This enables proactive quality control by identifying when prediction intervals fall within acceptable control limits, transforming traditional reactive quality management into a predictive framework. The findings provide practical guidelines for implementing robust MLOps strategies in manufacturing environments where computational efficiency and reliable uncertainty quantification are paramount for operational success.

</details>


### [295] [Rethinking State Tracking in Recurrent Models Through Error Control Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07755)
*Jiwan Chung,Heechan Choi,Seon Joo Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Affine recurrent networks (including State-Space Models and Linear Attention) cannot correct errors along state-separating subspaces, leading to finite-horizon state tracking rather than robust long-term tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing theory on state tracking in recurrent architectures has focused too much on expressive capacity (whether models can theoretically realize symbolic transition rules), while overlooking the equally important issue of error control - how hidden-state drift affects state separation over time.

Method: Theoretical analysis proving that affine recurrent networks cannot correct errors along state-separating subspaces once they preserve state representations. Empirical demonstration on group state-tracking tasks showing predictable breakdown when distinguishability ratio crosses a readability threshold.

Result: Affine trackers don't learn robust state tracking but finite horizon solutions governed by accumulated state-relevant error. Tracking remains readable only while within-class spread remains small relative to between-class separation. The point where distinguishability ratio crosses readability threshold predicts when downstream accuracy fails.

Conclusion: Robust state tracking depends not only on an architecture's theoretical expressivity but crucially on its error control mechanisms. Affine recurrent architectures fundamentally lack the ability to correct certain types of state-separation errors, limiting their practical tracking capabilities.

Abstract: The theory of state tracking in recurrent architectures has predominantly focused on expressive capacity: whether a fixed architecture can theoretically realize a set of symbolic transition rules. We argue that equally important is error control, the dynamics governing hidden-state drift along the directions that distinguish symbolic states. We prove that affine recurrent networks, a class of models encompassing State-Space Models and Linear Attention, cannot correct errors along state-separating subspaces once they preserve state representations. Consequently, practical affine trackers do not learn robust state tracking; rather, they learn finite horizon solutions governed by accumulated state-relevant error. We characterize the mechanics of this failure, showing that tracking remains readable only while the accumulating within-class spread remains small relative to the initial between-class separation. We demonstrate empirically on group state-tracking tasks that this breakdown is predictable: tracking collapses when the distinguishability ratio crosses the readability threshold of the trained decoder. Across trained models, the point of this crossing predicts the horizon at which downstream accuracy fails. These results establish that robust state tracking is determined not only by an architecture's theoretical expressivity but crucially by its error control.

</details>


### [296] [Efficient Verification of Neural Control Barrier Functions with Smooth Nonlinear Activations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07757)
*Jun Zhang,Haibo Zhang,Chun Liu,Xiaofan Wang,Liang Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LightCROWN is a new method that provides tighter Jacobian bounds for verifying neural control barrier functions, improving verification success rates up to 100% while being faster and more scalable than existing CROWN-based approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Formal verification of neural control barrier functions (NCBFs) is challenging, especially for neural networks with nonlinear activations like tanh. Existing CROWN-based methods use conservative linear relaxations for Jacobian bounds, which limits scalability and verification success rates.

Method: The authors propose LightCROWN, which computes tighter Jacobian bounds by exploiting the analytical properties of activation functions rather than relying on conservative linear relaxations like previous CROWN-based methods.

Result: Experiments on nonlinear control systems (inverted pendulum, Dubins car, planar quadrotor) show LightCROWN improves verification success rates up to 100%, while also enhancing speed and scalability compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: LightCROWN provides a generalizable improvement for CROWN-based verification frameworks, enabling more efficient verification of complex neural control barrier functions, with open-source code available for reproducibility.

Abstract: Formal verification of neural control barrier functions (NCBFs) remains challenging, especially for neural networks with nonlinear activations like \(\tanh\). Existing CROWN-based methods rely on conservative linear relaxations for Jacobian bounds, limiting scalability. We propose LightCROWN, which computes tighter Jacobian bounds by exploiting the analytical properties of activation functions. Experiments on nonlinear control systems including the inverted pendulum, Dubins car, and planar quadrotor demonstrate that LightCROWN improves verification success rates up to 100\%, while enhancing speed and scalability. Our approach provides a generalizable improvement for CROWN-based frameworks, enabling more efficient verification of complex NCBFs. The code can be found at github.com/Autonomous-Systems-and-Control-Lab/verify-neural-CBF.

</details>


### [297] [Pre-trained Tabular Foundation Models as Versatile Summary Networks for Neural Posterior Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07765)
*Elliot Pickens,Chiraag Gohel,Sidharth Satya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PFN-NPE: A training-free SBI method using TabPFN as a fixed summary network for simulator outputs, paired with modular downstream inference heads (default: normalizing flows).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TabPFN's pretraining on synthetic tabular data and in-context learning capabilities make it naturally suitable for SBI tasks, where learning informative summary statistics from simulated observations is crucial for posterior estimation.

Method: Propose PFN-NPE, which uses a pretrained TabPFN encoder as a fixed summary network for simulator outputs. The TabPFN-generated summaries are then paired with modular inference heads (default being normalizing flows) chosen based on the specific problem requirements.

Result: PFN-NPE matches or sometimes outperforms established posterior approximation methods. Diagnostic probes show TabPFN-derived summaries preserve useful posterior location and marginal information, though they may struggle with joint posterior structure representation even when marginals are well recovered.

Conclusion: TabPFN can effectively serve as a summary network across diverse SBI settings, offering a training-free, modular approach where the inference component remains task-dependent and interchangeable.

Abstract: In this work, we study TabPFN as a training-free, modular summary network for simulation-based Bayesian inference (SBI). Tabular foundation models such as TabPFN are pretrained on broad families of synthetic tabular data-generating processes and adapt at test time through in-context learning, making them natural candidates for SBI, where posterior estimation often depends on learning informative summaries of simulated observations. We propose PFN-NPE: a general recipe that uses a pretrained TabPFN encoder as a fixed summary network for simulator outputs, then pairs the resulting summaries with a downstream inference head chosen for the problem. With normalizing flows as the default inference head, PFN-NPE matches established posterior approximation methods and sometimes outperforms them. More importantly, diagnostic probes show that the TabPFN-derived summaries often preserve useful posterior location and marginal information. These analyses also reveal a limitation in that TabPFN-derived summaries may struggle to represent the joint posterior structure even when the marginals are well recovered. Still, our experiments show that TabPFN can serve as an effective summary network across a diverse set of SBI settings, with the inference network left modular and task-dependent.

</details>


### [298] [Training-Induced Escape from Token Clustering in a Mean-Field Formulation of Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07772)
*Noboru Isobe,Daisuke Inoue,Masaaki Imaizumi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A mean-field theory of Transformer training that shows how training can cause token distributions to leave clustered regimes established by attention mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing mean-field analyses of Transformer dynamics focus on inference and treat model parameters as prescribed, leaving open how training reshapes the clustering behavior of attention mechanisms.

Method: Study a noisy mean-field Transformer where only a parameter-linear FFN is trained under L² regularization, and analyze training-induced phase transitions using entropy-regularized interaction energy.

Result: Discovered a training-induced phase where token distributions initially follow attention-driven clustering but can leave the clustered regime near final layers, indicating training reshapes the clustering picture.

Conclusion: Points toward a training-aware mean-field theory of Transformer dynamics where training and inference dynamics should be treated together, rather than separately.

Abstract: Transformers perform inference by iteratively transforming token representations across layers. This layerwise computation has been studied empirically, and recent mean-field theories of Transformer dynamics explain how attention can drive token distributions toward clustering. However, existing mean-field analyses largely treat model parameters as prescribed, leaving open how training reshapes this clustering picture. We study this question in a noisy mean-field Transformer in which only a parameter-linear FFN is trained under $L^2$ regularization. We find and analyze a training-induced phase in the dynamics: after initially following attention-driven clustering, the token distribution can leave the clustered regime near the final layers. Our mathematical analysis is based on an entropy-regularized interaction energy that captures the clustering bias of attention. More broadly, our results point toward a training-aware mean-field theory of Transformer dynamics, in which training and inference dynamics are treated together.

</details>


### [299] [Spectral Surgery: Class-Targeted Post-Hoc Rebalancing via Hessian Spike Perturbation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07790)
*Hugo Vigna,Samuel Bontemps*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spectral Surgery: A post-hoc optimization method that perturbs deep network weights along spike eigenvectors to rebalance per-class accuracy without retraining, improving classification performance by targeting weak classes while preserving strong ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The Hessian spectrum of trained deep networks shows a characteristic structure with a bulk of near-zero eigenvalues and a few large outlier eigenvalues (spikes), where spike count matches number of classes minus one. While this structure has been described in prior work, no method has operationally exploited it to improve classification performance.

Method: Proposes Spectral Surgery, a post-hoc optimization method with three key components: (1) a spike-class sensitivity matrix that quantifies directional derivative of each class's accuracy along each spike eigenvector, (2) constrained optimization of perturbation coefficients targeting weak classes while preserving strong ones, and (3) adaptive amplitude control adjusting perturbation budget based on iteration-level improvement signals.

Result: Obtains encouraging results on CIFAR-10 and ISIC-2019 datasets, showing improvements in both balanced accuracy and standard deviation of per-class performance.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that the spike structure in deep network Hessian spectra can be operationally exploited to improve classification performance without retraining, providing a practical post-hoc optimization method that effectively rebalances per-class accuracy.

Abstract: The Hessian spectrum of trained deep networks exhibits a characteristic structure: a continuous bulk of near-zero eigenvalues and a small number of large outlier eigenvalues (spikes), confirming the relevance of Random Matrix Theory in deep learning. The spike count matches the number of classes minus one. While prior work has described this structure, no method has exploited it operationally to improve classification performance. We propose Spectral Surgery, a post-hoc optimization method that directly perturbs model weights along spike eigenvectors to rebalance per-class accuracy without retraining. We introduce (i) a spike-class sensitivity matrix that quantifies the directional derivative of each class's accuracy along each spike eigenvector, (ii) a constrained optimization of perturbation coefficients that targets weak classes while preserving strong ones, and (iii) an adaptive amplitude control that raises or lowers the perturbation budget based on iteration-level improvement signals. We obtain encouraging results on CIFAR-10 and ISIC-2019 on both balanced accuracy and standard deviation.

</details>


### [300] [Flexible Routing via Uncertainty Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07805)
*Charlotte Peale,Siddartha Devic,Parikshit Gopalan,Udi Wieder,Aravind Gollakota*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new uncertainty-aware routing method that dynamically directs queries between low-cost models and expensive oracles, with theoretical guarantees and empirical benefits when reducible and irreducible uncertainties are not highly correlated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern ML systems need to balance performance and cost. Current routing approaches may waste expensive oracle calls on inherently ambiguous queries and lack flexibility to adapt to different loss functions and cost parameters without retraining.

Method: Proposes an uncertainty-aware router based on decomposing total uncertainty into irreducible (inherent ambiguity) and reducible (model limitation) components using higher-order predictors. The router predicts with weak model when uncertainty is low, routes to oracle when reducible uncertainty is high, and abstains when irreducible uncertainty is high. The method requires only multiple independent annotations per input and adapts through simple hyperparameter changes without retraining.

Result: The router provides strong theoretical guarantees bounding regret relative to optimal task-specific routers. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate benefits in suitable regimes, particularly when reducible and irreducible uncertainty are not too correlated.

Conclusion: The proposed uncertainty-aware router effectively balances performance and cost by making intelligent routing decisions, avoiding unnecessary oracle calls on ambiguous queries, and offering flexibility through hyperparameter tuning rather than retraining, with theoretical and empirical validation of its effectiveness.

Abstract: A key strategy for balancing performance and cost in modern machine learning systems is to dynamically route queries to either a low-cost model or a more expensive oracle (such as a large pretrained model or human expert), an approach known as model routing. In this work we present a new uncertainty-aware router that (1) avoids unnecessary oracle calls on inherently ambiguous queries, and (2) adapts dynamically to different loss functions and cost parameters through simple hyperparameter changes, without retraining. Our method, applicable to any classification setting where multiple independent annotations per input are available, is based on decomposing total uncertainty into irreducible and reducible components using higher-order predictors [Ahdritz et al., 2025]. This enables a unified approach to both routing and abstention: predict with the weak model when uncertainty is low, route to the oracle when reducible uncertainty is high, and abstain when irreducible uncertainty is high. Our router comes with strong theoretical guarantees bounding regret relative to optimal task-specific routers. We conduct experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets that demonstrate the benefits of our approach in suitable regimes -- in particular, whenever reducible and irreducible uncertainty are not too correlated.

</details>


### [301] [The Minimax Rate of Second-Order Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07808)
*Kamil Ciosek,Banafsheh Rafiee,Sina Ghiassian,Nicolò Felicioni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper establishes the minimax optimal rate (Õ(1/√n)) for estimating second-order calibration error in binary classification, improving over previous O(n^{-1/4}) methods, and provides a new bucket-free definition and finite-sample guarantees for second-order Platt scaling recalibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Second-order calibration quantifies whether a predictor's epistemic uncertainty estimate matches the conditional variance of label probability on its level sets. Existing estimation methods (bucketing, kernel smoothing) achieve suboptimal O(n^{-1/4}) rates, and there's a need for bucket-free definitions and finite-sample guarantees for recalibration procedures like second-order Platt scaling.

Method: Key insight: the sech perturbation kernel makes calibration functions analytic in a strip of half-width hπ/2. Using this property, the authors apply polynomial regression to estimate calibration error, achieving Õ(1/√n) rate with explicit constants. They also provide matching Ω(1/√n) lower bound and relate bucket-free to bucketed formulations.

Result: Established minimax optimal rate Õ(1/√n) for second-order calibration error estimation (matching upper and lower bounds). Provided first finite-sample guarantee for second-order Platt scaling recalibration. Introduced bucket-free definition of second-order calibration and quantitative relationship to bucketed formulation. Experimental validation confirms predicted rates and recalibration quality.

Conclusion: The sech perturbation kernel enables analytic calibration functions and optimal Õ(1/√n) estimation rate, a qualitative improvement over prior methods. This provides practical, theoretically-grounded tools for assessing and improving higher-order predictors' uncertainty estimates through second-order Platt scaling recalibration.

Abstract: We characterize the minimax rate of estimating the second-order calibration error for binary classification, which quantifies whether a higher-order predictor's epistemic-uncertainty estimate matches the conditional variance of the label probability on its level sets. Our key observation is that the sech perturbation kernel, previously used only to enforce smoothness of calibration functions, in fact makes them analytic in a strip of half-width $hπ/2$. Polynomial regression then estimates the calibration error at rate $\tilde{O}(1/\sqrt{n})$, with explicit constants, a qualitative improvement over the $O(n^{-1/4})$ rate achievable by bucketing or kernel smoothing. A matching $Ω(1/\sqrt{n})$ lower bound establishes minimax optimality up to logarithmic factors. As a corollary, we give the first finite-sample guarantee for second-order Platt scaling, yielding a post-hoc procedure that recalibrates both the mean prediction and the epistemic-variance estimate of any higher-order predictor. Along the way, we provide a bucket-free definition of second-order calibration and relate it quantitatively to the bucketed formulation of Ahdritz et al. [2025]. Our experiments confirm the predicted rate and the quality of the recalibrated uncertainties.

</details>


### [302] [OrScale: Orthogonalised Optimization with Layer-Wise Trust-Ratio Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07815)
*Yuxuan Lou,Yang You*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OrScale is a trust-ratio extension of Muon that adds layer-wise adaptive learning rates by measuring actual parameter-space update directions, improving training performance across vision and language tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While Muon orthogonalizes matrix-valued updates to improve neural network training, it lacks layer-wise adaptation—each layer's update magnitude is primarily controlled by a global learning rate rather than being individually scaled based on the layer's characteristics.

Method: OrScale extends Muon with trust-ratio scaling based on a simple rule: the denominator in layer-wise ratios should measure the Frobenius norm of the actual parameter-space direction that will be applied. The method includes OrScale for general matrix layers and OrScale-LM for language models, which combines Moonlight shape scaling with one-time per-layer calibration to ensure all trust ratios start at one.

Result: OrScale achieves state-of-the-art performance on CIFAR-10/DavidNet (improving from 93.70% to 94.05% validation top-1), and OrScale-LM improves FineWeb-Edu pre-training versus Muon+Moonlight at three of four scales from 125M to 1.1B parameters, while outperforming AdamW at every scale.

Conclusion: OrScale successfully addresses the limitations of Muon by introducing effective layer-wise adaptive learning rates through trust-ratio scaling based on actual parameter-space update directions, providing both theoretical convergence guarantees and empirical performance improvements across vision and language modeling tasks.

Abstract: Muon improves neural-network training by orthogonalizing matrix-valued updates, but it leaves each layer's update magnitude controlled mostly by a global learning rate. We introduce OrScale, a trust-ratio extension of Muon built on a simple rule: the denominator of a layer-wise ratio should measure the Frobenius norm of the actual parameter-space direction that will be applied. This yields OrScale for general matrix layers and OrScale-LM for language models, where Moonlight shape scaling is combined with one-time per-layer calibration so every trust ratio starts at one. We analyze why three natural Muon-LAMB hybrids fail through shape-degenerate denominators, raw-momentum clip saturation, and decoupled weight-decay runaway, and show that the real-update-direction denominator with coupled weight decay avoids these failures. Theoretically, OrScale admits an O(1/sqrt(T)) nonconvex convergence guarantee in a nuclear-norm criterion, a strict layer-adaptive descent gain under measurable layer heterogeneity, and calibration properties that preserve muP-style learning-rate transfer at initialization. Empirically, OrScale ranks first on CIFAR-10/DavidNet across three seeds, improving Muon from 93.70% to 94.05% validation top-1, and OrScale-LM improves FineWeb-Edu pre-training versus Muon+Moonlight at three of four scales from 125M to 1.1B parameters while outperforming AdamW at every scale.

</details>


### [303] [Scaling Categorical Flow Maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07820)
*Oscar Davis,Anastasiia Filippova,Pierre Ablin,Victor Turrisi,Amitis Shidani,Marco Cuturi,Louis Béthune*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper demonstrates that Continuous Flow Matching (CFM) models can scale effectively to 1.7B parameters, achieving high-quality text generation in as few as 4 inference steps while maintaining competitive performance on language modeling benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous diffusion and flow matching models offer advantages over autoregressive approaches (faster sampling, tilting capabilities) but had only been tested at small scales (<1B parameters). The paper aims to test the scalability of Categorical Flow Maps (CFMs) for language modeling.

Method: Trained a 1.7B-parameter base flow model on 2.1T tokens, then self-distilled it into a Categorical Flow Map (CFM) that enables few-step inference. Introduced a likelihood bound for CFMs in semi-discrete settings and addressed scaling challenges with loss weighting and time scheduling.

Result: CFM generates diverse, high-quality text in as few as 4 inference steps while maintaining near-data-level token entropy. Achieves LM benchmark results comparable to discrete diffusion methods, demonstrating successful scaling to 1.7B parameters.

Conclusion: CFMs scale effectively for language modeling, offering fast sampling while maintaining quality. The work provides practical insights for training these models at scale and shows they can be competitive with existing discrete diffusion methods.

Abstract: Continuous diffusion and flow matching models could represent a powerful alternative to autoregressive approaches for language modelling (LM), as they unlock a host of advantages currently reserved for continuous modalities, including accelerated sampling and tilting. Recently, several works have demonstrated the possibility of generating discrete data continuously by a simple flow matching process between a Gaussian and the one-hot encoded data distribution. They have further shown the feasibility of accelerated sampling via Categorical Flow Maps (CFMs), resulting in competitive sample quality in the few-step regime. However, this method had only been evaluated at relatively modest scales ($<1$B), leaving the question of its scalability completely open. In this article, we train a $1.7$B-parameter base flow model on $2.1$T tokens and self-distill it into a CFM that generates diverse, high-quality text in as few as $4$ inference steps while maintaining near-data-level token entropy. Furthermore, we introduce a likelihood bound for CFMs in the semi-discrete setting, and show that they can be used to score the model on standard LM benchmarks, achieving results in the same range as discrete diffusion methods. Finally, we uncover some of the challenges that arise from training these models at scale, and we provide prescriptive insights on loss weighting and time scheduling.

</details>


### [304] [RelAgent: LLM Agents as Data Scientists for Relational Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07840)
*Xingyue Huang,Louis Tichelman,Jinwoo Kim,Krzysztof Olejniczak,İsmail İlkan Ceylan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RelAgent is an LLM-based autonomous data scientist for relational learning that uses LLM agents to construct SQL feature programs and select models in a search phase, then executes deterministic, interpretable predictions using standard database systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Relational learning is challenging with various existing approaches (graph-based, tabular, sequence-based) having different advantages and limitations. There's a need for a system that can automate feature engineering and model selection while maintaining interpretability and scalability for relational data.

Method: Two-phase approach: 1) Search phase where an LLM agent uses database, validation, and evaluation tools to construct SQL feature programs and select predictive models; 2) Inference phase where the resulting program executes without LLM calls, using SQL queries and classical models.

Result: The system produces a final predictor consisting of SQL queries and a classical model that enables fast, deterministic, and intrinsically interpretable predictions. Features are human-readable queries, and predictions depend only on the resulting query-defined feature map, enabling scalable deployment using standard database systems.

Conclusion: RelAgent provides an effective LLM-based autonomous data science solution for relational learning that combines the power of LLMs for exploration with the efficiency and interpretability of classical database systems for deployment.

Abstract: Relational learning is a challenging problem that has motivated a wide range of approaches, including graph-based models (e.g., graph neural networks, graph transformers), tabular methods (e.g., tabular foundation models), and sequence-based approaches (e.g., large language models), each with its own advantages and limitations. We propose RelAgent, an LLM-based autonomous data scientist for relational learning, which operates in two phases. In the search phase, an LLM agent uses database, validation, and evaluation workspace tools to construct SQL feature programs and select a predictive model. In the inference phase, the resulting program is executed without further LLM calls. The final predictor consists of SQL queries and a classical model, enabling fast, deterministic, and intrinsically interpretable predictions: features are human-readable queries, and predictions depend only on the resulting query-defined feature map, enabling scalable deployment using standard database systems.

</details>


### [305] [Distributional simplicity bias and effective convexity in Energy Based Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07844)
*Aurélien Decelle,Alfonso de Jesús Navas Gómez,Beatriz Seoane*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Energy-based learning for generative modeling exhibits convergence to proper fixed points that match target distributions, with lower-order interactions learned first due to gradient dynamics hierarchy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Energy-based learning is powerful but suffers from non-convex training problems including sensitivity to initialization, poor local optima, and unstable gradient dynamics. The paper aims to understand these training dynamics better.

Method: Uses dynamical analysis through the effective model framework (generalized Ising model with higher-order interactions or Fourier expansion of energy). Analyzes gradient flow for learning strictly positive distributions over binary variables.

Result: Identifies two types of fixed points: data-consistent points that exactly reproduce target distributions, and spurious points that satisfy stationarity without matching targets. Shows perturbations around data-consistent points are stable or neutral. Demonstrates gradient dynamics induce hierarchy where lower-order interactions are learned before higher-order ones.

Conclusion: Provides mechanistic explanation for distributional simplicity bias and clarifies why fixed points not data-consistent at low orders aren't observed in practice, offering insights into the robustness of energy-based learning despite non-convexity.

Abstract: Energy-based learning is a powerful framework for generative modelling, but its training is inherently non-convex, leading potentially to sensitivity to initialisation, poor local optima, and unstable gradient dynamics. We present a dynamical analysis of energy-based learning through the lens of the effective model, which can be interpreted as either a generalised Ising model with higher-order interactions or the Fourier expansion of the energy. Under sufficient expressivity, we show that the gradient flow induced by learning strictly positive distributions over binary variables admits two types of fixed points: data-consistent points, which exactly reproduce the target distribution, and spurious points, which satisfy stationarity without matching the target distribution. Around data-consistent points, we show that perturbations are either stable or neutral, with neutral directions leaving the effective model invariant. Finally, we show that gradient dynamics induce a hierarchy in which lower-order interactions are learned before higher-order ones. This provides a mechanistic explanation for the distributional simplicity bias and clarifies why fixed points that are not data-consistent at low orders are not observed in practice.

</details>


### [306] [Actor-Critic Algorithm for Dynamic Expectile and CVaR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07857)
*Yudong Luo,Erick Delage*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel model-free actor-critic algorithm for optimizing dynamic risk using stochastic policies without transition perturbation, leveraging elicitability for value learning and demonstrating superior risk-averse performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optimizing dynamic risk with stochastic policies faces dual challenges: policy updates typically require transition perturbation (disturbing state transitions), and value learning often relies on model-based approaches which can be inefficient or impractical.

Method: Proposes surrogate policy gradient without transition perturbation under softmax parameterization, develops model-free value learning for dynamic expectile and conditional value-at-risk using elicitability, and constructs a model-free off-policy actor-critic algorithm inspired by Expected SARSA and Expected Policy Gradient.

Result: Empirical results in domains with verifiable risk-averse behavior show the algorithm successfully learns risk-averse policies and consistently outperforms existing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses challenges in dynamic risk optimization by combining model-free policy updates without transition perturbation with elicitability-based value learning, resulting in superior performance for learning risk-averse behaviors.

Abstract: Optimizing dynamic risk with stochastic policies is challenging in both policy updates and value learning. The former typically requires transition perturbation, while the latter may rely on model-based approaches. To address these challenges, we propose a surrogate policy gradient without transition perturbation under softmax policy parameterization. We further develop model-free value learning methods for dynamic expectile and conditional value-at-risk by leveraging elicitability. Finally, inspired by Expected SARSA and Expected Policy Gradient, a model-free off-policy actor-critic algorithm is constructed. Empirical results in domains with verifiable risk-averse behavior show that our algorithm can learn risk-averse policy and consistently outperforms other existing methods.

</details>


### [307] [ADKO: Agentic Decentralized Knowledge Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07863)
*Lucas Nerone Rillo,Zhanhong Jiang,Nastaran Saadati,Aditya Balu,Baskar Ganapathysubramanian,Chinmay Hegde,Soumik Sarkar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ADKO is a decentralized framework where autonomous agents collaborate on black-box optimization using compressed knowledge tokens instead of sharing raw data, achieving sample efficiency, privacy, and communication efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of collaborative optimization across multiple agents where privacy concerns, communication constraints, and heterogeneous objectives prevent sharing raw data or model parameters. Traditional approaches lack efficient mechanisms for knowledge exchange while preserving privacy and minimizing communication overhead.

Method: Each agent maintains a private Gaussian Process surrogate trained on local data. Agents communicate through compact "knowledge tokens" containing directional signals, advantage scores, and optional language model insights. The framework unifies GP-Upper Confidence Bound, parallel Bayesian optimization, decentralized learning, and LM-guided discovery, with formal analysis of dual information loss (token compression and LM approximation error).

Result: Theoretical analysis shows cumulative regret decomposes into GP error, LM bias, LM noise, and compression loss, with necessary/sufficient conditions for sublinear regret. Experiments on neural architecture search and scientific discovery demonstrate consistent improvements over baselines, validating the theory.

Conclusion: ADKO provides a principled framework for decentralized collaborative optimization that balances privacy, communication efficiency, and performance through formal analysis of information compression and approximation errors, enabling practical applications where data sharing is constrained.

Abstract: We present Agentic Decentralized Knowledge Optimization (ADKO), a framework for collaborative black-box optimization across autonomous agents that achieves sample efficiency, privacy preservation, heterogeneous-objective handling, and communication efficiency. Each agent maintains a private Gaussian Process (GP) surrogate trained on local data and communicates only through knowledge tokens-compact, lossy summaries containing directional signals, advantage scores, and optional language-model (LM) insights-without sharing raw data or model parameters. ADKO unifies GP-Upper Confidence Bound (GP-UCB), parallel Bayesian optimization, decentralized learning, and LM-guided discovery. We provide the first formal analysis of dual information loss: token compression, quantified via mutual-information-based fidelity, and LM approximation error, decomposed into bias and stochastic noise. Our main result shows cumulative regret decomposes into GP error, LM bias, LM noise, and compression loss, with necessary and sufficient conditions for sublinear regret. We also propose fidelity-aware token pruning to preserve high-information tokens under memory budget. Experiments on neural architecture search and scientific discovery validate the theory and show consistent improvements over strong baselines.

</details>


### [308] [Black-box model classification under the discriminative factorization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07878)
*Hayden Helm,Merrick Ohata,Carey Priebe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces discriminative factorization framework to distinguish high- vs low-quality query sets for black-box model classification, showing probability of chance-level classification decays exponentially with query budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When auditing black-box generative systems via API queries, model representations based on embedded responses are highly sensitive to query set quality. Need systematic way to distinguish good vs bad query sets for accurate model-level classification.

Method: Introduces discriminative factorization framework to measure query set quality. Analyzes probability of chance-level classification as function of query budget, showing exponential decay. Estimates factorization parameters from data.

Result: On three auditing tasks, estimated factorization parameters accurately predict empirical performance decay rates. Query sets selected using estimated discriminative field reproduce empirical ordering of oracle query sets.

Conclusion: Discriminative factorization provides principled framework for selecting high-quality query sets in black-box model auditing, enabling efficient and accurate model-level classification with theoretical guarantees on performance decay.

Abstract: Access to modern generative systems is often restricted to querying an API (the ``black-box" setting) and many properties of the system are unknown to the user at inference time. While recent work has shown that low-dimensional representations of models based on the relationship between their embedded responses to a set of queries are useful for inferring model-level properties, the quality of these representations is highly sensitive to the query set. We introduce the \emph{discriminative factorization} to distinguish between high- and low-quality query sets in the context of black-box model-level classification. Under this framework, the probability of chance-level classification decays exponentially in the query budget. On three auditing tasks, estimated factorization parameters predict the empirical performance decay rate. We conclude by showing that query sets selected using the estimated discriminative field reproduce the empirical ordering of oracle query sets.

</details>


### [309] [Enhancing Federated Quadruplet Learning: Stochastic Client Selection and Embedding Stability Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07888)
*Ozgu Goksu,Nicolas Pugeault*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedQuad is a novel federated learning method that uses metric learning to minimize intra-class representations and maximize inter-class splits across clients, improving generalization under data heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated Learning suffers from degraded global model generalization due to data heterogeneity across clients, especially with limited data and class imbalance. Representation misalignment during model aggregation further exacerbates this problem.

Method: FedQuad explicitly enforces minimizing intra-class representations while enabling inter-class splits across clients. It jointly minimizes distances between positive pairs and maximizes distances between negative pairs to mitigate representation misalignment during model aggregation.

Result: The method demonstrates consistent improvements over existing baselines on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet under diverse non-IID settings with varying numbers of clients. The paper also provides comprehensive analysis showing metric learning approaches effectively alleviate representation collapse under heterogeneous data distributions.

Conclusion: FedQuad successfully addresses federated learning challenges under data heterogeneity by leveraging metric learning principles, showing that explicit enforcement of intra-class compactness and inter-class separation improves model generalization in decentralized settings.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables decentralised model training across distributed clients without requiring data centralisation. However, the generalisation performance of the global model is usually degraded by data heterogeneity across clients, particularly under limited data availability and class imbalance. To address this challenge, we propose FedQuad, a novel method that explicitly enforces minimising intra-class representations while enabling inter-class splits across clients. By jointly minimising distances between positive pairs and maximising distances between negative pairs, the proposed approach mitigates representation misalignment introduced during model aggregation. We evaluate our method on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet under diverse non-IID settings and varying numbers of clients, demonstrating consistent improvements over existing baselines. Additionally, we provide a comprehensive analysis of metric learning-based approaches in both centralised and federated environments, highlighting their effectiveness in alleviating representation collapse under heterogeneous data distributions.

</details>


### [310] [Adaptive Regularization for Sparsity Control in Bregman-Based Optimizers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07892)
*Ahmad Aloradi,Tim Roith,Emanuël A. P. Habets,Daniel Tenbrinck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An adaptive regularization scheme for sparse training that automatically adjusts the regularization parameter λ to achieve target sparsity, overcoming the parameter sensitivity issues of traditional ℓ₁ penalty methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional sparse training methods using ℓ₁ penalties suffer from parameter sensitivity, where the mapping between regularization parameter λ and final sparsity rate is non-trivial. This is especially problematic for Bregman-based optimizers (LinBreg and AdaBreg), which require expensive trial-and-error sweeps to achieve user-specified sparsity.

Method: Proposes an adaptive regularization scheme that dynamically updates λ based on the difference between the model's current sparsity and the target sparsity. This eliminates the need for manual parameter tuning and expensive sweeps.

Result: The method reliably achieves sparsity targets ranging between 75% and 99% on automatic speaker verification tasks using ECAPA-TDNN and ResNet34 on VoxCeleb and CNCeleb datasets. It converges faster during early training than oracle-tuned baselines and matches or surpasses their final performance in equal error rate.

Conclusion: The adaptive regularization scheme effectively addresses parameter sensitivity in sparse training while maintaining performance benefits. It inherits robustness properties from non-adaptive methods and offers practical advantages for achieving precise sparsity targets without extensive hyperparameter tuning.
