Abstract: Sparse training reduces the memory and computational costs of deep neural networks. However, sparse optimization methods, e.g., those adding an $\ell_1$ penalty, often control sparsity only indirectly through a regularization parameter $λ$, whose mapping to the final sparsity rate is non-trivial. In our experiments, we found this parameter sensitivity to be particularly pronounced for Bregman-based optimizers. Specifically, the two variants LinBreg and AdaBreg reach the same sparsity at $λ$ values that differ by up to two orders of magnitude, requiring expensive trial-and-error sweeps to achieve a user-specified sparsity. To address this, we propose an adaptive regularization scheme that updates $λ$ based on the difference between the model's current sparsity and the target sparsity. We analyze the resulting algorithm and evaluate it on automatic speaker verification with ECAPA-TDNN and ResNet34 on VoxCeleb and CNCeleb. The proposed method reliably achieves sparsity targets ranging between 75% and 99%. It also converges faster than the oracle-tuned non-adaptive baseline during early training and matches or surpasses its final performance in equal error rate. We further show that the adaptive scheme inherits key properties from its non-adaptive counterpart, including improved out-of-distribution robustness over the dense baselines.

</details>


### [311] [Curvature Beyond Positivity: Greedy Guarantees for Arbitrary Submodular Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07902)
*Yixin Chen,Alan Kuhnle*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends classical curvature concept to all submodular functions (including non-monotone and negative-valued), achieving first multiplicative approximation guarantees for general submodular optimization beyond monotonicity and non-negativity constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Submodular optimization typically assumes monotone non-negative functions, but many practical objectives have costs making them negative on some inputs. Existing work handles negativity and non-monotonicity separately with additive bounds or special parameters, lacking a unified classical framework.

Method: Extends curvature parameter to all submodular functions, handles both non-monotonicity and negativity through this single classical concept. Uses greedy algorithm with pruning to achieve curvature-controlled multiplicative ratio for any submodular function. Also develops multilinear-extension variant for general combinatorial constraints.

Result: First multiplicative approximation guarantee for any submodular function (including negative values). For non-monotone regime (1 ≤ c_g < 2.2), bound beats best known uniform ratio of 0.401 for non-negative functions. Recovers classical (1-e^{-c_g})/c_g guarantee for monotone functions. Experimental validation on cost-penalized experimental design, coverage, feature selection, and Multi-News passage selection.

Conclusion: Curvature provides a unified framework extending classical submodular optimization theory to handle both non-monotonicity and negativity simultaneously, with practical applications validated through experiments and theoretical guarantees that recover or improve upon existing bounds.

Abstract: Submodular functions -- functions exhibiting diminishing returns -- are central to machine learning. When the objective is monotone and non-negative, the greedy algorithm achieves a tight $63\%$ approximation. But many practical objectives incorporate costs that make them negative on some inputs, and all existing multiplicative guarantees require non-negativity. Prior work handles negativity through additive bounds for the special class of decomposable functions and non-monotonicity through partial-monotonicity parameters, but these address each difficulty in isolation and neither extends the classical structural theory. We extend \emph{curvature} -- a parameter measuring how far a function deviates from linearity -- to all submodular functions, handling both non-monotonicity and negativity through a single classical concept. A greedy algorithm with pruning achieves a curvature-controlled multiplicative ratio for \emph{any} submodular function, including those taking negative values -- the first such guarantee beyond monotonicity and non-negativity. In the non-monotone regime $1 \le c_g < 2.2$, the bound strictly beats the best known uniform ratio of $0.401$ (for non-negative $f$), and it recovers the classical $(1-e^{-c_g})/c_g$ guarantee for monotone functions. A multilinear-extension variant extends the framework to general combinatorial constraints via multilinear relaxation. Experiments on cost-penalized experimental design, coverage, feature selection, and a curvature sweep on Multi-News passage selection support the theory.

</details>


### [312] [Flatness and Gradient Alignment Are Both Necessary: Spectral-Aware Gradient-Aligned Exploration for Multi-Distribution Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07914)
*Aristotelis Ballas,Christos Diou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper shows that both loss landscape flatness (sharpness) and gradient alignment must be jointly considered for generalization, proposes SAGE method that targets both properties, and demonstrates SOTA results on domain generalization and multi-task learning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods target either sharpness-aware minimization (flatness) or gradient alignment separately, but ignore the other geometric property. The authors show this omission is structurally unavoidable, and both must be considered in multi-distribution learning for optimal generalization.

Method: Proposes SAGE (Spectral-Aware Gradient-Aligned Exploration) with two components: 1) curvature component using the polar factor of each layer's gradient matrix (via Newton-Schulz iteration) to probe all directions equally, and 2) alignment component injecting isotropic noise scaled by cross-distribution gradient disagreement during descent.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows neither flatness nor gradient alignment alone can guarantee low excess risk. Empirically, SAGE establishes new SOTA on DomainBed domain-generalization benchmarks and improves base multi-task learning solvers, competing with or surpassing specialized SOTA methods.

Conclusion: Both loss landscape curvature and gradient alignment are essential for generalization in multi-distribution learning. The proposed SAGE method effectively addresses both properties simultaneously, leading to improved generalization performance across diverse benchmarks.

Abstract: Sharpness-aware and gradient-alignment methods have been shown to improve generalization, however each family of methods targets a single geometric property of the loss landscape, while ignoring the other. In this paper, we show that this omission is structurally unavoidable and that both flatness and gradient alignment should be considered in multi-distribution learning settings. Specifically, we derive an excess-risk decomposition that yields two additive leading-order terms: (i) an alignment term, controlled by the trace of $\bar{H}^{-1}Σ_g$ and (ii) a curvature term, controlled by $\bar{H}$, where $\bar{H}$ is the average Hessian and $Σ_g$ is the covariance of the gradient across distributions. Notably, $\bar{H}$ appears inverted in one and non-inverted in the other. We further show, via a counterexample, that neither quantity bounds the other in general, so no algorithm targeting only one term can guarantee low excess risk. Motivated by this decomposition, we propose SAGE (Spectral-Aware Gradient-Aligned Exploration) that targets both terms. The curvature component replaces SAM's gradient-scaled perturbation with the polar factor of each layer's gradient matrix, computed via Newton-Schulz iteration, so that the ascent step probes all directions with similar magnitude. On the other hand, the alignment component injects isotropic noise at the descent step, the magnitude of which scales with cross-distribution gradient disagreement. Experiments on five domain-generalization and two multi-task learning benchmarks show that the proposed method establishes a new state-of-the-art on DomainBed and acts as a general-purpose improvement to base MTL solvers, remaining competitive with, or even surpassing, state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [313] [Tree SAE: Learning Hierarchical Feature Structures in Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07922)
*Tue M. Cao,Hoang X. Nhat,Raed Alharbi,My T. Thai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tree SAE combines activation and reconstruction constraints to learn hierarchical feature structures, outperforming existing SAEs while maintaining competitive benchmark performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) methods for learning hierarchical features rely only on activation coverage, which often produces false positives where parent and child features are semantically unrelated. The activation condition alone is insufficient for capturing true hierarchical relationships.

Method: Introduced a novel reconstruction condition that enforces a deeper functional link between hierarchical levels. Combined both activation and reconstruction constraints to create the Tree SAE model, which learns hierarchical structures directly from within the feature set.

Result: Tree SAEs significantly surpass existing SAEs at learning hierarchical pairs while maintaining competitive performance to state-of-the-art on several key benchmarks. Also demonstrates practical utility in mapping child feature subspaces and uncovering complex hierarchical concept structures in large language models.

Conclusion: The proposed Tree SAE with both activation and reconstruction constraints is more effective at learning true hierarchical relationships than existing methods that rely solely on activation coverage, enabling better understanding of hierarchical concept structures in neural networks.

Abstract: Learning hierarchical features in Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) is essential for capturing the structured nature of real-world data and mitigating issues like feature absorption or splitting. Existing works attempt to identify hierarchical relationships within independent feature sets by relying on activation coverage, the assumption that child feature should only activate when its parent feature activates. However, we demonstrate that this condition alone is insufficient; that is, it often produces false positives where parent and child concepts are semantically unrelated. To address this, we introduce a novel reconstruction condition that enforces a deeper functional link between hierarchical levels. By combining both activation and reconstruction constraints, we propose the Tree SAE, a model designed to learn hierarchical structures directly from within the feature set. Our results demonstrate that Tree SAEs significantly surpass the existing SAEs at learning hierarchical pairs while maintaining competitive performance to the state-of-the-art on several key benchmarks. Finally, we demonstrate the practical utility of our Tree SAE in mapping the geometry of child feature subspaces and uncovering the complex hierarchical concept structures encoded within large language models.

</details>


### [314] [Prototype Guided Post-pretraining for Single-Cell Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07938)
*Sachini Weerasekara,Natasha Darras,Sagar Kamarthi,Colles Price,Jacqueline Isaacs*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CellRefine is a post-pretraining method that refines single-cell foundation models by incorporating marker-gene sets as structural priors between pretraining and fine-tuning stages, achieving up to 15% performance gains on downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current single-cell pretrained models (inspired by language models treating genes as tokens and cells as sentences) are limited by the long-tailed nature of cell-type distributions and struggle to generalize under covariate shifts in gene expression data. Fine-tuning alone doesn't fully address these limitations, as performance remains bounded.

Method: CellRefine is a post-pretraining method that operates between the pretraining and fine-tuning stages. It uses a multi-faceted objective that incorporates marker-gene sets as structural priors to guide post-pretraining and refine the latent embedding manifold of cells.

Result: Empirical results across multiple computational biology tasks show that CellRefine consistently improves downstream performance, yielding gains up to 15%.

Conclusion: Introducing a post-pretraining stage with marker-gene set priors between pretraining and fine-tuning effectively addresses limitations of current single-cell foundation models, enabling better generalization and performance on downstream tasks.

Abstract: Single-cell representation learning (SCRL) from gene expression data offers a way to uncover the complex regulatory logic underlying cellular function. Inspired by large language models in natural language modeling, several single-cell pretrained models have recently been proposed that treat genes as tokens and cells as sentences. However, these models are fundamentally limited by the long-tailed nature of cell-type distributions and struggle to generalize under covariate shifts in gene expression data. While fine-tuning is often used to mitigate these issues, we observe that performance remains bounded. To address this challenge, we introduce CellRefine, a post-pretraining method that operates between the pretraining and fine-tuning stages of a single-cell foundation model. CellRefine uses a multi-faceted objective that incorporates marker-gene sets as structural priors to guide post-pretraining and refine the latent embedding manifold of cells. Across multiple computational biology tasks, empirical results show that CellRefine consistently improves downstream performance, yielding gains up to 15%.

</details>


### [315] [Slowly Annealed Langevin Dynamics: Theory and Applications to Training-Free Guided Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07950)
*Atsushi Nitanda,Dake Bu,Yueming Lyu,Tanya Veeravalli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SALD is a sampler that tracks moving target distributions via time slowdown with convergence guarantees, and VA-SALD extends it for training-free guided generation with pretrained score-based models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by training-free guided generation with pretrained score-based generative models, where tracking moving target distributions is needed for sampling from guided distributions without retraining.

Method: Introduces Slowly Annealed Langevin Dynamics (SALD) with time slowdown for tracking moving targets, and Velocity-Aware SALD (VA-SALD) which explicitly incorporates marginal distributions of pretrained models and uses slowdown to correct guidance-induced deviation.

Result: Establishes non-asymptotic convergence guarantees via KL differential inequality, showing slowdown improves tracking through contraction of intermediate targets. Provides principled framework for training-free guided generation with convergence guarantees.

Conclusion: SALD and VA-SALD offer theoretically grounded frameworks for sampling from moving target distributions and training-free guided generation with pretrained models, with convergence analysis clarifying roles of intermediate functional inequalities and guidance bias.

Abstract: We study Slowly Annealed Langevin Dynamics (SALD), a sampler for tracking a path of moving target distributions and approximating the terminal target through time slowdown. We establish non-asymptotic convergence guarantees via a KL differential inequality, showing that slowdown improves tracking through contraction of intermediate targets and the complexity of the path. Motivated by training-free guided generation with pretrained score-based generative models, we further introduce Velocity-Aware SALD (VA-SALD), which explicitly incorporates the underlying marginal distributions of the pretrained model and uses slowdown to correct the additional deviation induced by guidance. This yields a principled framework for training-free guided generation for diffusion-based and related generative model families, together with convergence guarantees that clarify the roles of intermediate functional inequalities and guidance bias. Code is available at https://github.com/anitan0925/sald.

</details>


### [316] [Convergent Stochastic Training of Attention and Understanding LoRA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07959)
*Zhengkai Sun,Dibyakanti Kumar,Alejandro F Frangi,Anirbit Mukherjee,Mingfei Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformers with attention layers and LoRA adaptation can be proven trainable via stochastic methods without data or architecture size assumptions, by establishing Poincaré inequalities for their Gibbs measures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers with attention layers are standard in many applications, and LoRA is commonly used for large models to achieve better accuracy-size trade-offs. However, there's a need to rigorously establish the trainability of such models under stochastic optimization methods.

Method: The authors use a unified framework to prove that for any mild regularization, the empirical regression loss on attention layers and LoRA on shallow neural networks both induce Poincaré inequalities for their corresponding Gibbs measures. They then invoke recent results showing that certain SDEs (which mimic SGD) minimize these losses.

Result: First-of-its-kind trainability results for attention layers and neural networks with LoRA adaptation are established. These results hold without any assumptions on the data or the size of the architecture.

Conclusion: The work provides rigorous theoretical foundations for the trainability of attention-based models and LoRA-adapted neural networks under stochastic optimization methods, establishing that SGD-like dynamics can successfully minimize their losses under mild regularization conditions.

Abstract: Transformers have revolutionized machine learning and deploying attention layers in the model is increasingly standard across a myriad of applications. Further, for large models, it is common to implement Low Rank Adaptation (LoRA), whereby a factorized parameterization of them is trained, to achieve a surprisingly beneficial accuracy-size trade-off. In this work, via a unified framework we rigorously establish trainability of such models under stochastic methods. We prove that for any mild regularization, the empirical regression loss on a attention layer and LoRA on a shallow neural net, both induce Poincaré inequality for the corresponding Gibbs' measure. Then it follows via invoking recent results that a certain SDE, which mimics the SGD, minimizes the corresponding losses. In both the cases, our first-of-its-kind results of trainability on attention and nets, do not rely on any assumptions on the data or the size of the architecture.

</details>


### [317] [Graph Representation Learning Augmented Model Manipulation on Federated Fine-Tuning of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07961)
*Hanlin Cai,Kai Li,Houtianfu Wang,Haofan Dong,Yichen Li,Falko Dressler,Ozgur B. Akan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AugMP is a novel attack strategy that uses graph representation learning and augmented Lagrangian optimization to craft stealthy yet effective malicious updates that degrade performance in federated fine-tuning of LLMs while evading conventional defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated fine-tuning (FFT) enables privacy-preserving collaborative LLM adaptation but remains vulnerable to model manipulation attacks where adversarial participants can corrupt the aggregation process by uploading malicious updates that degrade global model performance.

Method: 1) Design a graph representation learning framework to capture feature correlations among benign LLM updates to guide malicious update generation. 2) Develop an iterative manipulation algorithm based on augmented Lagrangian dual formulation to optimize malicious updates that embed adversarial objectives while preserving benign-like parameter characteristics.

Result: AugMP achieves strongest manipulation performance among all baselines, reducing global LLM accuracy by up to 26% and degrading average local LLM agent accuracy by up to 22%. Maintains high statistical and geometric consistency with benign updates, enabling evasion of conventional distance- and similarity-based defense methods.

Conclusion: The AugMP strategy effectively demonstrates serious security vulnerabilities in FFT-based LLMs, highlighting the need for more robust defense mechanisms against sophisticated model manipulation attacks in federated learning settings.

Abstract: Federated fine-tuning (FFT) has emerged as a privacy-preserving paradigm for collaboratively adapting large language models (LLMs). Built upon federated learning, FFT enables distributed agents to jointly refine a shared pretrained LLM by aggregating local LLM updates without sharing local raw data. However, FFT-based LLMs remain vulnerable to model manipulation threats, in which adversarial participants upload manipulated LLM updates that corrupt the aggregation process and degrade the performance of the global LLM. In this paper, we propose an Augmented Model maniPulation (AugMP) strategy against FFT-based LLMs. Specifically, we design a novel graph representation learning framework that captures feature correlations among benign LLM updates to guide the generation of malicious updates. To enhance manipulation effectiveness and stealthiness, we develop an iterative manipulation algorithm based on an augmented Lagrangian dual formulation. Through this formulation, malicious updates are optimized to embed adversarial objectives while preserving benign-like parameter characteristics. Experimental results across multiple LLM backbones demonstrate that the AugMP strategy achieves the strongest manipulation performance among all competing baselines, reducing the global LLM accuracy by up to 26% and degrading the average accuracy of local LLM agents by up to 22%. Meanwhile, AugMP maintains high statistical and geometric consistency with benign updates, enabling it to evade conventional distance- and similarity-based defense methods.

</details>


### [318] [FLAM: Evaluating Model Performance with Aggregatable Measures in Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07962)
*Fabian Stricker,Jose A. Peregrina,David Bermbach,Christian Zirpins*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FLAM is a federated learning evaluation method that produces the same results as centralized evaluation without needing a global test dataset, addressing discrepancies between participant-based and centralized metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Performance evaluation in federated learning is challenging because data is distributed across participants. Existing aggregation strategies (like weighted averaging) don't always match centralized evaluation results, and current definitions are tailored to accuracy but don't generalize to other metrics, leading to inconsistencies that conflict with FL objectives.

Method: The paper proposes FLAM (Federated Learning Aggregatable Measures), a performance evaluation method based on aggregatable measures. It examines underlying reasons for discrepancies between participant-based and centralized evaluation, then develops a method that yields identical results to centralized evaluation without requiring a global test dataset.

Result: FLAM addresses the discrepancy problem by providing aggregatable measures that produce the same evaluation results as centralized evaluation, enabling accurate performance assessment in federated settings without access to a global test dataset.

Conclusion: FLAM solves a fundamental problem in federated learning evaluation by ensuring consistency between participant-based and centralized metrics through aggregatable measures, enabling reliable performance assessment that aligns with FL objectives.

Abstract: Performance evaluation is essential for assessing the quality of machine learning (ML) models and guiding deployment decisions. In federated learning (FL), assessing the performance is challenging because data are distributed across participants. Consequently, the coordinator must rely on locally computed evaluation metrics and aggregate them to assess the global model. A key challenge is that common aggregation strategies, such as weighted averaging based on the local samples per participant, do not always produce the same results as centralized evaluation. Existing definitions of performance evaluation are largely tailored to accuracy and do not generalize to other metrics, leading to inconsistencies between participant-based and centralized evaluation. However, such discrepancies are inconsistent with the FL objective and lead to a wrong calculation of the metric.
  To address this issue, we examine the underlying reasons for these discrepancies and propose FLAM, a performance evaluation method based on aggregatable measures that yields the same results as centralized evaluation without the need for a global test dataset.

</details>


### [319] [Aggregation in conformal e-classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07963)
*Vladimir Vovk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cross-conformal e-prediction and its simplified modifications offer computationally efficient and flexible aggregation of conformal e-predictors while maintaining validity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard conformal predictors often face a trade-off between predictive efficiency and computational efficiency when aggregated. The paper aims to address this by studying methods that can aggregate conformal e-predictors without sacrificing validity while offering better conceptual simplicity and flexibility.

Method: The paper studies cross-conformal e-prediction (an existing aggregation method) and introduces modifications to make it conceptually simpler and more flexible. It focuses on experimental evaluation of these methods.

Result: The experimental study demonstrates that the proposed modifications to cross-conformal e-prediction maintain validity while offering improved conceptual simplicity and flexibility compared to the original method.

Conclusion: Modified cross-conformal e-prediction methods provide a practical solution for aggregating conformal e-predictors that balances computational efficiency, validity, and flexibility, addressing limitations of standard aggregation approaches.

Abstract: Aggregating conformal predictors is a standard way of balancing their predictive and computational efficiency while retaining their validity, at least approximately. An important advantage of conformal e-predictors is that they are easier to aggregate without sacrificing their validity. This paper studies experimentally cross-conformal e-prediction, which is an existing method of aggregating conformal e-predictors, and its modifications that are conceptually simpler and more flexible.

</details>


### [320] [When Diffusion Model Can Ignore Dimension: An Entropy-Based Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07969)
*Ahmad Aghapour,Erhan Bayraktar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion models remain efficient in high dimensions due to dependence on latent entropy rather than ambient dimension, particularly for Gaussian mixture targets where complexity scales linearly with Shannon entropy of latent mixture components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing convergence theory fails to fully explain why diffusion samplers remain efficient in high-dimensional spaces like images, as prior KL guarantees bound discretization error by ambient dimension rather than intrinsic structure.

Method: Develop an information-theoretic perspective on diffusion sampler convergence, analyzing Gaussian mixture targets to prove discretization error is controlled by Shannon entropy of latent mixture components rather than ambient dimension.

Result: For Gaussian mixture targets, discretization error depends on latent entropy, leading to step complexity that scales linearly with latent entropy and only logarithmically with data's second moment. Extension to discrete targets shows complexity depends on target entropy rather than embedding space dimension.

Conclusion: Diffusion sampling can remain efficient in high dimensions when data distributions have compact latent representations, explaining practical success with images and providing theoretical justification for observed efficiency.

Abstract: Diffusion models perform remarkably well on high-dimensional data such as images, often using only a modest number of reverse-time steps. Despite this practical success, existing convergence theory does not fully explain why such samplers remain efficient in high dimensions. Many prior KL guarantees bound the discretization error in terms of the ambient dimension, while other improved results replace this dependence using intrinsic-dimensional or geometric structure assumptions. In this work, we develop an alternative information-theoretic perspective on diffusion sampler convergence. We prove that, for Gaussian mixture targets, the discretization error is controlled by the Shannon entropy of the latent mixture component rather than by the ambient dimension. Consequently, the leading step complexity scales linearly with latent entropy and depends only logarithmically on the second moment of the data. Our analysis also extends to discrete target distributions, where the relevant complexity is the entropy of the target rather than the dimension of the embedding space. These results suggest that diffusion sampling can remain efficient in high-dimensional spaces when the data distribution admits a compact latent representation, as is widely believed to be the case for natural images.

</details>


### [321] [Self-Play Enhancement via Advantage-Weighted Refinement in Online Federated LLM Fine-Tuning with Real-Time Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07977)
*Seohyun Lee,Wenzhi Fang,Dong-Jun Han,Seyyedali Hosseinalipour,Christopher G. Brinton*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPEAR is an efficient online federated learning algorithm for LLM fine-tuning that uses feedback-guided self-play to create contrastive training pairs without needing expensive group generations or ground-truth contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current feedback-based learning systems have three major limitations: 1) they require offline setups, 2) need privileged ground-truth contexts for training, and 3) don't adequately address federated learning scenarios which are ideal for incorporating user feedback across large networks but require efficient methods for resource-constrained edge devices.

Method: SPEAR uses a feedback-guided self-play loop to construct naturally contrastive pairs per prompt. These pairs are then trained on using: (i) standard maximum likelihood on correct completions, and (ii) confidence-weighted unlikelihood on tail tokens of incorrect completions. The method works online without expensive group generations and only requires partial, non-answer feedback rather than full ground-truth contexts.

Result: SPEAR demonstrates superior performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines across various benchmark datasets. The implementation is publicly available on GitHub.

Conclusion: SPEAR successfully addresses key limitations of existing feedback-based learning systems by enabling efficient online federated fine-tuning of LLMs without requiring expensive group generations or privileged ground-truth contexts, making it suitable for real-world applications on resource-constrained edge devices.

Abstract: Recent works have advanced feedback-based learning systems, whereby a foundation model is able to intake incoming feedback (e.g., a user) to self-improve, creating a self-loop system of training. However, existing works are limited in needing to consider an offline setup to allow for such feedback-based methods, and are further limited in the need of requiring privileged ground-truth contexts for training. Moreover, there is limited consideration of federated learning (FL), which is particularly well-suited for incorporating external feedback across large networks of end users, for example, but requires methods to be efficient for training on resource-constrained edge devices. Therefore, we introduce SPEAR (Self-Play Enhancement via Advantage-Weighted Refinement), an efficient online learning algorithm for federated LLM fine-tuning. SPEAR utilizes a feedback-guided self-play loop to construct naturally contrastive pairs per prompt which are utilized to be trained on (i) standard maximum likelihood on correct completions and (ii) confidence-weighted unlikelihood on tail tokens of incorrect completions. Without the need of expensive group generations and ground-truth contexts for training (i.e., only partial, non-answer feedback), in contrast with existing works, SPEAR can be trained both online and in a resource-efficient manner. We validate SPEAR across various benchmark datasets, demonstrating its superior performance in comparison to state-of-the-art baselines. The implementation code is publicly available at https://github.com/lee3296/SPEAR.

</details>


### [322] [Susceptibilities and Patterning: A Primer on Linear Response in Bayesian Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07980)
*Chris Elliott,Daniel Murfet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a theory of susceptibilities for neural networks, defining how observables respond to data perturbations via posterior derivatives, connecting to influence matrices and solving patterning problems through linearized inverses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a theoretical framework for interpreting neural networks by understanding how they respond to data perturbations, building on statistical mechanics foundations and connecting to influence functions and structural analysis.

Method: Defines susceptibility as derivative of posterior expectation (equals posterior covariance by fluctuation-dissipation theorem), analyzes different observable choices yielding influence matrix and structural susceptibility matrix, establishes matrix as Jacobian of data-to-structure map, uses pseudo-inverse for linearized patterning solutions.

Result: Developed comprehensive susceptibility theory linking data perturbations to neural network responses, showed susceptibilities relate to influence functions and structural coordinates, demonstrated susceptibility matrix's role as data-to-structure Jacobian, provided linearized solution to patterning problems via matrix pseudo-inverse.

Conclusion: Susceptibility theory provides a unified framework for neural network interpretability through statistical mechanics foundations, connecting influence analysis, structural patterning, and loss landscape geometry, with practical applications for understanding and manipulating network behavior through data perturbations.

Abstract: These notes introduce the theory of susceptibilities as developed in [arXiv:2504.18274, arXiv:2601.12703] for interpreting neural networks. The susceptibility of an observable $φ$ to a data perturbation is defined as a derivative of a posterior expectation, which by the fluctuation--dissipation theorem equals a posterior covariance. Different choices of $φ$ yield different objects: per-sample losses give the influence matrix (the Bayesian influence function of [arXiv:2509.26544]), while component-localized observables give the structural susceptibility matrix that pairs model components with data patterns. The susceptibility matrix is (up to a factor of $nβ$) the Jacobian of the map from data distributions to structural coordinates; its pseudo-inverse provides a linearized solution to the patterning problem of [arXiv:2601.13548]: finding data perturbations that produce a desired structural change. We motivate the theory from its statistical-mechanical foundations, then give a detailed exposition of susceptibilities, their empirical estimators, and their connection to the geometry of the loss landscape.

</details>


### [323] [Bayesian Sensitivity of Causal Inference Estimators under Evidence-Based Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07993)
*Nikita Dhawan,Daniel Shen,Leonardo Cotta,Chris J. Maddison*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues that worst-case sensitivity analysis in causal inference can be overly pessimistic and proposes a Bayesian alternative called Bayesian Sensitivity Value (BSV) that uses prior knowledge for more realistic sensitivity assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing sensitivity analysis frameworks in causal inference rely on worst-case scenario assumptions, which can lead to uninformative conclusions or results that contradict prior knowledge about the world. The authors argue these pessimistic criteria often become problematic in practical applications.

Method: The authors generalize the s-value framework (Gupta & Rothenhäusler, 2023) to assess sensitivity of three common causal assumptions, then extend it with a new Bayesian Sensitivity Value (BSV) that computes expected sensitivity under priors constructed from real-world evidence, using Monte Carlo approximations for estimation.

Result: Empirical analysis shows worst-case sensitivity conclusions can rely on unrealistic changes in data-generating processes. The proposed BSV framework provides a more realistic sensitivity assessment, demonstrated through an observational study on diabetes treatments and weight loss.

Conclusion: Bayesian Sensitivity Value offers a more practical alternative to worst-case sensitivity analysis in causal inference by incorporating prior knowledge and real-world evidence, leading to more realistic and informative sensitivity assessments that better align with domain expertise.

Abstract: Causal inference, especially in observational studies, relies on untestable assumptions about the true data-generating process. Sensitivity analysis helps us determine how robust our conclusions are when we alter these underlying assumptions. Existing frameworks for sensitivity analysis are concerned with worst-case changes in assumptions. In this work, we argue that using such pessimistic criteria can often become uninformative or lead to conclusions contradicting our prior knowledge about the world. To demonstrate this claim, we generalize the recent s-value framework (Gupta & Rothenhäusler, 2023) to estimate the sensitivity of three different common assumptions in causal inference. Empirically, we find that, indeed, worst-case conclusions about sensitivity can rely on unrealistic changes in the data-generating process. To overcome this, we extend the s-value framework with a new sensitivity analysis criterion: Bayesian Sensitivity Value (BSV), which computes the expected sensitivity of an estimate to assumption violations under priors constructed from real-world evidence. We use Monte Carlo approximations to estimate this quantity and illustrate its applicability in an observational study on the effect of diabetes treatments on weight loss.

</details>


### [324] [STEPS: A Temporal Smooth Error Propagation Solver on the Manifolds for Test-Time Adaptation in Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08005)
*Jiaqi Liu,Yifan Ouyang,Zhifei Song,Sim Kuan Goh,Ashwaq Qasem*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STEPS: A Smooth Temporal Error Propagation Solver for test-time adaptation in time-series forecasting that reformulates the problem as a Dirichlet Boundary Value Problem to address issues with sparse or noisy prefix data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) for time series forecasting faces challenges in source-free online settings where adaptation signals are short, temporally correlated, and noisy. Existing methods suffer from weak identifiability, error accumulation, and unstable long-horizon corrections with sparse or contaminated prefixes.

Method: STEPS reformulates forecasting TTA as a Dirichlet Boundary Value Problem on a temporal manifold, using revealed prefix error as boundary condition for unknown future error field. It solves a smooth and bounded correction field using: 1) Local Solver for temporal smoothness propagation, 2) Global Solver for cross-window error memory, 3) Spatiotemporal Manifold Fusion (SMF) to integrate both solutions.

Result: Across six standard benchmarks and four frozen backbones, STEPS achieves average relative MSE reduction of 26.82% over zero-shot backbone, exceeding strongest TTA baseline by 12.77%. Additional sparse prefix and contamination tests confirm robustness under limited and noisy prefixes.

Conclusion: STEPS effectively addresses challenges in forecasting TTA by modeling error propagation as a smooth boundary value problem, demonstrating superior performance and robustness compared to existing methods in handling sparse and noisy adaptation signals.

Abstract: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) aims to improve time series forecasting under distribution shifts by using limited observations revealed during inference. However, forecasting TTA must operate in a source-free online setting, where the adaptation signal is short, temporally correlated, and potentially noisy. Existing methods can therefore suffer from weak identifiability, error accumulation, and unstable long-horizon corrections when the revealed prefix is sparse or contaminated. To address these issues, we propose STEPS, a Smooth Temporal Error Propagation Solver for TTA in time-series forecasting. STEPS reformulates forecasting TTA as a Dirichlet Boundary Value Problem on a temporal manifold, where the revealed prefix error serves as the boundary condition for the unknown future error field. Then, STEPS solves a smooth and bounded correction field in prediction space: a Local Solver propagates prefix errors under temporal smoothness, a Global Solver retrieves stable cross-window error memory and Spatiotemporal Manifold Fusion (SMF) integrates both solutions into the final correction. Across six standard benchmarks and four frozen backbones, STEPS achieves an average relative MSE reduction of 26.82% over the zero-shot backbone, exceeding the strongest compared TTA baseline by 12.77%. Additional sparse prefix and contamination tests confirm the robustness of STEPS under limited and noisy prefixes.

</details>


### [325] [Interpreting Reinforcement Learning Agents with Susceptibilities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08007)
*Chris Elliott,Einar Urdshals,David Quarel,Daniel Murfet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper extends susceptibilities (a neural network interpretability technique) to deep reinforcement learning regret analysis, showing they reveal internal model development features not visible through policy learning alone, validated with activation-steering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors want to apply susceptibilities—which study how neural network predictions change with loss perturbations—to reinforcement learning, specifically to understand internal model development beyond what's visible from learned policies alone.

Method: Generalize susceptibilities from supervised learning to deep RL regret analysis, apply to a simple gridworld with non-trivial stagewise development, use activation-steering for validation, and discuss extension to RLHF post-training.

Result: Susceptibilities in RL reveal internal features of model development in parameter space that are undetectable by studying policy development alone, as validated through activation-steering experiments.

Conclusion: Susceptibilities provide valuable interpretability insights for RL models, revealing hidden development patterns, with potential applications in RLHF post-training analysis and broader RL interpretability.

Abstract: Susceptibilities are a technique for neural network interpretability that studies the response of posterior expectation values of observables to perturbations of the loss. We generalize this construction to the setting of the regret in deep reinforcement learning and investigate the utility of susceptibilities in a simple gridworld model that nevertheless exhibits non-trivial stagewise development. We argue that susceptibilities reveal internal features of the development of the model in parameter space that one cannot detect purely by studying the development of the learned policy. We validate these results with activation-steering, and discuss the framework's extension to RLHF post-training.

</details>


### [326] [Adaptive Domain Decomposition Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Traffic State Estimation with Sparse Sensor Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08028)
*Eunhan Ka,Ludovic Leclercq,Satish V. Ukkusuri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ADD-PINN is a two-stage residual-guided framework that uses adaptive domain decomposition to improve traffic state estimation from sparse sensors while maintaining training efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for traffic state estimation tend to over-smooth shockwaves in LWR models when using sparse fixed sensor data, reducing accuracy in capturing traffic transitions.

Method: Two-stage residual-guided framework: First trains a coarse global PINN, then uses its spatial residual profile to place subdomain boundaries and initialize child subnetworks. Includes data-driven shock indicator for single-domain fallback when transition evidence is weak.

Result: Achieved lowest relative L2 error in 18/25 configurations and 14/15 sparse-sensing cases, with 2.4x faster training than XPINN baseline. Ablation shows spatial-only decomposition works best. NGSIM experiments confirm fallback mechanism works when decomposition isn't needed.

Conclusion: Residual-guided spatial decomposition is effective for offline traffic reconstruction with sparse sensors near transition regions, offering better accuracy and efficiency than standard PINN approaches.

Abstract: Traffic state estimation from sparse fixed sensors is challenging because physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) tend to over-smooth the shockwaves admitted by the Lighthill-Whitham-Richards (LWR) model. This study proposes Adaptive Domain Decomposition Physics-Informed Neural Networks (ADD-PINN), a two-stage residual-guided framework for LWR-based offline speed-field reconstruction. A coarse global PINN is first trained; its spatial residual profile is then used to place subdomain boundaries and initialize child subnetworks in a decomposition-enabled mode, while a data-driven shock indicator can retain a single-domain fallback when localized evidence of transition is weak. The primary offline I-24 MOTION evaluation spans five days, five sensor configurations, and ten seeds per configuration, yielding 1,500 runs in total. Against neural and physics-informed baselines, ADD-PINN attains the lowest relative L2 error in 18 of 25 configurations and in 14 of 15 sparse-sensing cases, while training 2.4 times faster than the extended PINN (XPINN) baseline. An ablation study supports spatial-only decomposition as an effective default for fixed-sensor traffic reconstruction in the evaluated settings. Supplementary Next Generation Simulation (NGSIM) experiments serve as a negative control: the shock indicator suppresses decomposition in all 50 runs, and the default single-domain fallback ranks first across all sensor configurations. These results support residual-guided spatial decomposition as an effective PINN-family design for offline reconstruction when sparse fixed sensing coincides with localized transition regions.

</details>


### [327] [Don't Get Your Kroneckers in a Twist: Gaussian Processes on High-Dimensional Incomplete Grids](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08036)
*Mads Greisen Højlund,August Smart Lykke-Møller,Henry Moss,Ove Christiansen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CUTS-GPR enables numerically exact Gaussian process regression with near-linear scaling in training data size and polynomial scaling in dimensionality, making high-dimensional Bayesian modeling feasible.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a longstanding challenge in computational chemistry for Bayesian modeling of high-dimensional potential energy surfaces. Traditional GPR methods struggle with scalability when dealing with large datasets (high N) and high dimensions (high D), creating a bottleneck for applications in fields like computational chemistry.

Method: CUTS-GPR combines an additive kernel with an incomplete grid structure to enable extremely fast kernel matrix-vector products. This approach exploits the mathematical structure resulting from this combination to achieve near-linear or linear scaling with training data size (N) and low-order polynomial scaling with dimensionality (D).

Result: The method demonstrates scalability with benchmarks showing billions of data points and thousands of dimensions. Full GPR calculations including hyperparameter optimization are completed in hours for N=447,265 and D=24. The approach enables Bayesian modeling of high-dimensional potential energy surfaces, solving a longstanding computational chemistry challenge.

Conclusion: CUTS-GPR provides a breakthrough solution for performing numerically exact Gaussian process regression in high-dimensional settings, with practical applications in computational chemistry for modeling potential energy surfaces that were previously computationally infeasible.

Abstract: We introduce CUTS-GPR, a new method for performing numerically exact Gaussian process regression (GPR) in high-dimensional settings. The key component of CUTS-GPR is an extremely fast kernel matrix-vector product, which exhibits near-linear or even linear scaling with the amount of training data, $N$, and low-order polynomial scaling with dimensionality, $D$. This is obtained by combining an additive kernel with an incomplete grid and exploiting the resulting structure of the kernel matrix. We demonstrate the scalability of the matrix-vector product by running benchmarks with billions of data points and thousands of dimensions. Full GPR calculations, including hyperparameter optimization, are completed in a matter of hours for $N = 447 265$ and $D = 24$. We demonstrate that our CUTS-GPR enables Bayesian modeling of high-dimensional potential energy surfaces - a longstanding challenge in computational chemistry.

</details>


### [328] [Reinforcement Learning for Exponential Utility: Algorithms and Convergence in Discounted MDPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08053)
*Gugan Thoppe,L. A. Prashanth,Ankur Naskar,Sanjay Bhat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces the first principled value-based RL algorithms for exponential-utility optimization in discounted MDPs, deriving two Q-value extensions and proving convergence guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning for exponential-utility optimization lacks principled value-based algorithms. While exponential utility functions are important for risk-sensitive decision-making, existing RL approaches don't provide solid theoretical foundations for value-based methods in this setting.

Method: The authors build on Bellman-type equations for exponential utility and derive two Q-value-style extensions. They prove these operators contract in L∞ and sup-log/Thompson metrics. They then develop two model-free algorithms: 1) a two-timescale Q-learning style algorithm with timescale separation, and 2) a one-timescale algorithm using sublinear power-law operators.

Result: The paper shows the greedy stationary policy induced by the fixed points is optimal for exponential-utility objectives among stationary policies. They prove almost-sure convergence and finite-time rates for the two-timescale algorithm. For the one-timescale algorithm, they establish convergence using arguments based on local Lipschitzness, monotonicity, homogeneity, and Dini derivatives.

Conclusion: This work establishes the first foundation for value-based reinforcement learning under exponential-utility objectives, filling a critical gap in risk-sensitive RL theory and providing rigorous algorithmic frameworks with proven convergence properties.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for exponential-utility optimization in discounted Markov decision processes (MDPs) lacks principled value-based algorithms. We address this gap in the fixed risk-aversion setting. Building on the Bellman-type equation for exponential utility studied in \cite{porteus1975optimality}, we derive two Q-value-style extensions and show that the associated operators are contractions in the $L_\infty$ and sup-log/Thompson metrics, respectively. We characterize their fixed points and prove that the induced greedy stationary policy is optimal for the exponential-utility objective among stationary policies. These structural results lead to two model-free algorithms: a two-timescale Q-learning--style algorithm, for which we establish almost-sure convergence and provide finite-time convergence rates via timescale separation, and a one-timescale algorithm governed by a sublinear power-law operator. Since the latter does not admit a global contraction in standard metrics, we prove its convergence using delicate arguments based on local Lipschitzness, monotonicity, homogeneity, and Dini derivatives, and provide a scalar finite-time analysis that highlights the challenges in obtaining convergence rates in the vector case. Our work provides a foundation for value-based RL under exponential-utility objectives.

</details>


### [329] [GRAPHLCP: Structure-Aware Localized Conformal Prediction on Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08074)
*Peyman Baghershahi,Fangxin Wang,Debmalya Mandal,Sourav Medya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRAPHLCP: A graph-localized conformal prediction framework that incorporates graph topology and dependencies for efficient uncertainty quantification in GNNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Applying conformal prediction to graph neural networks is challenging due to graphs' combinatorial nature leading to uncertain predictions and indiscriminative embeddings. Existing methods rely on embedding-space proximity which can be unreliable for graphs and yield inefficient prediction sets.

Method: Proposes GRAPHLCP, a proximity-based localized CP framework that incorporates graph topology and inter-node dependencies. Uses feature-aware densification to mitigate locality bias in sparse graphs, followed by Personalized PageRank-based kernel computation to model structural proximity. This enables topology-dependent anchor sampling and calibration weighting capturing both local and long-range dependencies.

Result: Extensive experiments on several regression and classification datasets demonstrate that GRAPHLCP guarantees marginal coverage with finite samples while efficiently attaining favorable test conditional coverage across various conditioning scenarios.

Conclusion: GRAPHLCP effectively addresses the challenges of applying conformal prediction to graph neural networks by incorporating graph topology and dependencies, providing reliable uncertainty quantification with theoretical guarantees and practical efficiency.

Abstract: Conformal prediction (CP) provides a distribution-free approach to uncertainty quantification with finite-sample guarantees. However, applying CP to graph neural networks (GNNs) remains challenging as the combinatorial nature of graphs often leads to insufficiently certain predictions and indiscriminative embeddings. Existing methods primarily rely on embedding-space proximity for localization, which can be unreliable for graphs and yield inefficient prediction sets. We propose GRAPHLCP, a proximity-based localized CP framework that explicitly incorporates graph topology and inter-node dependencies into localization and weighting. Our approach introduces a feature-aware densification step to mitigate locality bias in sparse graphs, followed by a Personalized PageRank-based kernel computation to model structural proximity. This enables topology-dependent anchor sampling and calibration weighting that captures both local and long-range dependencies. Extensive experiments on several regression and classification datasets demonstrate that GRAPHLCP guarantees marginal coverage with finite samples while efficiently attaining favorable test conditional coverage across various conditioning scenarios.

</details>


### [330] [Zero-Shot Imagined Speech Decoding via Imagined-to-Listened MEG Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08075)
*Maryam Maghsoudi,Shihab Shamma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Researchers developed a three-stage pipeline to decode imagined speech from MEG recordings by leveraging listened speech data, using trained musicians for better alignment, and achieving above-chance word decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decoding imagined speech is challenging due to scarce datasets and poor temporal alignment across subjects/sessions. Imagined speech datasets are hard to collect and align compared to listened speech recordings.

Method: Three-stage approach: 1) Train models to map imagined MEG responses to listened responses using paired recordings from trained musicians. 2) Train contrastive word decoder exclusively on listened MEG responses using multiple embedding strategies. 3) Process imagined MEG through mapping pipeline and decode using listened decoder with rank-based analysis.

Result: Imagined words are decodable significantly above chance. The approach is validated on held-out subjects, shows consistent relationships between imagining and listening neural activity, and performance improves with training data size.

Conclusion: The proof-of-concept demonstrates that leveraging listened speech data enables reliable imagined speech decoding, suggesting the approach is scalable and applicable to realistic brain-computer interface scenarios.

Abstract: Decoding imagined speech from non-invasive brain recordings is challenging because imagined datasets are scarce and difficult to align temporally across subjects and sessions In this work, we propose a new approach to the decoding of imagined speech that leverages the richer and more reliably labeled recordings during listening to speech. We collected paired listened and imagined MEG recordings to rhythmic melodic and spoken stimuli from trained musicians. Using trained musicians helped improve temporal alignment across conditions. We then developed a three-stage decoding pipeline that revealed consistent and meaningful relationships between neural activity evoked by imagining and listening to the same stimuli. First, we trained six linear and neural models to map imagined MEG responses to listened responses. We evaluated these models against a null baseline from unseen subjects to validate that the predicted-listening responses preserve stimulus-specific information. In the second stage, we trained a contrastive word decoder exclusively on the listened MEG responses, and evaluated it using four embedding strategies including semantic, acoustic, and phonetic representations. In the third stage, we process the imagined MEG responses from held-out subjects through the mapping pipeline to compute the corresponding listening responses that are then decoded by the listened decoder. Using rank-based analysis, we show that the imagined words are decodable significantly above chance. We shall report here the results of a proof-of-concept implementation to decode imagined speech, where all evaluations are performed on held-out subjects. We also demonstrate that performance improves with training data size, suggesting that this approach is scalable and can directly be made applicable to realistic brain-computer interface scenarios.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [331] [Generalising Travel Time Prediction To Varying Route Choices In Urban Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06918)
*Łukasz Gorczyca,Kacper Drozd,Michał Bujak,Rafał Kucharski*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: GenTTP is a general travel time predictor that captures varying route choices and demand patterns, going beyond traditional graph neural networks that only work for typical recurring patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous graph neural network methods are limited to typical recurring demand patterns and fail to capture varying route choices. They only approximate single demand realizations and cannot handle scenarios where the same demand can produce different network-wide outcomes based on how travelers distribute over available paths.

Method: Generalised Travel Time Predictor (GenTTP) framework that learns to uncover complex spatiotemporal traffic patterns and microscopic relationships between route choices and resulting travel times. It differentiates route choices and offers accurate flow and travel time predictions.

Result: The proposed method successfully differentiates route choices and offers accurate flow and travel time predictions, addressing the critical gap of models that can generalize across varying route assignments.

Conclusion: GenTTP addresses a critical gap in travel time prediction by developing a model that generalizes across varying route assignments, capturing how the same demand can produce substantially different network-wide outcomes depending on traveler path distribution.

Abstract: Previous methods that predict system-wide travel time, predominantly grounded in graph neural networks, remain limited to typical and recurring demand patterns. While they successfully predict future congestion following daily commute, they inherently approximate a single demand realisation and fail to capture varying route choices. In this work, we propose a Generalised Travel Time Predictor (GenTTP) that successfully differentiates route choices and offers accurate flow and travel time predictions. Our framework learns to uncover complex spatiotemporal traffic patterns and microscopic relationships between route choices and the resulting travel times. This addresses a critical gap: the lack of travel time prediction models that generalise across varying route assignments, where the same demand can produce substantially different network-wide outcomes depending on how travellers are distributed over available paths.

</details>


### [332] [The Cost of Consensus: Malignant Epistemic Herding and Adaptive Gating in Distributed Multi-Agent Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06988)
*David Farr,Iain Cruickshank,Kate Starbird,Jevin West*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper addresses how communication frequency and content affect collective belief states in distributed multi-agent systems, introducing 'epistemic alignment' as a key property that distinguishes effective from ineffective belief sharing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a critical tension between the need for coordination/communication in distributed systems and the costs of excessive communication (bandwidth, latency, operational costs). Current approaches focus on exploration and communication strategies but don't address how communication patterns jointly shape collective beliefs, which is especially important in bandwidth-constrained deployments like distributed sensing, autonomous reconnaissance, and cyber defense.

Method: The authors formalize the distinction between effective and ineffective belief sharing through the concept of 'epistemic alignment' - the degree to which agents maintain compatible internal beliefs about the environment. They demonstrate that this alignment cannot be detected using traditional coordination metrics like Jensen-Shannon Divergence or consensus rates.

Result: The paper establishes that when agents share beliefs effectively through well-designed communication, they converge on correct hypotheses; conversely, poorly designed communication can lead agents to converge confidently on wrong hypotheses, with this distinction being undetectable by standard coordination metrics.

Conclusion: The paper introduces a formal framework for understanding how communication design affects collective reasoning in distributed systems, highlighting that proper epistemic alignment is crucial for correct belief convergence and that existing metrics are insufficient for detecting whether belief sharing is working effectively.

Abstract: Distributed agents in real-world settings frequently must coordinate under uncertainty with only partial observations. Coordination is necessary to share beliefs to aid in task completion, but communication costs bandwidth, introduces latency, and if done poorly, can degrade collective reasoning. This tension is especially acute in bandwidth-constrained deployments such as distributed sensing networks, autonomous reconnaissance, and collaborative cyber defense, where excessive transmission carries direct operational costs. Existing work has focused on multi-agent exploration and communication strategies, but not on how communication frequency and content jointly shape the collective belief state. Central to this challenge is the degree to which agents maintain compatible internal beliefs about the environment, a property we term \textit{epistemic alignment}. When agents share beliefs effectively, they converge on correct hypotheses; when communication is poorly designed, agents may converge confidently on wrong ones. We formalize this distinction and show it is not detectable from coordination metrics alone such as Jensen-Shannon Divergence or rate to consensus.

</details>


### [333] [Social Theory Should Be a Structural Prior for Agentic AI: A Formal Framework for Multi-Agent Social Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07069)
*Lynnette Hui Xian Ng,Iain J. Cruickshank,Adrian Xuan Wei Lim,Kathleen M. Carley*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Multi-Agent Social Systems (MASS) framework that models agentic AI systems using social theory as structural priors, arguing that emergent AI behavior in social environments should be understood through social scientific principles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic AI systems are increasingly deployed in social environments (social media, multi-agent LLM pipelines, robotics fleets) where system behavior emerges from multi-agent interactions over time, not from individual agents alone. Social scientists have long studied emergent dynamics in human social groups, suggesting similar frameworks should apply to AI systems.

Method: The paper formalizes a Multi-Agent Social Systems (MASS) framework as a class of dynamical system of information generation, local influence and interaction structure. It introduces four structural priors anchored in social theory: strategic heterogeneity, networked-constrained dependence, co-evolution, and distributional instability.

Result: The paper demonstrates the importance of each structural prior through formal propositions and articulates a research agenda for how MASS should be modeled, evaluated and governed. The framework provides a way to understand and analyze emergent behavior in multi-agent AI systems operating in social contexts.

Conclusion: Agentic AI systems must be modeled with social theory as a structural prior, and the proposed MASS framework offers a principled approach for understanding how agents interact and influence each other to generate system-level outcomes in complex social environments.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems are increasingly deployed not in isolation, but inside social environments populated by other agents and humans, such as in social media platforms, multi-agent LLM pipelines or autonomous robotics fleets. In these settings, system behavior emerges not from individual agents alone, but from the multi-agent interactions over time. Emergent dynamics of individuals in a social group have been long studied by social scientists in human contexts. \textbf{This position paper argues that agentic AI systems must be modeled with social theory as a structural prior, and formalizes a Multi-Agent Social Systems (MASS) framework for how agents interact and influence to generate system-level outcomes.} We represent MASS as a class of dynamical system of information generation, local influence and interaction structure, formulated by four structural priors anchored in social theory: strategic heterogeneity, networked-constrained dependence, co-evolution and distributional instability. We demonstrate the importance of each structural prior through formal propositions, and articulate a research agenda for how MASS should be modeled, evaluated and governed.

</details>


### [334] [Decentralized Diffusion Policy Learning for Enhanced Exploration in Cooperative Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07101)
*Yuyang Zhang,Haldun Balim,Na Li*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: DDPL replaces Gaussian policies in multi-agent RL with expressive diffusion models for better exploration, achieving improved performance across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decentralized softmax policy gradient (DecSPG) methods in multi-agent RL use Gaussian policies which are limited in expressiveness, severely hindering exploration especially as the number of agents grows. Energy-based policies are intractable, so they need to be projected onto simpler policy classes.

Method: Proposes decentralized diffusion policy learning (DDPL), which parameterizes each agent's policy with a denoising diffusion probabilistic model that captures multi-modal action distributions. Uses importance sampling score matching (ISSM) for efficient online training with theoretical guarantees.

Result: DDPL consistently improves performance on representative continuous-action MARL benchmarks including multi-agent particle environment, multi-agent MuJoCo, IsaacLab, and JAX-reimplemented StarCraft multi-agent challenge.

Conclusion: Diffusion models provide more expressive policy parameterization for multi-agent RL, addressing the exploration limitations of Gaussian policies and enabling better performance through improved exploration capabilities.

Abstract: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) involves complex agent interactions and requires effective exploration strategies. A prominent class of MARL algorithms, decentralized softmax policy gradient (DecSPG), addresses this through energy-based policy updates. In practice, however, such energy-based policies are intractable to maintain and are commonly projected onto the Gaussian policy class. In this work, we show that the limited expressiveness of Gaussian policies severely hinders exploration in DecSPG, and this limitation worsens as the number of agents grows. To address this issue, we propose decentralized diffusion policy learning (DDPL), which parameterizes each agent's policy with a denoising diffusion probabilistic model, an expressive generative model that captures multi-modal action distributions for enhanced exploration. DDPL enables efficient online training of diffusion policies via importance sampling score matching (ISSM), a novel training method with theoretical guarantee. We evaluate DDPL on representative continuous-action MARL benchmarks, including multi-agent particle environment, multi-agent MuJoCo, IsaacLab, and JAX-reimplemented StarCraft multi-agent challenge, and observe consistently improved performance.

</details>


### [335] [Rethinking Priority Scheduling for Sequential Multi-Agent Decision Making in Stackelberg Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07240)
*Xiangyu Liu,Liang Zhang,Bo Jin,Ziqi Wei*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Proposes Hierarchical Priority Adjustment method to optimize agent decision order in N-level Stackelberg Games, achieving better performance on multi-agent control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current N-level Stackelberg Game applications use default environment-provided agent decision orders, but it's unclear if order affects the final equilibrium point. Changing decision order typically creates an overdetermined system that shifts equilibrium unless special conditions are met.

Method: Hierarchical Priority Adjustment (HPA): Upper policy selects optimal decision order dynamically; Lower-level agents execute strategies in Spatio-Temporal Sequential Markov Game (STMG); Slow-fast update scheme with shared intrinsic rewards derived from upper policy's advantage function.

Result: HPA outperforms benchmark algorithms on high-precision control tasks including multi-agent MuJoCo, and robustly adapts to changing environments.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that optimizing agent decision order is crucial for N-level Stackelberg Games, with HPA effectively adapting to changing environments and outperforming benchmark algorithms.

Abstract: Current research applying N-level Stackelberg Game to multi-agent systems often uses the default decision order of agents provided by the environment. However, this raises the question: does the order of agents necessarily affect the final equilibrium point of the game? To address this, we formally analyze the N-level Stackelberg Game, where changing the order in which agents make decisions typically leads to an overdetermined system. As a result, the equilibrium point shifts unless special structural conditions are satisfied. Based on this analysis, we propose the Hierarchical Priority Adjustment (HPA) method, which adjusts and selects the agents' decision order. At the upper level, an upper policy dynamically selects the optimal decision order of agents based on the current game state. At the lower level, agents execute strategies in the Spatio-Temporal Sequential Markov Game (STMG) according to the selected order. To coordinate learning across time scales, we employ a slow-fast update scheme with shared intrinsic rewards derived from the advantage function of the upper policy. Experimental results on high-precision control tasks, including multi-agent MuJoCo, show that HPA outperforms benchmark algorithms and robustly adapts to changing environments. These results highlight the crucial role of optimizing the agents' decision order in N-level Stackelberg Game.

</details>


### [336] [OrchJail: Jailbreaking Tool-Calling Text-to-Image Agents by Orchestration-Guided Fuzzing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07414)
*Jianming Chen,Yawen Wang,Junjie Wang,Zhe Liu,Qing Wang,Fanjiang Xu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: OrchJail is a fuzzing framework that jailbreaks tool-calling text-to-image agents by exploiting high-risk tool-orchestration patterns, rather than just surface-level text manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool-calling T2I agents create new safety vulnerabilities through multi-step tool orchestration, where individually benign steps combine to produce harmful outputs. Traditional prompt-only jailbreak techniques are insufficient for this new attack surface.

Method: OrchJail learns from successful jailbreak tool-calling traces and their causal relationships to prompt wording, then uses this knowledge to guide fuzzing search toward prompts likely to trigger unsafe multi-step tool behaviors.

Result: Extensive experiments show OrchJail improves jailbreak effectiveness and efficiency across representative tool-calling T2I agents, achieving higher attack success rates, better image fidelity, lower query costs, and robustness against common defenses.

Conclusion: Tool orchestration represents a critical, previously unexplored attack surface in T2I agents. OrchJail provides a novel framework for uncovering safety risks in these systems by focusing on multi-step tool-behavior vulnerabilities.

Abstract: Tool-calling text-to-image (T2I) agents can plan and execute multi-step tool chains to accomplish complex generation and editing queries. However, this capability introduces a new safety attack surface: harmful outputs may arise from tool orchestration, where individually benign steps combine into unsafe results, making prompt-only jailbreak techniques insufficient. We present OrchJail, an orchestration-guided fuzzing framework for jailbreaking tool-calling T2I agents. Its core idea is to exploit high-risk tool-orchestration patterns: by learning from successful jailbreak tool-calling traces and their causal relationships to prompt wording, OrchJail directly guides the fuzzing search toward prompts that are more likely to trigger unsafe multi-step tool behaviors, rather than relying on surface-level textual perturbations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OrchJail improves jailbreak effectiveness and efficiency across representative toolcalling T2I agents, achieving higher attack success rates, better image fidelity, and lower query costs, while remaining robust against common jailbreak defenses. Our work highlights tool orchestration as a critical, previously unexplored attack surface and provides a novel framework for uncovering safety risks in T2I agents.

</details>


### [337] [Synchronizing Minds through Collective Predictive Coding: A Computational Model of Parent-Infant Homeostatic Co-Regulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07524)
*Yushi Tsubamoto,Takato Horii*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: A computational model of parent-infant co-regulation using active interoceptive inference and a naming game shows that inter-brain synchrony can emerge from interaction without requiring fully shared world models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current computational accounts don't adequately explain how inter-brain synchrony (IBS) arises between agents with asymmetric knowledge and local sensory access, as seen in parent-infant interactions.

Method: A POMDP-based active interoceptive inference model combined with Metropolis-Hastings Naming Game (MHNG), with asymmetric knowledge: parent knows action effects but must interpret infant signals; infant senses internal state but must learn action effects.

Result: MHNG-mediated interaction regulated infant's visceral state better than one-sided control, with rapid latent-state alignment emerging earlier than convergence of learned generative models.

Conclusion: Representational synchrony doesn't require fully shared world models; this model offers a computational basis for inter-brain alignment compatible with hyperscanning observations.

Abstract: Inter-brain synchrony (IBS) observed in real-time dyadic interactions, including parent--infant exchanges, suggests that two agents come to share aligned latent representations through interaction. Yet computational accounts of how such alignment can arise between agents that have only local sensory access and asymmetric internal knowledge remain underdeveloped. We propose a constructive model of parent--infant homeostatic co-regulation that integrates a POMDP formulation of active interoceptive inference with the Metropolis--Hastings Naming Game (MHNG) derived from the Collective Predictive Coding (CPC) hypothesis. In our model, the parent observes the infant only through an exteroceptive signal while the infant directly senses its own interoceptive state; the two agents agree on regulatory actions through a shared communicative variable whose acceptance is determined by a locally computable Metropolis--Hastings probability. The agents are further endowed with asymmetric generative-model knowledge: the parent knows how actions transform visceral states but must learn what the infant's body is communicating, whereas the infant perceives its visceral state directly but must learn how actions affect it. In a $6 \times 6$ visceral-state grid world, MHNG-mediated interaction regulated the infant's visceral state more adaptively than one-sided control conditions, and the two posteriors became rapidly aligned. Notably, this latent-state alignment emerged far earlier than the convergence of the learned generative matrices, indicating that representational synchrony does not presuppose fully shared world models. These results offer a minimal constructive account of latent-state alignment compatible with IBS reported in hyperscanning studies and support CPC as a candidate computational basis for inter-brain alignment.

</details>


### [338] [Emergence of Social Reality of Emotion through a Social Allostasis Model with Dynamic Interpretants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07761)
*Kentaro Nomura,Yushi Tsubamoto,Takato Horii*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Simulation shows how emotion concepts emerge as social reality through interactive agents adapting interoceptive preferences and symbol interpretations via active inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To computationally simulate how social reality emerges from consensus on emotion concepts, testing the theory of constructed emotion through interactive agent models.

Method: Computational model integrating symbol emergence with degrees of freedom in symbol interpretation and active inference. Two agents receive interoceptive signals, exchange inferred symbols, and adapt bodily control goals and symbol interpretations to each other.

Result: Experimental results show convergence of interoceptive prior preferences and symbol probability distributions between the two agents, confirming emergence of social reality grounded in social consensus.

Conclusion: The study successfully demonstrates computational emergence of social reality through interactive adaptation of emotion concepts, supporting the theory of constructed emotion.

Abstract: The theory of constructed emotion defines social reality as the community-level consensus on emotion concepts assigned to interoceptive sensations arising from bodily allostasis and social interaction. In this study, we simulate this emergence process using a computational model that integrates symbol emergence with degrees of freedom in symbol interpretation and active inference. Two agents receive interoceptive signals, exchange inferred symbols, and simultaneously adapt their bodily control goals and symbol interpretations to each other. Experimental results show that the interoceptive prior preferences and symbol probability distributions of the two agents converge, confirming the emergence of social reality grounded in social consensus.

</details>
